#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a two-phase AC servo motor. Discuss the role of the reference and control windings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the conditions for a pure circular rotating magnetic field in a two-phase servo motor. Show that the field magnitude is then constant. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core carrying two windings, the **reference winding (R)** and the **control winding (C)**, displaced spatially by **90° electrical** in slots.
- **Rotor:** Either a **squirrel-cage rotor** (high-resistance aluminium bars short-circuued by end rings) or a **drag-cup rotor** (thin non-magnetic aluminium cup rotating in the air gap between an inner and outer stator core). The drag-cup type has the *lowest inertia* and the *fastest response*.
- **Shaft:** Coupled to a precision **encoder/resolver** for feedback.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The reference winding is energised from a **fixed AC supply**; a **capacitor** in series shifts its current by ~90°.
- The control winding receives a **variable-amplitude, variable-phase** voltage from a servo amplifier.
- The two pulsating fields add vectorially to produce a **rotating magnetic field** whose magnitude is proportional to $V_c$.
- The rotating field cuts the rotor, induces currents, and produces a torque $T \propto V_c$.
- The rotor accelerates until developed torque equals load torque + losses; it follows any change in $V_c$ within a few milliseconds.
- When $V_c = 0$ the field collapses and the rotor comes to a *prompt stop* — enabling positional accuracy.

**Role summary (1 mark):** The **reference winding** sets up a fixed-amplitude quadrature flux component; the **control winding** modulates the resultant flux vector's *magnitude* and *direction*, effectively steering the motor.

---

**(b) — Derivation of Pure Circular Field Condition (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* Write the MMF equations for the two windings (1 mark):

$$
F_R(\theta,t) = F_R \cos(\omega t) \cos(p\theta)
$$

$$
F_C(\theta,t) = F_C \cos(\omega t - \phi) \sin(p\theta)
$$

*Step 2:* Sum to get the resultant MMF (1 mark):

$$
F(\theta,t) = F_R \cos(\omega t)\cos(p\theta) + F_C \cos(\omega t - \phi)\sin(p\theta)
$$

*Step 3:* Expand using trigonometric identities (1 mark):

$$
F(\theta,t) = \tfrac{1}{2}F_R[\cos(\omega t - p\theta) + \cos(\omega t + p\theta)] + \tfrac{1}{2}F_C[\sin(\omega t - p\theta + \phi) + \sin(\omega t + p\theta - \phi)]
$$

*Step 4:* Identify forward and backward components (1 mark):

$$
F_{fwd} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_R^{\,2} + F_C^{\,2} + 2F_R F_C \cos\phi} \cdot \cos(\omega t - p\theta - \psi_f)
$$

$$
F_{bwd} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_R^{\,2} + F_C^{\,2} - 2F_R F_C \cos\phi} \cdot \cos(\omega t + p\theta - \psi_b)
$$

*Step 5:* For a **pure circular field**, the backward component must vanish → $F_R = F_C$ and $\phi = 90°$ (2 marks).

*Step 6:* Under this condition, the resultant is $F = F_R \cos(\omega t - p\theta - \psi)$ — a constant-amplitude wave rotating at $\omega$ (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the linearised torque equation $T = k_t V_c - k_\omega \omega_r$ and Newton's torque equation, derive the **mechanical time constant** of an AC servo motor. Explain how it can be reduced. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the torque–speed characteristics of a two-phase AC servo motor for at least three values of control voltage. Explain why the characteristic is approximately linear and why a high rotor resistance is used. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) — Mechanical Time Constant Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* Write Newton's equation (1 mark):

$$
T_{em} = J\dfrac{d\omega_r}{dt} + B\omega_r + T_L
$$

*Step 2:* Substitute the linearised servo torque $T_{em} = k_t V_c - k_\omega \omega_r$ (1 mark):

$$
k_t V_c - k_\omega \omega_r = J\dfrac{d\omega_r}{dt} + B\omega_r + T_L
$$

*Step 3:* Rearrange (1 mark):

$$
k_t V_c - T_L = J\dfrac{d\omega_r}{dt} + (B + k_\omega)\omega_r
$$

*Step 4:* For load-free step response with $V_c$ applied, the natural response decays with $e^{-t/\tau_m}$ where (2 marks):

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_m = \dfrac{J}{B + k_\omega} = \dfrac{J R_r}{k_t^{\,2}}\;}
$$

*Step 5:* Methods to **reduce $\tau_m$** (2 marks — list any two):
- Use a **drag-cup rotor** to minimise moment of inertia $J$.
- Use **high-resistivity rotor alloys** (aluminium with copper traces) to increase $R_r$.
- Use a **high-energy reference field** (or replace with permanent-magnet field) to raise $k_t$.
- Reduce friction and windage to lower $B$.

---

**(b) — Torque–Speed Characteristic (7 marks):**

*Diagram description (3 marks):* Plot speed $\omega_r$ on the x-axis and torque $T$ on the y-axis. Draw a family of **straight lines** with **negative slope**, all converging at the same point on the **torque axis** (the slope of each line being different). Label three lines for $V_c = V_{max}, \;0.5V_{max}, \;0.25 V_{max}$, and one coinciding with the horizontal axis for $V_c = 0$.

*Reason for linearity (2 marks):* High rotor resistance flattens and straightens the normally hyperbolic torque–slip curve. With $R_r \gg X$, the slip at maximum torque shifts close to 1, and the operating region (0 ≤ s ≤ 0.5) lies on the *linear descending part* of the curve.

*Why high $R_r$ is used (2 marks):* To obtain (i) a linear, stable torque–voltage relationship, (ii) elimination of the breakdown-torque peak that would cause instability, (iii) a positive, controllable braking torque when the control voltage is reduced, and (iv) acceptable starting torque at standstill.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AC Servo Motor Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 90° spatial displacement** between reference and control windings — students often state they are "two separate windings" without specifying the *spatial* 90° displacement. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing rotor resistance with rotor inductance** when discussing the linear torque–speed curve. The linearity arises from $R_r$ being *large*, not $L_r$.
> 3. **Drawing a single torque–speed curve** instead of a *family* of straight lines for different $V_c$. A 7-mark question on characteristics *must* show at least three curves.
> 4. **Not stating the time constant numerically** in derivations. Examiners expect $\tau_m = J/B_{eff}$ in closed form.
> 5. **Skipping the control-signal polarity reversal** in working explanations — the ability to reverse direction by reversing $V_c$ polarity is a *core* property worth 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting units** in derivations (e.g., writing $T = k_t V_c$ without "N·m = (N·m/V)·V") leads to a ½-mark deduction in long-answer solutions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- AC servo motors are **two-phase induction machines** built for *closed-loop position/speed control*, not for continuous-duty industrial drives.
- Two stator windings: **Reference (fixed supply)** and **Control (variable supply)**, **spatially 90° apart**.
- **Phase-shifting capacitor** in series with the reference winding ensures ~90° electrical phase shift between the two winding currents.
- A pure circular rotating field requires **$F_R = F_C$ and $\phi = 90°$** — both conditions must hold simultaneously.
- **High rotor resistance** → linear torque–speed curve, no breakdown peak, stable operation.
- **Two rotor types:** cage (industrial) and **drag-cup** (high-precision, low inertia, aerospace).
- **Linearised torque equation:** $T = k_t V_c - k_\omega \omega_r$ — the basis of all transfer-function derivations.
- **Mechanical time constant:** $\tau_m = \dfrac{J R_r}{k_t^{\,2}}$ — the *figure of merit*; minimised by low $J$, high $R_r$, high $k_t$.
- **Zero-control stopping** (dead-band-free) is the unique property that makes it a *servo* motor and not an ordinary induction motor.
- **Direction reversal** is achieved by **reversing the polarity (180° phase) of the control voltage**.
- **Applications** span robotics, CNC, aerospace, radar, and semiconductor manufacturing.
- **Exam-favourite numericals** typically ask for: $N_s$, $\tau_m$, the linearised $T$–$\omega$ relation, or identification of the operating slip.
- Always draw a **family of straight lines** for torque–speed, not a single curve.
- Always state **units** in derivations — examiners are strict in KTU 2024 scheme valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stepper Motors: Variable Reluctance, Permanent Magnet, and Hybrid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motors: Variable Reluctance, Permanent Magnet, and Hybrid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Stepper Motor?

A **stepper motor** is a **brushless, synchronous electromagnetic actuator** that converts a train of discrete digital input pulses into precise, discrete mechanical shaft rotations. Each electrical pulse supplied to its stator windings causes the rotor to advance by a precisely fixed angular displacement known as the **step angle (β)**. Because position is directly proportional to the number of input pulses, the stepper motor is inherently a *digital-to-mechanical transducer* and forms the workhorse of **open-loop position control systems** in modern mechatronics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRUT503 / Module 1):**
> A stepper motor is a special type of synchronous motor in which the rotor moves in discrete angular steps in response to a train of input pulses. The magnitude of each step (step angle) is determined by the number of stator phases and the construction of the rotor.

The cumulative angular displacement of the rotor shaft after **N** input pulses is given by the simple, high-yield relationship:

$$\theta = N \cdot \beta$$

This linear, pulse-count-based positioning is the reason stepper motors are classified as **incremental motion devices**, distinct from conventional continuous-rotation AC or DC machines.

## 1.2 The Three Major Families

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically groups stepper motors into three principal topologies:

| Topology | Rotor Composition | Step Range | Key Trait |
|----------|-------------------|------------|-----------|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft iron, salient toothed | 5° – 15° | No rotor magnet, cheap |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized PM | 30° – 90° | Rotor carries the field |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | PM + toothed iron caps | 0.9° – 5° | Combines both advantages |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Watch Hand"**
> Imagine the second hand of a quartz wristwatch. It does **not** sweep continuously like an analog meter; it jerks forward in *discrete* 6° jumps once per second. A stepper motor behaves identically, only its "ticks" are electronically commanded by digital pulses rather than a quartz crystal. If the input pulse train stops, the rotor *holds* its position (holding torque) — just as a stopped watch hand remains still.

> [!NOTE]
> A second analogy is a **combination lock**: each digit input produces a precise, repeatable mechanical displacement, and the lock can be "parked" at any digit combination with full mechanical stiffness.

## 1.4 Why Stepper Motors? — Engineering Relevance

Stepper motors are indispensable in applications demanding **precision, open-loop simplicity, and digital compatibility**:

- **CNC machines and 3D printers** (X-Y-Z axis positioning)
- **Robotic joint actuators**
- **Printers, plotters, and scanners** (paper/head feed)
- **Hard disk drives** (head positioning)
- **Medical infusion pumps and analytical instruments**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** A stepper motor **does not require a position sensor (encoder/resolver)** in open-loop mode, which dramatically reduces system cost and complexity compared to a servomotor drive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor

### 2.1.1 Construction
- **Stator:** Salient-pole laminated silicon steel stack carrying concentrated phase windings. Most common is the **3-stack, 3-phase** or **4-stack, 4-phase** multi-stack configuration.
- **Rotor:** Plain **soft iron, salient toothed cylinder** — *no permanent magnet, no rotor winding*. The rotor has uniform magnetic permeability and depends entirely on geometry to create magnetic saliency.
- **Shaft:** Non-magnetic stainless steel for mechanical support.

### 2.1.2 Operating Principle — Minimum Reluctance Path
When a stator phase is energised, a magnetic field is established across the air-gap. The rotor — being free to rotate — automatically aligns so that the **magnetic reluctance of the flux path is minimised** (i.e., rotor teeth align directly under the excited stator teeth). Sequentially energising the phases in **ABCD, BCDA, CDAB, DABC** order produces continuous rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it rotates:** The torque produced is purely **reluctance torque** given by $T = \tfrac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \,\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$. Energy stored in the air-gap decreases as the rotor approaches the aligned position, and this energy difference is converted to mechanical work.

### 2.1.3 Multi-Stack VR Stepper — Stacking Principle
A single-stack VR motor gives a very large step angle. To achieve fine resolution, multiple *stator stacks* (each carrying one phase) are mounted axially on a common shaft, with the **rotor tooth axes progressively displaced** between stacks. The result is a finer effective step angle.

## 2.2 Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor

### 2.2.1 Construction
- **Stator:** Salient poles with concentrated phase windings (typically 2-phase bifilar, or 4-phase).
- **Rotor:** Cylindrical **axially-magnetized permanent magnet** (Alnico or rare-earth). The N and S poles of the magnet form the salient pole pairs.
- No teeth on rotor; the magnetic poles of the PM act as the salient rotor.

### 2.2.2 Operating Principle — Magnetic Attraction/Repulsion
When a stator phase is excited, the resulting stator field either **attracts** or **repels** the permanent magnet poles. Sequential switching rotates the rotor by a step. The torque arises from interaction of stator field and PM field — i.e., **magnet alignment torque** plus a small reluctance component.

### 2.2.3 Characteristic Features
- Step angle typically **45° or 90°** (large, hence low resolution).
- **Excellent holding torque** even at zero pulse rate (rotor is magnetically locked by PM).
- **Better damping** than VR because eddy currents and hysteresis in the PM pole faces absorb oscillation.
- Smaller step angle variants use **skewed rotor poles** to reduce cogging.

## 2.3 Hybrid (HB) Stepper Motor

### 2.3.1 Construction — The "Best of Both Worlds"
The hybrid rotor is the most refined in the family:
1. A **cylindrical axially-magnetized permanent magnet** is mounted centrally on the shaft.
2. Two **identical toothed soft-iron end-caps** are pressed onto the ends of the magnet.
3. The teeth of the two end-caps are **displaced by exactly half a tooth pitch (½ × 360°/N_r)** relative to each other.
4. The N-pole cap and S-pole cap therefore act as two intermeshed salient-pole structures.

### 2.3.2 Operating Principle
- The PM magnetises the two end-caps with **opposite polarities** (one end N, other end S).
- When a stator phase is excited, the **N-cap teeth are pulled toward the energised stator pole**, while the **S-cap teeth are simultaneously repelled** — this doubling of active teeth per phase halves the effective step.
- The torque has **both PM and reluctance components**, making it the highest-torque, finest-resolution type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question:** *"Why are hybrid stepper motors preferred for high-precision applications?"* — Answer: combination of PM flux (high torque) and toothed construction (fine resolution) gives the smallest step angle (0.9° / 1.8°) with the largest holding torque per unit volume.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table completely. Every KTU 2024 / 2023 / 2022 Part B question on stepper motors draws from these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Step angle (general)** | $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N}$ | $N$ = steps/rev | Foundation equation |
| 2 | **Steps per revolution** | $N = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\beta}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of (1) |
| 3 | **VR multi-stack step angle** | $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_{r}}$ | $m$ = phases, $N_r$ = rotor teeth/stack | Each stack offset |
| 4 | **PM step angle** | $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{2 \, N_{p}}$ | $N_p$ = no. of PM pole pairs | 2 pulses per pole pair |
| 5 | **Hybrid step angle** | $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_{s} - N_{r}}$ | $N_s, N_r$ = stator / rotor tooth counts | Tooth-pitch dependent |
| 6 | **Rotor position (open-loop)** | $\theta = N_{pulse} \cdot \beta$ | $\theta$ in degrees/rad | Cumulative |
| 7 | **Synchronous speed** | $n_{s} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = poles | Stepping frequency $f = \dfrac{N \cdot s}{60}$ |
| 8 | **Mechanical speed** | $n = \dfrac{\beta \cdot f_{step}}{6}$ | $f_{step}$ in pulses/s, $n$ in rpm | Direct from (7) |
| 9 | **Reluctance torque** | $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | $L$ = phase inductance, $\theta$ in rad | VR motors only |
| 10 | **Holding torque** | $T_{h} \propto \phi \, I$ | $\phi$ = air-gap flux, $I$ = current | Torque at standstill |
| 11 | **Stepping frequency** | $f_{step} = \dfrac{1}{T_{step}}$ | $T_{step}$ = pulse period (s) | Digital rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Why $|i|^{2}$ and not $i$?**
> The torque equation $T = \tfrac{1}{2} i^{2} \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ shows that VR torque depends on the **square** of current. Therefore VR stepper motors are insensitive to current polarity — a great simplification because the drive electronics need not be bipolar.

## 2.5 Comparative Engineering Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Comparison Question (frequently asked):**

| Parameter | VR | PM | Hybrid |
|-----------|----|----|--------|
| Step angle | 5° – 15° | 45° – 90° | **0.9° – 5° (best)** |
| Holding torque | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| Rotor inertia | Low | Medium | Higher |
| Damping | Poor | Good | Good |
| Cost | Cheapest | Medium | Most expensive |
| Power consumption at standstill | Lower (no PM) | Higher (PM always magnetised) | Higher |
| Resolution | Low | Very low | **Excellent** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Step Angle for a Multi-Stack VR Stepper

### Statement
Show that for an $m$-stack variable reluctance stepper with $N_{r}$ rotor teeth per stack, the step angle is $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_{r}}$.

### Step 1 — Define the tooth pitch
The angle between two adjacent rotor teeth (tooth pitch) is:

$$
\tau_{r} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_{r}}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the electrical offset between adjacent stacks
In a multi-stack construction, the rotor teeth of stack-$k$ are angularly displaced by $\dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_{r}}$ relative to stack-$(k-1)$. This is done to ensure that when the rotor of one stack is aligned, the rotor of the next stack is offset by exactly one *electrical step*.

### Step 3 — Count the steps per mechanical revolution
One complete mechanical revolution (360°) must be divided by the smallest angular increment commanded by one phase transition. Because there are $m$ stacks and $N_{r}$ teeth per stack, the number of *commutations* per revolution is:

$$
N = m \cdot N_{r}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the definition of step angle
By the fundamental step-angle definition:

$$
\beta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_{r}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** Take $m = 3$ phases and $N_{r} = 12$ teeth/stack. Then $\beta = 360^{\circ}/36 = 10^{\circ}$. For a typical $m=4, N_{r}=25$ hybrid rotor, $\beta = 360^{\circ}/(4 \times 25) = 3.6^{\circ}$. These values agree with standard industrial motors.

## 3.2 Derivation of Step Angle for a Permanent Magnet Stepper

### Statement
A PM stepper has $N_{p}$ pole pairs. Show that $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{2 \, N_{p}}$.

### Step 1 — Total electrical poles
A pole pair comprises one N and one S pole, so the total number of magnet poles is $2N_{p}$.

### Step 2 — Pulses required to traverse one pole pair
The rotor must be advanced from alignment with a N-pole stator tooth to alignment with the next stator tooth of the *same* magnetic polarity. Because each pole pair has both an N and an S tooth, **2 pulses** are needed to sweep across one full pole pair.

### Step 3 — Total steps per revolution
A full revolution contains $N_{p}$ pole pairs, hence:

$$
N = 2 \, N_{p}
$$

### Step 4 — Final step angle

$$
\beta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{2 \, N_{p}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** For a 2-phase, 4-pole PM stepper ($N_{p}=2$), $\beta = 360^{\circ}/4 = 90^{\circ}$, the classic "big-step" PM motor.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Reluctance-Torque Equation

### Statement
For a salient-pole magnetic circuit, derive $T = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.

### Step 1 — Co-energy in a single-phase inductor
Magnetic co-energy stored in a phase winding of inductance $L(\theta)$ carrying current $i$ is:

$$
W'_{f} = \frac{1}{2} \, L(\theta) \, i^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Mechanical work by virtual displacement
If the rotor moves by an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ at constant current (linear magnetic system), the mechanical work done is:

$$
\delta W_{m} = T \, d\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Energy conservation
This mechanical work equals the decrease in field co-energy:

$$
T \, d\theta = -dW'_{f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = -\frac{\partial W'_{f}}{\partial \theta}
$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate
Differentiating the co-energy expression with respect to $\theta$ (with $i$ constant):

$$
T = -\frac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

The negative sign is dropped by the convention that $T$ is taken positive in the direction of decreasing reluctance, giving the standard **reluctance-torque equation**:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \frac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Torque is generated *only* in regions where inductance varies with rotor position. The aligned position (minimum reluctance, maximum $L$) produces **zero torque**, and the unaligned position (minimum $L$, maximum $dL/d\theta$) gives the **maximum starting torque**.

## 3.4 Derivation of Stepping Frequency vs. Shaft Speed

### Statement
Relate the electrical pulse rate $f_{step}$ (pulses per second) to the mechanical speed $n$ (rpm) of a stepper with step angle $\beta$.

### Step 1 — Angular displacement per second
Each pulse advances the shaft by $\beta$ degrees. The pulses per second give:

$$
\omega_{mech} = \beta^{\circ} \cdot f_{step} \;\;\; \text{[degrees/s]}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to rpm
One revolution = 360° and 1 minute = 60 seconds:

$$
n = \frac{\beta \cdot f_{step}}{360} \times 60 = \frac{\beta \, f_{step}}{6} \;\;\; \text{[rpm]}
$$

### Step 3 — Inverse — pulse rate for desired speed

$$
f_{step} = \frac{6 \, n}{\beta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU numerical:** A 1.8° hybrid stepper must run at 120 rpm. Find $f_{step}$.
> $f_{step} = \dfrac{6 \times 120}{1.8} = 400 \; \text{pulses per second} = 400 \; \text{Hz}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — VR vs PM vs Hybrid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024-Style 7-Mark Comparison Question (model answer outline):**

| Attribute | Variable Reluctance | Permanent Magnet | Hybrid |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|--------|
| Rotor material | Soft iron, toothed | Axial PM | PM + 2 toothed caps |
| Source of torque | Reluctance variation | PM-stator field interaction | Both |
| Step angle range | 5°–15° | 45°–90° | 0.9°–5° |
| Holding torque | Lowest (no PM) | High | **Highest** |
| Damping | Poor (no rotor damping) | Good (PM losses) | Good |
| Inertia | Lowest | Medium | Medium |
| Detent torque | Lowest | High | High |
| Open-loop accuracy | Moderate | Low (large steps) | **High** |
| Typical applications | Cheap printers, instruments | Clocks, low-resolution drives | CNC, robotics, 3D printers |
| Cost per step resolution | Poor | Poorest | **Best (lowest cost per step)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Single-Stack VR Stepper

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional/architectural layout** of a 2-stack, 2-phase variable reluctance stepper (the cleanest visual for KTU board diagrams).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Supply 5V to 24V] --> B[Microcontroller Pulse Generator]
    B -->|Pulse Train| C[Stepper Drive Circuit Transistor H-Bridge]
    C -->|Phase A Current| D1[Stator Stack 1 Phase A Winding]
    C -->|Phase B Current| D2[Stator Stack 2 Phase B Winding]
    D1 --> E1[Rotor 1 Soft Iron Toothed Disc Stack 1]
    D2 --> E2[Rotor 2 Soft Iron Toothed Disc Stack 2 Offset by Half Pitch]
    E1 --> F1[Mechanical Shaft Common to Both Stacks]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G[Output Mechanical Step Increments of Beta Degrees]
    D1 -.->|Reluctance Torque| E1
    D2 -.->|Reluctance Torque| E2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The microcontroller produces the pulse train; the H-bridge switches each phase; the rotor of each stack aligns to the energised stator; the two rotor discs share a single shaft but are angularly offset to produce the fine step angle.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Hybrid Stepper

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Step Pulse Input] --> L[Logic Sequencer Phase Commutator]
    L --> P1[Phase A Driver H-Bridge]
    L --> P2[Phase B Driver H-Bridge]
    P1 --> SA[Stator Phase A Winding]
    P2 --> SB[Stator Phase B Winding]
    SA --> R1[Front Iron Cap N Pole Toothed]
    SB --> R2[Rear Iron Cap S Pole Toothed]
    PM[Axial Permanent Magnet] --> R1
    PM --> R2
    R1 --> SH[Common Shaft with Half Tooth Pitch Offset]
    R2 --> SH
    SH --> O[Output Rotation Step Angle Beta = 0point9 to 1point8 degrees]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Constructional insight encoded in the diagram:** The single axially-magnetised PM magnetises *both* end-caps with opposite polarity, and the half-tooth-pitch offset is what halves the effective step angle compared to a single-cap PM motor.

## 4.3 Phase Excitation State Machine — Stepper Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST0[State 0 Phase A ON Phase B OFF] -->|Next Pulse| ST1[State 1 Phase A ON Phase B ON]
    ST1 -->|Next Pulse| ST2[State 2 Phase A OFF Phase B ON]
    ST2 -->|Next Pulse| ST3[State 3 Phase A OFF Phase B OFF Half Step]
    ST3 -->|Next Pulse| ST0
    ST0 -.->|Rotor Angle| RA0[Theta equals 0]
    ST1 -.->|Rotor Angle| RA1[Theta equals Beta by 2]
    ST2 -.->|Rotor Angle| RA2[Theta equals Beta]
    ST3 -.->|Rotor Angle| RA3[Theta equals 3 Beta by 2]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU interpretation:** This is the **half-stepping** excitation sequence. The motor advances by $\beta/2$ per state change instead of $\beta$, effectively doubling the resolution. The states labelled "all OFF" still produce detent torque from the PM (in PM and hybrid motors) and from rotor saliency (in VR motors), so the rotor is held firmly between active steps.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Open-Loop Stepper Drive System

```mermaid
graph LR
    CTRL[Controller Microcontroller PC PLC] -->|Pulse Train and Direction| DRV[Power Drive H-Bridge Chopper]
    DRV -->|Phase Currents| MOT[Stepper Motor VR or PM or Hybrid]
    MOT -->|Mechanical Output| LD[Load Linear or Rotary]
    MOT -.Feedback Optional Closed Loop with Encoder.-> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted line indicates that stepper motors can run **open-loop** (no encoder needed) — a key advantage for cost-sensitive applications — but can be upgraded to **closed-loop** with a shaft encoder for stall detection in high-reliability CNC systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a stepper motor and a servomotor in terms of construction, control, and feedback requirement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Stepper Motor | Servomotor |
|---------|---------------|------------|
| Construction | Open-loop, brushless, salient-pole stator & rotor | Closed-loop DC/AC motor with encoder |
| Control | Pulse train → fixed step angle | Analogue reference voltage/current |
| Feedback | **Not required** (open-loop) | **Mandatory** (encoder/resolver) |
| Holding at standstill | Holds by detent/holding torque | Holds only if continuously energised |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher due to feedback sensor |

*(1 mark each for any three valid points — full marks for the comparison table above.)*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Why is the rotor of a variable reluctance stepper motor made of soft iron without any permanent magnet or winding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. A soft-iron rotor has **uniform permeability but varying reluctance with angular position** because of its toothed/salient geometry — this is the *only* property required to generate reluctance torque. *(1 mark)*
2. Absence of PM means the rotor is **magnetically neutral**, so the rotor aligns purely to the position of **minimum reluctance** of the excited stator phase — no residual magnetism interferes with positioning. *(1 mark)*
3. No rotor winding simplifies construction, eliminates slip rings/brushes, and reduces rotor inertia — improving step response and acceleration capability. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B carries 14 marks. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each are presented. The student attempts *one* full question of their choice.

---

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. Derive the expression for the step angle in terms of the number of rotor teeth and stator phases. Compare VR stepper with Permanent Magnet stepper. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working and Step-Angle Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Construction (3 marks)**
A VR stepper consists of:
- A **stator** with salient poles carrying concentrated phase windings (typically 3 or 4 phases).
- A **rotor** made of soft, high-permeability silicon steel laminations with salient teeth — *no permanent magnet, no rotor winding*.
- For high resolution, the motor is built as a **multi-stack** assembly with $m$ stator stacks mounted axially on a common shaft, each stack having $N_{r}$ rotor teeth.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks)**
When phase-A of the stator is energised, a magnetic field is established. The rotor rotates to the position where the **magnetic reluctance of the flux path is minimum** — i.e., the rotor teeth align directly under the excited stator teeth. Sequential energisation A → B → C → D → A produces **stepwise rotation in the forward direction**, while reverse sequencing produces reverse rotation.

**Step 3 — Step-Angle Derivation (2 marks)**
Each stack contributes a tooth pitch of $\tau_{r} = 360^{\circ}/N_{r}$. Across $m$ stacks, the rotor moves in increments of $360^{\circ}/(m \cdot N_{r})$ per phase transition. Therefore:

$$
\beta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_{r}}
$$

For $m = 3$ and $N_{r} = 12$: $\beta = 360^{\circ}/36 = 10^{\circ}$.

#### Part (b) — Comparison of VR vs PM Stepper (7 marks)

| Parameter | Variable Reluctance | Permanent Magnet |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| Rotor | Soft iron, toothed | Axially magnetised PM |
| Source of torque | Reluctance variation only | PM field-stator field interaction |
| Holding torque | Low (no PM) | High (PM contributes) |
| Step angle | 5°–15° (small) | 30°–90° (large) |
| Damping | Poor | Good |
| Detent torque | Low | Significant |
| Power consumption at standstill | Low | High (PM always energises rotor) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium |
| Best use | Cheap instruments, low-torque apps | Clocks, low-resolution positioning |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students write "VR has no rotor magnet so it has no holding torque." This is **wrong**. The rotor *is* held in the detent (aligned) position by the residual reluctance torque plus any holding current. A PM stepper has higher *detent* torque from the permanent magnet, but a VR motor can have comparable *holding* torque if the drive maintains phase current at standstill. Marks are lost for this confusion.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2022, Reframed for 2024 Scheme]`

**Explain the construction and working of a **Hybrid Stepper Motor** with a neat diagram. Derive the step angle in terms of the number of stator and rotor teeth. List four advantages of hybrid steppers over VR and PM steppers. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working and Step-Angle Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Construction (3 marks)**
A hybrid stepper motor consists of:
- A **stator** with $N_{s}$ salient teeth per phase, carrying concentrated windings (typically 2 phases, bifilar wound).
- A **rotor** composed of a central **axially-magnetised permanent magnet** with two **identical toothed soft-iron end-caps** pressed onto its ends.
- The teeth of the two end-caps are **axially displaced by half a tooth pitch** ($180^{\circ}/N_{r}$).
- The PM magnetises the two end-caps with **opposite polarities** — one end N, the other S.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks)**
When stator phase A is energised:
- The N-cap rotor teeth are **attracted** to the excited stator pole.
- Simultaneously, the S-cap rotor teeth are **repelled** from the same pole.
This double action produces a small but powerful step. Commutating to phase B advances the rotor by one step. Because the displacement is half a tooth pitch, the effective step is half of a PM-only motor of similar geometry.

**Step 3 — Step-Angle Derivation (2 marks)**
The tooth pitch of the rotor is $\tau_{r} = 360^{\circ}/N_{r}$. With the half-tooth-pitch offset between the two caps, the electrical cycle completes in $2/N_{r}$ of a revolution per phase. For an $m$-phase stator with $N_{s}$ stator teeth, the hybrid step angle is:

$$
\beta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_{s} - N_{r}} \approx \frac{360^{\circ}}{m \cdot N_{r}} \;\;\; \text{(for matched teeth)}
$$

For a typical 2-phase hybrid with $N_{r} = 50$: $\beta = 360^{\circ}/(2 \times 50) = 3.6^{\circ}$ or with $N_{r} = 200$: $\beta = 0.9^{\circ}$.

#### Part (b) — Four Advantages of Hybrid Steppers (7 marks — 1.75 marks each)

1. **Smallest step angle in the family** (0.9° and 1.8° are industry standards), enabling high-precision open-loop positioning without gearing.
2. **Highest holding torque per unit volume**, because torque has *both* PM and reluctance components.
3. **Excellent detent torque and damping** — the PM pole-faces provide magnetic damping, suppressing resonance and improving settling time after a step.
4. **Lower rotor inertia than a pure PM stepper** (because the iron caps are lighter than a solid PM cylinder of equivalent size), giving faster acceleration and dynamic response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Do **not** confuse "step angle" with "tooth pitch." The hybrid motor's *tooth pitch* is $360^{\circ}/N_{r}$, but its *step angle* is half the tooth pitch per phase change, giving $\beta = 180^{\circ}/(m \cdot N_{r})$. Writing $\beta = 360^{\circ}/N_{r}$ (the tooth pitch) instead of the actual step angle is a 1-mark deduction that recurs in every valuation cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [x] A **stepper motor** converts **digital pulses** to **discrete angular steps** — open-loop, no encoder needed.
- [x] The cumulative rotor angle is $\theta = N_{pulse} \cdot \beta$.
- [x] **Variable Reluctance (VR):** soft-iron toothed rotor, **reluctance torque only** $T = \tfrac{1}{2} i^{2} (dL/d\theta)$, step angle $\beta = 360^{\circ}/(m N_{r})$.
- [x] **Permanent Magnet (PM):** axially magnetised rotor, **magnet-alignment torque**, step angle $\beta = 360^{\circ}/(2 N_{p})$, large steps (45°–90°).
- [x] **Hybrid (HB):** PM + two toothed iron caps **offset by half tooth pitch**, **both torque components**, finest step (0.9°–5°), highest torque.
- [x] **Mechanical speed** in rpm: $n = \beta \cdot f_{step} / 6$.
- [x] **Stepping frequency** for given rpm: $f_{step} = 6n/\beta$.
- [x] Half-stepping doubles resolution by interleaving "all-ON" and "all-OFF" states.
- [x] VR torque is **independent of current polarity** — drive electronics are simpler.
- [x] PM and hybrid motors have **detent torque at zero current**; VR motors do not.
- [x] Open-loop accuracy is limited by **stall** (loss of synchronism) — a stepper must always be operated below its **pull-out torque curve** at the chosen stepping rate.
- [x] Hybrid steppers dominate **CNC, 3D printing, robotics** because of their fine step + high torque.
- [x] Comparison memory hook: **"VR = cheap + coarse, PM = torque + coarse, Hybrid = precise + powerful."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BLDC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** BLDC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** is a synchronous electric machine in which the rotor consists of permanent magnets (PMs) producing a trapezoidal (rectangular) air-gap flux density distribution, while the stator carries a multi-phase (usually three-phase) concentrated or distributed winding that is energized by a DC source through an electronic commutator (a 3-phase voltage source inverter). The absence of mechanical brushes and commutator is compensated by an electronic controller that switches the stator phases in synchronism with the rotor position, which is sensed using Hall-effect sensors, encoders, or sensorless back-EMF detection algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> BLDC = *Synchronous* + *PM rotor* + *Trapezoidal back-EMF* + *Self-controlled electronic commutation*. It is NOT a true DC motor internally — it is a synchronous AC motor driven by a DC bus through an inverter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnet on Wheels"

Imagine a **child's battery car** where the spinning shaft holds a strong bar magnet. Surrounding the shaft are three fixed coils arranged at 0°, 120°, and 240°. There are **no brushes** to switch the current. Instead, an electronic "traffic controller" (the inverter) continuously watches the magnet's position and energizes the *correct pair* of coils that will *push* or *pull* the magnet in the desired direction. The magnet chases the moving magnetic field, much like a horse chasing a carrot dangling from a pole — the *rotor* (carrot-magnet) always tries to *catch up* with the *stator field* (the pole), but because the field keeps moving ahead at the same pace, the rotor rotates smoothly in lock-step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Perceptual Insight:** A BLDC is essentially an *inside-out* DC motor — the permanent magnet (which was on the stator in brushed DC) is moved to the rotor, and the brushes are replaced by smart power electronics.

### 1.3 Distinguishing Physical Constants and Standards

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range/Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of poles (BLDC is usually multi-pole) | $P$ | 4, 6, 8, 12 | – |
| Supply DC bus voltage | $V_{dc}$ | 12, 24, 48, 310, 540 | **V** |
| Back-EMF constant | $K_e$ | 0.05 – 0.5 | **V·s/rad** |
| Torque constant | $K_t$ | 0.05 – 0.5 | **N·m/A** |
| Rated speed | $N$ | 1000 – 100 000 | **rpm** |
| Torque ripple target | $\Delta T$ | < 5 – 10 % | **N·m** |
| Electrical-to-mechanical ratio | – | $\omega_e = (P/2)\,\omega_m$ | **rad/s** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Brushless DC"?** Although the internal voltages/currents are trapezoidal AC waveforms, the *external* terminal behavior (linear torque-current, linear speed-voltage) mirrors a conventional DC shunt motor — that is why the industry kept the name "DC" in the label.

### 1.4 Why BLDC Dominates Modern Drives

* **High efficiency (85 – 95 %)** because rotor copper losses are absent (PM rotor has no I²R).
* **High power-to-weight ratio** — rotor is just magnets, very low inertia.
* **Excellent dynamic response** — low rotor inertia + electronic commutation → fast acceleration.
* **Maintenance-free** — no brush wear, no commutator sparking, long life (> 10 000 h).
* **Better thermal management** — losses occur in the stator which can be mounted on the outer frame for direct cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Applications covered in KTU syllabus: EV traction, CNC spindle drives, robotics, computer fans, drones, HVAC compressors, hard-disk drives, washing machines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy

**Stator:**
* Laminated silicon-steel core with semi-closed slots.
* Three-phase windings (A, B, C) — usually star-connected, often with the neutral point *not* brought out.
* Distributed or concentrated (fractional-slot) winding — concentrated is preferred for trapezoidal EMF.

**Rotor:**
* Surface-mounted (SPM) or interior (IPM) permanent magnets.
* Magnet material: Ferrite (cheap), NdFeB (high energy), SmCo (high temperature).
* Magnet arc designed to produce **120° flat-top trapezoidal** back-EMF.

**Position Sensors:**
* Three Hall-effect switches placed 120° electrical apart on the non-drive end of the shaft.
* Output: digital (0/1) signals — three bits encode six valid rotor sectors.

**Power Electronic Inverter:**
* Six switching devices (MOSFET/IGBT) arranged in a 3-phase full-bridge.
* Two switches conduct at any instant — one from the upper leg, one from the lower leg — feeding two phases in series, the third phase floats.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Six-Step (120°) Commutation

For a 3-phase BLDC with **one Hall sensor every 60° electrical**, the controller runs through six discrete switching states per electrical revolution. Each state energizes two phases (the phase aligned ~30° ahead of the rotor magnet axis, and the phase 30° behind) such that the stator MMF vector is always 90° ahead of the rotor MMF — this is the *condition for maximum torque per ampere (MTPA)*.

| Step | Rotor Angle (°) | Active Switches (High/Low) | Energized Phases |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 – 60 | T1 / T6 | A+, B− |
| 2 | 60 – 120 | T1 / T2 | A+, C− |
| 3 | 120 – 180 | T3 / T2 | B+, C− |
| 4 | 180 – 240 | T3 / T4 | B+, A− |
| 5 | 240 – 300 | T5 / T4 | C+, A− |
| 6 | 300 – 360 | T5 / T6 | C+, B− |

### 2.3 Back-EMF and Torque Generation

The trapezoidal back-EMF in each phase is a piecewise function of rotor angle $\theta_e$:

$$
e_a(\theta_e) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{K_e\,\omega_m}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\theta_e}{30^\circ}, & 0 \le \theta_e < 30^\circ \\[6pt]
\dfrac{K_e\,\omega_m}{2}, & 30^\circ \le \theta_e < 150^\circ \\[6pt]
\dfrac{K_e\,\omega_m}{2}\cdot\dfrac{210^\circ-\theta_e}{30^\circ}, & 150^\circ \le \theta_e < 210^\circ \\[6pt]
-\dfrac{K_e\,\omega_m}{2}, & 210^\circ \le \theta_e < 330^\circ \\[6pt]
-\dfrac{K_e\,\omega_m}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\theta_e-330^\circ}{30^\circ}, & 330^\circ \le \theta_e < 360^\circ
\end{cases}
$$

During each 60° sector, only two phases conduct with **constant current $I_s$**, and the back-EMFs of these two phases are flat and equal in magnitude (one positive, one negative). Hence the developed torque is **ripple-free** in the ideal case:

$$
T_e \;=\; \frac{(e_a - e_b)\,I_s}{\omega_m} \;=\; \frac{2\,K_e\,I_s}{2} \;=\; K_t\,I_s
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 7 equations — they cover ~ 90 % of KTU 2024 ESE numericals.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_e \, \omega_m = E_b$ | Back-EMF proportional to speed | V |
| 2 | $T = K_t\, I_a$ | Torque proportional to armature current (linear) | N·m |
| 3 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_{dc} - 2 I_a R_a}{2 K_e}$ | No-load → $I_a \approx 0$ | rad/s |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{K_t}{R_a}\,(V_{dc} - 2 K_e\,\omega_m)$ | Torque–speed *linear* characteristic | N·m |
| 5 | $P_{dev} = T\,\omega_m = E_b\,I_a$ | Developed (air-gap) mechanical power | W |
| 6 | $\omega_{e} = \dfrac{P}{2}\,\omega_m$ | Electrical angular speed relates to mechanical | rad/s |
| 7 | $N = \dfrac{60\,f}{P/2}$ | Synchronous speed relation (where $f$ = electrical Hz) | rpm |

> [!WARNING]
> In a BLDC, the factor is **2** (not 1) in the denominator of equations 3 and 4, because **two phases conduct in series** at any instant. A common KTU mistake is to write $V - I_aR_a$ as for a brushed motor. Always mark the doubled resistance!

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV, Mahindra eVerito use BLDC/PMSM traction drives for their high efficiency and regenerative braking capability.
* **Computer Cooling:** Every CPU/GPU fan is a 4-pole or 8-pole BLDC — it must run for years silently, so brush wear is unacceptable.
* **Aerospace Drones:** High power density and reliability under vibration.
* **Medical Pumps & Ventilators:** Spark-free, clean operation, biocompatible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The Back-EMF Constant $K_e$ from Faraday's Law

Starting from Faraday's law for a single phase winding of $N_{turns}$ turns linked to a rotor magnet of flux $\Phi$ per pole, the instantaneous EMF is:

$$
e(t) \;=\; -N_{turns}\,\dfrac{d\Phi(\theta_e)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1.** Since $\theta_e = \omega_e\,t$, the chain rule gives $\dfrac{d}{dt} = \omega_e\,\dfrac{d}{d\theta_e}$.

$$
e(\theta_e) \;=\; -N_{turns}\,\omega_e\,\dfrac{d\Phi(\theta_e)}{d\theta_e}
$$

**Step 2.** For a trapezoidal flux distribution with peak $\Phi_{max}$ and flat top of width $120°$, the average rate of change of flux over the active $60°$ conducting interval is $\dfrac{\Phi_{max}}{30°} \times 60° = 2\,\Phi_{max}$. Integrating and normalising:

$$
e_{flat} \;=\; N_{turns}\,\omega_e\,\dfrac{2\,\Phi_{max}}{2\pi}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{3}
$$

**Step 3.** Simplify using $\omega_e = \dfrac{P}{2}\,\omega_m$:

$$
e_{flat} \;=\; \frac{P}{2}\,N_{turns}\,\Phi_{max}\,\omega_m
$$

**Step 4.** Define the back-EMF constant $K_e$ such that $E_b = K_e\,\omega_m$:

$$
K_e \;=\; \frac{P}{2}\,N_{turns}\,\Phi_{max}
$$

This constant is **machine-specific** and directly measurable: drive the rotor at known $\omega_m$ as a generator and read the line-to-line RMS voltage.

### 3.2 Derivation II — The Torque–Speed Linear Characteristic

**Step 1.** Apply KVL to the active phase pair (A and B) during one 60° sector. Each phase has resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$. The applied DC bus is $V_{dc}$ across the two phases in series.

$$
V_{dc} \;=\; 2\,I_a\,R_a + 2\,L_a\,\frac{dI_a}{dt} + (e_a - e_b)
$$

**Step 2.** In the **flat-top region** (steady-state operation, $dI_a/dt \approx 0$ when PWM has settled), the two back-EMFs are equal and opposite, so $e_a - e_b = 2\,K_e\,\omega_m$ (since each phase contributes $K_e\,\omega_m$ with one positive, one negative):

$$
V_{dc} \;=\; 2\,I_a\,R_a + 2\,K_e\,\omega_m
$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the current:

$$
I_a \;=\; \frac{V_{dc} - 2\,K_e\,\omega_m}{2\,R_a}
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute into $T = K_t\,I_a$ and use the SI identity $K_t = K_e$ (when $K_e$ is in V·s/rad and $K_t$ in N·m/A):

$$
T \;=\; \frac{K_t}{2\,R_a}\,(V_{dc} - 2\,K_e\,\omega_m)
$$

**Step 5.** The no-load speed (when $T = 0 \Rightarrow I_a = 0$):

$$
\omega_{0} \;=\; \frac{V_{dc}}{2\,K_e}
$$

**Step 6.** The stall / starting torque (when $\omega_m = 0$):

$$
T_{stall} \;=\; \frac{K_t\,V_{dc}}{2\,R_a}
$$

Hence the **mechanical characteristic is a perfect straight line** in the $T$–$\omega$ plane — exactly analogous to a separately-excited DC shunt motor.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Torque Ripple in Real BLDC (60° Conduction)

In practice, the current cannot switch instantaneously because of winding inductance $L_a$. The current rise/fall time $\tau = L_a / R_a$ causes the actual current waveform to deviate from the ideal rectangular shape. The torque ripple is:

$$
\%\,\text{Ripple} \;=\; \frac{T_{max}-T_{min}}{T_{avg}}\times 100
$$

For a 120° conduction (six-step) inverter with overlap angle $\mu$ (in electrical degrees) due to commutation, the average torque becomes:

$$
T_{avg} \;=\; \frac{3\,K_t\,I_s}{\pi}\left[\sin\!\left(60°+\frac{\mu}{2}\right) - \sin\!\left(60°-\frac{\mu}{2}\right)\right] \cdot \frac{1}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** Increasing $\mu$ (faster commutation) reduces torque ripple but increases switching losses. PWM frequency and dead-time compensation are the practical tuning knobs.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Brushed DC and PMSM

| Property | Brushed DC | **BLDC (this topic)** | PMSM (sinusoidal) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Commutation | Mechanical (brush) | Electronic, 6-step | Electronic, sinusoidal PWM |
| Back-EMF | Trapezoidal (distorted) | Trapezoidal (ideal) | Sinusoidal |
| Current waveform | DC (with ripple) | Rectangular (quasi-square) | Sinusoidal |
| Torque ripple | Medium | **5 – 15 % (KTU note)** | < 2 % |
| Speed range | 1000:1 (with care) | > 100:1 (sensorless) | > 1000:1 (vector ctrl) |
| Efficiency at rated | 75 – 85 % | **85 – 95 %** | 90 – 97 % |
| Cost (per kW) | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Rotor position sensor | Not needed (brush) | **Hall sensors (typical)** | Resolver / encoder |
| KTU exam weightage | Low (legacy) | **HIGH (this module)** | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BLDC Drive System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete closed-loop BLDC drive architecture** as covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus. It is deliberately drawn as a *functional* flow (signals, sensors, controllers, power) because a physical cross-section is impossible to render in Mermaid and would not aid exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCBus[("DC Supply V_dc")] --> Rectifier[Three-Phase Rectifier with LC Filter]
    Rectifier --> DCLink[("DC Link Capacitor C_dc")]
    DCLink --> Inverter[Three-Phase VSI Inverter]
    Inverter -->|PWM Gate Pulses| GateDrv[Gate Driver Circuit]
    GateDrv -->|6 Gate Signals| Inverter
    Inverter -->|3-Phase Stator Windings| Stator[BLDC Stator A B C]
    Stator -.magnetic coupling.-> Rotor[("PM Rotor with Trapezoidal Flux")]
    Rotor -->|Mechanical Shaft| Load[Mechanical Load]
    Rotor -->|Shaft Mounted| Hall[Three Hall Sensors 120 deg Apart]
    Hall -->|Digital 3-bit Position| Decoder[Hall Decoder and Commutation Logic]
    Vref[("Reference Speed omega_ref")] --> Summator{{Sum Junction}}
    Tachog[Tachogenerator or Back EMF Estimator] -->|omega_m feedback| Summator
    Summator -->|Speed Error e_omega| PIctrl[PI Speed Controller]
    PIctrl -->|Current Reference I_ref| PWM[PWM Generator]
    Decoder -->|Commutation Sector| PWM
    PWM --> GateDrv
    style DCBus fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style DCLink fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style Rotor fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style Stator fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style Hall fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#365314
    style PIctrl fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8
    style PWM fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8
```

### 4.2 Six-Step Commutation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Step1
    Step1: Step 1: A+ B-  (Hall 101)
    Step1 --> Step2: Rotor moves 60 deg
    Step2: Step 2: A+ C-  (Hall 100)
    Step2 --> Step3: Rotor moves 60 deg
    Step3: Step 3: B+ C-  (Hall 110)
    Step3 --> Step4: Rotor moves 60 deg
    Step4: Step 4: B+ A-  (Hall 010)
    Step4 --> Step5: Rotor moves 60 deg
    Step5: Step 5: C+ A-  (Hall 011)
    Step5 --> Step6: Rotor moves 60 deg
    Step6: Step 6: C+ B-  (Hall 001)
    Step6 --> Step1: Rotor moves 60 deg
    note right of Step1: Two phases ON\none floats\ntorque constant
```

### 4.3 Torque–Speed Characteristic (KTU 2024 — Linear Plot)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "BLDC Torque vs Speed (Linear Characteristic)"
    x-axis "Speed (rpm)" [0, 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, 5000, 6000]
    y-axis "Torque (N.m)" 0 --> 10
    line [9.5, 7.9, 6.3, 4.7, 3.2, 1.6, 0.0]
```

**Reading the chart:** The straight line is the **natural** characteristic for fixed $V_{dc}$. Reducing $V_{dc}$ (via PWM duty cycle $D$) shifts the line *parallel* downward — this is how the KTU 2024 numericals handle "speed control by varying duty ratio."

### 4.4 Three-Phase Inverter Topology (Schematic via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vplus(("V_dc +")) --> T1up[T1 - High Side Phase A]
    Vplus --> T3up[T3 - High Side Phase B]
    Vplus --> T5up[T5 - High Side Phase C]
    T1up -->|Phase A| PhA(("A"))
    T3up -->|Phase B| PhB(("B"))
    T5up -->|Phase C| PhC(("C"))
    PhA --> T4dn[T4 - Low Side Phase A]
    PhB --> T6dn[T6 - Low Side Phase B]
    PhC --> T2dn[T2 - Low Side Phase C]
    T4dn --> Vminus(("V_dc -"))
    T6dn --> Vminus
    T2dn --> Vminus
    style T1up fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207
    style T3up fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207
    style T5up fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207
    style T4dn fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style T6dn fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style T2dn fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*"What is a Brushless DC Motor? List any four advantages over conventional brushed DC motor."*

**Model Answer:**

A *Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)* is a synchronous machine with permanent-magnet rotor and electronically commutated stator windings, producing trapezoidal back-EMF. The commutation function of mechanical brushes is performed by an electronic inverter controlled by rotor position feedback.

*Advantages (any four):*
1. Higher efficiency (no rotor copper losses, no brush drop).
2. Lower maintenance and longer life (no brush wear).
3. Better heat dissipation (losses concentrated in stator which is mounted on the housing).
4. Higher power-to-weight ratio and lower rotor inertia → faster dynamic response.
5. No sparking → suitable for explosive or洁净 environments.
6. Higher speed range (no mechanical commutation limit).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*"What is meant by electronic commutation in a BLDC motor? Explain the role of Hall-effect sensors."*

**Model Answer:**

*Electronic commutation* is the process of sequentially switching the stator phase windings of a BLDC using power electronic devices (MOSFET/IGBT) in synchronism with the rotor angular position, replacing the function of mechanical brushes and commutator.

*Role of Hall-effect sensors:*
Three Hall sensors are mounted on the stator at $120°$ electrical apart. As the PM rotor rotates, each sensor outputs a digital HIGH or LOW depending on the polarity of the magnetic field crossing it. The three binary outputs form a 3-bit Gray code that uniquely identifies one of the six 60° rotor sectors. This information is fed to the controller which then activates the appropriate pair of inverter switches to maintain a stator MMF vector that is always ~ 90° ahead of the rotor MMF — ensuring continuous torque production.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each)

> **Internal Choice:** KTU 2024 ESE convention allows the student to answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a three-phase BLDC motor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4-pole, 3-phase BLDC motor has $K_e = 0.1$ V·s/rad and phase resistance $R_a = 1\,\Omega$. It is supplied from a 48 V DC bus. Compute: (i) the no-load speed in rpm, (ii) the starting torque, and (iii) the developed torque at 1500 rpm. Take $K_t = K_e$. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

*Construction* — Refer to the diagram in Section 4.4 and Section 4.1.
* Stator: laminated silicon-steel core, three-phase distributed or concentrated windings, star-connected.
* Rotor: 4 (or more) surface-mounted or interior permanent magnets producing a trapezoidal air-gap flux.
* Position sensors: 3 Hall-effect devices, 120° apart, on the non-drive end.
* Inverter: 6-switch three-phase VSI with gate drivers.

*Working principle:*
When DC bus voltage $V_{dc}$ is applied and the rotor is at, say, electrical angle 0° (Hall code 101), switches T1 (upper) and T6 (lower) are turned ON, energising phase A (positive) and phase B (negative). The stator MMF axis points 90° ahead of the rotor magnet axis, producing a clockwise torque. As the rotor advances 60°, the Hall code changes to 100, and the controller commutates to T1 and T2 (phases A+, C−). The process repeats through six discrete states per electrical revolution, yielding smooth rotation in the same direction. *(3 marks for working, 4 marks for construction + diagram description.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $K_e = K_t = 0.1$ V·s/rad = 0.1 N·m/A, $R_a = 1\,\Omega$ (per phase), $V_{dc} = 48$ V, $P = 4$.

**Step 1 — No-load speed $\omega_0$ (rad/s):**
Using $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2 K_e}$:

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{48}{2 \times 0.1} \;=\; \frac{48}{0.2} \;=\; 240 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

Convert to rpm:

$$
N_0 \;=\; \frac{60 \times 240}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{14400}{6.2832} \;\approx\; 2291.8 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Starting torque $T_{stall}$:**
At $\omega_m = 0$, the back-EMF is zero, so all 48 V drops across $2 R_a = 2\,\Omega$:

$$
I_{stall} \;=\; \frac{V_{dc}}{2 R_a} \;=\; \frac{48}{2 \times 1} \;=\; 24 \;\text{A}
$$

$$
T_{stall} \;=\; K_t\,I_{stall} \;=\; 0.1 \times 24 \;=\; 2.4 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Torque at 1500 rpm:**

Convert 1500 rpm to rad/s:

$$
\omega_m \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;\approx\; 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

Compute back-EMF:

$$
E_b \;=\; 2 K_e\,\omega_m \;=\; 2 \times 0.1 \times 157.08 \;=\; 31.416 \;\text{V}
$$

Armature current:

$$
I_a \;=\; \frac{V_{dc} - 2 K_e\,\omega_m}{2 R_a} \;=\; \frac{48 - 31.416}{2 \times 1} \;=\; \frac{16.584}{2} \;=\; 8.292 \;\text{A}
$$

Developed torque:

$$
T_{1500} \;=\; K_t \times I_a \;=\; 0.1 \times 8.292 \;=\; 0.8292 \;\text{N·m} \;\approx\; 0.83 \;\text{N·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgot the factor of 2** in $\omega_0 = V_{dc}/K_e$ (brushed-DC formula) — will yield *double* the correct no-load speed → **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Used $K_t = 1.085 \times 10^{-3}$** (mixing SI and CGS units from random references) — always verify $K_t$ has units of N·m/A before plugging.
> 3. **Did not convert rpm ↔ rad/s** in Step 3 — the most common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **No diagram in (a)** — KTU 2024 rubric requires a *labelled* block/schematic for full marks.

**Final Answers:** (i) $N_0 \approx 2292$ rpm, (ii) $T_{stall} = 2.4$ N·m, (iii) $T_{1500} \approx 0.83$ N·m.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2, Module 1]*

**(a)** Explain the six-step (120°) commutation sequence in a three-phase BLDC motor with the help of switching table and Hall-sensor truth table. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 6-pole BLDC has $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$/phase, $L_a = 2$ mH, and $K_e = K_t = 0.08$ in SI units. The bus is 100 V. If the load demands a constant torque of 1.2 N·m, determine the steady-state speed in rpm. Also, what is the speed if the load torque doubles? Comment on the regulation. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Six-Step Commutation (7 marks):**

Refer to the table in Section 2.2 above. The student must reproduce:

* A switching state diagram (6 states per electrical revolution).
* The Hall-sensor truth table (3-bit codes from 101 down to 001, going through all six non-zero codes).
* Active switches per state (one from upper leg, one from lower leg).
* Statement: at any instant, **two phases conduct** with the third floating; this produces a quasi-square current waveform of 120° electrical width per phase.
* Mention: commutation is synchronised such that the stator MMF leads the rotor MMF by 90° electrical (MTPA).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $K_e = K_t = 0.08$, $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$, $V_{dc} = 100$ V.

**Step 1 — Find current for $T = 1.2$ N·m:**

$$
I_a \;=\; \frac{T}{K_t} \;=\; \frac{1.2}{0.08} \;=\; 15 \;\text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF drop across $2 R_a$:**

$$
V_{drop} \;=\; 2 \times 0.5 \times 15 \;=\; 15 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Back-EMF remaining:**

$$
2 K_e\,\omega_m \;=\; V_{dc} - 2 I_a R_a \;=\; 100 - 15 \;=\; 85 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical speed in rad/s:**

$$
\omega_m \;=\; \frac{85}{2 \times 0.08} \;=\; \frac{85}{0.16} \;=\; 531.25 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to rpm:**

$$
N_1 \;=\; \frac{60 \times 531.25}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{31875}{6.2832} \;\approx\; 5072.5 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 6 — Doubled load ($T = 2.4$ N·m → $I_a = 30$ A):**

$$
V_{drop} \;=\; 2 \times 0.5 \times 30 \;=\; 30 \;\text{V}
$$

$$
2 K_e\,\omega_m' \;=\; 100 - 30 \;=\; 70 \;\text{V}
$$

$$
\omega_m' \;=\; \frac{70}{0.16} \;=\; 437.5 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
N_2 \;=\; \frac{60 \times 437.5}{2\pi} \;\approx\; 4177.5 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 7 — Speed regulation:**

$$
\%\,\text{Reg} \;=\; \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{5072.5 - 4177.5}{5072.5} \times 100 \;\approx\; 17.65\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B)**
> 1. **Using $V_{dc} - I_aR_a$ instead of $V_{dc} - 2 I_aR_a$** — BLDC conducts two phases in series; full mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing $K_t$ (N·m/A) with $\Psi_m$ (Wb, flux-linkage)** — though numerically identical in SI, KTU expects the student to state the relationship: $K_t = (3/2)\cdot(P/2)\cdot\Psi_m$ for sinusoidal machines, $K_t = K_e$ for BLDC.
> 3. **Forgetting unit consistency** in pole number (KTU problem statements vary between 4, 6, 8 poles — but for BLDC the formula $\omega_0 = V_{dc}/(2K_e)$ is *already* in mechanical rad/s, so $P$ is **not needed**; many students mistakenly divide by $P/2$ again).

**Final Answers:** $N_1 \approx 5073$ rpm, $N_2 \approx 4178$ rpm, Regulation $\approx 17.6$ % (BLDC has **inherently poor speed regulation** without closed-loop control — a key KTU conclusion).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1, Topic: BLDC Motors**

* **Definition:** Synchronous, PM-rotor, electronically commutated, trapezoidal back-EMF.
* **Conduction:** Two phases ON, one floats; 60° sector, 6 states per electrical cycle.
* **Position feedback:** 3 Hall sensors, 120° apart → 3-bit Gray code → 6 sectors.
* **Inverter:** 3-phase VSI, 6 switches (MOSFET < 1 kW, IGBT > 1 kW), PWM for speed control.
* **Critical formula (the ONE to memorise):**
  $\boxed{\,\omega_m = \dfrac{V_{dc} - 2 I_a R_a}{2 K_e}\,}$
* **Torque equation:** $T = K_t I_a$ with $K_t = K_e$ in SI.
* **No-load speed:** $\omega_0 = V_{dc}/(2K_e)$.
* **Stall torque:** $T_{stall} = K_t V_{dc}/(2R_a)$.
* **Electrical–mechanical link:** $\omega_e = (P/2)\omega_m$.
* **Synchronous speed (alternative form):** $N_s = 120 f / P$ — note that in a BLDC, $f$ is **not** independent; it is set by the rotor speed and Hall decoder (self-controlled).
* **Speed control methods:** (i) Vary $V_{dc}$ (DC-DC chopper), (ii) vary PWM duty cycle at fixed $V_{dc}$ — *preferred KTU answer.*
* **Torque control:** Limit/regulate the DC-link current using hysteresis or PI current controller.
* **Advantages (always cite ≥ 3):** High efficiency, long life, low maintenance, high power density.
* **Disadvantages (cite ≥ 2):** Higher cost of electronics, requires controller, torque ripple 5 – 15 %, sensor failure possible (sensorless methods exist).
* **Closed-loop:** Speed → PI → Current reference → PWM → Inverter → BLDC; with Tacho or sensorless back-EMF feedback.
* **Comparison triplet:** Brushed DC ↔ BLDC ↔ PMSM (memorise the *Analytical Matrix* in §3.4).
* **Common numericals:** (i) Find $N_0$ given $V_{dc}$, $K_e$. (ii) Find $T$ at given $\omega_m$. (iii) Find $V_{dc}$ for given $\omega_0$ and $T_{stall}$. (iv) Plot the straight-line $T$–$\omega$ characteristic.

> **Last KTU tip:** Always start the answer with the *block diagram* and the *commutation table* — these two are "free marks" in the KTU 2024 valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMSM: Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PMSM: Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is a rotating electrical machine in which the field winding on the rotor of a conventional synchronous machine is replaced by high-energy **permanent magnets**, producing a constant air-gap flux without requiring slip-rings, brushes, or rotor field excitation. The stator carries a balanced **3-phase sinusoidal AC winding** supplied from a variable-frequency inverter, forcing the rotor to lock into synchronism with the rotating stator field at synchronous speed given by $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.

Where:
- $f$ = Supply frequency (**Hz**)
- $P$ = Number of **poles**
- $N_s$ = Synchronous mechanical speed (**rpm**)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Magnet and the Magnetized Door"**
> Imagine a powerful neodymium disc fixed to the centre of a ceiling fan (the **rotor**). Around it, invisible rotating magnetic poles are created by switching current in coils hidden in the fan's frame (the **stator**). The disc is not pushed by friction — it is *pulled* by magnetic locking. Because the disc itself is a magnet, there is no need to "light up" or energize the rotor; it just chases the rotating magnetic field at exactly the same speed — hence **synchronous**. If the field rotates at 1500 rpm, the disc rotates at exactly 1500 rpm — no slip, no compromise.

### 1.3 Why PMSM Matters in Modern Drives

- **High efficiency (95–98 %)** because there are no rotor copper losses.
- **High power density** and **torque-to-inertia ratio**.
- **Self-excitation** — eliminates the slip-ring/brush assembly.
- Used in **EVs (Tata Tiago EV, Tesla Model 3 rear motor)**, **CNC spindles**, **robotic servo joints**, **HVAC compressors**, and **aerospace actuators**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key reference metrics used in PMSM design & KTU problems:
> - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
> - **Remanent flux density (NdFeB):** $B_r = 1.0$ to $1.4$ **T**
> - **Coercivity (NdFeB N52):** $H_c \approx -796$ **kA/m**
> - **Typical air-gap length:** $0.5$ to $1.5$ **mm**
> - **Supply waveform:** Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) / SVPWM
> - **KTU standard pole configurations:** 4-pole, 6-pole, 8-pole
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Constructional Subsystems of a PMSM

A PMSM is assembled from the following major sub-assemblies, each critical for KTU-level analysis:

#### A. Stator Assembly
- **Stator Core** — Laminated **silicon steel (M19 / M43 / M47 grade)** of thickness **0.35 mm** to **0.5 mm**, stacked and insulated by a thin varnish coat to suppress **eddy-current losses**.
- **Stator Slots** — Semi-closed slots of shape *trapezoidal, rectangular, or circular*, distributed as 36, 48, 54, or 60 slots for a 3-phase, 4-pole machine to achieve a near-sinusoidal MMF distribution.
- **Stator Winding** — Double-layer, short-pitched, **distributed 3-phase winding** ($60^\circ$ phase belt). Each phase carries sinusoidally time-varying currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$.
- **End Windings** — Form the overhang that connects slot conductors; moulded and braced for mechanical rigidity.

#### B. Rotor Assembly (Heart of PMSM)
- **Shaft** — High-grade alloy steel (EN24 / 40Cr) carrying the rotor lamination stack.
- **Rotor Core** — Solid or laminated silicon-steel stack, providing a low-reluctance flux path.
- **Permanent Magnets (PMs)** — Embedded on/inside the rotor in one of three configurations.
- **Retaining Sleeve** — Carbon-fibre or inconel sleeve over surface-mounted magnets to contain centrifugal force.

#### C. Mechanical & Auxiliary Subsystems
- **End Shields / Bearing Housing** — Aluminium or cast-iron housings supporting ball/roller bearings.
- **Position / Speed Sensors** — **Incremental encoder, absolute encoder, or resolver** providing rotor-position feedback (essential for vector control).
- **Terminal Box & Cooling Fins** — For IP23/IP55 enclosures and TEFC (Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled) designs.

### 2.2 Rotor Magnet Configurations (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Type | Magnet Location | Saliency ($L_d \neq L_q$) | Inductance | Typical Use |
|---|------|----------------|---------------------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Surface PMSM (SPMSM)** | Glued on rotor surface | Non-salient | $L_d \approx L_q$ | Servo, CNC |
| 2 | **Inset PMSM** | Recessed into surface slots | Slightly salient | $L_d < L_q$ | Industrial |
| 3 | **Interior PMSM (IPMSM)** | Buried inside rotor lamination | Strongly salient | $L_d < L_q$ | EV traction (Toyota Prius, Tesla) |

> [!NOTE]
> In **IPMSM**, the difference between $L_d$ and $L_q$ produces **reluctance torque** in addition to magnet torque — this is what gives IPMSM its **constant-power high-speed region (field-weakening capability)** and is the most favourite KTU viva topic.

### 2.3 Materials Used for Permanent Magnets

| Magnet Material | $B_r$ (T) | $H_c$ (kA/m) | Max. Temp (°C) | Cost | KTU Note |
|----------------|-----------|--------------|----------------|------|----------|
| **NdFeB (Neodymium)** | 1.0 – 1.4 | 750 – 2000 | 80 – 220 | High | Highest energy product; default in modern PMSM |
| **SmCo (Samarium Cobalt)** | 0.8 – 1.1 | 600 – 1500 | 250 – 350 | Very High | Aerospace & high-temp |
| **AlNiCo** | 0.6 – 1.3 | 50 – 200 | 500 – 600 | Medium | Old machines |
| **Ferrite (Ceramic)** | 0.2 – 0.4 | 150 – 300 | 250 | Low | Low-cost appliances |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Unit |
|---------|------------------|---------|------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | **rpm** |
| 2 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous electrical angular speed | **rad/s** |
| 3 | $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, K_w$ | RMS induced EMF (per phase) | **V** |
| 4 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \, \dfrac{P}{2} \, \psi_m \, I_q$ | Electromagnetic torque (SPMSM) | **N·m** |
| 5 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \, \dfrac{P}{2} \, \left[ \psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]$ | Total torque (IPMSM) | **N·m** |
| 6 | $P_{cu,rotor} = 0$ | Rotor copper loss in PMSM | **W** |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{cu,st} + P_{iron} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}}$ | Efficiency | **p.u.** |
| 8 | $\phi_m = B_r \cdot A_m$ | Magnet flux | **Wb** |

> Where $K_w$ = winding factor, $N_{ph}$ = turns/phase, $P$ = number of poles, $\psi_m$ = PM flux linkage, $I_d, I_q$ = d-axis and q-axis currents, $L_d, L_q$ = d-axis and q-axis inductances, $B_r$ = remanent flux density, $A_m$ = magnet area.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Interior PMSMs (IPMSMs) are now the industry standard for traction motors (e.g., **BMW i3**, **Tata Nexon EV**, **Hyundai Kona**) because of their wide constant-power speed range (CPSR > 1:4).
- **Industrial Servos:** Surface PMSMs (SPMSMs) deliver smooth torque at low speeds for **CNC machining centres**, **3D printers**, and **robotic arms**.
- **Aerospace:** SmCo-PMSMs operate at temperatures exceeding 200 °C in **flight control actuators**.
- **White Goods:** Inverter-controlled PMSMs replace induction motors in **LG / Samsung inverter air-conditioner compressors**, delivering 30–40 % energy savings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Synchronous Speed of PMSM

**Given:**
- Electrical supply frequency $f$
- Number of magnetic poles $P$

**Step 1 — Frequency of rotating magnetic field:**

The rotating magnetic field completes one electrical revolution per supply cycle. In one mechanical revolution, the field passes $P/2$ pairs of poles.

$$
\text{Number of mechanical revolutions/second} = \frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to revolutions per minute (rpm):**

Multiply by 60.

$$
N_s = \frac{2f}{P} \times 60
$$

**Step 3 — Final KTU formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,N_s = \frac{120\,f}{P}\,} \quad \text{[rpm]}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A 3-phase, 8-pole PMSM is fed from a VFD at 50 Hz. Find the synchronous speed.

$$
N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{8} = \frac{6000}{8} = 750 \text{ rpm}
$$

The rotor of this PMSM will therefore lock and run at exactly **750 rpm** — no slip.

### 3.2 Derivation — EMF Equation of PMSM Stator Winding

The stator of a PMSM is essentially identical to a synchronous machine, so the per-phase RMS EMF induced in the stator winding is:

**Step 1 — Flux linking the coil:**

The PM flux per pole $\phi_m$ is provided by the permanent magnet of area $A_m$:

$$
\phi_m = B_r \, A_m
$$

**Step 2 — Induced EMF in one conductor:**

By Faraday's law, the average EMF per conductor for a coil of $N$ turns is:

$$
E_{av} = 4 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m
$$

**Step 3 — Form factor correction (sinusoidal distribution):**

A pure sine wave has a form factor of 1.11, so the RMS EMF per phase is:

$$
E_{ph,rms} = 1.11 \times E_{av}
$$

**Step 4 — Winding factor $K_w$:**

$K_w = K_d \times K_p$ (distribution factor × pitch factor). For a typical short-pitched distributed winding, $K_w \approx 0.9$ to $0.96$.

**Step 5 — Final EMF equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,E_f = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, K_w\,} \quad \text{[V/phase]}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why PMSM Rotor Has No Copper Loss

| Step | Induction Motor Rotor | PMSM Rotor |
|------|----------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Rotor bars/winding present | No rotor winding |
| 2 | Current induced by slip | Current absent (no slip) |
| 3 | $P_{cu,rot} = I_r^2 R_r > 0$ | $P_{cu,rot} = 0$ |
| 4 | Cooling needed for rotor | Rotor stays cooler |
| 5 | Lower efficiency (typically 88–92 %) | Higher efficiency (95–98 %) |

### 3.4 Derivation — Torque Equation of PMSM (IPMSM)

**Step 1 — Magnet torque component:**

The fundamental alignment torque is produced by the PM flux linkage $\psi_m$ interacting with the q-axis current $I_q$:

$$
T_{mag} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q
$$

**Step 2 — Reluctance torque component (only in IPMSM):**

$$
T_{rel} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) \cdot I_d \cdot I_q
$$

**Step 3 — Total electromagnetic torque:**

$$
\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{3P}{4} \left[ \psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]\,} \quad \text{[N·m]}
$$

**Step 4 — KTU interpretation:**
- The first term $\psi_m I_q$ = **magnet (excitation) torque**.
- The second term $(L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$ = **reluctance torque**, exists only if $L_d \neq L_q$ (i.e., for salient IPMSM).
- For **SPMSM**, $L_d = L_q$, so $T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4} \psi_m I_q$ — pure magnet torque.

### 3.5 Practical Significance in EV Traction

In a typical EV IPMSM, the reluctance torque contributes **30–40 % of the total peak torque**, allowing smaller magnets to be used and improving the **field-weakening range** for high-speed cruising.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam]**
**"List any three advantages of PMSM over conventional wound-rotor synchronous motor."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **No rotor copper loss** — Since permanent magnets provide the field, there is no field winding current in the rotor, giving higher efficiency (95–98 %) and eliminating the need for slip-rings and brushes.
2. **Higher power density and compactness** — Rare-earth magnets (NdFeB) provide high flux density (1.0–1.4 T) in a small volume, making the motor lighter and more compact.
3. **Better dynamic response and self-excitation** — Absence of field winding time-constant gives very fast dynamic response suitable for high-performance servo and EV applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Differentiate between SPMSM and IPMSM with respect to construction, saliency, and torque production."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SPMSM | IPMSM |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Magnet location | Glued on rotor surface | Buried inside rotor slots |
| Saliency | Non-salient, $L_d \approx L_q$ | Salient, $L_d < L_q$ |
| Torque production | Pure magnet torque, $T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4} \psi_m I_q$ | Magnet + reluctance torque, $T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4}\left[ \psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]$ |
| Application | Servo drives, CNC | EV traction, industrial |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In constructional diagrams, students often forget to **label the air-gap**, the **sleeve**, and the **encoder position** — these fetch 1–2 marks easily.
> - In EMF/torque derivations, **forgetting the winding factor $K_w$** is the most common single-mark loss.
> - For IPMSM torque, students frequently **drop the reluctance term** — full marks require both components.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a) With a neat constructional diagram, explain the major parts of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM). Discuss the function of each subsystem. (7 marks)**
**(b) Derive the EMF equation and torque equation of a 3-phase PMSM and explain why the rotor copper loss is zero. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction of PMSM (7 marks):**

A PMSM consists of the following major parts:
1. **Stator** — Same construction as a 3-phase induction motor. It is built of laminated silicon-steel (0.35 mm) stampings with semi-closed slots carrying a double-layer, short-pitched, distributed 3-phase winding. The stator produces a rotating magnetic field at synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / P$ when supplied from a 3-phase inverter.
2. **Rotor with Permanent Magnets** — The rotor carries high-energy rare-earth magnets (NdFeB or SmCo) in three possible configurations:
   - **Surface-mounted (SPMSM)** — magnets glued to the rotor surface, mechanically retained by a carbon-fibre or inconel sleeve.
   - **Inset** — magnets recessed in surface slots.
   - **Interior (IPMSM)** — magnets buried inside the rotor lamination, giving high mechanical strength and salient-pole behaviour.
