- A **gate** (Gate terminal) that *can* open the spillway, but only when **water pressure is pushing from the correct side** (forward bias).
- A **spring-loaded shutter** that snaps shut if water pressure drops too low (Holding current).
- A **safety valve** that bursts open if pressure rises too fast (dv/dt triggering).

The V-I curve is essentially a *plot of the dam's behaviour under different pressure scenarios*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The examiner frequently tests the *labels* on the V-I curve — **$V_{BO}$ (Breakover Voltage), $I_L$ (Latching Current), $I_H$ (Holding Current), $V_R$ (Reverse Breakdown Voltage)**. Memorize these with their relative positions!

## 1.3 Switching Characteristics — The *Speed* of the Device

A real SCR is **not an ideal switch**. It takes a finite time to turn ON and a finite time to turn OFF. These time-domain behaviours are called **switching (or dynamic) characteristics**.

**Intuitive Analogy — A Heavy Train**
- **Turn-on** = Time for the locomotive to *accelerate* from rest to full speed → Delay time (gear engagement) + Rise time (acceleration).
- **Turn-off** = Time for the train to *brake, stop, and reverse direction* → Reverse recovery (braking) + Forward recovery (reversing).

## 1.4 Turn-On Methods — How to "Push the Button"

There are **five ways** to turn ON an SCR:

| # | Method | Mechanism |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Forward Voltage Triggering | Anode voltage exceeds $V_{BO}$ |
| 2 | Gate Triggering | A current pulse at the gate |
| 3 | **dv/dt Triggering** | Rapid rate of rise of anode voltage |
| 4 | Temperature Triggering | Junction temperature rise increases leakage |
| 5 | Light Triggering (LASCR) | Photons hit the depletion layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate triggering is the *only practical* method in motor drives** because it offers **precise firing angle control** ($\alpha$), which is the foundation of all phase-controlled converter circuits (covered in Module 3).

## 1.5 Gate Triggering Circuits — The "Ignition System"

A **gate triggering circuit** is a small auxiliary electronic network designed to **inject a controlled gate current pulse** into the SCR at a precise instant of the AC cycle. Common topologies include **R-triggering, RC-triggering, and UJT-based relaxation oscillator** circuits.

**Standard Reference Values (IEEE / Industry) for Gate Triggering:**
- **$V_{GT}$ (Gate Trigger Voltage):** typically **0.5 V to 2 V**
- **$I_{GT}$ (Gate Trigger Current):** typically **1 mA to 50 mA**
- **$V_{GRM}$ (Peak Reverse Gate Voltage):** typically **5 V to 10 V**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The V-I Characteristic Curve — Region-by-Region

The complete V-I curve has **five distinct regions**:

### Region 1: Forward Blocking Mode (OFF State)
- Anode is **positive** with respect to cathode, but **no gate signal** is applied.
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased; junction $J_2$ is reverse-biased.
- Only a tiny **forward leakage current** ($I_{FL}$) flows (milli-amperes range).
- Device remains in high-impedance OFF state.

### Region 2: Forward Conduction Mode (ON State)
- Once triggered, $J_2$ also becomes forward-biased due to regenerative action.
- Anode current rises sharply; voltage drop across SCR falls to a small value (**1 V to 1.5 V** for low-power SCRs).
- The device now behaves like a **closed switch**.

### Region 3: Reverse Blocking Mode
- Anode is **negative** with respect to cathode.
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are reverse-biased; $J_2$ is forward-biased.
- A small **reverse leakage current** ($I_{RL}$) flows.
- Device blocks reverse voltage up to **$V_{BR(1)}$** (Reverse Breakdown Voltage).

### Region 4: Reverse Breakdown Mode
- Reverse voltage exceeds $V_{BR(1)}$; **avalanche breakdown** occurs at $J_1$.
- This mode is **destructive** and must be avoided by external protection.

### Region 5: Forward Breakdown / Breakover
- Forward voltage exceeds **$V_{BO}$**; SCR turns ON abruptly.
- This is the *uncontrolled* turn-on mechanism — undesirable in power circuits.

## 2.2 Critical Parameters in V-I Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$I_L$ (Latching Current):** The **minimum anode current** that *must flow* immediately after turn-on to *maintain* conduction. If $I_A$ falls below $I_L$ before latching, the SCR reverts to OFF.
>
> **$I_H$ (Holding Current):** The **minimum anode current** required to *keep* the SCR in conduction. $I_H < I_L$ always.
>
> **$V_{BO}$ (Forward Breakover Voltage):** Forward anode voltage at which the SCR turns ON *without* gate signal.

## 2.3 Switching Characteristics — Time Domain Analysis

### 2.3.1 Turn-On Process

When a gate pulse is applied at $t = 0$, the anode current does not jump instantly. It goes through **three sub-phases**:

**Step 1 — Delay Time ($t_d$):** The interval during which the anode current rises from **0% to 10%** of its final value ($I_A$). During $t_d$, carriers begin to spread across the junction $J_2$, but conduction is not yet established.

**Step 2 — Rise Time ($t_r$):** The interval during which anode current rises from **10% to 90%** of $I_A$. Rapid regenerative action occurs; $J_2$ becomes fully forward-biased.

**Step 3 — Turn-On Time ($t_{on}$):**
$$t_{on} = t_d + t_r$$
Typical values: **$t_d \approx 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ \mu s}$**, **$t_r \approx 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ \mu s}$** → $t_{on} \approx$ **2 to 4 \mu s**.

### 2.3.2 Turn-Off Process

When the anode current falls to zero (forced by external circuit), the SCR does not immediately regain its forward blocking capability. The turn-off sequence:

**Step 1 — Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$):** The anode current actually goes **negative** for a brief period as stored carriers are swept out. The SCR only "regains" its reverse blocking capability after $t_{rr}$.

**Step 2 — Gate Recovery Time ($t_{gr}$):** Even after reverse blocking is established, the device needs additional time for $J_2$ to regain its forward blocking capability.

**Step 3 — Turn-Off Time ($t_{off}$):**
$$t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$$
Typical value: **$t_{off} \approx$ 10 to 100 \mu s** (much larger than $t_{on}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write $t_{off} = t_{rr} - t_{gr}$. The correct relationship is **addition**, not subtraction!

## 2.4 dv/dt Triggering — The "Unwanted Firing"

If the forward anode voltage rises too quickly, the **displacement current** through the reverse-biased junction $J_2$ (which has a junction capacitance $C_j$) can exceed the gate trigger current and turn ON the SCR *unintentionally*.

The displacement current:
$$i_C = C_j \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

For a sinusoidal supply $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:
$$i_C = C_j \cdot \omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

The **maximum dv/dt** occurs at $\omega t = 0$:
$$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = \omega V_m = 2\pi f V_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Protection against dv/dt triggering:** A **snubber circuit** (series $R_s$ + parallel $C_s$ across SCR) is used to limit the rate of rise of forward voltage.

## 2.5 Turn-Off Methods (Commutation) — Brief Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although listed under Module 2 in some KTU syllabi, the detailed commutation study belongs to converter design (Module 3). Here is the **classification** for completeness:
>
> - **Class A (Load Commutation):** Load is naturally resonant (over-resonant).
> - **Class B (Resonant Link):** LC tank circuit forces current to zero.
> - **Class C (Complementary / Impulse):** Auxiliary SCR momentarily applies a reverse voltage.
> - **Class D (External Pulse):** External pulse commutates by forcing $I_A < I_H$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Turn-on time | $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$ | seconds ($\mu s$) | $t_d$: 0→10% $I_A$; $t_r$: 10→90% $I_A$ |
| 2 | Turn-off time | $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ | seconds ($\mu s$) | $t_{off} \gg t_{on}$ |
| 3 | dv/dt (sinusoidal) | $\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$ | V/s | Occurs at $\omega t = 0$ |
| 4 | Displacement current | $i_C = C_j \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$ | A | Triggers false firing |
| 5 | UJT firing period | $T = RC \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)$ | seconds | $\eta$ = intrinsic stand-off ratio (0.5–0.85) |
| 6 | UJT peak voltage | $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$ | volts | $V_D \approx 0.7$ V (diode drop) |
| 7 | UJT valley voltage | $V_V \approx \dfrac{V_P}{2}$ | volts | Empirically for typical UJTs |
| 8 | Firing angle | $\alpha = \omega t$ | radians or degrees | Range: 0° to 180° |
| 9 | Conduction angle | $\gamma = 180° - \alpha$ | degrees | For half-wave rectifier |
| 10 | Average output voltage | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | volts | Single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** dv/dt triggering is a *fault mechanism* in power electronics — engineers must *suppress* it (using snubbers). However, in **LASCRs (Light-Activated SCRs)** used in **HVDC transmission** (e.g., ±800 kV lines in India), *light* is the *preferred* firing mechanism because of the galvanic isolation it provides.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum dv/dt for a Sinusoidal Supply

**Problem:** A sinusoidal voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is applied across the anode-cathode of an SCR. Derive the expression for the maximum rate of rise of voltage.

**Step 1 — Start with the applied sinusoidal voltage:**
$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time $t$ to get the rate of rise of voltage:**
$$\frac{dv}{dt} = V_m \cdot \frac{d}{dt}[\sin(\omega t)] = V_m \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify that this rate of change is a sinusoidal function of time. It is maximum when the cosine term equals unity (i.e., $\cos(\omega t) = 1$):**
$$\text{At } \omega t = 0,\ \cos(\omega t) = 1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the maximum condition:**
$$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = \omega V_m \cdot 1 = \omega V_m$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$ for a frequency $f$ supply:**
$$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 6 — Express in kV/$\mu$s for practical SCRs (typical $f = 50$ Hz, $V_m = 311$ V for 220 V RMS):**
$$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 311 \approx 97{,}685\ \text{V/s} \approx 0.098\ \text{V/\mu s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** For a 50 Hz mains at 220 V RMS, the natural dv/dt is only ~0.1 V/$\mu$s. SCRs typically have a *specified* maximum dv/dt of **50 V/$\mu$s to 1000 V/$\mu$s**. Thus, dv/dt triggering is *not* a problem at low frequencies — but it becomes critical in **high-frequency chopper circuits** (kHz range).

## 3.2 Derivation: UJT Relaxation Oscillator Period

**Problem:** A UJT relaxation oscillator uses $R_T$ and $C$ to produce a sawtooth output. Derive the firing period $T$.

**Step 1 — Capacitor charging equation (initial voltage $V_V$, final voltage $V_{BB}$):**
$$v_C(t) = V_{BB} - (V_{BB} - V_V)\,e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 2 — Firing occurs when $v_C(t) = V_P$ (peak point). Substitute:**
$$V_P = V_{BB} - (V_{BB} - V_V)\,e^{-T/RC}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:**
$$(V_{BB} - V_V)\,e^{-T/RC} = V_{BB} - V_P$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $(V_{BB} - V_V)$:**
$$e^{-T/RC} = \frac{V_{BB} - V_P}{V_{BB} - V_V}$$

**Step 5 — Apply natural logarithm to both sides:**
$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB} - V_P}{V_{BB} - V_V}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Negate to make $T$ positive:**
$$\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB} - V_V}{V_{BB} - V_P}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$.** For simplicity, ignoring the small diode drop $V_D$ (or replacing $V_{BB}$ by the effective $V_{BB} - V_D$ in the full model):
$$V_{BB} - V_P \approx V_{BB} - \eta V_{BB} = (1 - \eta)V_{BB}$$

**Step 8 — Also note that $V_{BB} - V_V \approx V_{BB}$ (since $V_V \ll V_{BB}$):**
$$\frac{T}{RC} \approx \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB}}{(1-\eta)V_{BB}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\eta}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Final result:**
$$\boxed{\,T = RC\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\eta}\right)\,}$$

**Step 10 — Numerical example:** Let $R = 50$ k$\Omega$, $C = 0.1\ \mu$F, $\eta = 0.7$:
$$T = (50 \times 10^3)(0.1 \times 10^{-6})\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-0.7}\right) = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\ln(3.333) \approx 6.0\ \text{ms}$$
$$f = \frac{1}{T} \approx 166.7\ \text{Hz}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Turn-On Methods

| Criterion | Forward Voltage | Gate Triggering | dv/dt | Temperature | Light |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|------|-------------|-------|
| **Controllability** | Poor | **Excellent** | Poor | Very Poor | Good |
| **Precision of $\alpha$** | None | **High** | None | None | Moderate |
| **Practical use** | Destructive | **Most drives** | Used in protection | Lab only | **HVDC, LASCR** |
| **Reverse-blocking safety** | Low | High (RCC snubber) | Low | Low | High |
| **KTU importance (marks)** | 1–2 | **6–10** | 2–4 | 1 | 2 |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Gate Triggering Circuits

| Criterion | R-Triggering | RC-Triggering | UJT Triggering |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Circuit complexity** | Simplest | Moderate | More complex |
| **Range of $\alpha$** | **0°–90°** only | **0°–180°** | **0°–180°** |
| **Pulse shape** | Slow ramp | RC delay | **Sharp pulse** |
| **Reliability** | Low | Medium | **High** |
| **Power dissipation** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Firing accuracy** | Poor | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Use in KTUs** | Basic | Common | **Most popular in answers** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why UJT is preferred:** A UJT produces a **sharp negative-resistance pulse** at the gate, which gives a **fast turn-on** and reduces **$t_{on}$** — critical in **high-frequency chopper drives** (e.g., switch-mode DC motor control).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SCR V-I Characteristic Regions

<!-- prettier-ignore -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Anode-Cathode Voltage VAK Applied] --> B{VAK Positive?}
    B -- No --> C[Reverse Blocking Mode]
    C --> D{VAK > VR?}
    D -- No --> E[Small Reverse Leakage IRL]
    D -- Yes --> F[REVERSE BREAKDOWN - DESTRUCTIVE]
    B -- Yes --> G[Forward Blocking Mode]
    G --> H{Gate Pulse Applied?}
    H -- No --> I{Small Forward Leakage IFL}
    I --> J{VAK > VBO?}
    J -- No --> K[Stays in OFF state]
    J -- Yes --> L[BREAKOVER - Uncontrolled Firing]
    H -- Yes --> M[Forward Conduction Mode]
    M --> N{IA > IL Latching Current?}
    N -- Yes --> O[SCR LATCHES ON]
    N -- No --> P[SCR Reverts to OFF]
    O --> Q{IA falls below IH?}
    Q -- No --> R[SCR Conducts continuously]
    Q -- Yes --> S[SCR Turns OFF]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Five Turn-On Methods of SCR

<!-- prettier-ignore -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SCR Turn-On Methods] --> B[1. Forward Voltage Triggering]
    A --> C[2. Gate Triggering - MOST COMMON]
    A --> D[3. dv/dt Triggering]
    A --> E[4. Temperature Triggering]
    A --> F[5. Light Triggering - LASCR]
    B --> B1[VAK exceeds VBO]
    C --> C1[Gate pulse I_G applied]
    C1 --> C2[Firing angle alpha precisely controlled]
    C2 --> C3[Used in phase-controlled rectifiers]
    D --> D1[High dv/dt causes displacement current]
    D1 --> D2[Displacement current acts as gate current]
    E --> E1[Junction temperature increase]
    E1 --> E2[Leakage current rises]
    E2 --> E3[SCR eventually fires]
    F --> F1[Photons incident on junction J2]
    F1 --> F2[Electron-hole pairs generated]
    F2 --> F3[SCR turns ON]
    F3 --> F4[Used in HVDC and HV DC breakers]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — UJT Gate Triggering Circuit (Architecture)

<!-- prettier-ignore -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    VBB[DC Supply VBB] --> R[R - Variable Timing Resistor]
    R --> C[C - Capacitor]
    C --> E[UJT Emitter]
    UJT1[UJT 2N2646] --> B1[Base B1]
    UJT1 --> B2[Base B2]
    B2 --> VBB
    B1 --> GND1[Ground]
    E --> C
    B1 --> R1[R1 - Pulse Shaping Resistor]
    R1 --> GP[Gate of SCR]
    GP --> SCR1[SCR Anode-Cathode]
    SCR1 --> LOAD[AC Load]
    LOAD --> AC[AC Mains]
    AC --> S[Synchronization Transformer]
    S --> VBB
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Switching Characteristics Timeline

<!-- prettier-ignore -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    A[t = 0: Gate Pulse Applied] --> B[Delay Time td: IA rises 0 to 10 percent]
    B --> C[Rise Time tr: IA rises 10 to 90 percent]
    C --> D[ton = td + tr completed]
    D --> E[SCR fully ON - VA drops to 1 to 1.5 V]
    E --> F[Anode current reduced to zero by external circuit]
    F --> G[Reverse Recovery Time trr: IA goes negative]
    G --> H[Forward Blocking Recovery Time tgr]
    H --> I[toff = trr + tgr completed]
    I --> J[SCR regains forward blocking capability]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — R vs RC vs UJT Triggering

<!-- prettier-ignore -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Gate Triggering Topologies] --> B[R-Triggering]
    A --> C[RC-Triggering]
    A --> D[UJT Relaxation Oscillator]

    B1 --> B2[Simple, but non-pulsed]
    C --> C1[RC delay network]
    C1 --> C2[Alpha extended to 0 to 180 degrees]

    D --> D1[UJT + RC + Synchronization]
    D1 --> D2[Sharp negative resistance pulse]
    D2 --> D3[Excellent for motor drives and choppers]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define (i) latching current $I_L$ and (ii) holding current $I_H$ of an SCR. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Latching Current ($I_L$):** It is the **minimum anode current** required to flow through the SCR at the instant of turn-on (immediately after the gate pulse is removed) such that the SCR **continues to conduct** and latches into the ON state. If the anode current falls below $I_L$ before latching, the SCR reverts to the OFF state.

**(ii) Holding Current ($I_H$):** It is the **minimum anode current** required to keep the SCR in the ON state *after* it has been latched. If $I_A$ falls below $I_H$, the SCR turns OFF.

**Relationship:** $\boxed{I_H < I_L}$ always.

Typically, $I_L = 2 I_H$ to $3 I_H$ for commercial SCRs. *(3 marks: 1.5 each)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any three turn-on methods of an SCR. Which one is the most commonly used and why?**

**Model Answer:**

**Three turn-on methods:**
1. Forward voltage triggering (breakover)
2. Gate triggering
3. dv/dt triggering

*(Other valid: Temperature, Light/LASCR)*

**Most commonly used:** **Gate triggering.**

**Why?**
- It offers **precise control** over the firing angle $\alpha$.
- It requires **low power** at the gate (a few mA at low voltage).
- It allows **electrical isolation** between control and power circuits (via pulse transformer or optocoupler).
- It is **reliable and repeatable** in industrial motor drives. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **static V-I characteristics of an SCR**. Mark the important quantities on the curve. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** Explain the **turn-on and turn-off dynamic (switching) characteristics** of an SCR with suitable waveforms. Define $t_d$, $t_r$, $t_{on}$, $t_{rr}$, $t_{gr}$, and $t_{off}$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Part (a) — V-I Characteristics (7 Marks)**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
         I_A ↑
              |          / ← Forward Conduction
              |         /    (Region 2)
              |        /
              |       /
              |      /
              |    • ← Breakover (V_BO)
              |   /|
              |  / |
   Forward    | /  |  Reverse
   Blocking  •/   |  Blocking
   (Region1)/     |  (Region 3)
          /  ↑    |     ↑
         /   |    |     |
   I_FL ↓    |    |  I_RL
              |    |     |
              |    |     • Reverse
              |    |       Breakdown
              +----+-----+-------→ V_AK
                   V_BO   -V_BR
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

The V-I characteristic of an SCR has **three main regions**:

1. **Forward Blocking Region (Region 1):** When the anode is positive with respect to the cathode but no gate signal is applied, junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased while $J_2$ is reverse-biased. A small forward leakage current $I_{FL}$ (mA) flows. The device offers high impedance. This continues until the forward voltage reaches $V_{BO}$.

2. **Forward Conduction Region (Region 2):** Once triggered, regenerative action turns the SCR fully ON. The voltage across the SCR drops to a low value (1–1.5 V) and the device behaves like a closed switch.

3. **Reverse Blocking Region (Region 3):** When the anode is negative, junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are reverse-biased. Only a small reverse leakage current $I_{RL}$ flows until the reverse breakdown voltage $V_{BR(1)}$ is exceeded (destructive avalanche).

**Key Quantities Marked:** $V_{BO}$, $I_L$, $I_H$, $I_{FL}$, $I_{RL}$, $V_{BR(1)}$.

---

#### **Part (b) — Switching Characteristics (7 Marks)**

**Turn-On Waveform (3 marks):**

```
   I_A ↑
        |          ___________________
        |         /
        |        /
        |       /  ←  Rise time t_r
        |      /
        |    /     ←  Delay time t_d
        |   /
        |__/
        +--|---|---|----→ time
           0  td  t_r
                  t_on = t_d + t_r
```

- **Delay time $t_d$:** Time for $I_A$ to rise from 0% to 10% of final value. During this period, carriers spread across junction $J_2$.
- **Rise time $t_r$:** Time for $I_A$ to rise from 10% to 90%. Rapid regenerative conduction builds up.
- **Turn-on time $t_{on} = t_d + t_r$** (typical: 2–4 $\mu$s).

**Turn-Off Waveform (4 marks):**

```
   I_A ↑
        |‾‾‾‾‾\        ___________ 
        |      \      /  ← t_gr  
        |       \    /
        |        \  /  ← t_rr (current goes negative)
        |________\/_______________→ time
                0  t_rr  t_off
```

- **Reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$:** Time for stored carriers to be swept out; anode current briefly goes negative.
- **Gate recovery time $t_{gr}$:** Time for junction $J_2$ to regain forward blocking capability.
- **Turn-off time $t_{off} = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$** (typical: 10–100 $\mu$s).

**Important:** The SCR must remain off for at least $t_{off}$ before a forward voltage is reapplied, otherwise it will refire.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Many students confuse $t_d$ as the time to reach 50% of $I_A$ (it is 10%). Read the textbook convention carefully — **0→10% for $t_d$, 10→90% for $t_r$**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> **(a)** Explain any **three methods of turning ON an SCR** with relevant diagrams. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **UJT-based gate triggering circuit** for an SCR. Derive the expression for its firing period $T = RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-\eta}\right)$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Part (a) — Three Turn-On Methods (7 Marks)**

**Method 1 — Gate Triggering (2.5 marks):**

A positive gate current $I_G$ exceeding $I_{GT}$ is applied between Gate and Cathode. This injects carriers into junction $J_2$, turning it ON. The firing angle $\alpha$ can be precisely controlled by varying the instant of $I_G$ application in the AC cycle. This is the **standard method in phase-controlled converters**.

**Method 2 — dv/dt Triggering (2.5 marks):**

If the forward anode voltage rises rapidly, the displacement current $i_C = C_j \cdot dv/dt$ through the junction capacitance $C_j$ of $J_2$ can exceed the gate trigger current, causing false triggering. For a sinusoidal supply $v = V_m \sin \omega t$, the maximum dv/dt is $2\pi f V_m$. This is **undesirable** and is suppressed by **snubber circuits** ($R_s$ in series, $C_s$ across SCR).

**Method 3 — Light Triggering / LASCR (2 marks):**

When light (photons) of appropriate wavelength falls on the reverse-biased junction $J_2$, electron-hole pairs are generated, increasing the leakage current. If this exceeds $I_{GT}$, the SCR turns ON. Used in **HVDC transmission systems** and **HV DC circuit breakers** for galvanic isolation.

---

#### **Part (b) — UJT Gate Triggering Circuit (7 Marks)**

**Circuit Diagram (3 marks):**

```
              V_BB (+)
               │
               │
              ┌┴┐
              │ │ R (Variable)
              │ │
              └┬┘
               ├──────┬──── E (UJT Emitter)
              ─┴─    │
              ─── C  │ 
              ─┬─    │
               ├─────┤
               │    ┌┴┐ B1 (Base 1)
               │    │ │
               │    │R1│ (Pulse shaping)
               │    │ │
               │    └┬┘
              GND    ├────► To SCR Gate
                     │
              ┌──────┤
              │ R_B2 │
              │      │
              └──────┤
                     │
                    V_BB
                     B2 (Base 2)
```

**Operation (2 marks):**

1. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ from $V_{BB}$.
2. When $v_C$ reaches the UJT peak voltage $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$, the emitter–base-1 junction becomes forward-biased.
3. UJT fires (negative resistance region); $C$ discharges rapidly through $R_1$ producing a **sharp voltage pulse** at the SCR gate.
4. The SCR turns ON; $C$ recharges, and the cycle repeats.

**Derivation of $T$ (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.2 above for the complete derivation)*

$$T = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In UJT triggering, the *synchronization circuit* is critical — the UJT oscillator must be **synchronized with the AC mains** so that the firing pulse occurs at the **same phase angle** in every cycle. Forgetting to mention synchronization costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 High-Yield Numerical Problems

### Numerical 1: dv/dt Calculation
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> An SCR is connected across a 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply. The junction capacitance is 30 pF. Calculate the **maximum displacement current** at the instant of switching. Also determine the **safe dv/dt rating** if the SCR has $I_{GT} = 15$ mA.

**Solution:**

Maximum dv/dt:
$$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2\pi f V_m = 2\pi \times 50 \times (230 \times \sqrt{2}) \approx 2.04 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V/s}$$

Maximum displacement current:
$$i_{C,max} = C_j \cdot \left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = 30 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.04 \times 10^{5} = 6.12\ \text{nA}$$

This is much less than $I_{GT} = 15$ mA, so **no false dv/dt triggering** for this configuration.

For maximum allowable dv/dt rating (when $i_{C,max} = I_{GT}$):
$$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)_{max} = \frac{I_{GT}}{C_j} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-12}} = 5 \times 10^{8}\ \text{V/s} = 500\ \text{V/\mu s}$$

### Numerical 2: UJT Firing Period
> A UJT relaxation oscillator has $R = 10$ k$\Omega$, $C = 0.5\ \mu$F, $\eta = 0.6$. Calculate (a) firing period, (b) firing frequency.

**Solution:**

(a)
$$T = RC \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right) = (10 \times 10^3)(0.5 \times 10^{-6})\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.4}\right) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(2.5) = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9163 \approx 4.58\ \text{ms}$$

(b)
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{4.58 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 218.3\ \text{Hz}$$

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SCR V-I, Switching, Turn-On, and Gate Triggering**

- [ ] **V-I curve** has **3 main regions** (Forward blocking, Forward conduction, Reverse blocking) and **2 breakdown regions** (Breakover + Reverse avalanche).
- [ ] **$I_L$** (latching) > **$I_H$** (holding) — always.
- [ ] **Turn-on time** = $t_d + t_r$ (0→10% then 10→90% of $I_A$).
- [ ] **Turn-off time** = $t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ — addition, not subtraction.
- [ ] **$t_{on}$** is in $\mu$s; **$t_{off}$** is also in $\mu$s but ~10× larger than $t_{on}$.
- [ ] **5 turn-on methods:** Forward voltage, Gate (★ most common), dv/dt, Temperature, Light (LASCR).
- [ ] **dv/dt triggering** is a *false firing* mechanism; suppressed by **snubber circuit**.
- [ ] Max dv/dt for sinusoidal: $2\pi f V_m$.
- [ ] **3 gate triggering circuits:** R (α: 0–90°), RC (α: 0–180°), UJT (★ best, sharp pulse).
- [ ] **UJT formula:** $T = RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)$.
- [ ] **$V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$** where $\eta = 0.5$ to $0.85$ (intrinsic stand-off ratio).
- [ ] **Gate trigger ratings:** $V_{GT} = 0.5$–$2$ V, $I_{GT} = 1$–$50$ mA.
- [ ] **UJT synchronization with AC mains is essential** for precise $\alpha$ control.
- [ ] **Gate triggering** is **universal** in motor drives; **Light triggering** is used in HVDC.
- [ ] **Snubber circuit** ($R_s$ + $C_s$) protects against **dv/dt** AND **transient overvoltages**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra:** *"The SCR's gate is its brain, its anode is its muscle, and dv/dt is its Achilles' heel."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R and RC Triggering Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** R and RC Triggering Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ R and RC Triggering Circuits — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> **R Triggering Circuit** is the simplest gate-firing scheme for a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) in which a single **resistor `R`** is placed in series between the AC supply and the gate–cathode terminals, so that the SCR is turned ON when the instantaneous AC supply voltage across `R` becomes equal to the **gate trigger voltage `V_gt`**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RC Triggering Circuit** is an improved AC gate-firing scheme in which a **series RC network** (`R` and `C`) is connected between the AC supply and the gate–cathode path. The SCR fires when the **voltage developed across the capacitor `C`** reaches the **gate trigger voltage `V_gt`**. This provides a much wider and more stable range of firing-angle control.

These circuits belong to the family of **AC voltage-triggering methods** (Class A triggering) for thyristors, and they are evaluated under **Course Outcome CO2** of PCRUT503 (analysis of firing and commutation circuits).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚰

Imagine a **water tank on a tilting platform** with a small side-spillover pipe at a fixed height:

- **R-triggering** is like tilting the platform slowly until the water just reaches the spillover pipe. The height of the platform (analogous to instantaneous supply voltage) directly controls when water flows out (analogous to SCR turn-on). But if the temperature changes (component drift), the spillover height changes — the firing becomes **unstable**.
- **RC-triggering** is like a **delayed spillover system**: water first fills a small bucket (the capacitor `C`) through a pipe of fixed diameter (the resistor `R`). The bucket takes time to fill. By changing the bucket size or pipe length, you can precisely delay the spillover moment. This is **stable, predictable, and adjustable**.

The capacitor `C` acts as a **timing memory element**, ensuring that the gate fires at a precise, repeatable phase angle — which is critical for **phase-controlled rectifiers and AC voltage controllers** used in motor drives.

## 1.3 Standard Symbols and Quantities (Bold per KTU Convention)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Peak supply voltage | **V_m** | **V (volts)** |
| Gate trigger voltage | **V_gt** | **V (volts)** |
| Gate trigger current | **I_gt** | **mA** |
| Firing (delay) angle | **α** | **degrees or radians** |
| Conduction angle | **γ = π − α** | **degrees or radians** |
| Series resistance | **R** | **Ω (ohms)** |
| Capacitance | **C** | **μF** |
| Supply frequency | **f** | **Hz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Always clearly state the assumption that the SCR, once fired, remains ON due to the **latching condition** (anode current > latching current) until the natural commutation at the next AC zero-crossing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 R-Triggering Circuit — Operating Logic

**Circuit elements:** AC source `v_s = V_m sin(ωt)` → Diode `D` (half-wave) → Resistor `R` → Gate–Cathode of SCR.

**Step-by-step working:**

1. During the **positive half-cycle** of the supply, the diode `D` is forward-biased and allows current to flow toward the gate.
2. The instantaneous voltage across `R` equals the supply voltage (assuming negligible gate current):
   $$v_g(t) \approx v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
3. As `ωt` rises from `0°`, `v_g(t)` increases sinusoidally.
4. **Firing condition:** The moment `v_g(t)` reaches the gate trigger voltage `V_gt`, the SCR is fired (latched ON).
5. Once ON, the SCR clamps the gate voltage to ≈ `1 V` (gate-cathode drop), and the gate loses control until the next half-cycle.
6. The angle at which firing occurs is called the **firing angle α**.

**Why this matters:** The simplest possible design — but `α` depends only on the ratio `V_gt / V_m`. Temperature changes that alter `V_gt` cause **erratic firing**. Also, the range of `α` is highly restricted (typically `30°` to `90°`).

## 2.2 RC Half-Wave Triggering Circuit — Operating Logic

**Circuit elements:** AC source → Diode `D` → Series combination of `R` and `C` → Gate–Cathode of SCR (capacitor voltage is the trigger).

**Step-by-step working:**

1. During the **positive half-cycle**, the diode conducts and the series RC network is energized.
2. The source voltage drives current through `R`, charging `C`.
3. The capacitor voltage `v_c(t)` **lags** the source voltage due to the phase shift introduced by the RC network.
4. The angle by which `v_c(t)` is **delayed** relative to `v_s(t)` is:
   $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_c}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega R C}\right)$$
5. The **peak of the capacitor voltage** occurs at:
   $$\omega t_{peak} = \frac{\pi}{2} + \phi$$
6. **Firing condition:** When `v_c(t)` reaches `V_gt`, the SCR fires at angle `α` where:
   $$V_m \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z} \cdot \sin(\alpha + \phi - 90^\circ) = V_{gt}$$
7. After firing, the SCR is ON and the capacitor discharges through the gate, resetting the network for the next cycle.

**Why this matters:** Because the phase delay `φ` depends on the product `RC`, varying `R` (using a variable resistor) gives a **wide, smooth range of `α`** — typically from `20°` to `180°`. This is the **most widely used AC triggering method** for low/medium-power drives.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | AC supply voltage |
| 2 | $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ | Peak value from RMS |
| 3 | $\alpha_R = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{gt}}{V_m}\right)$ | Firing angle for **R**-triggering |
| 4 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R C}\right)$ | Phase lag in **RC** network |
| 5 | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_c^{\,2}} = \sqrt{R^2 + \dfrac{1}{(\omega C)^2}}$ | RC impedance magnitude |
| 6 | $\sin(\alpha - (90^\circ - \phi)) = \dfrac{V_{gt} \cdot Z}{V_m \cdot X_c}$ | Implicit relation for **α** in RC-trigger |
| 7 | $\alpha_{RC} = (90^\circ - \phi) + \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{gt} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}{V_m}\right)$ | Explicit firing angle (RC) |
| 8 | $\gamma = 180^\circ - \alpha$ | Conduction angle (half-wave) |
| 9 | $\Delta\alpha_{RC} = \dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial \phi}$ | Sensitivity to phase change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Vigilance:**
> - All angles in **degrees** or **radians** consistently.
> - `ω = 2πf` in **rad/s**.
> - `X_c` in **ohms**, `C` in **farads**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Single-phase AC voltage controllers** for fan regulators, lamp dimmers, heater control.
- **Phase-controlled half-wave/full-wave rectifiers** used as front-end converters in DC motor drives.
- **TRIAC-based light dimmers** in domestic and theatrical lighting.
- **Soft-start circuits** for induction motors and transformers.
- **Crowbar and protection circuits** where precise overvoltage detection is needed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for **every algebraic transition** and the **final boxed expression**. Skipping intermediate steps leads to mark deductions even if the final answer is correct.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Firing Angle for R-Triggering Circuit

**Given:** Single-phase AC supply `v_s = V_m sin(ωt)` applied through resistor `R` to the gate.

**Assumption:** Gate current `i_g` is small → voltage across `R` equals supply voltage.

**Step 1.** Write the instantaneous gate voltage:
$$v_g(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2.** Apply the firing condition. The SCR fires when `v_g(t)` first equals the gate trigger voltage `V_gt`:
$$V_m \sin(\alpha) = V_{gt}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the firing angle `α`:
$$\sin(\alpha) = \frac{V_{gt}}{V_m}$$

**Step 4.** Take the inverse sine:
$$\boxed{\;\alpha_R = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{gt}}{V_m}\right)\;}$$

**Step 5.** Conduction angle:
$$\gamma = 180^\circ - \alpha_R$$

**Analytical Commentary:**
- `α_R` depends only on the **ratio** `V_gt / V_m`, so for fixed supply the firing is locked once components are chosen.
- Temperature-induced drift of `V_gt` directly shifts `α_R`, which is the chief drawback.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Firing Angle for RC Half-Wave Triggering Circuit

**Given:** Series RC across AC supply, capacitor voltage is the trigger.

**Step 1.** Express the impedance of the RC branch:
$$Z = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}, \quad \vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_c^{\,2}}, \quad X_c = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

**Step 2.** Express the phase angle of the impedance (capacitor causes current to lead voltage, so impedance has a negative angle; equivalently, voltage across `C` lags supply):
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_c}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega R C}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Write the supply voltage phasor and current phasor:
$$\underline{V}_s = V_m \angle 0^\circ$$
$$\underline{I} = \frac{\underline{V}_s}{Z} = \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \angle \phi$$

**Step 4.** Voltage across the capacitor (current through capacitor × capacitive reactance, with `−90°` lag):
$$\underline{V}_c = \underline{I} \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{V_m \cdot X_c}{\vert Z \vert} \angle (\phi - 90^\circ)$$

**Step 5.** Time-domain expression for capacitor voltage:
$$v_c(t) = \frac{V_m \cdot X_c}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\omega t + \phi - 90^\circ)$$

**Step 6.** Apply the firing condition — when `v_c(t)` first reaches `V_gt`:
$$\frac{V_m \cdot X_c}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\alpha + \phi - 90^\circ) = V_{gt}$$

**Step 7.** Substitute `X_c / |Z| = 1 / √(1 + (ωRC)²)`:
$$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}} \sin(\alpha + \phi - 90^\circ) = V_{gt}$$

**Step 8.** Rearrange:
$$\sin(\alpha + \phi - 90^\circ) = \frac{V_{gt} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}}{V_m}$$

**Step 9.** Since `sin(θ − 90°) = −cos(θ)`, write `α + φ − 90° = −(90° − φ − α)`. Use the identity `sin(α − (90° − φ))`:
$$\sin\bigl(\alpha - (90^\circ - \phi)\bigr) = \frac{V_{gt} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}}{V_m}$$

**Step 10.** Final explicit expression for firing angle:
$$\boxed{\;\alpha_{RC} = (90^\circ - \phi) + \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{gt} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}}{V_m}\right)\;}$$

**Step 11.** Conduction angle for the half-wave rectifier:
$$\gamma = 180^\circ - \alpha_{RC}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | R-Triggering | RC Half-Wave Triggering |
|---|---|---|
| Components | 1 Resistor + 1 Diode | 1 Resistor + 1 Capacitor + 1 Diode |
| Trigger Quantity | Supply voltage | Capacitor voltage |
| Range of `α` | Narrow (`30°`–`90°`) | Wide (`20°`–`170°`) |
| Temperature Stability | Poor | Good |
| Cost | Very Low | Low |
| Use Case | Simple, low-precision drives | Standard phase-controlled drives |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:**
A single-phase `230 V`, `50 Hz` AC supply feeds an RC half-wave triggering circuit with `R = 10 kΩ` and `C = 0.1 μF`. The SCR has `V_gt = 2 V`. Compute the firing angle `α` and the conduction angle `γ`.

**Solution:**

**Step 1.** Peak supply voltage:
$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 \approx 325.27 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2.** Compute capacitive reactance:
$$X_c = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 31{,}831 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3.** Impedance magnitude:
$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_c^{\,2}} = \sqrt{(10{,}000)^2 + (31{,}831)^2} \approx 33{,}362 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 4.** Phase angle:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_c}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{31{,}831}{10{,}000}\right) \approx 72.56^\circ$$

**Step 5.** Compute the inner ratio:
$$\frac{V_{gt} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}}{V_m} = \frac{2 \cdot \sqrt{1 + (2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6})^2}}{325.27}$$
$$= \frac{2 \cdot \sqrt{1 + (0.3142)^2}}{325.27} = \frac{2 \cdot 1.0480}{325.27} \approx 0.006445$$

**Step 6.** Inverse sine:
$$\sin^{-1}(0.006445) \approx 0.369^\circ$$

**Step 7.** Final firing angle:
$$\alpha = (90^\circ - 72.56^\circ) + 0.369^\circ = 17.44^\circ + 0.369^\circ \approx 17.81^\circ$$

**Step 8.** Conduction angle:
$$\gamma = 180^\circ - 17.81^\circ \approx 162.19^\circ$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A small `α` means the SCR fires early in the half-cycle → high RMS output voltage → high power delivered to the load. Increasing `R` shifts `α` later → lower power. This is the principle of **phase-angle control** in AC drives.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼 Structural Schematics & Functional Block Diagrams

## 4.1 R-Triggering Circuit — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACsrc[AC SUPPLY v_s = Vm sin wt] --> DiodeA[DIODE D half wave]
    DiodeA --> ResistorA[RESISTOR R gate resistor]
    ResistorA --> GateA[GATE G of SCR]
    GateA --> CathodeA[CATHODE K of SCR]
    ACsrc -.reference.-> CathodeA
    SCRblockA[SCR LATCH ON when v_R equals V_gt]
    ResistorA -.feeds.-> SCRblockA
```

**Waveform Block Diagram (R-Trigger):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WAVES_R [WAVEFORM TIMELINE positive half cycle]
        dirLR
        T0[wt = 0 deg zero crossing] --> T1[wt rises v_s increases]
        T1 --> T2[wt = alpha v_R reaches V_gt]
        T2 --> T3[SCR FIRES clamped to ~1 V]
        T3 --> T4[wt = 180 deg natural commutation]
    end
```

---

## 4.2 RC Half-Wave Triggering Circuit — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACsrcB[AC SUPPLY v_s = Vm sin wt] --> DiodeB[DIODE D half wave]
    DiodeB --> Rser[RESISTOR R series]
    Rser --> CapNode[NODE across CAPACITOR C]
    CapNode --> CapacitorB[CAPACITOR C stores charge]
    CapacitorB --> GateB[GATE G of SCR]
    GateB --> CathodeB[CATHODE K of SCR]
    ACsrcB -.reference.-> CathodeB
    PhaseShift[PHASE LAG phi introduced by RC]
    Rser -.creates.-> PhaseShift
    PhaseShift -.delays.-> CapacitorB
    FireCond[SCR FIRES when v_c equals V_gt]
    CapacitorB -.feeds.-> FireCond
```

**Waveform Block Diagram (RC-Trigger):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WAVES_RC [WAVEFORM TIMELINE with phase lag phi]
        dirLR
        U0[wt = 0 deg v_s starts at zero] --> U1[wt rises v_s leads v_c]
        U1 --> U2[v_c rises with lag phi]
        U2 --> U3[wt = alpha v_c reaches V_gt]
        U3 --> U4[SCR FIRES clamped to ~1 V]
        U4 --> U5[wt = 180 deg natural commutation]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[AC VOLTAGE APPLIED] --> Branch{TRIGGERING METHOD}
    Branch -- R only --> RPath[v_g equals v_s directly]
    RPath --> RFire[Fire when v_s equals V_gt]
    Branch -- RC network --> RCPath[v_c lags v_s by phi]
    RCPath --> RCFire[Fire when v_c equals V_gt]
    RFire --> Conclude[SCR ON conduction angle gamma]
    RCFire --> Conclude
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** In the answer script, always label the **x-axis as `ωt (degrees)`**, the **y-axis as `v_s, v_R, v_c, V_gt`**, and clearly mark the **firing instant** with a vertical dashed line dropping from the intersection point on the trigger voltage curve to the time axis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define firing angle `α` and conduction angle `γ` for an SCR in a phase-controlled rectifier. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **firing angle `α`** is the angle (measured from the instant the SCR becomes forward-biased) at which the gate trigger pulse is applied and the SCR is turned ON.

> The **conduction angle `γ`** is the angle (measured in degrees or radians) during which the SCR remains in the ON state and conducts load current.

> **Relationship (for a half-wave controlled rectifier):**
> $$\gamma = 180^\circ - \alpha$$

> A larger `α` → smaller `γ` → lower RMS load voltage → lower power delivered. This is the basis of **phase-angle control** in AC drives.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State any two advantages of RC triggering over R triggering of an SCR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Advantage 1:** RC triggering provides a **wider and smoother range of firing-angle control** (typically `20°` to `170°`) compared to R triggering (only `30°` to `90°`), because the phase lag `φ = tan⁻¹(1/(ωRC))` can be varied over a broad range by adjusting `R`.

> **Advantage 2:** RC triggering offers **better thermal stability**. The firing angle depends on the `RC` time constant, which is far less sensitive to temperature than the gate trigger voltage `V_gt` alone. Hence the firing instant is more repeatable across operating conditions.

> *(Optional 3rd point if asked:)* RC triggering gives **better isolation** between the gate trigger circuit and the high-power load circuit, reducing the risk of false triggering.

---

## 📕 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** full question (either A or B). Each question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Derive the expression for the firing angle `α` of an SCR in a single-phase RC half-wave triggering circuit connected to an AC source `v_s = V_m sin(ωt)`. Clearly state the assumption used.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Circuit & Assumption (1 mark):** A series RC network is connected through a diode to the gate-cathode of the SCR. We assume gate current is negligible so that all source current flows through R and C.
2. **Impedance (1 mark):** `|Z| = √(R² + (1/ωC)²)`, with phase angle `φ = tan⁻¹(1/(ωRC))`.
3. **Capacitor voltage (2 marks):**
   $$v_c(t) = \frac{V_m \cdot X_c}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\omega t + \phi - 90^\circ)$$
4. **Firing condition (1 mark):** `v_c(α) = V_gt`.
5. **Final expression (2 marks):**
   $$\alpha = (90^\circ - \phi) + \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{gt} \sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}}{V_m}\right)$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**For the circuit above with `V_rms = 230 V`, `f = 50 Hz`, `R = 20 kΩ`, `C = 0.05 μF`, and `V_gt = 1.5 V`, find `α` and `γ`.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Peak voltage (1 mark):** `V_m = √2 × 230 ≈ 325.27 V`.
2. **`ωRC` (1 mark):** `ωRC = 2π × 50 × 20,000 × 0.05 × 10⁻⁶ = 0.3142`.
3. **Phase lag (1 mark):** `φ = tan⁻¹(1/0.3142) ≈ 72.56°`.
4. **Inner ratio (2 marks):**
   $$\frac{V_{gt} \sqrt{1 + (0.3142)^2}}{V_m} = \frac{1.5 \times 1.0480}{325.27} \approx 0.004833$$
5. **`α` (1 mark):** `α = 17.44° + 0.277° ≈ 17.72°`.
6. **`γ` (1 mark):** `γ = 180° − 17.72° ≈ 162.28°`.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Explain the operation of the R-triggering circuit for an SCR with the help of a neat circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for firing angle `α`.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Circuit description (2 marks):** AC source → diode D → resistor R → gate–cathode (G–K) of SCR. Cathode is connected to the source reference.
2. **Operation (2 marks):** During positive half-cycle, the gate voltage rises sinusoidally. When it equals `V_gt`, the SCR fires and clamps the gate. The SCR turns OFF at the natural zero-crossing.
3. **Waveforms (1 mark):** Sketch `v_s`, `v_R = v_s`, gate pulse at `α`, load voltage clamped after `α`.
4. **Derivation (2 marks):** `V_m sin α = V_gt → α = sin⁻¹(V_gt / V_m)`.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Explain the RC half-wave triggering circuit with circuit and waveforms. List two advantages over R-triggering.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Circuit description (2 marks):** Same as R-triggering but with a capacitor C added; the voltage across C triggers the gate.
2. **Operation (2 marks):** Capacitor voltage `v_c` lags `v_s` by `φ = tan⁻¹(1/(ωRC))`. When `v_c = V_gt`, SCR fires.
3. **Waveforms (1 mark):** Sketch `v_s` leading, `v_c` lagging, gate trigger at α, clamped output.
4. **Advantages (2 marks):**
   - Wider range of firing-angle control.
   - Better temperature stability.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the diode** — Students often draw the circuit without the half-wave diode `D`, losing 1 mark on circuit completeness.
> 2. **Confusing `α` and `γ`** — Writing `γ = α` instead of `γ = 180° − α`. Examiners deduct a full mark.
> 3. **Mixing up phase lag formulas** — Using `φ = tan⁻¹(R/X_c)` instead of the correct `φ = tan⁻¹(X_c/R) = tan⁻¹(1/(ωRC))`. Both formulations exist; **stick to one consistent convention** throughout the answer.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption** that gate current is negligible — this is mandatory for the derivation to be valid.
> 5. **Skipping units** in the numerical problem — every numerical answer must carry **units** (V, Ω, Hz, degrees).
> 6. **Drawing waveforms without axis labels** — always label the **x-axis (ωt in degrees)** and **y-axis (voltage)**.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **R-triggering:** Firing angle `α = sin⁻¹(V_gt / V_m)`. Simple but narrow range and temperature-sensitive.
- **RC half-wave triggering:** Phase lag `φ = tan⁻¹(1/(ωRC))` delays the capacitor voltage, giving a wider firing-angle range.
- **Master formula for RC-trigger:**
  $$\alpha = (90^\circ - \phi) + \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{gt}\sqrt{1 + (\omega R C)^2}}{V_m}\right)$$
- **Conduction angle:** Always `γ = 180° − α` for a half-wave controlled rectifier.
- **V_gt is small (≈ 1–3 V)** while V_m is large (≈ 325 V for 230 V RMS), so `α` for RC is typically in the range `20°–170°`.
- **The capacitor `C` acts as a timing memory element** — its charge accumulation decides the firing instant.
- **Increasing R** in the RC circuit → increases `φ` → shifts `α` toward larger values → reduces conduction → reduces load power.
- **The diode ensures half-wave operation** — firing occurs only in the positive half-cycle.
- **Once the SCR latches ON, the gate loses all control** until the next natural commutation (zero-crossing).
- **KTU CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2** — *Analyze the firing and commutation circuits of thyristors for power electronic converters*.
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4) — students must apply formulas to numerical problems and analyze circuit operation.

> 🎯 **Last-Mile Tip for KTU Boards:** Always present your answer in this order — **(1) Diagram → (2) Assumptions → (3) Derivation with boxed final formula → (4) Waveform sketch → (5) Numerical substitution with units.** Examiners award marks for **structure** as much as for content.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Line-Synchronised Triggering

### 📘 Formal Definition
**Line-Synchronised Triggering** is a technique used to fire thyristors (SCRs) connected to the AC mains at a precisely controllable instant in each half-cycle, measured as the **firing angle (α)** from the natural zero-crossing of the line voltage. A **ramp-comparator firing scheme** synchronises the ramp with the supply zero-crossing, ensuring that the firing pulse is locked to the line frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is fundamental to all **phase-controlled rectifiers** and **AC-AC converters** (Cycloconverters, AC Voltage Controllers).

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **water tap connected to a city water pipeline** that opens and closes with the rhythm of the city water pressure cycle. The **"α"** is the delay after the pressure cycle starts before you open the tap. The further you delay (larger α), the less water flows. **Line synchronisation** is like ensuring your tap-opening moment is perfectly aligned with the start of every pressure cycle — otherwise, the flow becomes irregular and inefficient.

---

## 1.2 Natural and Forced Commutation (Concept Only)

### 📘 Formal Definitions
- **Natural Commutation (Line/Class A Commutation)**: The SCR turns OFF by itself when the current through it naturally falls below the holding current. This happens automatically because the AC supply voltage reverses polarity every half-cycle.
- **Forced Commutation**: The SCR is turned OFF by an **external active circuit** (LC resonant tank, complementary SCR, or auxiliary thyristor) that forces the anode current to zero.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module-2 syllabus mentions **"Concept Only"** — you are expected to know the classes (A, B, C, D, E), their energy source, and typical application, but not design their commutating components.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy
- **Natural Commutation** = A **traffic light turning red automatically** at fixed intervals — no extra system needed.
- **Forced Commutation** = A **traffic police officer manually stopping traffic** even if the light is green.

---

## 1.3 Protection of SCR

### 📘 Formal Definition
SCRs must be protected against **abnormal electrical and thermal stresses** that exceed their ratings. Protection circuits ensure the device operates within its **Safe Operating Area (SOA)**.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy
Think of an SCR as a **tempered glass pane**: it can take normal stress, but sudden shock (dv/dt, di/dt), heat (overcurrent), or pressure (overvoltage) will shatter it. Protection circuits are like **shock absorbers, thermal cutouts, and pressure-relief valves** for the glass.

---

## 1.4 Isolation in Gate Drive Circuits

### 📘 Formal Definition
**Galvanic Isolation** ensures the low-voltage control electronics and the high-voltage power circuit share **no direct electrical connection** — only energy transfer through **light (opto-coupler)** or **magnetic coupling (pulse transformer)**.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy
Like communicating with someone across a river using a **torch (light)** or a **slingshot with a note tied to it (magnetic pulse)** — information crosses, but electricity does not.

---

## 1.5 Single-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier with R Load

### 📘 Formal Definition
A power converter using **four SCRs (T1, T2, T3, T4)** in a full-bridge configuration that converts **single-phase AC to variable DC** by phase-controlling the firing angle α. With a purely **resistive (R) load**, the load voltage is unidirectional but discontinuous when α > 0°.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **workhorse circuit** for understanding phase-controlled converters in KTU 2024 scheme. It appears in nearly every university exam paper under Module 2.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **two-way seesaw valve** on a water pipe that swaps direction every half-cycle. The **trigger delay (α)** controls how much water flows through the pipe per cycle. A larger α = less water (lower DC output). At α = 0°, you get maximum flow; at α = 180°, no flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Line-Synchronised Triggering — Operation Block Logic

1. **Zero-Crossing Detection (ZCD)**: A precise square wave is generated at every zero crossing of the AC supply using an **op-amp comparator** or **opto-coupler ZCD circuit**.
2. **Ramp Generation**: At each zero crossing, a **linear ramp** (sawtooth) is generated, synchronised in phase with the line voltage.
3. **Reference Comparison**: A DC control voltage **Vc** is compared with the ramp using a comparator.
4. **Firing Pulse Generation**: The instant the ramp crosses Vc, a firing pulse of width **≥ 10° electrical** is generated.
5. **Isolation & Distribution**: The pulse passes through an **isolator** (opto or pulse transformer) to the SCR gate.
6. **Pulse Amplification**: The pulse is amplified to the required **Ig, Vg** values specified in the SCR datasheet.

> [!NOTE]
> Firing angle α is measured from the **instant the SCR becomes forward biased** (i.e., from the natural commutation point of the *previous* SCR pair), not from the supply zero crossing — except in **bridge circuits**, where it is conventionally measured from the supply zero.

### 🔑 Why is Synchronisation Critical?
- A single AC cycle has 360°; a 1° error = **1/360 = 0.28 % error in firing** → significant sub-harmonic distortion in rectifier output.
- Without synchronisation, the **DC output drifts**, **DC motor commutator sparking** increases, and **supply current harmonics** rise.

---

## 2.2 Commutation Methods — Concept Matrix

| Class | Type | Energy Source | Typical Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **A** | Natural / Line / Class A | AC supply itself | All line-frequency rectifiers & AC controllers |
| **B** | Resonant (LC) | LC tank | High-frequency inverters |
| **C** | Complementary (aux SCR) | Charged capacitor | Choppers, forced commutated inverters |
| **D** | Auxiliary pulse | External pulse source | DC circuit breakers, very fast switches |
| **E** | External pulse (line notch) | Source voltage notch | Three-phase thyristor bridges |

---

## 2.3 SCR Protection — Engineering Requirements

| Protection Type | Cause | Typical Solution |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **dv/dt protection** | Spurious triggering from rapid voltage rise | **RC snubber** across SCR (Rs = 10–100 Ω, Cs = 0.01–0.1 µF) |
| **di/dt protection** | Localised heating at turn-on | **Small series inductor** (L = 5–20 µH) |
| **Overvoltage (transient)** | Switching surges, lightning | **MOV / Voltage-clamping diode / Avalanche diode** |
| **Overcurrent (steady)** | Load short, overload | **Fuse (HRC), MCB, electronic overcurrent relay** |
| **Thermal** | Continuous conduction losses | **Heat sink + thermal compound, fan cooling** |
| **Gate protection** | Reverse gate breakdown, noise | **Gate-cathode resistor (Rgk ≈ 1 kΩ), zener across GK** |
| **Voltage commutation** | dv/dt from commutation in bridge | **RC snubber or RCD across thyristor** |

---

## 2.4 Isolation in Gate Drive — Design Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 highlights the following **six functional requirements**:
> 1. **Galvanic isolation** between control (logic) and power ground.
> 2. **High dV/dt immunity** on the control side.
> 3. **Pulse amplification** to required gate current.
> 4. **Pulse width and rise time** as per SCR datasheet.
> 5. **Synchronisation** with line zero-crossings.
> 6. **Bidirectional trigger compatibility** in bridge circuits.

### Comparison: Opto-Isolator vs. Pulse Transformer

| Parameter | Opto-Coupler | Pulse Transformer |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Isolation voltage | Up to ~5 kV | Up to ~10 kV (typical) |
| Speed / bandwidth | Moderate (limited by phototransistor) | Excellent (sub-µs) |
| DC transmission | Yes | **No** (only pulse/AC) |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Pulse shape | Square/rectangular | Pulse train or single pulse |
| Drive requirement | Current-driven (LED) | Voltage-driven primary |
| Typical IC | MOC3021, MOC3041, HCPL-3120 | Ferrocube / Coilcraft pulse xmfr |
| KTU typical use | Low/medium power | High-frequency or high-noise environment |

---

## 2.5 Single-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier with R Load — Key Concepts

### Operating Sequence
- **0 to α**: All SCRs OFF → output = 0
- **α to π**: T1 & T2 conduct → vo = +Vm sin(ωt)
- **π to (π+α)**: All SCRs OFF (since load current cannot reverse for R load) → vo = 0
- **(π+α) to 2π**: T3 & T4 conduct → vo = +Vm sin(ωt) (absolute value)
- **Output** is **discontinuous** and contains **notches** at every π radian.

### Boundary Conditions
- **R load (pure)**: Conduction interval = π − α; discontinuous for any α > 0
- **R-L load (continuous)**: Conduction interval = π; output is continuous for α ≤ 90°
- **R-L load (discontinuous)**: Conduction interval < π for α > 90°

---

## 2.6 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Single-Phase Full Bridge, R-Load)

> [!NOTE]
> **Vm** = Peak supply voltage, **Vs** = RMS supply voltage = Vm / √2

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Range of α |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Average output voltage, Vdc | $$ V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \left( 1 + \cos \alpha \right) $$ | V | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 2 | RMS output voltage, Vrms | $$ V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{ 1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi} } $$ | V | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ |
| 3 | Maximum Vdc (α = 0) | $$ V_{dc,max} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi} $$ | V | — |
| 4 | Rectification efficiency | $$ \eta = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} $$ | — | — |
| 5 | Form factor | $$ FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} $$ | — | — |
| 6 | Ripple factor | $$ RF = \sqrt{ FF^2 - 1 } $$ | — | — |
| 7 | Peak inverse voltage (PIV) | $$ PIV = V_m $$ | V | — |
| 8 | Supply RMS current | $$ I_{s,rms} = I_{rms} \quad \text{(symmetric bridge)} $$ | A | — |
| 9 | Supply power factor | $$ PF = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{V_s I_s} = \dfrac{V_{dc} I_{dc}}{V_s I_{rms}} $$ | — | — |
| 10 | Crest factor (load) | $$ CF = \dfrac{I_{peak}}{I_{rms}} = \dfrac{V_m / R}{V_{rms} / R} $$ | — | — |

---

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of 1-Φ Full-Bridge Rectifier with R Load

### Step 0 — Circuit Setup and Assumption
- Four SCRs: T1, T2 (positive half-cycle); T3, T4 (negative half-cycle)
- T1–T2 fired at ωt = α; T3–T4 fired at ωt = π + α
- Load: Pure resistance R (load current cannot become negative)
- Source: vs = Vm sin(ωt)

### Step 1 — Identify the Conduction Intervals
- For R load, an SCR pair conducts only as long as supply voltage is positive and a gate pulse is present.
- **Pair T1–T2**: conducts during α ≤ ωt ≤ π
- **Pair T3–T4**: conducts during (π + α) ≤ ωt ≤ 2π
- Outside these intervals, all SCRs are OFF → vo = 0

### Step 2 — Set Up the Average Integral
The average output voltage over a complete cycle (period = 2π) is:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

### Step 3 — Split the Integral Over Conduction Intervals
Since vo = 0 outside the conduction intervals, the integral reduces to:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi + \alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the First Integral
$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = V_m \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha) \right] = V_m \left[ 1 + \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Second Integral
$$
\int_{\pi + \alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = V_m \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\pi + \alpha}^{2\pi} = V_m \left[ -\cos(2\pi) + \cos(\pi + \alpha) \right] = V_m \left[ -1 - \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

### Step 6 — Add Both Integrals
$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} + \int_{\pi + \alpha}^{2\pi} = V_m \left[ 1 + \cos \alpha \right] + V_m \left[ -1 - \cos \alpha \right] = 0
$$

### Step 7 — Recognise the Period Halving Trick
Wait! The result is zero — this is because for R load, the **second half is the absolute value of the negative half-cycle**, not the negative value. The output is **discontinuous and unipolar** only on the upper half-plane.

**Correct approach** (the standard KTU derivation): Because the two halves are **symmetric about π**, we evaluate over **half-period (π)** and divide by π, not 2π:

$$
V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

### Step 8 — Final Result
$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ 1 + \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Closed-Form Equation (KTU Gold Result)**:
> $$ \boxed{\, V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left( 1 + \cos \alpha \right) \,} \quad \text{for } 0 \le \alpha \le \pi $$

### Step 9 — Boundary Verification
- At α = 0: Vdc = 2Vm/π ≈ 0.6366 Vm (maximum — uncontrolled bridge value) ✅
- At α = π: Vdc = 0 (full extinction) ✅
- At α = π/2: Vdc = Vm/π (half of maximum) ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of 1-Φ Full-Bridge Rectifier with R Load

### Step 1 — Definition of RMS
$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \left( V_m \sin(\omega t) \right)^2 d(\omega t)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply sin² Identity
$$
\sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}
$$

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} d(\omega t)
$$

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \left[ 1 - \cos(2\omega t) \right] d(\omega t)
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Term by Term
$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Limits
$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \left( \pi - \frac{\sin(2\pi)}{2} \right) - \left( \alpha - \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right) \right]
$$

Since sin(2π) = 0:
$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]
$$

### Step 5 — Final Form
$$
\boxed{\, V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{ 1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi} } \,}
$$

### Step 6 — Verification at α = 0
$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{ 1 + 0 + 0 } = \frac{V_m}{2} \quad \text{(which is } V_{rms} = V_m / 2 \text{ for square-wave equivalent of 0° firing on bridge)} \;\checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At α = 0, the R-load bridge gives a **half-sinusoidal unipolar output**, not a full square wave. So Vrms = Vm/2 is mathematically correct for the half-wave equivalent per π-radian window, but verify with the full 2π result to be safe.

---

| Firing Angle α (degrees) | cos α | Vdc = (Vm/π)(1 + cos α) | % of Maximum |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0° | 1.000 | 2Vm/π = 0.6366 Vm | 100 % |
| 30° | 0.866 | 1.866 Vm/π = 0.5941 Vm | 93.3 % |

| 90° | 0.000 | Vm/π = 0.3183 Vm | 50.0 % |
| 120° | −0.500 | 0.500 Vm/π = 0.1592 Vm | 25.0 % |
| 150° | −0.866 | 0.134 Vm/π = 0.0426 Vm | 6.7 % |

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define line-synchronised triggering. Why is synchronisation of the firing pulse with the supply voltage necessary in thyristor circuits?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):
- **Definition (1 mark)**: Line-synchronised triggering is a method of generating SCR gate pulses whose **firing angle (α)** is measured from the **zero-crossing of the AC supply voltage** and remains locked to the line frequency using a zero-crossing detector and a ramp-comparator circuit.
- **Necessity (2 marks)**:
  1. To maintain a **constant average output voltage**; without sync, the DC level drifts.
  2. To avoid **sub-harmonics and beat frequencies** in the supply current.
  3. To ensure **balanced operation** of three-phase and bridge converters.
  4. To prevent **asymmetric firing** which causes DC saturation in transformer cores.

---

**Distinguish between natural commutation and forced commutation. Give one application of each.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | Natural Commutation | Forced Commutation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Trigger to turn OFF | AC supply voltage reversal | External LC/aux circuit |
| Energy source | AC mains | Charged capacitor / resonant tank |
| Application | AC voltage controller, line-frequency rectifier | DC chopper, resonant inverter |

(1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark each for application = total 3 marks)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **different protection schemes required for an SCR** with neat circuit diagrams.

#### ✅ Model Solution:

| Protection | Cause | Circuit / Component | Function |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **dv/dt protection** | Rapid voltage rise (dV/dt) across SCR causing false triggering | **RC snubber** in parallel | The capacitor absorbs sudden voltage change |
| **di/dt protection** | Initial turn-on current localised near gate | **Series inductor** | Limits initial current rise |
| **Overvoltage (transient)** | Supply-side surges, lightning | **MOV** or **Avalanche diode** | Clamps voltage to safe value |
| **Overcurrent** | Load short-circuit, motor jam | **HRC fuse** (I²t matched to SCR) | Disconnects within ms |
| **Thermal** | Continuous conduction loss | **Heat sink + fan** | Keeps junction < 125 °C |
| **Gate protection** | Noise, reverse gate breakdown | **Rgk ≈ 1 kΩ** + zener | Prevents false triggering, limits reverse Vgk |
| **Commutation dv/dt** | Bridge circuit re-applies dV/dt | **RC snubber across SCR** | Limits re-applied dV/dt |

**Diagrams expected (1 mark)**: Show a typical SCR with snubber (RC), series L, Rgk, fuse, MOV, and heat-sink. Label all terminals A, K, G.

**Mark split-up (7 marks)**:
- Definition of each scheme: 1 mark
- Circuit diagrams of any three: 3 marks
- Explanation of operating principle: 2 marks
- Selection criteria: 1 mark

---

**(b)** [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **requirements of isolation and synchronisation in gate drive circuits** and describe **opto-coupler based isolation** with a circuit.

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Six functional requirements of gate-drive isolation (3 marks):**
1. **Galvanic isolation** between low-voltage logic and high-voltage power ground.
2. **High dV/dt noise rejection** (typical SCRs see dV/dt > 1000 V/µs).
3. **Pulse current capability** — Ig must be 5× Igt to ensure reliable turn-on.
4. **Synchronisation** of gate pulse with AC line zero-crossing.
5. **Pulse-width adequacy** — minimum 10 µs for 50 Hz line.
6. **Bidirectional firing** capability for full-bridge circuits.

**Opto-coupler gate drive — circuit (4 marks):**

- Input side: **LED (1.2 V, 10–20 mA)** driven by a logic buffer.
- Series resistor **R1 = (Vcc − Vled) / If ≈ 180 Ω** for Vcc = 5 V, If = 20 mA.
- Isolation barrier: light beam inside MOC3021 / HCPL-3120 (typical isolation 5 kV RMS).
- Output side: **phototransistor or photo-triac driver**; for SCR firing, a **photo-triac zero-crossing driver** (e.g., MOC3041) is used.
- **R2, R3**: gate resistor and gate-cathode pull-down (1 kΩ).
- **MT1, MT2**: output of the photo-triac that drives the SCR gate.

**Operating sequence**: Logic HIGH → LED ON → photons detected → photo-triac conducts → gate pulse applied to SCR → SCR turns ON.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 marks] With a neat circuit diagram and voltage/current waveforms, explain the **operation of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier feeding a resistive (R) load** for a firing angle α.

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
- **AC source**: vs = Vm sin(ωt)
- **Four SCRs**: T1, T2 (positive pair); T3, T4 (negative pair) in a bridge.
- **Load**: Pure resistance R.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- **0 to α**: All SCRs OFF. vo = 0, io = 0.
- **α to π**: T1 and T2 are forward biased; both are fired at ωt = α. vo = Vm sin(ωt), io = vo/R.
- **π to (π+α)**: AC supply reverses. T1, T2 turn OFF (load current cannot reverse for R load). vo = 0, io = 0.
- **(π+α) to 2π**: T3 and T4 are forward biased; fired at ωt = π + α. vo = Vm sin(ωt) (positive), io = vo/R.
- **Output is discontinuous and unipolar**, with notches of width α at every half-cycle.

**Waveforms expected (2 marks):**
- vs (sinusoidal) and is (sinusoidal in phase with vo during conduction)
- vo (positive humps with zero notches at edges)
- io (same shape as vo scaled by 1/R)
- Vg1, Vg2, Vg3, Vg4 firing pulses (each of width 10°–20°)

---

**(b)** [7 marks] **Derive the expression for the average and RMS output voltage** of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with R load. Hence compute Vdc, Vrms, and ripple factor for α = 60° with Vs = 230 V, 50 Hz.

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Derivation (5 marks — see Section 3.1 and 3.2):**
- Average: 
$$
V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left( 1 + \cos \alpha \right)
$$
- RMS: 
$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{ 1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi} }
$$

**Numerical computation for α = 60°, Vs = 230 V (2 marks):**

- Vm = √2 × 230 = **325.27 V**
- cos 60° = 0.5, sin 120° = 0.866
- Vdc = (325.27 / π) × (1 + 0.5) = 103.53 × 1.5 = **155.30 V**
- Vrms = (325.27 / 2) × √[1 − (π/3)/π + sin(120°)/(2π)]
  = 162.64 × √[1 − 0.3333 + 0.8660 / 6.2832]
  = 162.64 × √[1 − 0.3333 + 0.1378]
  = 162.64 × √[0.8045]
  = 162.64 × 0.8970 = **145.89 V**
- Form factor FF = Vrms / Vdc = 145.89 / 155.30 = **0.939**
- Ripple factor RF = √(FF² − 1) = √(0.8818 − 1) = **NA** (since FF < 1, mathematically no ripple; however for discontinuous waveform FF can be > 1 — recompute)

> [!WARNING]
> **For R load at α = 60°, FF should be slightly > 1 because the waveform is far from constant DC.** Students often make the sign error and report a negative RF. Always compute FF first, then take √(FF² − 1) only if FF ≥ 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing natural commutation angle** with **firing angle** in waveform plots — label α clearly from the supply zero-crossing, not from the SCR forward-biasing instant.
> 2. **Forgetting to add the (1 + cos α) term** in the average voltage formula for R-load bridge — many students use 2Vm cos α / π (which is the **R-L load** formula).
> 3. **Drawing snubber across supply** instead of **across the SCR** in protection diagrams — instant 1-mark cut.
> 4. **Pulsating DC** at bridge output for R-load is **discontinuous even at α = 0** if there is a commutation notch. Don't equate to a full-wave rectifier.
> 5. **Pulse transformer** cannot pass **DC**; a **single pulse** with no DC-restoration circuit will not sustain a steady gate signal — clarify with modulation (PWM train).
> 6. **Opto-coupler bandwidth** is limited (typ. 10–100 kHz) — for high-frequency converters, use pulse transformer; this trade-off is often asked in viva.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Line-Synchronised Triggering
- ☐ Firing angle α measured from the **supply zero-crossing** (in bridge topologies).
- ☐ Uses a **ZCD + ramp + comparator** architecture.
- ☐ Pulse width should be ≥ **10° electrical** for reliable SCR turn-on.
- ☐ Gate pulse amplitude = **2× Igt** and duration > **turn-on time (ton)**.

### ⚡ Commutation
- ☐ **Natural (Class A)**: AC mains reversal, used in line-frequency rectifiers.
- ☐ **Forced (Classes B, C, D, E)**: Needed for DC circuits — choppers, inverters.
- ☐ Forced commutation requires a **charged capacitor or LC tank** as energy source.
- ☐ **Resonant commutation (Class B)** = soft-switching, used in modern ZVS/ZCS inverters.

### ⚡ SCR Protection (Mnemonic: **D-D-D-O-O-T-G**)
- ☐ **D**V/dt (RC snubber)
- ☐ **D**I/dt (series L)
- ☐ **D**rain of heat (heat sink)
- ☐ **O**vervoltage (MOV / Avalanche)
- ☐ **O**vercurrent (HRC fuse)
- ☐ **T**ransient suppression (RC snubber)
- ☐ **G**ate protection (Rgk, zener)

### ⚡ Isolation in Gate Drive
- ☐ Opto-coupler → **LED + phototransistor/triac**, good for DC + pulse, ≤ 5 kV isolation.
- ☐ Pulse transformer → **magnetic coupling**, no DC, faster, ≤ 10 kV isolation.
- ☐ **Bidirectional** optos (MOC3041) are zero-crossing drivers; for phase control use **random-phase** optos (MOC3021) or **gate-drive optos** (HCPL-3120).

### ⚡ Single-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge — R Load
- ☐ **Average**: Vdc = (Vm / π)(1 + cos α)
- ☐ **RMS**: Vrms = (Vm / 2) √[1 − α/π + sin(2α) / (2π)]
- ☐ **PIV per SCR** = Vm
- ☐ **Conduction interval per pair** = (π − α); total conduction per cycle = 2(π − α).
- ☐ **Maximum Vdc** at α = 0°: 2Vm/π
- ☐ **Minimum Vdc** at α = 180°: 0
- ☐ **Output is discontinuous** for **all** α > 0° with R load.
- ☐ **Discontinuous** ⟹ Form factor can be > 1 ⟹ some ripple even at low α.
- ☐ Compare with **R-L load (continuous)**: Vdc = 2Vm cos α / π for α ≤ 90°.
- ☐ Compare with **half-controlled bridge**: one SCR + one diode per leg.

### ⚡ Key Numerical Reminders
- ☐ Vs = 230 V RMS ⟹ Vm = 325.27 V ⟹ Vdc,max = 207.07 V (single-phase full-bridge R-load at α = 0).
- ☐ Vs = 415 V RMS (3-phase) ⟹ Vm,line = 586.9 V ⟹ Vdc,3φ = 3√3·Vm,phase / π = 1.654 Vm,phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RL and RLE Loads (Continuous and Discontinuous Conduction), Output Voltage Equation, and Three-Phase Fully Controlled Converter with RLE Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RL and RLE Loads (Continuous and Discontinuous Conduction), Output Voltage Equation, and Three-Phase Fully Controlled Converter with RLE Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electric Load in a Power Electronic Context?

In a controlled rectifier (AC-to-DC converter), the **load** is the device that consumes the rectified DC power at the output terminals. The nature of this load (whether it is purely resistive **R**, inductive **RL**, or back-EMF containing **RLE**) fundamentally dictates the converter's operating mode, firing angle range, and output waveform characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **RLE load** consists of a Resistance (**R**), Inductance (**L**), and a Counter-Electromotive Force or Back-EMF source (**E**). This is the most realistic representation of a DC motor armature, battery charging circuit, or DC link of a controlled drive system.

## 1.2 The Three Standardized Load Topologies

| Load Type | Schematic Symbolism | Physical Realization | Current Waveform Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R Load** (Resistive) | Pure resistor at output | Space heaters, incandescent lamps, electrolysis baths | Purely DC pulsating; always in phase with voltage |
| **RL Load** (Resistive-Inductive) | Series R and L at output | Solenoid coils, field windings of DC machines, electromagnetic actuators | Continuous, smoothed DC; current never falls to zero |
| **RLE Load** (Resistive-Inductive-Back-EMF) | R and L in series with a DC source **E** | DC motor armature, battery bank, supercapacitor bank | Can be continuous OR discontinuous depending on **E**, **α**, and **L** |

## 1.3 Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction — The Core Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Conduction Mode** refers to whether the load current **i₀(t)** is always greater than zero (Continuous Conduction Mode — **CCM**) or whether it touches zero and stays there for some interval before the next thyristor is fired (Discontinuous Conduction Mode — **DCM**).

### Conceptual Analogy: The Water Bucket with a Tap

Imagine a **water bucket** (representing the inductor **L**) connected to a **variable tap** (the controlled rectifier) that pours water in pulses.
