The bucket has a small **leak** at the bottom (representing **R**), and there is a **sponge barrier** inside (representing **E**, which must be overcome before water flows).

- **Continuous Conduction (Bucket always has water):** The tap pours water fast enough that the bucket is *never empty*. The water level (current) ripples slightly but never reaches zero.
- **Discontinuous Conduction (Bucket empties completely):** The tap is opened at large angles (late firing), so less water is poured. Combined with the leak and the sponge, the bucket **drains completely** before the next pour. There is a **dead time** when no water flows.

## 1.4 The Three-Phase Fully Controlled Converter — A First Look

A **three-phase fully controlled bridge converter** uses **six thyristors** (T₁ to T₆) arranged in a 3-phase, 6-pulse bridge topology. The thyristors are fired in sequence every **60°** (i.e., one thyristor pair conducts at any given instant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:**
> The phase sequence of firing is **T₁ → T₂ → T₃ → T₄ → T₅ → T₆** in the order of the supply phases **R → Y → B → R' → Y' → B'** (or equivalently, the negative half-cycles). The conduction period per thyristor in CCM is exactly **120°** (or 2π/3 radians).

The load terminals of this converter (DC side) see a **pulsating DC voltage** whose average value depends on the firing angle **α** measured from the natural commutation point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Three-Phase Fully Controlled Converter with RLE Load

### Step-by-Step Operational Principle

- **Step 1 — Supply & Commutation:** The three-phase 415V (line-to-line RMS) AC supply feeds the bridge. Each thyristor can only conduct when it is **forward-biased AND a gate pulse is applied**.
- **Step 2 — Firing Sequence Initiation:** At ωt = α, T₁ is fired. Now current flows from phase R (positive terminal) through T₁, the RLE load, and returns through T₆ (or whichever thyristor in the negative group is conducting) to phase B.
- **Step 3 — Inductor Energy Storage:** As the instantaneous output voltage **v₀(ωt) > E**, current builds up in L. The inductor **stores magnetic energy** = ½ L i².
- **Step 4 — Inductor Energy Release:** When **v₀(ωt) < E**, the inductor **releases its stored energy** to maintain current flow. This is the **freewheeling action through L**, which is the root cause of continuous conduction.
- **Step 5 — Mode Determination:**
  - If at the end of the 60° conduction interval, the current **i₀ > 0**, the next thyristor pair takes over seamlessly → **CCM**.
  - If **i₀ = 0** before the next firing instant, the bridge stops delivering power. Current stays at zero until the next firing pulse → **DCM**.

## 2.2 Boundary Condition for Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction

The transition between CCM and DCM is governed by the **critical firing angle α_critical** or, equivalently, by the **no-load output voltage E₀**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Boundary Condition Theorem (KTU High-Yield):**
> - In a **single-phase fully controlled converter with RLE load**, continuous conduction begins when the average output voltage **V₀(avg) ≥ E**. Below this, DCM occurs.
> - In a **three-phase fully controlled converter with RLE load**, the boundary condition is:
>
> $$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{mL} \cos\alpha \geq E$$
>
> where **V_mL** is the **peak line-to-line voltage** of the AC supply.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Notation:**
> - **V_mL** = √2 × V_LL = peak line-to-line voltage
> - **V_LL** = RMS line-to-line voltage (e.g., 415 V)
> - **V_ph** = RMS phase voltage (e.g., 240 V)
> - **α** = firing angle (delay from natural commutation point)
> - **β** = extinction angle (angle at which load current becomes zero in DCM)
> - **γ** = conduction extinction angle in RLE load (alternative notation)
> - **E** = back-EMF of the load
> - **μ** = commutation (overlap) angle

| # | Parameter | Formula | Condition / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak Line Voltage | $V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{LL}$ | Volts (V) | $\sqrt{6} \cdot V_{ph}$ also valid |
| 2 | Avg. Output Voltage (1-ϕ Full Converter, RLE, CCM) | $V_{0,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$ | Volts (V) | R or RL load same; V_m is peak phase voltage |
| 3 | Avg. Output Voltage (1-ϕ Full Converter, RLE, DCM) | $V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ \cos\alpha - \cos\beta \right]$ | Volts (V) | β is extinction angle |
| 4 | **Avg. Output Voltage (3-ϕ Full Converter, RLE, CCM)** | $V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cos\alpha$ | Volts (V) | **Most critical KTU formula** |
| 5 | **Avg. Output Voltage (3-ϕ Full Converter, RLE, DCM)** | $V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{\cos\alpha + \cos\beta}{2} \right]$ | Volts (V) | β is extinction angle at which i₀ = 0 |
| 6 | Maximum Average Output Voltage (α = 0) | $V_{0,max} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} = \frac{3\sqrt{6} V_{ph}}{2\pi} \approx 1.17 V_{LL}$ | Volts (V) | At α = 0°, full rectification |
| 7 | RMS Output Voltage (3-ϕ, CCM) | $V_{0,rms} = V_{mL} \sqrt{\frac{3}{2\pi}\left(\frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right)}$ | Volts (V) | Constant for α; useful for heating loads |
| 8 | Avg. Load Current (RLE) | $I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg} - E}{R}$ | Amperes (A) | KVL applied to DC side |
| 9 | Condition for DCM | $V_{0,avg} < E$ | — | i.e., $\cos\alpha < \frac{\pi E}{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}$ |
| 10 | Boundary Firing Angle | $\alpha_{crit} = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\pi E}{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}\right)$ | Degrees / Radians | For α > α_crit, DCM occurs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Tip:**
> In problem statements, "**RLE load with continuous conduction**" always implies the use of Formula #4 above. If the problem specifies "**discontinuous conduction**" or provides an extinction angle, you must switch to Formula #5.

## 2.4 Why the Inductor Forces Continuous Conduction

The inductor **L** opposes any *change* in current. In a single-phase full converter with RLE load, the instantaneous current is governed by:

$$L \frac{di_0}{dt} + R i_0 + E = v_0(\omega t)$$

The **L di/dt** term means:
- When v₀ drops below E (the natural zero of the rectified sine), the inductor *reverses* its voltage polarity (acting as a source) to keep current flowing.
- Only when L has depleted its energy (i.e., i₀ = 0) does conduction stop. In CCM, L is sized so that depletion **never** happens within one cycle.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The three-phase fully controlled converter with RLE load is the **workhorse of industrial DC drives**:

- **Steel rolling mills:** The DC motor's back-EMF E depends on speed. The converter provides variable DC voltage for precise speed control.
- **DC Traction Systems:** Locomotives use three-phase fully controlled converters feeding DC series motors. The RLE model is perfect.
- **Battery Chargers / SMPS Front-End:** The DC link capacitor is modeled as an E source.
- **Electrochemical Plants:** Aluminum smelters use very high-current, low-voltage DC, precisely controlled by such converters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output Voltage Equation for a Three-Phase Fully Controlled Converter with RLE Load (Continuous Conduction Mode)

### Step 1 — Define the Three-Phase Source Voltages

Let the three-phase Y-connected source have phase voltages:

$$v_{RN}(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$v_{YN}(\omega t) = V_m \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right)$$
$$v_{BN}(\omega t) = V_m \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right)$$

where **V_m** is the peak phase voltage. The corresponding **line-to-line voltages** are:

$$v_{RY}(\omega t) = v_{RN} - v_{YN} = \sqrt{3} V_m \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) = V_{mL} \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

$$v_{YB}(\omega t) = v_{YN} - v_{BN} = V_{mL} \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$

$$v_{BR}(\omega t) = v_{BN} - v_{RN} = V_{mL} \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{7\pi}{6}\right)$$

> **Logic Note:** The line-to-line voltage is **√3 times** the phase voltage and is **leading by 30° (π/6)**. This 30° phase shift is critical for identifying the natural commutation points.

### Step 2 — Identify the Conduction Intervals in CCM

In continuous conduction mode, each thyristor conducts for **120° (2π/3 radians)**. The thyristor firing sequence and corresponding output voltage segments are:

| Conduction Interval (ωt) | Conducting Thyristors | Output Voltage v₀(ωt) |
|---|---|---|
| π/6 + α → 5π/6 + α | T₁, T₆ | v_RY |
| 5π/6 + α → 9π/6 + α | T₁, T₂ | v_RB |
| 9π/6 + α → 13π/6 + α | T₃, T₂ | v_YB |
| 13π/6 + α → 17π/6 + α | T₃, T₄ | v_YR |
| 17π/6 + α → 21π/6 + α | T₅, T₄ | v_BR |
| 21π/6 + α → 25π/6 + α | T₅, T₆ | v_BY |

> **Logic Note:** All six intervals are identical in shape — each is a 60° slice of a sine wave with peak V_mL. The integral over any one segment is the same.

### Step 3 — Set Up the Average Output Voltage Integral

The average output voltage over one complete cycle (T = 2π/ω) is:

$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Since the output is periodic with 60° periodicity (six-fold symmetry), we can simplify by integrating over **one 60° segment** and multiplying by **6 / 2π**:

$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{6}{2\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} v_{RY}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute v_RY and Expand the Limits

The line voltage is:

$$v_{RY}(\omega t) = V_{mL} \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{6}{2\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} V_{mL} \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) d(\omega t)$$

### Step 5 — Perform the Integration

Let **u = ωt + π/6**, then du = d(ωt).

- Lower limit: u_lower = (π/6 + α) + π/6 = π/3 + α
- Upper limit: u_upper = (π/2 + α) + π/6 = 2π/3 + α

The integral becomes:

$$\int_{\pi/3 + \alpha}^{2\pi/3 + \alpha} \sin(u) \, du = \left[ -\cos(u) \right]_{\pi/3 + \alpha}^{2\pi/3 + \alpha}$$

$$= -\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} + \alpha\right) + \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3} + \alpha\right)$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Cosine Identity

Using the identity **cos(A) − cos(B) = −2 sin((A+B)/2) sin((A−B)/2)**:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{3} + \alpha, \quad B = \frac{2\pi}{3} + \alpha$$

$$A + B = \pi + 2\alpha, \quad A - B = -\frac{\pi}{3}$$

$$\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3} + \alpha\right) - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} + \alpha\right) = -2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{2}\right) \sin\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

$$= -2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \alpha\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \alpha\right) = \cos\alpha$$

> **Logic Note:** The elegant collapse of the integral to **cos α** is the reason the three-phase fully controlled converter has such a clean output equation.

### Step 7 — Final Assembly

Substituting back into V₀,avg:

$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot \cos\alpha$$

$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cos\alpha$$

$$\boxed{V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cos\alpha \approx 1.17 V_{LL} \cos\alpha}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Verification:** At α = 0°, V₀,avg = 1.17 × 415 V ≈ 485 V (a known result). At α = 90°, V₀,avg = 0 V. At α = 180°, V₀,avg ≈ −485 V (full inversion). This is the canonical three-phase full converter equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Output Voltage for RLE Load in Discontinuous Conduction Mode

### Step 1 — Define the Boundaries of Discontinuous Conduction

In DCM, the load current i₀ reaches zero at an angle **β** (where β < π/3 + α in normalized terms, or in general, before the next thyristor pair would naturally take over). The current remains zero from β to the next firing instant.

### Step 2 — Set Up the Modified Integral

The integration limits change. Output is non-zero only from α to β:

$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\beta} V_{mL} \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) d(\omega t)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral

Following the same substitution u = ωt + π/6, with limits from π/3 + α to β + π/6:

$$\int_{\pi/3 + \alpha}^{\beta + \pi/6} \sin(u) \, du = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3} + \alpha\right) - \cos\left(\beta + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

Using the identity **cos(A) − cos(B) = −2 sin((A+B)/2) sin((A−B)/2)**:

$$= 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi/3 + \alpha + \beta + \pi/6}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{\beta + \pi/6 - \pi/3 - \alpha}{2}\right)$$

$$= 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\alpha + \beta}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{\beta - \alpha}{2} - \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Final DCM Output Voltage Equation

After trigonometric simplification and standard textbook result:

$$\boxed{V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cdot \left[ \frac{\cos\alpha + \cos\beta}{2} \right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** Some textbooks use V₀ = (3√3 V_mL / 2π) × [cos α − cos(β + π/6)] form. **Always check** the sign convention in the reference figure when solving numerical KTU problems.

### Step 5 — Condition Linking α, β, and E

At the boundary of conduction (i₀ = 0), the energy stored in L is zero. By integrating the loop equation from α to β:

$$E(\beta - \alpha) + R \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} i_0(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} v_0(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

This is a **transcendental equation** in β that must be solved iteratively for given E, R, L, and α. KTU typically tests only the **final V₀ formula** with β given.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A three-phase fully controlled bridge converter is supplied from a 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase source. The load is RLE with R = 1 Ω, L = 50 mH, and E = 200 V. The firing angle is α = 30°. Determine: (a) Average output voltage (b) Whether conduction is continuous.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Peak line voltage
$$V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \times 415 = 586.9 \, V$$

**Step 2:** Average output voltage assuming CCM
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 586.9}{2\pi} \times \cos 30° = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 586.9}{2\pi} \times 0.866$$
$$V_{0,avg} = 485.1 \times 0.866 \approx 420.1 \, V$$

**Step 3:** Load current
$$I_{0,avg} = \frac{420.1 - 200}{1} = 220.1 \, A$$

**Step 4:** Since V₀,avg = 420.1 V > E = 200 V, the condition $V_{0,avg} \geq E$ is satisfied → **Continuous Conduction Mode** is valid.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Converter Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    SRC["THREE PHASE AC SUPPLY 415V 50HZ"]:::supply
    R["PHASE R"]:::phase
    Y["PHASE Y"]:::phase
    B["PHASE B"]:::phase
    T1["THYRISTOR T1"]:::thy
    T3["THYRISTOR T3"]:::thy
    T5["THYRISTOR T5"]:::thy
    T4["THYRISTOR T4"]:::thy
    T6["THYRISTOR T6"]:::thy
    T2["THYRISTOR T2"]:::thy
    POS["POSITIVE DC BUS"]:::bus
    NEG["NEGATIVE DC BUS"]:::bus
    LOAD["RLE LOAD TERMINALS"]:::load
    RL["R AND L IN SERIES WITH E"]:::loadbox
    GATE["GATING PULSE GENERATOR"]:::gate

    SRC --> R
    SRC --> Y
    SRC --> B
    R --> T1
    Y --> T3
    B --> T5
    T1 --> POS
    T3 --> POS
    T5 --> POS
    T4 --> NEG
    T6 --> NEG
    T2 --> NEG
    R --> T4
    Y --> T6
    B --> T2
    POS --> LOAD
    LOAD --> NEG
    POS --> RL
    RL --> NEG
    GATE --> T1
    GATE --> T2
    GATE --> T3
    GATE --> T4
    GATE --> T5
    GATE --> T6

    classDef supply fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef phase fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef thy fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loadbox fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Diagram Description:** The 3-phase AC supply (R, Y, B) feeds the upper thyristor group (T₁, T₃, T₅) and the lower thyristor group (T₄, T₆, T₂). The DC output is taken between the positive and negative DC buses, which are connected to the RLE load. The gating pulse generator provides sequential firing pulses at 60° intervals.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Conduction Mode Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Three Phase Full Converter with RLE Load"]:::startnode
    B["Fire thyristor T1 at omega t equals alpha"]:::process
    C{"V0 greater than E"}:::decision
    D["Current i0 builds up in L"]:::process
    E["Inductor stores energy 0.5 L i squared"]:::process
    F{"V0 less than E"}:::decision
    G["Inductor releases energy through L"]:::process
    H["Current i0 decreases"]:::process
    I{"i0 reaches zero before next firing?"}:::decision
    J["CONTINUOUS CONDUCTION MODE CCM"]:::result
    K["DISCONTINUOUS CONDUCTION MODE DCM"]:::result
    L["Fire T2 at omega t equals alpha plus 60 degrees"]:::process
    M["Cycle repeats every 60 degrees"]:::result

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- YES --> D
    C -- NO --> G
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -- YES --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I -- NO --> J
    I -- YES --> K
    J --> L
    L --> M
    K --> L

    classDef startnode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Output Voltage Waveform in CCM (α = 30°)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W1["Time Axis: omega t in degrees"]:::axis
    W2["0 to 360 degree cycle with 6 identical segments"]:::seg
    W3["Each segment: 60 degrees of positive sine"]:::seg
    W4["Peak amplitude equals VmL"]:::seg
    W5["Firing delayed by alpha equals 30 degrees"]:::seg
    W6["Average value: 1.17 VLL cos alpha"]:::seg

    W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> W4 --> W5 --> W6

    classDef axis fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef seg fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Waveform Description:** The output voltage **v₀(ωt)** consists of 6 positive humps per cycle, each spanning 60°. The peak of each hump is **V_mL = √2 × 415 = 586.9 V**. The waveform is **shifted to the right by α = 30°** compared to an uncontrolled diode bridge. The average (DC) value is the constant horizontal line at **V₀,avg = 485.1 × cos α**.

## 4.4 Comparison: Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction

```mermaid
graph TB
    LHS["CONTINUOUS CONDUCTION MODE CCM"]:::ccm
    C1["Current i0 always greater than 0"]:::point
    C2["Conduction period = 60 degrees per thyristor pair"]:::point
    C3["V0 = 1.17 VLL cos alpha"]:::point
    C4["6 pulses per cycle at output"]:::point
    C5["Smoother DC current, lower ripple"]:::point
    C6["Boundary: V0 greater than or equal to E"]:::point

    RHS["DISCONTINUOUS CONDUCTION MODE DCM"]:::dcm
    D1["Current i0 reaches zero before next firing"]:::point
    D2["Conduction period less than 60 degrees"]:::point
    D3["V0 = 1.17 VLL times cos alpha plus cos beta divided by 2"]:::point
    D4["Current has dead zones between pulses"]:::point
    D5["Higher ripple, discontinuous torque in motor"]:::point
    D6["Condition: V0 less than E"]:::point

    LHS --- RHS

    classDef ccm fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dcm fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef point fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Distinguish between continuous and discontinuous conduction modes in a fully controlled converter with RLE load. State the condition that determines which mode of conduction occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM):** The load current i₀ is always positive (never falls to zero) throughout the operating cycle. This happens because the inductor L stores sufficient energy to maintain current flow even when the instantaneous output voltage v₀ drops below the back-EMF E.
- **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM):** The load current i₀ falls to zero and remains at zero for some interval before the next thyristor is fired.
- **Condition:** DCM occurs when the **average output voltage V₀,avg < E** (back-EMF). Equivalently, for a 3-phase full converter, DCM occurs when **α > α_critical**, where:
$$\alpha_{crit} = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\pi E}{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}\right)$$

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "firing angle" in a three-phase fully controlled converter. Why is the natural commutation point located 30° after the phase voltage zero-crossing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Firing Angle (α):** The angle (measured from the natural commutation point) by which the gate pulse to a thyristor is delayed after it becomes forward-biased.
- **Reason for 30° shift:** In a three-phase bridge, the **line-to-line voltage** (which appears at the output) leads the **phase voltage** by 30°. Hence the natural commutation point (where two line voltages intersect) occurs **30° after the corresponding phase voltage zero-crossing**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**A (a)** Derive the expression for the average output voltage of a three-phase fully controlled bridge converter feeding an RLE load operating in **continuous conduction mode**. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Thyristors are ideal (zero on-state voltage drop, infinite off-state impedance).
2. The AC source is a balanced, distortion-free three-phase sinusoidal supply.
3. Load inductance L is large enough to maintain continuous current.
4. No source inductance (no commutation overlap, μ = 0).

**Derivation:**

Let the three-phase source have phase voltages:
$$v_R = V_m \sin(\omega t), \quad v_Y = V_m \sin(\omega t - 2\pi/3), \quad v_B = V_m \sin(\omega t - 4\pi/3)$$

The corresponding line voltages are:
$$v_{RY} = V_{mL} \sin(\omega t + \pi/6)$$

In CCM, the thyristor T₁ is fired at ωt = (π/6 + α), and conduction of the pair (T₁, T₆) occurs from (π/6 + α) to (5π/6 + α). During this interval, the output voltage equals v_RY.

By symmetry (6-fold repetition), the average output voltage over one full cycle is:
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{6}{2\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{5\pi/6 + \alpha} V_{mL} \sin(\omega t + \pi/6) \, d(\omega t)$$

Wait, since each segment is only 60° wide, the limits reduce to (π/6 + α) to (π/2 + α):
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} \sin(\omega t + \pi/6) \, d(\omega t)$$

Substituting u = ωt + π/6, with limits from (π/3 + α) to (2π/3 + α):
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(u) \right]_{\pi/3 + \alpha}^{2\pi/3 + \alpha} = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ \cos(\pi/3 + \alpha) - \cos(2\pi/3 + \alpha) \right]$$

Using sum-to-product identity:
$$= \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi/3 + \alpha + 2\pi/3 + \alpha}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{2\pi/3 + \alpha - \pi/3 - \alpha}{2}\right)$$

$$= \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \alpha\right) \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cos\alpha \approx 1.17 V_{LL} \cos\alpha}$$

---

**A (b)** A three-phase fully controlled bridge converter operates from a 415 V, 50 Hz, 3-phase AC supply. The load is RLE with R = 5 Ω and E = 150 V. The firing angle is α = 45°. Compute: (i) Average output voltage, (ii) Average load current, (iii) Verify whether the conduction is continuous. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average output voltage:**

Peak line voltage:
$$V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \times 415 = 586.9 \, V$$

Applying the derived formula:
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 586.9}{2\pi} \times \cos 45° = 485.1 \times 0.7071 \approx 343.0 \, V$$

**(ii) Average load current:**

By KVL on DC side: $V_{0,avg} = I_{0,avg} R + E$
$$I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg} - E}{R} = \frac{343.0 - 150}{5} = 38.6 \, A$$

**(iii) Conduction continuity check:**

Since $V_{0,avg} = 343.0 \, V > E = 150 \, V$, the load current never becomes zero (the inductor always has stored energy to maintain flow). Hence **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) is valid**. ✓

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**B (a)** Explain with suitable diagrams and equations the operation of a three-phase fully controlled converter feeding an RLE load in **discontinuous conduction mode**. Derive the expression for average output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation with diagram description:**

In DCM, when the firing angle α is large (typically > 60°) and/or the back-EMF E is large, the load current i₀(ωt) does not persist throughout the entire 60° conduction interval. Instead, the current rises from zero, reaches a peak, falls back to zero at an angle **β** (the **extinction angle**), and remains at zero until the next thyristor pair is fired.

The output voltage waveform **v₀(ωt)** still follows the line voltage sinusoid from α to β, but from β to the next firing instant, the converter output is **decoupled** from the load (the load sees only E through the inductor). The current waveform consists of **discrete pulses** with zero intervals.

**Derivation:**

The integration limits in DCM are from α to β (not full 60°):
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\beta} V_{mL} \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) d(\omega t)$$

Substituting u = ωt + π/6, limits from (π/3 + α) to (β + π/6):
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(u) \right]_{\pi/3 + \alpha}^{\beta + \pi/6}$$

$$= \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3} + \alpha\right) - \cos\left(\beta + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) \right]$$

Using sum-to-product:
$$= \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot 2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi/3 + \alpha + \beta + \pi/6}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{\beta + \pi/6 - \pi/3 - \alpha}{2}\right)$$

$$= \frac{6 V_{mL}}{\pi} \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\alpha + \beta}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{\beta - \alpha}{2} - \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

$$= \frac{6 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cos\left(\frac{\alpha + \beta}{2}\right) \cdot 2 \sin\left(\frac{\beta - \alpha}{2}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\beta - \alpha}{2}\right)$$

After algebraic simplification:
$$\boxed{V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha + \cos\beta}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Check:** This form uses **+cos β** (some textbooks use **−cos(β + π/6)**). Always verify with the given waveform diagram.

---

**B (b)** A 3-phase full converter feeds an RLE load with R = 2 Ω, E = 300 V from a 400 V, 50 Hz supply. The extinction angle is observed to be β = 120° and firing angle α = 60°. Compute: (i) Average output voltage, (ii) Average load current, (iii) RMS value of the fundamental component of supply current. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Average output voltage:**

Peak line voltage:
$$V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \times 400 = 565.7 \, V$$

Using DCM formula:
$$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 565.7}{2\pi} \times \frac{\cos 60° + \cos 120°}{2}$$

$$= 467.6 \times \frac{0.5 + (-0.5)}{2} = 467.6 \times 0 = 0 \, V$$

> **Note:** With α = 60° and β = 120° giving cos α + cos β = 0, the average output is zero, which physically means equal positive and negative volt-second areas.

**(ii) Average load current:**

$$I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg} - E}{R} = \frac{0 - 300}{2} = -150 \, A$$

The negative sign indicates power flow is from **load to source** (inversion mode, characteristic of β > π − α in DCM).

**(iii) RMS supply current fundamental:**

For a three-phase full converter in DCM, the supply current waveform is non-sinusoidal. The fundamental RMS component (using Fourier analysis) is:

$$I_{s1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} I_{0,avg} \quad \text{(approximation for highly inductive load)}$$

$$I_{s1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \times 150 \approx 117.0 \, A$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 / 2π factor:** Students often write $V_0 = V_{mL} \cos\alpha$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct coefficient is $\frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2\pi} \approx 0.827$. This single error costs **3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up V_mL and V_m (phase):** $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m$ (peak line = √3 × peak phase). Students who use $V_m$ in the 3-phase formula get **half the correct answer** — deduct 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing CCM and DCM formulas:** A question stating "RLE load with discontinuous conduction" **MANDATES** use of the $(\cos\alpha + \cos\beta)/2$ formula. Using the CCM formula here is a **4-mark** error.
>
> 4. **Sign convention for α in inversion mode:** When α > 90°, V₀ becomes negative. Students sometimes forget the negative sign and write V₀ = +1.17 V_LL |cos α| — deduct 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Not verifying CCM assumption:** After computing V₀ in CCM, **ALWAYS check** if V₀ ≥ E. If V₀ < E, your assumption is wrong and the problem must be solved in DCM. Skipping this check costs 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to convert β in DCM problems:** The extinction angle β is sometimes given in **degrees** in the problem statement. The formula requires it in **radians** if you are using the standard integration form. Always include a `cos(β)` check before substituting.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Points — KTU Module 2 RL/RLE Loads and 3-Phase Full Converter:**

- ⭐ **Load Types:** R (resistive), RL (R + L), RLE (R + L + back-EMF E). The **RLE model** represents a DC motor or battery.

- ⭐ **Conduction Mode Rule:** CCM when $V_{0,avg} \geq E$; DCM when $V_{0,avg} < E$.

- ⭐ **3-Phase Full Converter (CCM) — Master Equation:** $V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cos\alpha \approx 1.17 V_{LL} \cos\alpha$

- ⭐ **3-Phase Full Converter (DCM) — Master Equation:** $V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha + \cos\beta}{2}$

- ⭐ **Six thyristors** arranged as 3 in upper group (T₁, T₃, T₅) and 3 in lower group (T₄, T₆, T₂). Each conducts for **120°** in CCM, **60° per pair**.

- ⭐ **Firing sequence:** T₁ → T₂ → T₃ → T₄ → T₅ → T₆, separated by **60°**.

- ⭐ **Natural commutation point** is **30° after** the phase voltage zero-crossing (because line voltage leads phase voltage by 30°).

- ⭐ **Maximum V₀** occurs at α = 0°: $V_{0,max} \approx 1.17 \times 415 = 485$ V (for 415 V supply).

- ⭐ **Inversion mode** (regenerative braking of DC motor) occurs at **90° < α < 180°**, giving negative V₀.

- ⭐ **Boundary firing angle formula:** $\alpha_{crit} = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\pi E}{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}}\right)$

- ⭐ **Load current formula (RLE):** $I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg} - E}{R}$

- ⭐ **The role of L:** L **enforces** CCM by storing energy during v₀ > E and releasing it during v₀ < E.

- ⭐ **DCM causes:** Large α, large E, small L, large R — all favor discontinuous operation.

- ⭐ **KTU trick question pattern:** "What happens if E > V₀,avg?" → Answer: The current becomes zero and the converter delivers no power; the motor cannot be controlled in this region; solution is to reduce α or reduce E (decrease load/speed).

- ⭐ **Real-world applications:** DC motor speed control (rolling mills, traction), HVDC converters, battery chargers, electroplating, mine winders, crane drives.

- ⭐ **Always check** in the solution: (a) Units consistency, (b) Whether the assumption "CCM" is self-consistent, (c) Whether α is in degrees or radians, (d) Whether V_LL or V_ph was given.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC-DC Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC-DC Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DC-DC Converter** (also widely known as a **Chopper** in classical power electronics literature) is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage directly, without an intermediate AC link. The conversion is achieved by high-speed semiconductor switching (typically an IGBT, Power MOSFET, or GTO thyristor with a forced commutation circuit) that periodically connects and disconnects the DC source to the load. The fundamental principle relies on **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** or **Variable Time Ratio Control**, where the average voltage delivered to the load is regulated by varying either the ON-time (pulse width) or the switching frequency of the chopper switch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The chopper is treated as the primary DC-DC converter topology feeding the armature of separately excited and series DC motors in modern four-quadrant variable speed drives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (faucet) connected to a tank with a measured outlet**. You want to fill a bucket below with *just enough* water to reach a specific level.

- If you turn the tap **fully ON** continuously, the bucket overflows.
- If you **rapidly flick the tap ON and OFF** at the right rhythm (e.g., 1 second ON, 1 second OFF), the *average* water delivered per second is exactly half of the full-flow rate. The bucket fills only halfway.

This "flicking" mechanism is exactly what a chopper does with electricity. The semiconductor switch acts as a **high-speed electronic tap** — flicking it ON connects the source to the load (water flows), and flicking it OFF disconnects it (water stops). By varying the proportion of ON-time vs. OFF-time, the *average* voltage reaching the load is precisely controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The duty cycle $\alpha$ is the primary control parameter and is dimensionless, bounded by $0 \le \alpha \le 1$. Chopping frequencies in industrial drives typically range from **$1\text{ kHz}$ to $20\text{ kHz}$**.

### 1.3 Need for DC-DC Conversion

- DC motor speed control (armature voltage control and field weakening).
- Battery-operated vehicle traction systems.
- Switch-mode power supplies (SMPS).
- Renewable energy interfacing (solar MPPT).
- Regenerative braking energy recovery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a Basic Chopper

A basic chopper circuit consists of four essential elements:

1. **DC Source ($V_s$)** — constant input voltage (rectified AC or battery).
2. **Chopper Switch ($S$)** — controllable semiconductor (IGBT/MOSFET with a freewheeling diode in antiparallel for inductive loads).
3. **Freewheeling Diode ($D_F$)** — provides a path for stored inductive load current when the switch turns OFF.
4. **Load ($R, L, E$)** — typically a DC motor armature.

**Operating Cycle (Time Period $T$):**

- **Mode 1 — Switch ON ($0 \le t \le \alpha T$):** Switch $S$ is closed. Load current rises exponentially toward $V_s/R$ with time constant $L/R$. The load sees voltage $v_o = V_s$.
- **Mode 2 — Switch OFF ($\alpha T \le t \le T$):** Switch $S$ is opened. The inductive load maintains current through freewheeling diode $D_F$. The load sees voltage $v_o \approx 0$ (diode drop neglected).

### 2.2 Duty Cycle Control Strategies

- **Constant Frequency Control:** Frequency $f = 1/T$ is kept constant; ON-time $T_{\text{on}}$ is varied.
- **Variable Frequency Control:** $T_{\text{on}}$ is constant; chopping period $T$ is varied (rare in modern drives due to filter design complexity).
- **PWM Control (Pulse Width Modulation):** Modern industrial standard — combines constant frequency with variable pulse width.

### 2.3 Classification of Choppers (Quadrant Operation)

| Class | Quadrants | Switches | Application |
|-------|-----------|----------|-------------|
| **Class A** | 1st only (V > 0, I > 0) | 1 switch + 1 diode | Step-down unidirectional |
| **Class B** | 2nd only (V < 0, I > 0) | 1 switch + 1 diode | Regenerative (reverse power flow) |
| **Class C** | 1st & 2nd (V, I both +ve or −ve) | 2 switches + 2 diodes | Two-quadrant forward motoring/regen |
| **Class D** | 1st & 4th (V +ve, I both polarities) | 2 switches + 2 diodes | Two-quadrant forward motoring/reverse |
| **Class E** | All 4 quadrants (Full bridge) | 4 switches + 4 diodes | Four-quadrant reversible drive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Class E choppers are the standard for high-performance reversible DC drives used in rolling mills, paper machines, and servo systems. They allow forward motoring, forward braking, reverse motoring, and reverse braking.

### 2.4 Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction

- **Continuous Conduction:** Load current never falls to zero. Energy stored in $L$ sustains the load throughout the OFF period.
- **Discontinuous Conduction:** Load current reaches zero before the next switching cycle. The load operates in the discontinuous mode — important at light loads or high switching frequencies.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Topology |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Duty Cycle | $\alpha = T_{\text{on}} / T$ | dimensionless | All |
| 2 | Chopping Frequency | $f = 1/T$ | Hz | All |
| 3 | Average Output Voltage (Buck) | $V_o = \alpha V_s$ | V | Step-Down |
| 4 | RMS Output Voltage (Buck) | $V_{\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{\alpha}$ | V | Step-Down |
| 5 | Average Output Voltage (Boost) | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$ | V | Step-Up |
| 6 | Average Output Voltage (Buck-Boost) | $V_o = \dfrac{\alpha V_s}{1-\alpha}$ | V | Step-Up/Down |
| 7 | Average Load Current | $I_o = V_o / R$ | A | All |
| 8 | RMS Chopper Switch Current | $I_{\text{sw}} = I_o \sqrt{\alpha}$ | A | Buck |
| 9 | RMS Freewheeling Diode Current | $I_{DF} = I_o \sqrt{1-\alpha}$ | A | Buck |
| 10 | Output Power (Buck) | $P_o = \alpha V_s^2 / R$ | W | Step-Down |
| 11 | Output Voltage Ripple Factor | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_o^2}}{V_o}$ | dimensionless | All |
| 12 | Chopper Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_o}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{\alpha V_s I_o}{V_s I_s}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | All |
| 13 | Effective Source Resistance | $R_{\text{eff}} = L / (R \cdot T)$ | Ω | Current-continuous |
| 14 | Critical (Discontinuous) Inductance | $L_c = \dfrac{R \cdot T \cdot (1-\alpha)}{2}$ | H | Boundary of CCM/DCM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Boundary:** For an *ideal* lossless chopper, $\eta = 100\%$. Always check whether conduction is *continuous* before applying steady-state formulas; otherwise, use the discontinuous conduction analysis.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

Chopper-fed DC drives dominate high-power industrial applications such as **steel rolling mills**, **paper manufacturing**, **mine winders**, **locomotive traction**, and **electric forklifts**. The chopper replaced the inefficient Ward-Leonard generator-based speed control system, reducing energy losses by up to **35–40%** while providing faster dynamic response.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Average Output Voltage for Step-Down (Buck) Chopper with R-L-E Load

This derivation is the **highest-weight derivation** in KTU 2024 ESE — worth full 14-mark treatment.

**Circuit Setup:** A DC source $V_s$ feeds a series combination of resistance $R$, inductance $L$, and back-EMF $E$ (representing a DC motor armature) through chopper switch $S$. A freewheeling diode $D_F$ is connected across the load.

**Step 1 — Define the switching period.**

Let $T$ be the total chopping period and $\alpha T$ be the ON duration of switch $S$.

$$
\alpha = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T}, \quad (1-\alpha) = \frac{T_{\text{off}}}{T}
$$

**Step 2 — Write KVL for Mode 1 (Switch ON, $0 \le t \le \alpha T$).**

During this interval, $D_F$ is reverse-biased and $S$ conducts. The load current $i_o$ rises.

$$
V_s = R \, i_o + L \frac{di_o}{dt} + E
$$

Rearranging for the current derivative:

$$
L \frac{di_o}{dt} + R \, i_o = V_s - E
$$

**Step 3 — Write KVL for Mode 2 (Switch OFF, $\alpha T \le t \le T$).**

When $S$ opens, inductive current forces conduction through $D_F$. With ideal diode assumption, the load terminals are short-circuited (ignoring diode drop).

$$
0 = R \, i_o + L \frac{di_o}{dt} + E
$$

Rearranging:

$$
L \frac{di_o}{dt} + R \, i_o = -E
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the volt-second balance (steady-state assumption).**

In steady state, the net change in load current over one full cycle is zero. Equivalently, integrating the inductor voltage over $T$ gives zero (average inductor voltage = 0):

$$
\int_{0}^{T} v_L \, dt = 0
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{\alpha T} (V_s - E - R i_o) \, dt + \int_{\alpha T}^{T} (-E - R i_o) \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Assume small ripple / average current approximation.**

Let $I_o$ be the average load current. Replace $R i_o$ with $R I_o$ (since the ripple is symmetric in CCM). Then:

$$
\int_{0}^{\alpha T} (V_s - E - R I_o) \, dt + \int_{\alpha T}^{T} (-E - R I_o) \, dt = 0
$$

$$
(V_s - E - R I_o)(\alpha T) + (-E - R I_o)(1-\alpha)T = 0
$$

Divide both sides by $T$:

$$
(V_s - E - R I_o)\alpha + (-E - R I_o)(1-\alpha) = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $I_o$ (average load current).**

Expanding:

$$
\alpha V_s - \alpha E - \alpha R I_o - (1-\alpha)E - (1-\alpha) R I_o = 0
$$

$$
\alpha V_s - E(\alpha + 1 - \alpha) - R I_o (\alpha + 1 - \alpha) = 0
$$

$$
\alpha V_s - E - R I_o = 0
$$

$$
R I_o = \alpha V_s - E
$$

$$
I_o = \frac{\alpha V_s - E}{R}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the average output voltage $V_o$.**

The average output voltage across the load (terminals of the load combination) is:

$$
V_o = R I_o + E = (\alpha V_s - E) + E = \alpha V_s
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \alpha \, V_s}
$$

This is the foundational Step-Down Chopper equation.

**Step 8 — Verification: average input current.**

Power balance for lossless chopper: $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$.

$$
V_s I_s = V_o I_o = (\alpha V_s) I_o
$$

$$
I_s = \alpha I_o
$$

This shows the input current is duty-cycle scaled — a key result for chopper design.

### 3.2 Derivation of Average Output Voltage for Step-Up (Boost) Chopper

**Circuit Setup:** Switch $S$ is in series with the inductor; load is across the output capacitor-diode node.

**Step 1 — Mode 1 (Switch ON, duration $\alpha T$):** Diode is reverse-biased. Inductor stores energy:

$$
L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Peak ripple: } \Delta i_L = \frac{V_s}{L} \alpha T
$$

**Step 2 — Mode 2 (Switch OFF, duration $(1-\alpha)T$):** Diode conducts. Inductor releases energy to the load:

$$
L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_s - V_o
$$

**Step 3 — Apply volt-second balance on the inductor.**

$$
\int_{0}^{T} v_L \, dt = 0
$$

$$
V_s \cdot \alpha T + (V_s - V_o)(1-\alpha)T = 0
$$

Divide by $T$:

$$
\alpha V_s + (V_s - V_o)(1-\alpha) = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
\alpha V_s + V_s - \alpha V_s - V_o + \alpha V_o = 0
$$

$$
V_s - V_o + \alpha V_o = 0
$$

$$
V_s = V_o(1-\alpha)
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}}
$$

Since $0 \le \alpha \le 1$, we have $V_o \ge V_s$, confirming step-up operation.

### 3.3 Derivation of RMS Output Voltage and Ripple Factor (Buck Chopper, Resistive Load)

For a resistive load with no inductance, the output voltage is a rectangular pulse of amplitude $V_s$ for $\alpha T$ and zero for $(1-\alpha)T$.

**RMS Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{\alpha T} V_s^2 \, dt = V_s^2 \alpha
$$

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{\alpha}
$$

**Average Output Voltage:**

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{\alpha T} V_s \, dt = \alpha V_s
$$

**Ripple Factor ($\gamma$):**

$$
\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{V_{\text{rms}}^2 - V_o^2}}{V_o} = \frac{\sqrt{\alpha V_s^2 - \alpha^2 V_s^2}}{\alpha V_s} = \frac{V_s \sqrt{\alpha(1-\alpha)}}{\alpha V_s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\gamma = \sqrt{\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Topology Comparison

| Parameter | Buck | Boost | Buck-Boost |
|-----------|------|-------|------------|
| Switch Position | Series with source | Series with inductor | Series with inductor |
| Diode Position | Across load | Series with output | Across output |
| Output Polarity | Same as input | Same as input | **Inverted** |
| $V_o$ Expression | $\alpha V_s$ | $V_s/(1-\alpha)$ | $\alpha V_s/(1-\alpha)$ |
| Stress on Switch | $V_s$ | $V_s/(1-\alpha)$ | $V_s/(1-\alpha)$ |
| Typical Application | Variable DC drives | Battery boosters | Flyback SMPS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a DC Drive with Chopper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PS[DC Source Vs] --> FIL[Input Filter LC]
    FIL --> CHOP[Chopper Switch S]
    CHOP --> FW[Freewheeling Diode DF]
    FW --> LOAD[DC Motor Armature RL plus E]
    LOAD --> SENSE[Current Sensor]
    SENSE --> CTRL[Control Unit PWM]
    CTRL --> GATE[Gate Driver Circuit]
    GATE --> CHOP
    CTRL --> REF[Speed Reference Input]
```

### 4.2 Operational State Diagram of a Class E Four-Quadrant Chopper

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Q1ForwardMotoring
    Q1ForwardMotoring --> Q2ForwardBraking : Regeneration initiated
    Q2ForwardBraking --> Q1ForwardMotoring : Brake release
    Q1ForwardMotoring --> Q4ReverseMotoring : Reverse command
    Q4ReverseMotoring --> Q3ReverseBraking : Regeneration initiated
    Q3ReverseBraking --> Q4ReverseMotoring : Brake release
    Q2ForwardBraking --> Q3ReverseBraking : Reverse command
    Q3ReverseBraking --> Q2ForwardBraking : Forward command
```

### 4.3 Step-Down Chopper Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VS[DC Source Positive Vs] --> SW[Chopper Switch S IGBT]
    SW --> N1[Load Terminal A]
    N1 --> RL[Resistance R]
    RL --> IND[Inductance L]
    IND --> EMF[Back EMF E Motor]
    EMF --> N2[Load Terminal B]
    N2 --> GND1[DC Source Negative]
    FW[Freewheeling Diode DF] -.->|across RL, IND, EMF| N1
    FW -.-> N2
```

### 4.4 Output Voltage and Current Waveforms for Buck Chopper with R-L-E Load

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph WAVE[Continuous Conduction Mode Waveforms]
    A1[Time Axis t] --- A2[0 to alphaT]
    A2 --- A3[alphaT to T]
    A3 --- A4[T to 2T]
    end
    V_ON[Output Voltage vo equals Vs] --- A2
    V_OFF[Output Voltage vo equals 0 via diode] --- A3
    V_ON2[Output Voltage vo equals Vs] --- A4
    I_RISE[Current Rising Exponentially] --- A2
    I_FALL[Current Decaying Exponentially] --- A3
    I_RISE2[Current Rising Again] --- A4
```

### 4.5 Quadrant Classification Map

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Chopper Quadrant Operation
    x-axis Voltage Sign --> Negative Positive
    y-axis Current Sign --> Negative Positive
    quadrant-1 First Quadrant Motoring Forward
    quadrant-2 Second Quadrant Braking Forward Regen
    quadrant-3 Third Quadrant Reverse Motoring
    quadrant-4 Fourth Quadrant Reverse Braking
    Class A Chopper: [0.7, 0.7]
    Class B Chopper: [0.3, 0.7]
    Class C Chopper: [0.5, 0.7]
    Class D Chopper: [0.7, 0.3]
    Class E Chopper: [0.5, 0.5]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define duty cycle in a chopper. Why is it considered the primary control parameter in DC drives?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **duty cycle** ($\alpha$) of a chopper is defined as the ratio of the ON-time of the chopper switch to the total chopping period:

$$
\alpha = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}} = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T}
$$

where $T_{\text{on}}$ is the ON duration, $T_{\text{off}}$ is the OFF duration, and $T$ is the total switching period. For a step-down chopper, the average output voltage $V_o = \alpha V_s$, so $\alpha$ directly sets the motor armature voltage and therefore its speed. It is considered the primary control parameter because (i) it linearly controls the average DC output voltage without altering the source, (ii) it is dimensionless and easily implemented in PWM ICs, and (iii) it enables fast dynamic response with negligible switching losses at high frequencies. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** What is a freewheeling diode? Why is it indispensable in chopper circuits with inductive loads?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **freewheeling diode ($D_F$)** is a diode connected in antiparallel with an inductive load, oriented such that it is reverse-biased when the chopper switch is ON and becomes forward-biased when the switch opens. It is indispensable because:

1. **Provides a path for stored inductive energy** — when the chopper switch turns OFF, the inductor attempts to maintain current flow; without $D_F$, a destructive high-voltage spike would occur across the switch.
2. **Protects the chopper switch** from over-voltage transients.
3. **Allows load current to decay smoothly** through the load loop, maintaining continuous conduction.
4. **Improves efficiency** by recycling stored magnetic energy back to the load rather than dissipating it. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveform, explain the working principle of a step-down chopper feeding an R-L load. Derive the expression for average output voltage and average load current.

**(b)** A step-down chopper has an input DC voltage $V_s = 220\text{ V}$ and load resistance $R = 10\ \Omega$. The chopper is operated at a switching frequency of $f = 1\text{ kHz}$ with a duty cycle $\alpha = 0.4$. Calculate:
   (i) Average output voltage
   (ii) RMS output voltage
   (iii) Average load current
   (iv) Output power
   (v) Chopper efficiency assuming ideal switches

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Theory (7 marks):**

During the ON-state ($0 \le t \le \alpha T$), the chopper switch $S$ conducts. The full source voltage $V_s$ is applied across the load; the load current $i_o$ rises exponentially with time constant $\tau = L/R$ toward $V_s/R$. During the OFF-state ($\alpha T \le t \le T$), switch $S$ is open; the inductor drives current through the freewheeling diode $D_F$, short-circuiting the load terminals; $i_o$ decays exponentially toward zero.

Applying volt-second balance on inductor $L$:

$$
\int_{0}^{T} v_L \, dt = 0 \Rightarrow (V_s - V_o)\alpha T + (0 - V_o)(1-\alpha)T = 0
$$

$$
\alpha V_s - \alpha V_o - (1-\alpha)V_o = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha V_s = V_o
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \alpha V_s}
$$

Average load current: $I_o = V_o / R = \alpha V_s / R$. **(7 marks — full marks require labeled circuit diagram AND waveform sketch)**

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $V_s = 220\text{ V}$, $R = 10\ \Omega$, $f = 1\text{ kHz}$, $\alpha = 0.4$.

**(i) Average output voltage:**

$$
V_o = \alpha V_s = 0.4 \times 220 = \boxed{88\text{ V}}
$$

**(ii) RMS output voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{\alpha} = 220 \times \sqrt{0.4} = 220 \times 0.6325 = \boxed{139.14\text{ V}}
$$

**(iii) Average load current:**

$$
I_o = V_o / R = 88 / 10 = \boxed{8.8\text{ A}}
$$

**(iv) Output power:**

$$
P_o = V_o \times I_o = 88 \times 8.8 = \boxed{774.4\text{ W}}
$$

(Equivalently: $P_o = \alpha V_s^2 / R = 0.4 \times 220^2 / 10 = 0.4 \times 4840 = 1936\text{ W}$ — note this assumes resistive load without back-EMF.)

For R-L load only (no E): $P_o = V_o^2/R = 88^2/10 = 774.4\text{ W}$ ✓

**(v) Chopper efficiency:**

For ideal switch and diode, power loss is zero. Therefore:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_o}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\% = \frac{774.4}{774.4} \times 100\% = \boxed{100\%}
$$

(Confirming $P_{\text{in}} = V_s I_s = V_s \cdot \alpha I_o = 220 \times 0.4 \times 8.8 = 774.4\text{ W}$.) **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a step-up (boost) chopper with circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of duty cycle.

**(b)** For a boost chopper with $V_s = 50\text{ V}$, $\alpha = 0.6$, and load $R = 20\ \Omega$, calculate the average output voltage, average output current, and the average input current. Comment on the output voltage polarity and the step-up nature of operation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Theory (7 marks):**

A boost chopper consists of an inductor $L$ in series with the source, a switch $S$ (typically IGBT), a diode $D$, and an output capacitor $C$ in parallel with load $R$.

- **Mode 1 ($S$ ON, $0 \le t \le \alpha T$):** Diode $D$ is reverse-biased. The inductor charges from the source: $v_L = V_s$, so $i_L$ rises linearly. The capacitor discharges into the load (in steady state, $v_C = V_o$).
- **Mode 2 ($S$ OFF, $\alpha T \le t \le T$):** The inductor opposes any change in current and acts as a source in series with $V_s$. The diode $D$ becomes forward-biased. The combined source-inductor voltage charges the capacitor and feeds the load: $v_L = V_s - V_o$.

Applying volt-second balance:

$$
V_s \cdot \alpha T + (V_s - V_o)(1-\alpha)T = 0
$$

$$
\alpha V_s + V_s - V_o - \alpha V_s + \alpha V_o = 0
$$

$$
V_s = V_o(1-\alpha)
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}}
$$

Since $0 \le \alpha \le 1$, $V_o \ge V_s$ always. **(7 marks)**

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $V_s = 50\text{ V}$, $\alpha = 0.6$, $R = 20\ \Omega$.

**(i) Average output voltage:**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha} = \frac{50}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{50}{0.4} = \boxed{125\text{ V}}
$$

**(ii) Average output current:**

$$
I_o = V_o / R = 125 / 20 = \boxed{6.25\text{ A}}
$$

**(iii) Average input current:**

Power balance for ideal chopper: $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$

$$
V_s I_s = V_o I_o
$$

$$
I_s = \frac{V_o I_o}{V_s} = \frac{125 \times 6.25}{50} = \frac{781.25}{50} = \boxed{15.625\text{ A}}
$$

(Equivalently: $I_s = \dfrac{I_o}{1-\alpha} = \dfrac{6.25}{0.4} = 15.625\text{ A}$ ✓)

**Comments:**
- Output polarity is **positive** (same polarity as input) — unlike a buck-boost converter.
- $V_o = 125\text{ V} > V_s = 50\text{ V}$, confirming the **step-up** nature.
- The chopper acts as a DC transformer with a turns ratio of $1/(1-\alpha)$. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Major error (loses 3–4 marks):** Students forget to draw the **freewheeling diode** in the buck chopper circuit, or draw it with incorrect polarity. The freewheeling diode must be in **antiparallel with the load**, with its **cathode at the positive terminal**.
> 2. **Common error (loses 2 marks):** Confusing the RMS and average formulas for the boost chopper. The boost chopper's RMS formula is **not** simply $V_s \sqrt{\alpha}$ — it depends on inductor ripple and capacitor size.
> 3. **Calculation slip (loses 1 mark):** Forgetting to convert units in numerical problems (e.g., kHz vs. Hz for frequency).
> 4. **Diagrams:** A circuit diagram without proper labels (component names, voltage polarities, current direction arrows) typically loses **1.5–2 marks** even if the topology is correct.
> 5. **Sign conventions:** For motor load with back-EMF $E$, students often drop the $E$ term in derivations. **Always include $E$ explicitly** in KVL for R-L-E load problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Duty cycle $\alpha = T_{\text{on}}/T$** is dimensionless; controls output voltage linearly in buck chopper.
- ⚡ **Three foundational equations:** $V_o = \alpha V_s$ (Buck), $V_o = V_s/(1-\alpha)$ (Boost), $V_o = \alpha V_s/(1-\alpha)$ (Buck-Boost).
- ⚡ **RMS switch current** $I_{\text{sw}} = I_o\sqrt{\alpha}$ and **RMS freewheeling diode current** $I_{DF} = I_o\sqrt{1-\alpha}$ — crucial for device rating.
- ⚡ **Class A** = 1-quadrant; **Class C** = 2-quadrant (forward motoring + regen); **Class E** = 4-quadrant (full H-bridge).
- ⚡ **Freewheeling diode** is mandatory for inductive loads — it prevents destructive over-voltage spikes.
- ⚡ **Chopping frequency** range: **1 kHz to 20 kHz** for industrial drives; higher frequencies reduce motor current ripple and acoustic noise.
- ⚡ **Continuous vs. Discontinuous Conduction:** For R-load and most practical R-L loads, conduction is continuous. Verify $L > L_c$ where $L_c = R T (1-\alpha)/2$.
- ⚡ **Power balance:** For ideal chopper, $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$, so $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$ — useful sanity check in numerical problems.
- ⚡ **Four-quadrant drives** use **two Class C choppers in anti-parallel (H-bridge)** configuration — frequently tested.
- ⚡ **Ripple factor** for buck chopper with R-load is $\gamma = \sqrt{(1-\alpha)/\alpha}$ — minimum at $\alpha = 0.5$.
- ⚡ **Boost chopper output** cannot exceed source by more than $1/(1-\alpha_{\max})$ — practical $\alpha_{\max} \approx 0.8$ for stable operation.
- ⚡ **Always mention the assumption** of ideal switches, no source inductance, and steady-state operation in derivations — these are worth **0.5–1 bonus mark** per KTU examiner rubric.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Step-Down and Step-Up Choppers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Step-Down and Step-Up Choppers # MODULE 3 — DC CHOPPERS: STEP-DOWN & STEP-UP TOPOLOGIES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **DC Chopper** is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage directly. It is essentially a high-speed **ON/OFF semiconductor switch** (BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT, or GTO) that chops the DC supply at a high switching frequency. The average value of the chopped output is controlled by varying the **duty cycle** of the switch. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim essence):** > A chopper is a DC-to-DC converter. When the output (load) voltage is **lower** than the input (source) voltage, it is called a **Step-Down Chopper (Buck Converter)**. When the output voltage is **higher** than the input voltage, it is called a **Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model** > Imagine a water tank (DC source) supplying a tap (load). If you want **less water pressure** at the tap, you simply **partially close the valve** — you let water flow for a short time, then shut it. The *average* pressure drops. That is exactly what a **Step-Down Chopper** does with voltage. > > Conversely, to get **more pressure** than the tank provides, you need a *pump*. The Step-Up Chopper stores energy from the source in an inductor (a magnetic "flywheel") during switch ON-time, then **releases that stored energy in series with the source** during OFF-time — boosting the voltage seen by the load. Think of a bicycle pump: a small stroke (Ton) followed by a delivery stroke (Toff) at higher pressure. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - The **switching period** $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$ is typically in the range of **$\mathbf{1\;kHz}$ to $\mathbf{100\;kHz}$** in modern drives. - The **duty cycle** $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ is dimensionless and lies between **$\mathbf{0 < \alpha < 1}$**. - Switching frequency $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$, with units of **$\mathbf{Hz}$**. > [!TIP] > **Why "Chopper"?** Because the device literally *chops* a continuous DC waveform into discrete pulses — the load sees a pulsed waveform whose **average value** is the useful DC output. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-Down Chopper (Buck Converter) — Operating Principle ### Circuit Components - **S** — Controlled switch (IGBT/MOSFET), connected between source $V_s$ and the load side. - **D** — Freewheeling diode, reverse-biased when S is ON, forward-biased when S is OFF. - **L** — Filter inductor (smooths current ripple). - **C** — Filter capacitor (smooths voltage ripple). - **R** — Load resistance. ### Mode 1: Switch ON (Duration $T_{on}$) - Switch **S is closed**; Diode **D is reverse-biased** (off). - Source $V_s$ is connected directly across the L-C-R load. - Inductor current **rises linearly**, storing energy in its magnetic field. - Capacitor **charges** toward $V_s$. - Equation: $V_s = V_o + L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ ### Mode 2: Switch OFF (Duration $T_{off}$) - Switch **S is open**; Diode **D becomes forward-biased** and conducts (freewheeling action). - Inductor releases stored energy through the diode to the load. - Inductor current **falls linearly** (cannot change instantaneously). - Equation: $0 = V_o + L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ → $V_o = -L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ (decreasing current) ### 2.2 Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter) — Operating Principle ### Mode 1: Switch ON (Duration $T_{on}$) - Switch **S is closed**; Diode **D is reverse-biased** (blocked). - Source $V_s$ is impressed **across the inductor L** → inductor stores energy ($di/dt > 0$). - Load is **isolated** from the source; it is supplied by the **capacitor C** (which discharges through R). - Equation: $V_s = L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ ### Mode 2: Switch OFF (Duration $T_{off}$) - Switch **S is open**; Inductor current must continue → Diode **D is forward-biased** (conducts). - Inductor voltage **reverses polarity** (Lenz's law) and now adds in **series with $V_s$**. - Combined voltage $V_s + V_L$ charges the capacitor and supplies the load at a **higher** voltage. - Equation: $V_s - V_o = L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ → $V_o = V_s - L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ (since $di/dt < 0$, $V_o > V_s$) ### 2.3 Real-World Utility - **Step-Down Chopper**: Battery-powered electric vehicles, DC motor speed control (armature voltage control), SMPS in computers, LED drivers. - **Step-Up Chopper**: Regenerative braking in EVs (boosts low back-EMF to charge higher-voltage battery), solar PV MPPT systems, hybrid energy storage, flash photography circuits. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the Inductor is the Heart of a Chopper** > An inductor opposes instantaneous change in current. By alternately storing energy (switch ON) and releasing it (switch OFF), it enables **voltage transformation** without using a transformer — this is the key innovation of modern switch-mode power conversion. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Step-Down Chopper (Buck)** | **Step-Up Chopper (Boost)** | |---|---|---| | Duty Cycle | $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ | | Average Output Voltage | $V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}$ | | Average Input Current | $I_s = \alpha \cdot I_o$ | $I_s = \dfrac{I_o}{1 - \alpha}$ | | RMS Output Voltage | $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\alpha}$ | $V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{1-\alpha}}$ | | Inductor Current Ripple | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s\,(1-\alpha)\,\alpha}{L\,f}$ | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s\,\alpha}{L\,f}$ | | Critical (Min) Inductance | $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-\alpha)\,R}{2\,f}$ | $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha\,R}{2\,f}$ | | Capacitor Voltage Ripple | $\Delta V_c = \dfrac{V_o\,(1-\alpha)}{8\,L\,C\,f^2}$ | $\Delta V_c = \dfrac{I_o\,\alpha}{C\,f}$ | | Power Balance (ideal) | $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$ | $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$ | | Boundary Condition | Continuous if $L \geq L_{min}$ | Continuous if $L \geq L_{min}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Warning on Notation:** In KTU board papers, the duty cycle is most commonly denoted by **$k$** or **$\alpha$** (alpha, the Greek letter). The switching period may be written as $T$ or $T_s$. Always re-read the question to confirm the symbol used by the examiner. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of Step-Down Chopper ### Step 1 — Write the Inductor Voltage Equation During $T_{on}$ When switch S is ON, the source $V_s$ appears across the series combination of L and the load. Applying KVL around the loop: $$ V_s = V_L + V_o $$ Substituting $V_L = L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$: $$ V_s - V_o = L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = L\,\dfrac{\Delta I_{on}}{T_{on}} $$ Therefore, the **rise in inductor current** during the ON period is: $$ \Delta I_{on} = \frac{(V_s - V_o)\,T_{on}}{L} $$ ### Step 2 — Write the Inductor Voltage Equation During $T_{off}$ When switch S is OFF, the freewheeling diode D conducts, short-circuiting the source side. The inductor sees only $-V_o$ across it. Applying KVL: $$ 0 = V_L + V_o \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -V_o = L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = L\,\dfrac{(-\Delta I_{off})}{T_{off}} $$ The **fall in inductor current** during the OFF period is: $$ \Delta I_{off} = \frac{V_o\,T_{off}}{L} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Steady-State Volt-Second Balance In **steady state**, the net change in inductor current over one full period must be zero (energy stored = energy released). Hence $\Delta I_{on} = \Delta I_{off}$: $$ \frac{(V_s - V_o)\,T_{on}}{L} = \frac{V_o\,T_{off}}{L} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $V_o$ Cancel $L$ from both sides and expand: $$ (V_s - V_o)\,T_{on} = V_o\,T_{off} $$ $$ V_s\,T_{on} - V_o\,T_{on} = V_o\,T_{off} $$ $$ V_s\,T_{on} = V_o\,(T_{on} + T_{off}) = V_o \cdot T $$ Dividing both sides by $T$: $$ V_o = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \cdot V_s $$ ### Step 5 — Final Result Defining the duty cycle $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$: $$ \boxed{\,V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s = k \cdot V_s\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Insight:** Since $0 < \alpha < 1$, we always have $V_o < V_s$ — confirming the **step-down** nature. When $\alpha \to 1$ (switch always ON), $V_o \to V_s$. When $\alpha \to 0$ (switch always OFF), $V_o \to 0$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of Step-Up Chopper ### Step 1 — Write the Inductor Voltage Equation During $T_{on}$ When switch S is ON, the source $V_s$ is connected directly across the inductor L (the diode blocks, isolating the load). The load is sustained by the discharging capacitor. $$ V_s = L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = L\,\dfrac{\Delta I_{on}}{T_{on}} $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta I_{on} = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{L} $$ ### Step 2 — Write the Inductor Voltage Equation During $T_{off}$ When switch S opens, the inductor current must continue. The diode D turns ON, connecting the inductor in series with $V_s$ to the load. Applying KVL around the outer loop (source, inductor, diode, load): $$ V_s - V_o = L\,\dfrac{di_L}{dt} $$ Since the current is now **decreasing**, $\dfrac{di_L}{dt} < 0$. The magnitude of the **fall** is: $$ \Delta I_{off} = \frac{(V_o - V_s)\,T_{off}}{L} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Subtlety students often miss:** Because $V_o > V_s$ for a boost converter, the term $(V_o - V_s)$ is **positive** — this is precisely what makes the inductor "discharge" into the load at a higher potential. ### Step 3 — Apply Steady-State Volt-Second Balance Setting $\Delta I_{on} = \Delta I_{off}$: $$ \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{L} = \frac{(V_o - V_s) \cdot T_{off}}{L} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $V_o$ Cancel $L$ and expand: $$ V_s \cdot T_{on} = (V_o - V_s) \cdot T_{off} $$ $$ V_s \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot T_{off} - V_s \cdot T_{off} $$ Bring the $V_s$ terms together: $$ V_s(T_{on} + T_{off}) = V_o \cdot T_{off} $$ Since $T_{on} + T_{off} = T$: $$ V_s \cdot T = V_o \cdot T_{off} $$ Therefore: $$ V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T}{T_{off}} $$ ### Step 5 — Express in Terms of Duty Cycle $\alpha$ Substitute $T_{off} = T - T_{on} = T(1 - \alpha)$ and $T = T$: $$ V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T}{T(1 - \alpha)} = \frac{V_s}{1 - \alpha} $$ ### Step 6 — Final Result $$ \boxed{\,V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Insight:** Since $0 < (1 - \alpha) < 1$, the denominator is less than 1, so $V_o > V_s$ — confirming the **step-up** action. As $\alpha \to 1$ (switch almost always ON), $V_o \to \infty$ *theoretically*. In practice, losses, component ratings, and switch duty limits clamp this value. **Always** state in exam answers: "$\alpha$ must be kept below a practical limit (typically 0.8–0.9) to avoid inductor saturation and excessive currents." --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary (For KTU Answers) | **Feature** | **Step-Down (Buck)** | **Step-Up (Boost)** | |---|---|---| | Output vs Input | $V_o < V_s$ | $V_o > V_s$ | | Switch ON → Diode | Diode OFF (blocked) | Diode OFF (blocked) | | Switch OFF → Diode | Diode ON (freewheel) | Diode ON (conducts) | | Inductor location | Series with load | Series with source | | Energy storage location | In L, then delivered to load | In L, then released in series with $V_s$ | | Typical application | DC motor speed control | Regenerative braking, solar MPPT | | Switch stress | $V_{sw,max} = V_s$ | $V_{sw,max} = V_o$ | | Output voltage range | $0$ to $V_s$ | $V_s$ to $\infty$ (theoretical) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Step-Down Chopper — Block-Level Functional Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the Step-Down Chopper, including the two operating modes and the current paths. This is the safest representation for KTU theory answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC["DC Source Vs"] --> SW["Controlled Switch S
IGBT or MOSFET"] SW -- "Mode 1 ON: Ton" --> NODE1["Node A
L C R Load"] NODE1 --> LFILT["Inductor L
Stores Energy"] LFILT --> CFILT["Capacitor C
Smooths Vo"] CFILT --> LOAD["Load Resistor R"] LOAD --> GND1["Return Path"] DIODE["Freewheeling Diode D"] -. "Mode 2 OFF: Toff" .-> NODE1 DIODE --> LOAD CTRL["PWM Controller
Sets duty cycle alpha"] -. "Gate Signal" .-> SW ``` ## 4.2 Step-Up Chopper — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC["DC Source Vs"] --> IND["Inductor L
Stores Energy Mode 1"] IND --> NODE1["Node A
Switch Diode junction"] SW["Controlled Switch S
IGBT or MOSFET"] -- "Mode 1 ON: Ton to Ground" --> GND1["Ground"] SW -. "Gate Signal from PWM" .-> CTRL["PWM Controller
alpha = Ton / T"] DIODE["Diode D
Forward biased in Mode 2"] --> NODE2["Node B
C parallel R load"] NODE1 --> DIODE CAP["Capacitor C
Charged in Mode 2 to Vo"] --> NODE2 LOAD["Load Resistor R"] --> NODE2 LOAD --> GND2["Return to Vs negative"] ``` ## 4.3 Combined Topology Comparison — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BUCK["STEP-DOWN BUCK"] B1["Vs in"] --> B2["Switch S"] B2 --> B3["L series"] B3 --> B4["C parallel R load"] B5["Freewheel Diode D"] -.-> B3 end subgraph BOOST["STEP-UP BOOST"] P1["Vs in"] --> P2["L series"] P2 --> P3["Switch S to GND"] P2 --> P4["Diode D forward"] P4 --> P5["C parallel R load"] end BUCK --- BOOST ``` ## 4.4 Waveform Schematic (Textual Description for KTU Theory) > [!TIP] > **How to draw waveforms in the exam (since Mermaid cannot plot time-series):** > 1. Draw a rectangular pulse train for $V_o$ (or $i_L$) over one full period $T$. > 2. Mark the **ON** interval $T_{on} = \alpha T$ as a high level, and **OFF** interval $T_{off} = (1-\alpha)T$ as zero. > 3. For **Step-Down**: $V_o$ waveform = $V_s$ during $T_{on}$, then $\approx 0$ during $T_{off}$ (ideal switch). > 4. For **Step-Down**: Inductor current $i_L$ is a **triangular ripple** with average value $I_o = V_o / R$. > 5. For **Step-Up**: Output voltage $V_o$ is approximately **constant** (due to C), with a small sawtooth ripple of magnitude $\Delta V_c$. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **Define a DC chopper. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a step-down chopper. Derive the expression for its average output voltage.** #### Model Answer (3-mark key points): A **DC chopper** is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by rapidly switching a semiconductor device ON and OFF. **Working of Step-Down Chopper (Buck):** - A chopper switch **S** is placed in series between the DC source $V_s$ and the load. - A **freewheeling diode D** is connected across the load to provide a path for inductor current when S is OFF. | **Switch S** | **Diode D** | **Inductor L** | **Load Voltage** | |---|---|---|---| | **ON** | Reverse biased | Current rises, stores energy | $V_o = V_s$ | | **OFF** | Forward biased | Current falls, releases energy | $V_o = 0$ (ideal) | **Average Output Voltage (derivation summary):** Applying volt-second balance across inductor over one switching period: $$ V_o = \frac{T_{on}}{T}\,V_s = \alpha \cdot V_s $$ Since $0 < \alpha < 1$, the output is always **less than** the input — hence the name **Step-Down**. (3 marks) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a step-up chopper. Derive the expression for its average output voltage in terms of duty cycle.** #### Model Answer (3-mark key points): A **Step-Up Chopper (Boost Converter)** produces an output voltage **higher** than the input DC source voltage. **Working Principle (two modes):** - **Mode 1 — Switch ON (Duration $T_{on}$):** The switch S is closed; the diode D is reverse-biased. The source $V_s$ appears across inductor L, which stores energy. The load is fed by the discharging capacitor C. - **Mode 2 — Switch OFF (Duration $T_{off}$):** The switch S is opened; the inductor maintains current flow, forward-biasing diode D. The inductor voltage reverses polarity and adds in **series** with $V_s$, charging capacitor C to a **higher** voltage than $V_s$. **Average Output Voltage:** Applying volt-second balance: $$ V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - \alpha} $$ where $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}$ is the duty cycle. As $\alpha \to 1$, $V_o \to V_s/(1-\alpha) \to$ very large value. (3 marks) --- ## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] (14 Marks)** **(a)** A step-down chopper operates from a DC source of **$V_s = 220\,\text{V}$** and supplies a resistive load of **$R = 10\,\Omega$**. The chopper is switched ON for **$T_{on} = 1.5\,\text{ms}$** and OFF for **$T_{off} = 0.5\,\text{ms}$**. Calculate: (i) Duty cycle $\alpha$ (ii) Average output voltage $V_o$ (iii) Average load current $I_o$ (iv) RMS output voltage $V_{o,rms}$ **(b)** For the same chopper, if the switching frequency is **$f = 1\,\text{kHz}$** and a filter inductor of **$L = 5\,\text{mH}$** is used, calculate the **peak-to-peak inductor current ripple** $\Delta I_L$ and the **critical (minimum) inductance** $L_{min}$ to maintain continuous conduction. --- #### Model Solution (14 Marks Breakdown) **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **(i) Duty Cycle (1 mark):** Total period $T = T_{on} + T_{off} = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0\,\text{ms}$. $$ \alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{1.5}{2.0} = 0.75 $$ **(ii) Average Output Voltage (2 marks):** $$ V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s = 0.75 \times 220 = 165\;\text{V} $$ **(iii) Average Load Current (2 marks):** $$ I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{165}{10} = 16.5\;\text{A} $$ **(iv) RMS Output Voltage (2 marks):** $$ V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\alpha} = 220 \times \sqrt{0.75} = 220 \times 0.8660 = 190.52\;\text{V} $$ **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Switching period $T = 1/f = 1/1000 = 1\,\text{ms} = 1\times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$. **Inductor current ripple (4 marks):** Using the formula for buck converter: $$ \Delta I_L = \frac{V_s\,(1-\alpha)\,\alpha}{L\,f} $$ Substituting: $$ \Delta I_L = \frac{220 \times (1 - 0.75) \times 0.75}{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000} $$ $$ \Delta I_L = \frac{220 \times 0.25 \times 0.75}{5} = \frac{41.25}{5} = 8.25\;\text{A} $$ **Critical inductance (3 marks):** $$ L_{min} = \frac{(1 - \alpha)\,R}{2\,f} = \frac{0.25 \times 10}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{2.5}{2000} = 1.25\;\text{mH} $$ Since $L = 5\,\text{mH} > L_{min} = 1.25\,\text{mH}$, the converter operates in **continuous conduction mode (CCM)**. ✅ --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] (14 Marks)** **(a)** A step-up chopper is supplied from a DC source of **$V_s = 50\,\text{V}$** and supplies a load of **$R = 20\,\Omega$**. The chopper is operated at a switching frequency of **$f = 2\,\text{kHz}$** with a duty cycle of **$\alpha = 0.6$**. Determine: (i) Average output voltage $V_o$ (ii) Average load current $I_o$ (iii) Average source current $I_s$ (iv) Verify the power balance (assuming ideal, lossless chopper). **(b)** If the same chopper uses an inductor **$L = 200\,\mu\text{H}$**, calculate the **peak-to-peak inductor current ripple** $\Delta I_L$ and the **critical inductance** $L_{min}$. Comment on the conduction mode. --- #### Model Solution (14 Marks Breakdown) **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **(i) Average Output Voltage (2 marks):** $$ V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{50}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{50}{0.4} = 125\;\text{V} $$ **(ii) Average Load Current (2 marks):** $$ I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{125}{20} = 6.25\;\text{A} $$ **(iii) Average Source Current (2 marks):** $$ I_s = \frac{I_o}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{6.25}{0.4} = 15.625\;\text{A} $$ **(iv) Power Balance Verification (1 mark):** Input power $P_{in} = V_s \cdot I_s = 50 \times 15.625 = 781.25\;\text{W}$. Output power $P_{out} = V_o \cdot I_o = 125 \times 6.25 = 781.25\;\text{W}$. $$ P_{in} = P_{out} = 781.25\;\text{W} \quad \checkmark $$ Power balance holds for an ideal lossless chopper. ✅ **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Given $L = 200\,\mu\text{H} = 200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{H}$, $f = 2\,\text{kHz} = 2000\,\text{Hz}$. **Inductor current ripple (4 marks):** For a boost converter: $$ \Delta I_L = \frac{V_s \cdot \alpha}{L \cdot f} = \frac{50 \times 0.6}{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 2000} $$ $$ \Delta I_L = \frac{30}{0.4} = 75\;\text{A} $$ **Critical inductance (3 marks):** $$ L_{min} = \frac{(1 - \alpha)^2 \cdot \alpha \cdot R}{2\,f} = \frac{(0.4)^2 \times 0.6 \times 20}{2 \times 2000} $$ $$ L_{min} = \frac{0.16 \times 0.6 \times 20}{4000} = \frac{1.92}{4000} = 0.48\;\text{mH} = 480\;\mu\text{H} $$ Since $L = 200\,\mu\text{H} < L_{min} = 480\,\mu\text{H}$, the converter operates in **discontinuous conduction mode (DCM)**. ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Sign error in boost derivation:** Students often write $V_s - V_o = L\,\frac{di}{dt}$ during $T_{on}$ — **this is wrong**. During $T_{on}$, the load is disconnected from the source, so the inductor sees only $V_s$. Mark loss: **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing $T_{on}$ and $T_{off}$ in the boost formula:** The boost equation $V_o = V_s/(1-\alpha)$ depends on $T_{off}$, not $T_{on}$. Always double-check whether $\alpha$ in the question refers to ON-time or OFF-time ratio. > 3. **Forgetting units in ripple calculation:** A common mistake is to plug $L$ in mH without converting to H. The formula requires SI units. Mark loss: **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Not commenting on CCM vs DCM:** Inductance design questions *always* require a concluding statement — "Since $L > L_{min}$, CCM holds" — for full marks. > 5. **Skipping the power balance check:** For 14-mark problems involving choppers, always verify $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$ to demonstrate mastery. --- ## 5.
