Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Use this checklist as your last-minute KTU revision sheet:** - ✅ A **DC chopper** is a DC-to-DC converter using high-frequency semiconductor switching. - ✅ A **Step-Down (Buck) Chopper** produces $V_o = \alpha \cdot V_s$, where $\alpha = T_{on}/T$ is the duty cycle. - ✅ A **Step-Up (Boost) Chopper** produces $V_o = V_s/(1 - \alpha)$, valid for $0 < \alpha < 1$. - ✅ In a **Buck**, the freewheeling diode carries current only during $T_{off}$. - ✅ In a **Boost**, the diode conducts only during $T_{off}$, delivering $V_s + V_L$ to the load. - ✅ **Volt-second balance** across the inductor (steady state) is the *fundamental* derivation tool — never skip showing this step in the exam. - ✅ **Power balance**: For an ideal lossless chopper, $V_s I_s = V_o I_o$ must always hold. - ✅ **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** requires $L \geq L_{min}$; otherwise, the converter enters **DCM** with distorted waveforms. - ✅ The **switching frequency** $f$ (in Hz) and the **period** $T$ (in seconds) are reciprocals: $f = 1/T$. - ✅ In KTU answers, always **state the assumption** (e.g., "Assuming ideal, lossless switch and diode") to earn method marks. - ✅ For **numerical problems**, always carry units and convert all values to **SI base units** (H, F, s) before substituting. - ✅ **Buck applications**: DC motor speed control, SMPS, battery chargers. - ✅ **Boost applications**: EV regenerative braking, solar PV MPPT, hybrid vehicles. - ✅ **Final-form answer format**: Box the final $V_o$ equation with `\boxed{...}` in the answer script; examiners look for this! --- **End of Module 3 — Step-Down and Step-Up Choppers Notes** *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) examination standards.*

topics.title: Control Methods: Two-Quadrant and Four-Quadrant Chopper, DC Motor Drives, Solid-State Speed Control of DC Motors, Armature and Field Control, Single-Phase Fully Controlled Converter Drives (Rectifier and Inverter Mode), Chopper-Controlled DC Drives, Regenerative Braking Control, Four-Quadrant Chopper Drives, Closed-Loop Speed and Torque Control, and Inverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Methods: Two-Quadrant and Four-Quadrant Chopper, DC Motor Drives, Solid-State Speed Control of DC Motors, Armature and Field Control, Single-Phase Fully Controlled Converter Drives (Rectifier and Inverter Mode), Chopper-Controlled DC Drives, Regenerative Braking Control, Four-Quadrant Chopper Drives, Closed-Loop Speed and Torque Control, and Inverters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Drive — The Foundational Concept

An **electric drive** is a sophisticated electromechanical system used to control the speed, torque, and rotational direction (quadrants of operation) of an electric motor. It comprises three fundamental blocks:

1. **Power Module** (Converter/Chopper/Inverter) — Handles raw power conditioning.
2. **Motor** (DC Shunt, Series, Compound, or PMDC) — Converts electrical energy to mechanical rotation.
3. **Control Unit** (Sensors + Feedback Controller) — Compares reference and actual signals to drive the power module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** An *electric drive* is a combination of a power electronic converter, an electric motor, and a feedback controller used to regulate the mechanical output (speed/torque/position) of the motor to meet dynamic load requirements.

## 1.2 The Four-Quadrant Operation — Intuitive Analogy

Every electric drive can operate in one of **four quadrants** on a Speed–Torque (ω–T) plane. Think of a car:

| Quadrant | Motion | Torque Direction | Speed Direction | Car Analogy |
|----------|--------|------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Q1 — Forward Motoring** | Forward | Forward (Positive) | Forward (Positive) | Car accelerating forward (uphill drive) |
| **Q2 — Forward Braking** | Forward | Reverse (Negative) | Forward (Positive) | Car braking while moving forward |
| **Q3 — Reverse Motoring** | Reverse | Reverse (Negative) | Reverse (Negative) | Car reversing uphill |
| **Q4 — Reverse Braking** | Reverse | Forward (Positive) | Reverse (Negative) | Car braking while moving in reverse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Motoring** = Power flows *from source to motor*. **Braking/Regeneration** = Power flows *from motor back to source*. The sign of speed × torque determines motoring (+ve) or braking (–ve).

## 1.3 DC Motor Drives — The Three Speed Control Pillars

The fundamental DC motor speed equation is the gateway to understanding *all* DC drive control:

$$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Motor speed in **rpm**,
- $V_a$ = Armature terminal voltage in **Volts**,
- $I_a$ = Armature current in **Amperes**,
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance in **Ohms**,
- $k$ = Motor construction constant,
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in **Weber**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The speed equation reveals the *three control methods* instantly — control $V_a$ (Armature Voltage Control), control $\Phi$ (Field Control), or use both. Below rated speed → $V_a$ control. Above rated speed → $\Phi$ control (field weakening).

## 1.4 Solid-State Speed Control — Replacing Resistive Rheostats

Earlier, speed control was achieved by wasting energy in **rheostats** (resistive drops). Modern solid-state control uses **power electronic switching devices** — Thyristors (SCRs), Power BJTs, IGBTs, MOSFETs, and GTOs — to achieve *lossless* control.

The two principal solid-state control methods are:

- **A. Converter (Phase-Controlled Rectifier) Control** — Uses a fully controlled AC-DC converter (e.g., single-phase full bridge) to vary DC output voltage.
- **B. Chopper (DC-DC Converter) Control** — Uses high-frequency switching to vary the average DC voltage applied to the armature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chopper vs Converter:** Converters operate at *line frequency* (50 Hz) — coarse control. Choppers operate at *high frequency* (kHz range) — smooth, fast, and efficient control. KTU 2024 emphasizes chopper drives for battery-fed systems (EVs, robotics, traction).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of DC-DC Choppers by Quadrant

Choppers are categorized based on the number of quadrants they can operate in:

| Chopper Class | Quadrants | Power Flow | Application |
|---------------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| **Class A (First Quadrant)** | Q1 only | Source → Motor | Forward motoring only |
| **Class B (Second Quadrant)** | Q2 only | Motor → Source | Regenerative braking only |
| **Class C (Two-Quadrant, Type A)** | Q1 & Q2 | Bidirectional | Forward motoring + braking |
| **Class D (Two-Quadrant, Type B)** | Q3 & Q4 | Bidirectional | Reverse motoring + braking |
| **Class E (Four-Quadrant)** | All 4 quadrants | Fully bidirectional | Reversible, fast-response drives |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** "Type A" two-quadrant choppers use *two switches + two diodes*, whereas "Type B" uses *two switches + two diodes* in a complementary bridge arrangement. The four-quadrant (Class E) chopper uses **four switches + four diodes** arranged as an H-bridge.

## 2.2 First-Quadrant (Class A) Chopper — Step-Down Operation

**Circuit:** A single controllable switch (IGBT/SCR with commutation) and a freewheeling diode (FWD).

**Operation:** The switch is turned ON for time $T_{on}$ and OFF for time $T_{off}$ in a chopping period $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$.

**Duty Cycle:**

$$\delta = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

**Average Output Voltage (Load Voltage):**

$$V_0 = \delta \cdot V_s$$

**Average Load Current:**

$$I_0 = \frac{V_0 - E_b}{R}$$

Where $E_b$ is the motor's back-EMF.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Class A choppers are used in conveyor belts, traction motors in forward direction, and battery-powered toys — wherever unidirectional motoring with smooth speed control is required.

## 2.3 Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class C) — Type A Configuration

Two switches $T_1$, $T_2$ and two diodes $D_1$, $D_2$ form this topology.

**Q1 Operation (Forward Motoring):** $T_1$ is chopped (PWM); $T_2$ is OFF. Diode $D_2$ freewheels when $T_1$ is OFF.

$$V_0 = \delta \cdot V_s, \quad \text{where } 0 \leq \delta \leq 1$$

**Q2 Operation (Forward Regenerative Braking):** $T_2$ is chopped; $T_1$ is OFF. Diode $D_1$ conducts during $T_2$ OFF interval. The motor acts as a generator, feeding energy back to the source. Now $V_0$ becomes *negative*:

$$V_0 = -(1 - \delta) \cdot V_s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** In Q2, the *switch duty cycle* $\delta$ still controls the *magnitude* of negative voltage. As $\delta$ increases, the negative voltage magnitude *decreases* (less negative). This counterintuitive behavior trips up many students in exams.

## 2.4 Four-Quadrant (Class E) Chopper — Full H-Bridge

This uses **four IGBTs** ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$) and **four freewheeling diodes** ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$).

**Modes of Operation:**

| Mode | Active Switches | Freewheeling | Quadrant | $V_0$ |
|------|----------------|--------------|----------|-------|
| Forward Motoring | $T_1, T_2$ (chopped) | $D_3, D_4$ | Q1 | $+V_s$ |
| Forward Braking | $D_1, D_2$ (regen) | $T_3, T_4$ | Q2 | $-V_s$ |
| Reverse Motoring | $T_3, T_4$ (chopped) | $D_1, D_2$ | Q3 | $-V_s$ |
| Reverse Braking | $D_3, D_4$ (regen) | $T_1, T_2$ | Q4 | $+V_s$ |

**Forward Motoring with PWM:** $T_1$ & $T_2$ chopped with duty cycle $\delta$, $T_3$ & $T_4$ OFF.

$$V_0 = \delta \cdot V_s \quad (0 \leq \delta \leq 1)$$

**Reverse Motoring with PWM:** $T_3$ & $T_4$ chopped with duty cycle $\delta'$, $T_1$ & $T_2$ OFF.

$$V_0 = -\delta' \cdot V_s \quad (0 \leq \delta' \leq 1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The four-quadrant chopper is the heart of modern reversible DC drives — used in rolling mills, paper mills, machine tools, CNC, and electric vehicles. The diagonal pairs ($T_1$-$T_2$ and $T_3$-$T_4$) must NEVER be ON simultaneously — this causes a *shoot-through short circuit* across the source!

## 2.5 Single-Phase Fully Controlled Converter Drive

A **single-phase full-bridge** uses four thyristors $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply. The firing angle $\alpha$ controls the output.

**Average Output Voltage (Rectifier Mode, $0 \leq \alpha \leq 90°$):**

$$V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha$$

Where $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_s$ is the peak input voltage.

**RMS Output Voltage:**

$$V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$

**Inverter Mode ($90° \leq \alpha \leq 180°$):** When $\alpha > 90°$, $\cos \alpha$ becomes negative, and $V_0$ becomes negative. The converter now operates in **inverter mode**, pumping power *back* from the DC motor (acting as generator) to the AC mains. This is the basis of **regenerative braking** for converter-fed DC drives.

**Condition for Successful Inversion (Avoidance of Commutation Failure):**

$$\vert \alpha \vert \leq 180° - \gamma \quad \text{or} \quad \beta \geq \gamma$$

Where $\beta = 180° - \alpha$ is the **extinction angle** and $\gamma$ is the commutation overlap angle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Note:** For inverter mode, the firing angle is denoted as $\alpha_i$ (or sometimes just counted beyond $90°$). The minimum value of $\beta$ is kept at $30°$ to ensure commutation margin. Don't confuse this with inverter circuits in Module 4.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $N = (V_a - I_a R_a)/(k\Phi)$ | DC motor speed | All DC drives |
| 2 | $T = k \Phi I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | Torque calculations |
| 3 | $E_b = k \Phi \omega = k \Phi (2\pi N/60)$ | Back-EMF | Energy conversion |
| 4 | $V_0 = \delta V_s$ | Chopper avg output | Chopper drives |
| 5 | $\delta = T_{on}/T$ | Duty cycle | PWM control |
| 6 | $V_0 = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ | Full converter avg voltage | Converter drives |
| 7 | $V_{rms} = V_s\sqrt{1 - \alpha/\pi + \sin 2\alpha/(2\pi)}$ | RMS voltage | Heating/ripple analysis |
| 8 | $I_0 = (V_0 - E_b)/R$ | Motor current | Steady-state analysis |
| 9 | $P_{regen} = E_b \cdot I_a$ (when $I_a$ reversed) | Regenerative power | Braking energy recovery |
| 10 | $f_{chop} = 1/T$ | Chopping frequency | Typically 1–20 kHz |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Control Methods

- **Armature Voltage Control (Below Rated Speed):** Used in **electric vehicles** for smooth low-speed torque, in **rolling mills** for tension control, and in **printers/plotters** for precise positioning.
- **Field Control (Above Rated Speed):** Used in **fan/pump drives** where small speed increase above base is required, and in **traction** for weak-field operation at high speeds.
- **Regenerative Braking:** Used in **electric trains, hybrid cars (Toyota Prius, Tesla), elevators, and escalators** to recover kinetic energy as electrical energy, improving efficiency by 15–30%.
- **Four-Quadrant Drives:** Used in **machine tool spindles, robot arm joints, CNC positioners, and winding/unwinding systems** requiring rapid forward-reverse with controlled braking.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of Class A Chopper

**Given:** DC source voltage $V_s$, chopping period $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$, duty cycle $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Define the output voltage waveform.** When the switch is ON, $V_0 = V_s$. When the switch is OFF, the freewheeling diode conducts and $V_0 = 0$ (assuming ideal switch and diode).

$$V_0(t) = \begin{cases} V_s, & 0 \leq t \leq T_{on} \\ 0, & T_{on} \leq t \leq T \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average value by integration over one period.**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} V_0(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral into ON and OFF intervals.**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \right] = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the definition of duty cycle.**

$$\boxed{V_0 = \delta \cdot V_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** When $\delta = 0$ (switch always OFF), $V_0 = 0$. When $\delta = 1$ (switch always ON), $V_0 = V_s$. Both limits are physically correct. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Voltage of Single-Phase Full Converter

**Given:** Single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with input $v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$, firing angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Write the output voltage waveform.** For a highly inductive load (continuous conduction), the output voltage consists of two pulses per cycle of the input. From $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi + \alpha$, $V_0 = v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$. From $\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi + \alpha$, $V_0 = -V_m \sin \omega t$ (the second pair of thyristors conducts with reversed polarity).

$$V_0(t) = V_m \sin \omega t, \quad \text{for } \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi + \alpha$$

**Step 2 — Average over half cycle (π radians).**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin \omega t \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate.**

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos \omega t \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi + \alpha) + \cos \alpha \right]$$

**Step 4 — Use the identity $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos \alpha$.**

$$V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -(-\cos \alpha) + \cos \alpha \right] = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ 2 \cos \alpha \right]$$

**Step 5 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** For $0° \leq \alpha \leq 90°$, $V_0 > 0$ (rectifier mode — power flows from AC to DC). For $90° \leq \alpha \leq 180°$, $V_0 < 0$ (inverter mode — power flows from DC back to AC). This dual behavior is what makes the fully-controlled converter suitable for regenerative braking.

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed-Torque Characteristic of a Chopper-Fed DC Motor

**Given:** Chopper-fed separately excited DC motor. $V_0 = \delta V_s$, $R_a$ = armature resistance, $\Phi$ = constant flux.

**Step 1 — Write the armature loop equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law).**

$$V_0 = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back-EMF in terms of speed.**

$$V_0 = k \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for armature current $I_a$.**

$$I_a = \frac{V_0 - k \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Write the torque equation. Mechanical torque = electrical torque developed.**

$$T = k \Phi I_a = \frac{k \Phi (V_0 - k \Phi \omega)}{R_a}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the standard $\omega = f(T)$ form.**

$$k \Phi \omega = V_0 - \frac{T R_a}{k \Phi}$$

$$\omega = \frac{V_0}{k \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k \Phi)^2} T$$

**Step 6 — Identify the no-load speed and slope.**

$$\boxed{\omega = \omega_0 - m \cdot T \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_0 = \frac{V_0}{k \Phi} \quad \text{and} \quad m = \frac{R_a}{(k \Phi)^2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The no-load speed $\omega_0$ varies linearly with $V_0$ (i.e., with duty cycle $\delta$). The slope $m$ is independent of $\delta$ — only the y-intercept changes! This produces a **family of parallel straight lines** on the $\omega$–$T$ plane, one for each value of $\delta$. This is the **key feature** of armature voltage control.

## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Recovery During Regenerative Braking

**Given:** A chopper-fed DC motor running at rated speed $\omega$ is commanded to brake. Let motor inertia + load kinetic energy be $E_k = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2$.

**Step 1 — Energy balance during braking.** If braking is purely regenerative (no mechanical brake), the kinetic energy is converted to electrical energy:

$$E_{electrical} = E_b \cdot I_{regen} \cdot t_{brake}$$

**Step 2 — Power flow direction.** During regeneration, $E_b > V_0$ (back-EMF exceeds the applied voltage), so the armature current reverses:

$$I_a = \frac{V_0 - E_b}{R_a} < 0 \quad (\text{since } E_b > V_0)$$

**Step 3 — Power delivered back to source.**

$$P_{regen} = E_b \cdot \vert I_a \vert = \frac{E_b (E_b - V_0)}{R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting chopper average voltage $V_0 = \delta V_s$:**

$$P_{regen} = \frac{E_b^2 - E_b \delta V_s}{R_a}$$

**Step 5 — Net energy recovered to source** (less resistive losses in $R_a$):

$$E_{recovered} = \int_0^{t_{brake}} P_{regen} \, dt - I_a^2 R_a \cdot t_{brake}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Implication:** In EV applications, regenerative braking can recover **15–30%** of the total energy used, dramatically extending range. However, **at very low speeds**, $E_b$ becomes small, and regenerative braking fails — mechanical friction brakes must take over.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Control Methods

| Parameter | Armature Voltage Control | Field Flux Control |
|-----------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **Speed range** | Below rated speed (0 to N_rated) | Above rated speed (N_rated to 2N_rated) |
| **Constant torque region** | Yes | No (constant power region) |
| **Maximum torque** | Rated torque (full) | Reduced as flux weakens |
| **Power loss in control** | Low (chopper/converter loss < 5%) | Very low (field loss is small) |
| **Response speed** | Fast (electrical time constant) | Slow (field winding time constant) |
| **Combined use** | Cascade: $\Phi$ constant below base; $\Phi$ weakened above base | — |
| **Converter/Chopper used** | Phase-controlled rectifier or DC chopper | AC-AC converter or DC-DC chopper on field |
| **Suitability for constant-torque loads** | Excellent | Poor |
| **Suitability for fan/pump loads** | Limited (overkill) | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** "Compare armature voltage control and field control of DC motor." This analytical matrix is your ready-reckoner for that question.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram of a DC Electric Drive

This block diagram illustrates the complete control flow in a modern DC drive — from command input to mechanical output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refSig[Reference Speed Nref] --> compA[Comparator]
    fbSig[Actual Speed Nact from Tachogenerator] --> compA
    compA --> errSig[Error Signal e = Nref - Nact]
    errSig --> ctrlBlock[Controller PI or PID]
    ctrlBlock --> ctrlOut[Control Signal uc]
    ctrlOut --> powerMod[Power Module Chopper or Converter]
    dcBus[DC Source Vs] --> powerMod
    powerMod --> motorBlock[DC Motor Armature and Field]
    motorBlock --> shaftOut[Mechanical Shaft Output T and omega]
    motorBlock -. Back EMF .-> powerMod
    shaftOut -. Coupled .-> loadMech[Load]
    motorBlock -. Speed Feedback .-> fbSig
    motorBlock -. Current Feedback .-> iFb[Current Sensor]
    iFb --> ctrlBlock
```

## 4.2 Four-Quadrant Chopper — H-Bridge Topology

This is the circuit-level functional architecture of a Class E (four-quadrant) chopper used for reversible DC drives.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vsTop[(Positive DC Bus Vs+)]
    vsBot[(Negative DC Bus Vs-)]
    vsTop --- t1[T1 IGBT]
    t1 --- t2[T2 IGBT]
    t2 --- vsBot
    vsTop --- t3[T3 IGBT]
    t3 --- t4[T4 IGBT]
    t4 --- vsBot
    t1 --- d1{D1 Diode}
    d1 --- t2
    t3 --- d3{D3 Diode}
    d3 --- t4
    motorNode((Armature A B Terminals)) --- t1
    motorNode --- t3
    motorNode --- t2
    motorNode --- t4
    q1mode[Q1 Forward Motoring] -. T1 T2 ON .-> t1
    q2mode[Q2 Forward Braking] -. D1 D2 Conduct .-> d1
    q3mode[Q3 Reverse Motoring] -. T3 T4 ON .-> t3
    q4mode[Q4 Reverse Braking] -. D3 D4 Conduct .-> d3
```

## 4.3 Single-Phase Fully Controlled Converter Drive

This flowchart maps the dual-mode (rectifier/inverter) operation of a single-phase full converter feeding a DC motor.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    acSrc[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> transformer[Step Down Transformer]
    transformer --> bridge[Full Bridge Four Thyristors T1 T2 T3 T4]
    ctrl[Gate Pulse Generator Firing Angle alpha] --> bridge
    bridge --> fwd[Average DC Output V0]
    fwd --> decision{Direction of Power Flow}
    decision -->|V0 positive Motoring| q1mode2[Quadrant 1 Forward Motoring]
    decision -->|V0 negative Regeneration| q2mode2[Quadrant 2 Forward Braking]
    q1mode2 --> motor[DC Motor M]
    q2mode2 --> motor
    motor --> loadShaft[Mechanical Load]
    motor -. Back EMF Eb .-> fwd
    alphaVal{Firing Angle Range}
    alphaVal -->|0 to 90 deg| rectMode[Rectifier Mode V0 positive]
    alphaVal -->|90 to 180 deg| invMode[Inverter Mode V0 negative]
    rectMode --> decision
    invMode --> decision
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Speed and Torque Control Architecture

This block diagram represents a typical dual-loop (inner current loop + outer speed loop) control structure used in industrial DC drives.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refS[Reference Speed omegaRef] --> sumA[Summer]
    measS[Tachogenerator omega] --> sumA
    sumA --> errS[Speed Error]

```

## 4.5 Quadrant Operation State Machine

This diagram explains the transition logic between the four operating quadrants based on direction and braking commands.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Q1ForwardMotoring
    Q1ForwardMotoring --> Q1ForwardMotoring: Increase delta
    Q1ForwardMotoring --> Q2ForwardBraking: Reverse Torque Command Regeneration
    Q2ForwardBraking --> Q1ForwardMotoring: Resume Forward Motion
    Q2ForwardBraking --> Q3ReverseMotoring: Reverse Direction Command
    Q3ReverseMotoring --> Q3ReverseMotoring: Increase delta Reverse
    Q3ReverseMotoring --> Q4ReverseBraking: Reverse Torque Command

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Four-Quadrant Chopper Drive and List Its Applications. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A four-quadrant chopper (Class E chopper) is a DC-DC converter topology that uses four controlled switches (typically IGBTs) and four freewheeling diodes arranged in an H-bridge configuration. It allows power flow in *both* directions between the source and the DC motor, enabling operation in all four quadrants of the speed-torque plane:

- **Q1 (Forward Motoring):** Switches $T_1$ and $T_2$ are chopped; the motor receives positive voltage and develops positive torque.
- **Q2 (Forward Braking/Regeneration):** Diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ conduct, returning energy to the source.
- **Q3 (Reverse Motoring):** Switches $T_3$ and $T_4$ are chopped, applying negative voltage.
- **Q4 (Reverse Braking/Regeneration):** Diodes $D_3$ and $D_4$ conduct during reverse braking.

**Applications:** Rolling mills, paper mills, CNC machine tools, electric vehicles, robotic arms, hoists, and any reversible high-performance industrial drive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that $T_1$–$T_4$ and $T_3$–$T_2$ diagonal pairs should *never* be turned ON simultaneously — this causes a *shoot-through* fault. (1 mark lost if not mentioned.)

---

### Question 2: What is Regenerative Braking? How is it Achieved in a Chopper-Controlled DC Drive? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Regenerative braking is a braking technique in which the kinetic energy of the rotating motor and load is *reconverted into electrical energy* and fed back to the source, rather than being dissipated as heat in resistive brakes.

**Mechanism in Chopper-Controlled DC Drive:** When the motor runs at speed $\omega$, its back-EMF is $E_b = k\Phi\omega$. To enter regenerative braking mode, the *applied* armature voltage $V_0$ is reduced *below* $E_b$. This reverses the direction of armature current:

$$I_a = \frac{V_0 - E_b}{R_a} < 0 \quad \text{since } E_b > V_0$$

The negative $I_a$ produces a negative torque (opposing motion), decelerating the motor. The product $E_b \cdot (-I_a) > 0$ means power flows *back to the source*, charging the supply. In a four-quadrant chopper, switching to the *regen diagonal pair* (e.g., $D_1$-$D_2$ in forward braking) enables this energy recovery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are deducted when students say "the chopper turns off to brake." The correct concept is that the chopper continues switching, but $V_0$ is *reduced* (via reduced $\delta$) below $E_b$. Don't confuse regenerative braking with *dynamic braking* (resistive dissipation).

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a): Explain the Operation of a Two-Quadrant (Class C) Chopper with a Circuit Diagram. Derive Expressions for Average Output Voltage in Both Quadrants. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A Class C two-quadant chopper consists of two controlled switches $T_1$, $T_2$ and two diodes $D_1$, $D_2$ connected across a DC source $V_s$ with the motor load $M$ in the middle.

**Step 1 — Quadrant 1 Operation (Forward Motoring):**

Switch $T_1$ is chopped (PWM with duty cycle $\delta = T_{on}/T$), and $T_2$ is kept OFF.

- **When $T_1$ is ON:** Source $V_s$ connects to the motor through $T_1$ and $D_2$ (which is forward-biased during $T_1$ ON state). Output voltage $V_0 = +V_s$.
- **When $T_1$ is OFF:** Motor current freewheels through $D_2$ and the closed loop. Output voltage $V_0 = 0$.

**Average Voltage in Q1:**

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt = \frac{T_{on}}{T} V_s = \delta V_s$$

$$V_0 = +\delta V_s, \quad 0 \leq \delta \leq 1$$

**Step 2 — Quadrant 2 Operation (Forward Braking/Regeneration):**

Switch $T_2$ is chopped, and $T_1$ is OFF. The motor's back-EMF $E_b$ exceeds the source voltage.

- **When $T_2$ is ON:** Motor current circulates through $T_2$ and $D_1$ in a short loop, building up energy. Output $V_0 = 0$.
- **When $T_2$ is OFF:** Motor current is forced through $D_1$ back to the source via the path: motor → $D_1$ → source → motor. Output $V_0 = -V_s$ (negative, since the source is being charged).

**Average Voltage in Q2:**

The OFF-time of $T_2$ is $T_{off} = T - T_{on} = (1-\delta)T$. The negative voltage is applied for this duration:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{T} \int_{T_{on}}^{T} (-V_s) \, dt = -\frac{T - T_{on}}{T} V_s = -(1-\delta) V_s$$

$$V_0 = -(1 - \delta) V_s, \quad 0 \leq \delta \leq 1$$

**Step 3 — Combined Transfer Characteristic:**

$$V_0 = \begin{cases} +\delta V_s & \text{(Q1 motoring)} \\ -(1-\delta) V_s & \text{(Q2 regenerative braking)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Circuit Diagram Description (use block-level schematic):**

```
   +Vs ----+---- T1 ----+---- Motor (+) ----+
            |            |                   |
            +---- D2 ----+                   |
            |                                |
            |            +---- Motor (-) ----+
            +---- T2 ----+                   
            |            |                   
            +---- D1 ----+                   
   -Vs ----+----+-------+----+--------------+
```

**Step 5 — Key Observation:** As $\delta$ increases from 0 to 1, $V_0$ smoothly varies from $-V_s$ (max negative) through $0$ to $+V_s$ (max positive). This makes the Class C chopper ideal for **forward motoring + forward braking** without changing the current direction (since $I_a$ is always positive in the motor armature).

---

#### Part (b): A 220 V, 1500 rpm, 50 A DC Shunt Motor Has Armature Resistance of 0.5 Ω. It is Fed from a Single-Phase Fully Controlled Converter with AC Source of 230 V, 50 Hz. Compute: (i) Firing Angle for Rated Motor Operation. (ii) Firing Angle for Operating Motor at 1000 rpm with Torque Equal to Half-Rated. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V_{rated} = 220$ V (motor rated armature voltage)
- $N_{rated} = 1500$ rpm
- $I_{a,rated} = 50$ A
- $R_a = 0.5 \ \Omega$
- $V_s = 230$ V (RMS AC source)
- $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 = 325.27$ V

**Step 1 — Compute Back-EMF Constant at Rated Condition:**

$$E_{b,rated} = V_{rated} - I_{a,rated} R_a = 220 - (50)(0.5) = 220 - 25 = 195 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Firing Angle for Rated Operation:**

For rated operation, the converter must supply $V_0 = 220$ V:

$$V_0 = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha_{rated}$$

$$\cos \alpha_{rated} = \frac{V_0 \pi}{2 V_m} = \frac{220 \times \pi}{2 \times 325.27} = \frac{691.15}{650.54} = 1.062$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Realization:** The computed $\cos \alpha > 1$, which is *physically impossible*. This indicates that the source voltage of 230 V is *insufficient* to operate the motor at 220 V rated voltage due to converter's natural voltage drop. The maximum possible $V_0$ is at $\alpha = 0$:

$$V_{0,max} = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} = 207.07 \text{ V}$$

This is less than 220 V, so the motor cannot be operated at rated 220 V from this 230 V supply. **The problem is best solved by taking the converter's maximum output as the reference.**

**Adjusted Interpretation:** The motor must be operated at the maximum achievable voltage. Let us assume the problem expects the use of an *ideal* converter and treats the AC supply as 230 V RMS, and the rated motor voltage is 220 V — meaning a small voltage drop is acceptable. So we proceed with:

$$\cos \alpha_{rated} = \frac{220 \pi}{2 \times 325.27} \approx 1.062$$

Since this is infeasible, the **practical** rated firing angle is $\alpha = 0°$, giving $V_0 = 207$ V (motor will run at slightly below rated speed).

**Reinterpreting the Problem (More Realistic):** If we use $V_s = 220$ V (matching the motor rating) to be self-consistent:

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 220 = 311.13 \text{ V}$$

$$\cos \alpha_{rated} = \frac{220 \pi}{2 \times 311.13} = \frac{691.15}{622.26} = 1.11$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Re-reinterpretation:** This problem is likely intended to ignore armature resistance drop (i.e., approximate $E_b \approx V_a$) for the firing angle calculation, OR use a higher AC source voltage. Let us solve using the standard textbook approach: **take $V_s = 230$ V and assume the motor is operated at 220 V through ideal converter (no $R_a$ drop considered in V0 calculation).** Many textbooks ignore $R_a$ drop when computing $\alpha$. We follow that convention.

**Assuming $E_b = V_0$ (ignoring small $R_a$ drop):**

$$\cos \alpha_{rated} = \frac{195 \times \pi}{2 \times 325.27} = \frac{612.6}{650.54} = 0.9417$$

$$\alpha_{rated} = \cos^{-1}(0.9417) = 19.6° \approx 19.6°$$

**Step 3 — Compute Motor Constant $k\Phi$:**

$$E_b = k \Phi \omega \Rightarrow k \Phi = \frac{E_{b,rated}}{\omega_{rated}} = \frac{195}{2 \pi \times 1500/60} = \frac{195}{157.08} = 1.241 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

**Step 4 — New Operating Condition: $N_2 = 1000$ rpm, $T_2 = 0.5 \times T_{rated}$**

Since torque $T = k\Phi I_a$, and flux is constant (assuming field current unchanged):

$$I_{a,2} = 0.5 \times I_{a,rated} = 0.5 \times 50 = 25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Compute New Back-EMF at 1000 rpm:**

$$E_{b,2} = k \Phi \cdot \omega_2 = 1.241 \times \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 1.241 \times 104.72 = 129.95 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Required Armature Voltage:**

$$V_{0,2} = E_{b,2} + I_{a,2} R_a = 129.95 + 25 \times 0.5 = 129.95 + 12.5 = 142.45 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Firing Angle for New Condition:**

$$\cos \alpha_2 = \frac{V_{0,2} \pi}{2 V_m} = \frac{142.45 \times \pi}{2 \times 325.27} = \frac{447.46}{650.54} = 0.6878$$

$$\alpha_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.6878) = 46.55° \approx 46.6°$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** $\alpha_{rated} \approx 19.6°$ (or $\alpha = 0°$ if exact voltage matching is required)
- **(ii)** $\alpha_2 \approx 46.6°$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** in $E_b$ calculation (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Using $V_{rms}$ instead of $V_m$** in the formula (lose 2 marks — critical error).
> 3. **Ignoring $I_a R_a$ drop** in the new operating condition (lose 1 mark).
> 4. **Forgetting to scale $I_a$ for half torque** (lose 1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

#### Part (a): Explain the Working of a Four-Quadrant Chopper Drive for a DC Motor. Discuss Its Operation in All Four Quadrants with Circuit Description. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A four-quadrant chopper (Class E chopper) consists of **four IGBTs** ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$) and **four freewheeling diodes** ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$) arranged in an H-bridge configuration across a DC source $V_s$. The DC motor is connected between the two midpoints (A and B) of the bridge arms. This topology allows the motor to operate as a motor or as a generator, in either rotational direction — covering all four quadrants of the $\omega$–$T$ plane.

**Quadrant I — Forward Motoring:**

- Switches $T_1$ and $T_2$ are **chopped together** with duty cycle $\delta$.
- $T_3$ and $T_4$ are kept OFF.
- During $T_1, T_2$ ON: Current flows from $+V_s \to T_1 \to \text{Motor}(A \to B) \to T_2 \to -V_s$. Output $V_{AB} = +V_s$.
- During $T_1, T_2$ OFF: Motor current freewheels through $D_3$ and $D_4$ (or through $D_3, D_4$ and source). Output $V_{AB} = 0$.

$$V_0 = +\delta V_s, \quad 0 \leq \delta \leq 1$$

Positive voltage and positive current produce positive torque and forward motion. ✅

**Quadrant II — Forward Braking (Regenerative):**

- $T_1$ and $T_2$ are kept OFF.
- $T_3$ and $T_4$ are OFF; motor back-EMF drives current through diodes.
- Diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ conduct: Current flows from Motor (B → A) → $D_1$ → $+V_s$ bus → $-V_s$ bus → $D_2$ → Motor.
- Output $V_{AB} = -V_s$ (negative).
- Motor current is positive; voltage is negative ⇒ negative torque (braking) and power flows back to source.

$$V_0 = -V_s, \quad \text{with } \omega > 0, T < 0$$

✅ Regenerative braking while moving forward.

**Quadrant III — Reverse Motoring:**

- Switches $T_3$ and $T_4$ are **chopped together** with duty cycle $\delta'$.
- $T_1$ and $T_2$ are kept OFF.
- During $T_3, T_4$ ON: Current flows from $+V_s \to T_3 \to \text{Motor}(B \to A) \to T_4 \to -V_s$. Output $V_{AB} = -V_s$.
- During $T_3, T_4$ OFF: Motor current freewheels through $D_1$ and $D_2$.

$$V_0 = -\delta' V_s, \quad 0 \leq \delta' \leq 1$$

Negative voltage and negative current produce negative torque and reverse motion. ✅

**Quadrant IV — Reverse Braking (Regenerative):**

- $T_3$ and $T_4$ are OFF.
- Diodes $D_3$ and $D_4$ conduct: Current flows from Motor (A → B) → $D_3$ → $+V_s$ bus → $-V_s$ bus → $D_4$ → Motor.
- Output $V_{AB} = +V_s$ (positive).
- Motor current is negative; voltage is positive ⇒ positive torque (braking in reverse direction).

$$V_0 = +V_s, \quad \text{with } \omega < 0, T > 0$$

✅ Regenerative braking while moving in reverse.

**Summary Table:**

| Quadrant | Active Devices | $V_0$ | $\omega$ | $T$ | Mode |
|----------|---------------|-------|----------|-----|------|
| I | $T_1, T_2$ (chopped) | $+\delta V_s$ | + | + | Forward Motoring |
| II | $D_1, D_2$ | $-V_s$ | + | – | Forward Braking |
| III | $T_3, T_4$ (chopped) | $-\delta V_s$ | – | – | Reverse Motoring |
| IV | $D_3, D_4$ | $+V_s$ | – | + | Reverse Braking |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Critical:** Marks are deducted for:
> 1. **Shoot-through risk:** Students must mention that diagonal pairs (T1-T4 or T2-T3) should never conduct simultaneously — this shorts the source.
> 2. **Freewheeling path confusion:** In Q1, the freewheeling current flows through D3-D4 (not D1-D2) during the OFF period.
> 3. **Current direction sign:** Many students confuse the sign of $I_a$ in different quadrants.

---

#### Part (b): A 400 V DC Series Motor Has Armature Resistance of 0.3 Ω and Field Resistance of 0.2 Ω. It Draws 80 A at 1200 rpm. The Motor is Fed by a Chopper from a 500 V DC Source. Find: (i) Duty Cycle for Rated Operation. (ii) New Speed for Half the Duty Cycle at Same Load Torque. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V_{rated} = 400$ V (motor rated terminal voltage)
- $R_a = 0.3 \ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.2 \ \Omega$
- $I_{a,rated} = 80$ A
- $N_{rated} = 1200$ rpm
- Source $V_s = 500$ V (chopper input)

**Step 1 — Total Series Resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_a + R_{se} = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5 \ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Verify Rated Operation Parameters:**

For a series motor, the total circuit current is the armature current. At rated condition:

$$E_{b,rated} = V_{rated} - I_{a,rated} \cdot R_{total} = 400 - 80 \times 0.5 = 400 - 40 = 360 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Duty Cycle for Rated Operation:**

The chopper's average output voltage must equal the motor's rated voltage (400 V). Chopper output:

$$V_0 = \delta \cdot V_s \Rightarrow \delta_1 = \frac{V_0}{V_s} = \frac{400}{500} = 0.8$$

$$\boxed{\delta_1 = 0.8 \text{ (or 80%)}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Motor Constant $k \cdot \Phi_{rated}$:**

For a series motor, $\Phi$ is proportional to $I_a$ (below saturation): $\Phi = k_f \cdot I_a$. So $k \cdot \Phi = k \cdot k_f \cdot I_a = c \cdot I_a$, where $c$ is a constant. Hence:

$$E_b = (c \cdot I_a) \cdot \omega$$

At rated condition:

$$c = \frac{E_{b,rated}}{I_{a,rated} \cdot \omega_{rated}} = \frac{360}{80 \times (2\pi \times 1200/60)} = \frac{360}{80 \times 125.66} = \frac{360}{10053} = 0.03581 \text{ V·s/(A·rad)}$$

**Step 5 — Half Duty Cycle Condition: $\delta_2 = 0.5 \times \delta_1 = 0.4$**

New chopper output voltage:

$$V_{0,2} = \delta_2 \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 500 = 200 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Same Load Torque Implication:**

For a series motor, $T = k \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = (k \cdot k_f \cdot I_a) \cdot I_a = c \cdot I_a^2$.

If $T_2 = T_1$ (same load torque), then:

$$c \cdot I_{a,2}^2 = c \cdot I_{a,1}^2 \Rightarrow I_{a,2} = I_{a,1} = 80 \text{ A}$$

So the current remains 80 A.

**Step 7 — New Back-EMF:**

$$E_{b,2} = V_{0,2} - I_{a,2} \cdot R_{total} = 200 - 80 \times 0.5 = 200 - 40 = 160 \text{ V}$$

**Step 8 — New Speed:**

Since $E_b = c \cdot I_a \cdot \omega$ and $c$ and $I_a$ are the same:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{E_{b,2}}{E_{b,1}} = \frac{160}{360} = 0.4444$$

$$N_2 = 0.4444 \times 1200 = 533.33 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_2 \approx 533 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i)** $\delta_1 = 0.8$ (80% duty cycle)
- **(ii)** $N_2 \approx 533$ rpm

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Deduction Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $R_{se}$ to $R_a$** in series motor — students often use only $R_a$, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Incorrectly assuming $\Phi$ is constant** for a series motor — flux is proportional to $I_a$ (below saturation). Marks deducted for ignoring this.
> 3. **Misinterpreting "same load torque"** — students sometimes reduce $I_a$, which violates the torque equation. The correct inference is $I_a$ stays the same.
> 4. **Missing unit conversion** for $\omega$ in rad/s.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DC Motor Speed Equation:** $N = (V_a - I_a R_a)/(k\Phi)$ — the master equation for all DC drive analysis.
- **Three Speed Control Methods:** (1) Armature voltage control (below rated), (2) Field flux control (above rated), (3) Combined (constant-torque + constant-power regions).
- **Class A Chopper:** First quadrant only; $V_0 = \delta V_s$.
- **Class C (Two-Quadrant) Chopper Type A:** Forward motoring ($V_0 = +\delta V_s$) + Forward regenerative braking ($V_0 = -(1-\delta)V_s$).
- **Class E (Four-Quadrant) Chopper:** Four IGBTs + four diodes in H-bridge; diagonal pairs must never conduct simultaneously.
- **Single-Phase Full Converter Average Voltage:** $V_0 = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ — for $0 \leq \alpha \leq 90°$ → rectification; for $90° \leq \alpha \leq 180°$ → inversion.
- **Regenerative Braking Condition:** $E_b > V_0$ (back-EMF exceeds applied voltage), causing $I_a$ to reverse.
- **Quadrant Identification:** Q1 (+ω, +T) = forward motoring; Q2 (+ω, –T) = forward braking; Q3 (–ω, –T) = reverse motoring; Q4 (–ω, +T) = reverse braking.
- **Power Flow Direction:** Source → Motor during motoring; Motor → Source during regeneration.
- **Speed-Torque Curve:** Under armature voltage control, curves are *parallel straight lines* with slope $m = R_a/(k\Phi)^2$ and intercept $\omega_0 = V_0/(k\Phi)$.
- **Closed-Loop Control:** Dual-loop (outer speed + inner current) is the industry standard; uses PI controllers; current loop is faster and provides inherent overcurrent protection.
- **Converter vs Chopper:** Converter = line-frequency AC-DC; chopper = high-frequency DC-DC. Choppers offer smoother control and are essential for battery-fed systems.
- **Inverter Mode of Converter:** When $\alpha > 90°$, $V_0 < 0$; power flows from motor to source. This is *line commutation* — natural commutation by AC mains — and is different from the forced-commutated inverters covered in Module 4.
- **Safety Recall:** Always ensure extinction angle $\beta \geq 30°$ in inverter mode to avoid commutation failure (for converter-fed drives).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 1-Phase Full-Bridge Voltage Source Inverters with R and RL Loads, and 3-Phase Bridge Inverter with R Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 1-Phase Full-Bridge Voltage Source Inverters with R and RL Loads, and 3-Phase Bridge Inverter with R Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) – Formal Definition

A **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a **fixed DC input voltage** into a **variable-magnitude, variable-frequency AC output voltage** suitable for feeding AC loads such as induction motors, synchronous machines, and transformers. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context for *Electric Drives and Control* (PCRUT503), the VSI is the front-end "DC-to-AC" block of modern variable-frequency drives (VFDs), feeding the stator of AC machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The focus is on the **square-wave / six-step** operation of single-phase full-bridge and three-phase bridge inverters, *not* on PWM modulation (PWM is covered later in Module 4 of PCRUT503). Students must master the 180° and 120° conduction modes and the associated harmonic structure.

## 1.2 1-Phase Full-Bridge Inverter – Topology and Intuition

A **1-Phase Full-Bridge VSI** (also called an **H-Bridge**) consists of **four controlled switches S1, S2, S3, S4** (typically IGBTs/MOSFETs) arranged in two legs, with **four anti-parallel freewheeling diodes D1–D4** to handle inductive load currents.

**Circuit Configuration (Two Legs):**
- **Leg-A:** S1 (top) and S3 (bottom) connect the load terminal "a" to +Vdc and –Vdc respectively.
- **Leg-B:** S2 (top) and S4 (bottom) connect the load terminal "b" to +Vdc and –Vdc respectively.
- Load is connected between terminals **a** and **b**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The "See-Saw" Switch:** Imagine a drawbridge in a medieval castle. Two winches (S1–S2 and S3–S4) alternately pull the bridge up and down across the moat. When one winch pair is active, the load sees +Vdc; when the other pair is active, it sees –Vdc. The bridge (output voltage) is *flipped* periodically, producing an AC waveform from a DC source.

**Switching Sequence (180° Conduction Mode):**
| Interval (ωt) | Switches ON | Load Voltage Vab |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0° – 180° | S1, S2 | +Vdc |
| 180° – 360° | S3, S4 | –Vdc |
| (0° and 180° are commutation instants — S1,S2 turn OFF exactly when S3,S4 turn ON) | | |

**Operation with Pure R-Load:**
- The current waveform is **identical in shape to the voltage waveform** (square wave), since $i = V_{ab}/R$.
- Peak current: $I_{peak} = V_{dc}/R$
- RMS current: $I_{rms} = V_{dc}/R$

**Operation with RL-Load:**
- The current **cannot change instantaneously** through an inductor, so it follows an **exponential rise–decay** profile during the +Vdc and –Vdc intervals.
- Steady-state current waveform is a **quasi-square wave** with an **average (DC) value of zero** over a cycle.
- The load current is given by:

$$i_o(t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + I_{min}\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

where the time constant $\tau = L/R$ and $I_{min}$ is the current at the start of the interval (which is negative during the +Vdc interval and positive during the –Vdc interval in steady state).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Need for Freewheeling Diodes:** When S1, S2 turn OFF and S3, S4 turn ON, the inductor tries to maintain current flow. The diodes D3, D4 provide a **freewheeling path** to dissipate the stored inductive energy, *and* permit negative current flow during parts of the cycle. Without these diodes, the switches would be destroyed by the inductive kick.

## 1.3 3-Phase Bridge Inverter – Topology and Intuition

A **3-Phase Voltage Source Inverter** consists of **six controlled switches S1–S6** (with anti-parallel diodes D1–D6) arranged as **three legs**, each leg feeding one phase of the load. The DC bus is a constant-voltage source (large capacitor + diode rectifier from mains).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The "Three-Phase Orchestra":** Picture three H-bridges fused together at the DC bus. Each leg is a violinist playing a 120° phase-shifted melody. When one violin plays its note, the others are silent — but their harmonics interlock to create a *rotating magnetic field* inside an AC motor. The 3-phase bridge is the "conductor" coordinating all three.

**Standard Operating Modes in Module 3:**
- **180° Conduction Mode:** Each switch conducts for 180° of a cycle; three switches (two upper, one lower, or vice versa) conduct at any instant.
- **120° Conduction Mode:** Each switch conducts for only 120°; only two switches conduct at any instant.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus specifically demands analysis **with R-load only** for the 3-phase bridge inverter. The RL-load case (with motor back-EMF) is a higher-syllabus topic covered in Power Electronics-II or Drives electives.

---

## 1.4 Summary of Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- DC input voltage: **Vdc**
- 1-Phase full-bridge output (square-wave): peak = **Vdc**, RMS = **Vdc**
- Fundamental peak of square wave: **4·Vdc/π ≈ 1.273·Vdc**
- 3-Phase 180° mode – line voltage fundamental peak: **(2√3/π)·Vdc ≈ 1.103·Vdc**
- 3-Phase 180° mode – phase voltage fundamental peak: **(2/π)·Vdc ≈ 0.637·Vdc**
- Conduction angle: **180°** (default square-wave operation)
- Six-step waveform: 3-Phase line voltage has 6 steps per cycle
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis – 1-Phase Full-Bridge VSI with R-Load

### Step 1: Define the Output Voltage
The instantaneous output voltage is a **bipolar square wave**:

$$V_{ab}(\omega t) = \begin{cases} +V_{dc}, & 0 \le \omega t < \pi \\ -V_{dc}, & \pi \le \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Why RMS = Vdc
Since the magnitude is constant (Vdc) over the full cycle, the RMS equals the peak:

$$V_{ab,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_0^{\pi} V_{dc}^2\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} V_{dc}^2\, d(\omega t)\right]} = V_{dc}$$

### Step 3: Fourier Series – The Heart of Harmonic Analysis
The square wave is an **odd function** (anti-symmetric about t = 0), so it contains **only sine terms**, and the average is zero. The Fourier series is:

$$V_{ab}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin(n\omega t)$$

- **No DC component** (zero average).
- **No even harmonics** (odd symmetry).
- **All odd harmonics present**: n = 1, 3, 5, 7, …
- The amplitude of the $n$-th harmonic is $4V_{dc}/(n\pi)$, which decreases as $1/n$.

### Step 4: Fundamental Component
For n = 1, peak of fundamental = $4 V_{dc}/\pi$, and RMS of fundamental = $4V_{dc}/(\pi\sqrt{2}) = 2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}/\pi$.

### Step 5: Real-World Utility
This square-wave inverter is the basic building block of **uninterruptible power supplies (UPS)**, **induction heating**, and **low-cost AC drives** where harmonic filtering or PWM is unnecessary. The strong 3rd, 5th, 7th harmonics cause torque pulsations in motors and overheating in transformers — hence the move to PWM in modern drives.

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis – 1-Phase Full-Bridge VSI with RL-Load

### The Current Equation
When S1, S2 are ON, the circuit equation is:

$$V_{dc} = R\cdot i_o(t) + L\cdot \frac{di_o(t)}{dt}$$

Solution (for $0 \le \omega t \le \pi$):

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\left(1 - e^{-\omega t/(\omega \tau)}\right) + I_{min}\,e^{-\omega t/(\omega \tau)}$$

where $\tau = L/R$ and $\omega \tau = \omega L / R$ is the **normalized load time constant** (dimensionless, in radians).

In steady state, due to symmetry, $I_{max} = -I_{min}$. The peak current is:

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\cdot\frac{1 - e^{-\pi/(\omega \tau)}}{1 + e^{-\pi/(\omega \tau)}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\tanh\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2\omega \tau}\right)$$

### Switching Instant (Commutation)
At $\omega t = \pi$, S1, S2 turn OFF and S3, S4 turn ON. The current $I_{max}$ (positive) is forced through D3, D4 — applying –Vdc to the load. The current then decays exponentially with the **same time constant**:

$$i_o(\omega t) = -\frac{V_{dc}}{R}\left(1 - e^{-(\omega t - \pi)/(\omega \tau)}\right) + I_{max}\,e^{-(\omega t - \pi)/(\omega \tau)}$$

This continues until $\omega t = 2\pi$, when the cycle repeats with $i_o = -I_{max}$.

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis – 3-Phase Bridge Inverter (180° Conduction, R-Load)

### Switching Sequence
| Switch | Conduction Interval |
|:---:|:---:|
| S1 | 0° – 180° |
| S2 | 60° – 240° |
| S3 | 120° – 300° |
| S4 | 180° – 360° |
| S5 | 240° – 60° (next cycle) |
| S6 | 300° – 180° (next cycle) |

At any instant, **three switches are ON** (one from each leg) — but in any leg, both upper and lower cannot be ON simultaneously (shoot-through is forbidden).

### Phase Voltage Waveform (Van)
For 180° conduction, the phase voltage measured at terminal 'a' with respect to the **fictitious DC-link mid-point** is a stepped waveform with values $\pm V_{dc}/2$. Without a neutral wire, the line-to-neutral voltage at the actual load is reconstructed using the relationship $V_{an} = (2/3)V_{ao} - (1/3)V_{bo} - (1/3)V_{co}$.

The **line voltage Vab = Van – Vbn** is a **six-step waveform** (a quasi-square wave with one extra step per half-cycle).

### Line Voltage Fourier Series
The line-to-line voltage $V_{ab}$ in 180° mode has the Fourier series:

$$V_{ab}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi}\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{6}\right)\sin\!\left(n\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right)$$

Eliminating harmonics that are multiples of 3 (since $\cos(n\pi/6) = 0$ for n = 3, 9, 15, …), the surviving harmonics are n = 1, 5, 7, 11, 13, … — *triplen harmonics cancel in the line voltage*.

### Phase Voltage Fourier Series
$$V_{an}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{2 V_{dc}}{n\pi}\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{6}\right)\sin\!\left(n\omega t\right)$$

The phase voltage **does contain triplen harmonics** (3rd, 9th, 15th, …), but they cancel in a balanced 3-phase system because they are co-phasal in all three phases and circulate only in the delta (or through the neutral if present).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** list for the KTU End-Semester Examination. Critical: `|` symbols inside formulas have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown breakage.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | 1-Phase VSI output peak voltage | $V_{o,peak} = V_{dc}$ | V | Square wave |
| 2 | 1-Phase VSI output RMS voltage | $V_{o,rms} = V_{dc}$ | V | Square wave |
| 3 | 1-Phase fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{o1} = 4V_{dc}/\pi$ | V | n = 1 |
| 4 | 1-Phase fundamental RMS | $V_{o1,rms} = 2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}/\pi$ | V | n = 1 |
| 5 | 1-Phase n-th harmonic peak | $\hat{V}_{on} = 4V_{dc}/(n\pi)$ | V | n odd only |
| 6 | 1-Phase total harmonic distortion | $THD = \sqrt{(\pi^2/8) - 1} \approx 0.483$ | – | 48.3 % for square wave |
| 7 | 3-Phase line voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{ab1} = (2\sqrt{3}/\pi)\,V_{dc}$ | V | 180° mode |
| 8 | 3-Phase line voltage fundamental RMS | $V_{ab1,rms} = \sqrt{6}\,V_{dc}/\pi$ | V | 180° mode |
| 9 | 3-Phase phase voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{an1} = (2/\pi)\,V_{dc}$ | V | 180° mode |
| 10 | 3-Phase phase voltage fundamental RMS | $V_{an1,rms} = \sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}/\pi$ | V | 180° mode |
| 11 | 3-Phase n-th harmonic line voltage peak | $\hat{V}_{abn} = (4V_{dc}/(n\pi))\cos(n\pi/6)$ | V | n odd, not mult. of 3 |
| 12 | 3-Phase RL-load peak current (1-Ph) | $I_{max} = (V_{dc}/R)\tanh(\pi/(2\omega\tau))$ | A | $\tau = L/R$ |
| 13 | 3-Phase RMS line voltage (180°) | $V_{ab,rms} = \sqrt{(2/3)}\,V_{dc}$ | V | Square-wave RMS |
| 14 | Switching frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Hz | For 1-Phase; for 3-Phase line output, $f_{out}$ is output frequency |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **1-Phase H-Bridge** is the workhorse of **solar micro-inverters**, **EV auxiliary drives**, and **low-power UPS** (rating 1–10 kW).
- **3-Phase Bridge VSI in 180° mode** is used in **high-power industrial drives** (>100 kW) where the harmonic losses are tolerated and a step-up transformer cleans the output — typical in **renewable energy grid-tie inverters** before LCL filtering.
- The elimination of triplen harmonics in 3-phase line voltage (even with square-wave excitation) is a fundamental reason **3-phase systems dominate power transmission** — they naturally reject 3rd, 9th, 15th harmonics in line quantities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Fourier Series of 1-Phase Square-Wave Output

**Starting Point:** The output voltage is piecewise-constant, odd-symmetric, and quarter-wave symmetric:

$$V_{ab}(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_{dc}, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -V_{dc}, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 1: Compute the DC component $a_0$.** Since the waveform has equal positive and negative halves:

$$a_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} V_{ab}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = 0$$

**Step 2: Compute the cosine coefficients $a_n$.** Odd symmetry means $V_{ab}(-\omega t) = -V_{ab}(\omega t)$, so all $a_n = 0$.

$$a_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} V_{ab}(\omega t)\cos(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = 0$$

**Step 3: Compute the sine coefficients $b_n$.**

$$b_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} V_{ab}(\omega t)\sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

Splitting into the two intervals:

$$b_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\int_0^{\pi} V_{dc}\sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} (-V_{dc})\sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the first integral.**

$$\int_0^{\pi} V_{dc}\sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{n}\left[-\cos(n\omega t)\right]_0^{\pi} = \frac{V_{dc}}{n}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the second integral.**

$$\int_{\pi}^{2\pi} (-V_{dc})\sin(n\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = -\frac{V_{dc}}{n}\left[-\cos(n\omega t)\right]_{\pi}^{2\pi} = \frac{V_{dc}}{n}\left[\cos(2n\pi) - \cos(n\pi)\right]$$

Since $\cos(2n\pi) = 1$:

$$= \frac{V_{dc}}{n}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]$$

**Step 6: Sum the two integrals.**

$$b_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{n}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right] + \frac{1}{\pi}\cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{n}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]$$

**Step 7: Simplify using parity of $\cos(n\pi)$.** 
- For even n: $\cos(n\pi) = +1$, so $b_n = 0$.
- For odd n: $\cos(n\pi) = -1$, so $b_n = \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\cdot 2 = \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi}$.

**Step 8: Final Fourier series.**

$$\boxed{V_{ab}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin(n\omega t)}$$

**Step 9: Fundamental component (n=1).**

$$V_{ab,1}(\omega t) = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}\sin(\omega t)$$

Peak: $\hat{V}_{ab,1} = 4V_{dc}/\pi$. RMS: $V_{ab,1,rms} = 4V_{dc}/(\pi\sqrt{2}) = 2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}/\pi \approx 0.9\,V_{dc}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – RMS Value of 1-Phase Square-Wave Output

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} V_{ab}^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_0^{\pi} V_{dc}^2\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} V_{dc}^2\, d(\omega t)\right]$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[V_{dc}^2 \cdot \pi + V_{dc}^2 \cdot \pi\right] = V_{dc}^2$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = V_{dc}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – THD of 1-Phase Square Wave

The Total Harmonic Distortion is defined as:

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}}$$

**Step 1:** $V_{rms} = V_{dc}$, $V_{1,rms} = 2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}/\pi$.

**Step 2:**

$$V_{rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2 = V_{dc}^2 - \frac{8V_{dc}^2}{\pi^2} = V_{dc}^2\left(1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:**

$$THD = \frac{V_{dc}\sqrt{1 - 8/\pi^2}}{2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{1 - 8/\pi^2}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify. $1 - 8/\pi^2 \approx 1 - 0.8106 = 0.1894$. So $\sqrt{0.1894} \approx 0.4352$.

$$THD \approx \frac{3.1416}{2.828}\cdot 0.4352 \approx 1.111 \cdot 0.4352 \approx 0.4834$$

$$\boxed{THD \approx 48.3\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the "famous" 48.3 % THD of a square wave — a result KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 – Steady-State Peak Current for 1-Phase RL-Load

The differential equation during the positive half-cycle ($0 \le \omega t \le \pi$):

$$L\frac{di_o}{dt} + R\cdot i_o = V_{dc}$$

**Step 1: Homogeneous solution.** $i_h = K\, e^{-Rt/L} = K\, e^{-\omega t/(\omega \tau)}$.

**Step 2: Particular solution.** $i_p = V_{dc}/R$.

**Step 3: General solution.**

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} + K\, e^{-\omega t/(\omega \tau)}$$

**Step 4: Apply initial condition.** At $\omega t = 0$, $i_o = -I_{max}$ (negative, because the previous half-cycle ended with current flowing in the opposite direction):

$$-I_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} + K \implies K = -I_{max} - \frac{V_{dc}}{R}$$

**Step 5: Apply final condition.** At $\omega t = \pi$, $i_o = +I_{max}$:

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} + K\, e^{-\pi/(\omega \tau)} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R} - \left(I_{max} + \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\right) e^{-\pi/(\omega \tau)}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $I_{max}$.** Let $x = e^{-\pi/(\omega \tau)}$.

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}(1 - x) - I_{max}\, x$$

$$I_{max}(1 + x) = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}(1 - x)$$

$$\boxed{I_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\cdot\frac{1 - x}{1 + x} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\tanh\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2\omega \tau}\right)}$$

**Limiting cases:**
- If $L \to 0$ (pure R-load): $\tau \to 0$, $\tanh(\infty) = 1$, so $I_{max} = V_{dc}/R$ ✓
- If $L \to \infty$ (pure L-load): $\tau \to \infty$, $\tanh(0) = 0$, so $I_{max} = 0$ ✓

## 3.5 Derivation 5 – 3-Phase Line Voltage Fundamental (180° Mode)

**Step 1: Van waveform.** With 180° conduction, S1 is ON for 0° to 180°, so $V_{ao} = +V_{dc}/2$ during this interval. The phase voltage with respect to the fictitious mid-point is:

$$V_{ao}(\omega t) = \begin{cases} +V_{dc}/2, & 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\ -V_{dc}/2, & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

This is again a square wave with amplitude $V_{dc}/2$. Its Fourier series is:

$$V_{ao}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin(n\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Similarly for Vbo and Vco**, lagging by 120° and 240°:

$$V_{bo}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin\!\left(n(\omega t - 2\pi/3)\right)$$

$$V_{co}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin\!\left(n(\omega t - 4\pi/3)\right)$$

**Step 3: Line voltage Vab = Vao – Vbo:**

$$V_{ab}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\left[\sin(n\omega t) - \sin\!\left(n(\omega t - 2\pi/3)\right)\right]$$

**Step 4: Use the identity** $\sin A - \sin B = 2\cos\!\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$A = n\omega t, \quad B = n\omega t - 2n\pi/3$$

$$\frac{A+B}{2} = n\omega t - n\pi/3, \quad \frac{A-B}{2} = n\pi/3$$

So:

$$V_{ab}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi}\cos\!\left(n\omega t - n\pi/3\right)\sin\!\left(n\pi/3\right)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate $\sin(n\pi/3)$ for odd n:**
- n=1: $\sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$
- n=3: $\sin(\pi) = 0$ → **3rd harmonic eliminated**
- n=5: $\sin(5\pi/3) = -\sqrt{3}/2$
- n=7: $\sin(7\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$

**Step 6: Fundamental component (n=1):**

$$V_{ab,1}(\omega t) = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\!\left(\omega t - \pi/3\right) = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_{dc}}{\pi}\cos\!\left(\omega t - \pi/3\right)$$

$$\boxed{\hat{V}_{ab,1} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 1.103\,V_{dc}}$$

$$V_{ab,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6}\,V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.7799\,V_{dc}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 – 3-Phase Phase Voltage Fundamental (180° Mode)

**Step 1:** Using the relationship $V_{an} = (2/3)V_{ao} - (1/3)V_{bo} - (1/3)V_{co}$ (valid for balanced 3-phase, no neutral current):

$$V_{an}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[2\sin(n\omega t) - \sin\!\left(n(\omega t - 2\pi/3)\right) - \sin\!\left(n(\omega t - 4\pi/3)\right)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Using sum-to-product identities, the bracketed expression simplifies to $3\sin(n\omega t)$ for $n$ not a multiple of 3, and 0 for $n$ a multiple of 3 (when the result is taken with the fundamental extraction).

For the **fundamental (n=1)**:

$$V_{an,1}(\omega t) = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi}\sin(\omega t)$$

$$\boxed{\hat{V}_{an,1} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.637\,V_{dc}}$$

$$V_{an,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.450\,V_{dc}$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix – 120° vs 180° Conduction Comparison

| Parameter | 180° Conduction | 120° Conduction |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Switches ON at any instant | 3 (one per leg) | 2 (one upper + one lower) |
| Phase voltage waveform | 6-step | Quasi-square (2 levels: 0, ±Vdc/2) |
| Line voltage waveform | 6-step | Quasi-square (2 levels) |
| Phase voltage fundamental | $(2/\pi)\,V_{dc}$ | $(\sqrt{3}/\pi)\,V_{dc}$ |
| Line voltage fundamental | $(2\sqrt{3}/\pi)\,V_{dc}$ | $(3/\pi)\,V_{dc}$ |
| Switch utilization | Higher | Lower |
| Application | General VFDs | High-power, low-current drives |
| Risk of shoot-through | Higher (3 switches at once) | Lower (only 2 switches) |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern, May 2024]**

### Question A1: 3 Marks

**Q:** Define a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) and explain the role of freewheeling diodes in a 1-phase full-bridge VSI supplying an RL-load.

**Model Answer:**

A **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a static power electronic converter that converts a **fixed DC input voltage** (Vdc) into a **variable-magnitude, variable-frequency AC output voltage** using fully controllable switches (IGBTs/MOSFETs/GTOs). The DC-link voltage is held nearly constant by a large capacitor.

**Role of Freewheeling Diodes in 1-Phase Full-Bridge VSI with RL-Load:**

The freewheeling diodes (D1–D4) are connected in **anti-parallel** with each of the four controlled switches. They serve three critical functions:

1. **Bidirectional Current Flow:** The load current through an RL-load becomes **bi-directional** (negative during part of the cycle in steady state). The switches are unidirectional in current carrying — so the diodes carry the **reverse direction** of current.
2. **Inductive Energy Feedback:** When the switches turn OFF, the inductor's stored magnetic energy $L\cdot i^2/2$ is released through the diodes back to the DC source, protecting the switches from voltage spikes.
3. **Decoupling Load from Source During Commutation:** During the brief interval when all switches are OFF (in some PWM strategies) or when the polarity reverses, the diodes provide a path for the load current to circulate, allowing the load voltage to "freewheel" at zero while the current decays naturally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 M):** Definition (1 M) + Three roles of diodes (1.5 M) + Conclusion (0.5 M).

---

### Question A2: 3 Marks

**Q:** For a 3-phase bridge inverter operating in 180° conduction mode with a DC input of Vdc = 400 V, calculate the RMS value of the fundamental component of the line voltage.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** Vdc = 400 V, 180° conduction mode.

**Step 1:** Formula for the peak of the fundamental component of line voltage:

$$\hat{V}_{ab,1} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi}\cdot V_{dc} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi}\cdot 400 \approx 1.103 \cdot 400 \approx 441.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Convert peak to RMS:

$$V_{ab,1,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{ab,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{441.3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 312.1 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Or directly using:

$$V_{ab,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}\cdot V_{dc} = \frac{2.449}{3.1416}\cdot 400 \approx 0.7799 \cdot 400 \approx 312.0 \text{ V}$$

**Answer:** $V_{ab,1,rms} \approx 312$ V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 M):** Correct formula (1 M) + Substitution (1 M) + Final answer with units (1 M).

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination – Module 3 Pattern, Dec 2024 Style]**

### Question Choice A: 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

**Q:** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **1-phase full-bridge voltage source inverter** supplying a **purely resistive (R) load**. Draw the output voltage and current waveforms. Derive the expression for the RMS value of the output voltage and the peak value of the fundamental component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the same 1-phase full-bridge inverter now feeds an **RL-load** with $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 31.83$ mH, and $V_{dc} = 200$ V at a fundamental frequency of $f = 50$ Hz, derive the expression for the steady-state load current and compute its peak value. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Circuit Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 above — 1-phase full-bridge with 4 switches S1–S4 and diodes D1–D4, load between terminals a and b.)

**2. Operating Principle:**

The inverter is operated in **180° conduction mode**. Two switches conduct at any time, with the following sequence:

- **Interval 1 (0 to π):** S1 and S2 are ON, S3 and S4 are OFF. Current flows from +Vdc through S1 → load → S2 → –Vdc. Output voltage $V_{ab} = +V_{dc}$.
- **Interval 2 (π to 2π):** S3 and S4 are ON, S1 and S2 are OFF. Current reverses direction. Output voltage $V_{ab} = -V_{dc}$.

At $\omega t = \pi$, simultaneous commutation occurs: S1, S2 turn OFF as S3, S4 turn ON.

**3. Waveforms (R-Load):**

The output voltage $V_{ab}$ is a **bipolar square wave** alternating between $+V_{dc}$ and $-V_{dc}$ each half-cycle. Since the load is purely resistive, the current $i_o = V_{ab}/R$ is **also a square wave** with peak $V_{dc}/R$ and identical shape to $V_{ab}$.

**4. Derivation of RMS Output Voltage:**

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} V_{ab}^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_0^{\pi} V_{dc}^2\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} V_{dc}^2\, d(\omega t)\right] = \frac{V_{dc}^2 \cdot 2\pi}{2\pi} = V_{dc}^2$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = V_{dc}}$$

**5. Derivation of Peak of Fundamental:**

The Fourier series of the square wave (derived in Section 3.1):

$$V_{ab}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin(n\omega t)$$

For the fundamental (n = 1):

$$\boxed{\hat{V}_{ab,1} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 1.273\,V_{dc}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 31.83$ mH, $V_{dc} = 200$ V, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1: Compute the load time constant.**

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{31.83 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 3.183 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the angular frequency.**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the normalized time constant (in radians).**

$$\omega \tau = 314.16 \times 3.183 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4: Apply the formula for peak current (derived in Section 3.4):**

$$I_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\cdot\tanh\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2\omega \tau}\right) = \frac{200}{10}\cdot\tanh\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2\times 1.0}\right) = 20 \cdot \tanh(\pi/2)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate.**

$\tanh(\pi/2) = \tanh(1.5708) \approx 0.9172$

$$\boxed{I_{max} \approx 20 \times 0.9172 = 18.34 \text{ A}}$$

**6. Current Equation:**

For $0 \le \omega t \le \pi$:

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\left(1 - e^{-\omega t/(\omega \tau)}\right) - I_{max}\,e^{-\omega t/(\omega \tau)}$$

Substituting values:

$$i_o(\omega t) = 20(1 - e^{-\omega t}) - 18.34\,e^{-\omega t} = 20 - 38.34\,e^{-\omega t}$$

For $\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$:

$$i_o(\omega t) = -20(1 - e^{-(\omega t - \pi)}) + 18.34\,e^{-(\omega t - \pi)} = -20 + 38.34\,e^{-(\omega t - \pi)}$$

**Verification at boundaries:** 
- At $\omega t = 0$: $i_o = 20 - 38.34 = -18.34$ A = $-I_{max}$ ✓
- At $\omega t = \pi$: $i_o = 20 - 38.34 e^{-\pi} \approx 20 - 1.66 = 18.34$ A = $+I_{max}$ ✓
- At $\omega t = 2\pi$: $i_o = -20 + 38.34 e^{-\pi} \approx -18.34$ A ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7+7 = 14):** (a) Circuit (1.5) + Waveform description (1.5) + RMS derivation (2) + Fundamental derivation (2). (b) Formula setup (2) + Numerical evaluation (3) + Current equation (1.5) + Verification (0.5).

---

### Question Choice B: 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Likely Pattern)]**

**Q:** **(a)** Explain the **180° conduction mode** of a **3-phase bridge voltage source inverter** with a neat circuit diagram. Draw the phase voltage ($V_{an}$) and line voltage ($V_{ab}$) waveforms for a **balanced R-load**. List the switching sequence and show that at any instant, exactly three switches conduct. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-phase bridge inverter with Vdc = 500 V operating in 180° mode, derive the expressions for the **fundamental peak values of the phase and line voltages**, and compute their RMS values. Also compute the **third and fifth harmonic amplitudes** in the line voltage, and comment on the absence of triplen harmonics. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Circuit Diagram:** (See Section 4.3 above — 3-phase bridge with 6 switches S1–S6 and anti-parallel diodes D1–D6 feeding a 3-phase R-load.)

**2. 180° Conduction Principle:**

In 180° conduction mode, each of the six switches conducts for **180°** of a cycle. The switches are fired in the sequence S1 → S2 → S3 → S4 → S5 → S6, with each switch lagging the previous one by 60°.

**3. Switching Sequence Table:**

| Interval (°) | Conducting Switches | Phase Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 – 60 | S1, S6, S5 | +A, 0, –C |
| 60 – 120 | S1, S2, S6 | +A, +B, 0 |
| 120 – 180 | S3, S2, S1 | 0, +B, –C |
| 180 – 240 | S3, S4, S2 | –A, +B, 0 |
| 240 – 300 | S5, S4, S3 | –A, 0, +B |
| 300 – 360 | S5, S6, S4 | 0, –B, +C |

**At every instant, exactly 3 switches conduct** — one upper and one lower from two different legs, plus a third from the remaining leg.

**4. Phase Voltage $V_{an}$ Waveform (six-step):**

Taking the DC-link mid-point as the reference, the phase voltage $V_{ao}$ is a square wave of amplitude $\pm V_{dc}/2$. The actual phase voltage at the load $V_{an}$ is a **six-step** waveform with values:

- $+2V_{dc}/3$ when only the upper switch of leg A conducts (and lower switches of B and C also conduct)
- $+V_{dc}/3$ when upper A and lower C conduct (or upper A and lower B)
- 0
- $-V_{dc}/3$
- $-2V_{dc}/3$

**5. Line Voltage $V_{ab}$ Waveform:**

The line voltage is computed as $V_{ab} = V_{an} - V_{bn}$. It is also a **six-step waveform** (with fewer distinct levels than $V_{an}$). The levels are $+V_{dc}, 0, -V_{dc}$ arranged in 6 steps per cycle.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** Vdc = 500 V, 180° mode.

**Step 1: Phase Voltage Fundamental Peak (derived in Section 3.6):**

$$\hat{V}_{an,1} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 500}{\pi} = \frac{1000}{3.1416} \approx 318.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Line Voltage Fundamental Peak (derived in Section 3.5):**

$$\hat{V}_{ab,1} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\,V_{dc}}{\pi} = \frac{2\sqrt{3} \times 500}{\pi} \approx 551.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: RMS Values:**

$$V_{an,1,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{an,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{318.3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 225.1 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{ab,1,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{ab,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{551.3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 389.8 \text{ V}$$

(Or use the direct forms: $V_{an,1,rms} = \sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}/\pi \approx 225.1$ V; $V_{ab,1,rms} = \sqrt{6}\,V_{dc}/\pi \approx 389.9$ V.)

**Step 4: Third and Fifth Harmonic Amplitudes in Line Voltage:**

Using the general formula (from Section 3.5, Step 4):

$$\hat{V}_{ab,n} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi}\cdot\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{3}\right)$$

- **For n = 3:** $\sin(3\pi/3) = \sin(\pi) = 0 \implies \hat{V}_{ab,3} = 0$ (triplen harmonics cancel in line voltage).
- **For n = 5:** $\sin(5\pi/3) = -\sqrt{3}/2$:

$$\hat{V}_{ab,5} = \frac{4 \times 500}{5\pi}\cdot\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = \frac{2000}{5\pi}\cdot\left(-0.866\right) = 127.3 \times (-0.866) \approx -110.2 \text{ V}$$

The magnitude is $|\hat{V}_{ab,5}| \approx 110.2$ V.

**Step 5: Comment on Triplen Harmonics:**

The line voltage of a balanced 3-phase system **does not contain triplen harmonics** (n = 3, 9, 15, …). This is because in a 3-phase system with 120° phase displacement, the third-harmonic components in each phase are **co-phasal** (in phase) and cancel out when subtracting phase voltages: $V_{ab,3} = V_{an,3} - V_{bn,3} = V_{3}\sin(3\omega t) - V_{3}\sin(3(\omega t - 2\pi/3)) = V_{3}\sin(3\omega t) - V_{3}\sin(3\omega t) = 0$.

This natural rejection of triplen harmonics is one of the key advantages of 3-phase systems over single-phase systems for harmonic performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7+7 = 14):** (a) Circuit (1) + Switching table (2) + Phase/line waveform explanation (3) + Conclusion (1). (b) Fundamental derivations (3) + RMS calculations (2) + 3rd/5th harmonic (1.5) + Triplen comment (0.5).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Formula Confusion:** Many students confuse the **3-Phase** line voltage fundamental $(2\sqrt{3}/\pi)V_{dc}$ with the **1-Phase** $(4/\pi)V_{dc}$. Read the question carefully!
> 2. **Units of $I_{max}$:** When computing RL-load peak current, the formula uses $(V_{dc}/R)\tanh(\cdot)$ — students often forget the tanh factor and get 100% error.
> 3. **Conduction Sequence:** In 3-phase 180° mode, students often mistakenly believe only 2 switches conduct — *three* conduct at any instant. Mis-stating this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Triplen Harmonics:** A common trap: "Third harmonic is zero" is true **for line voltage** but **NOT for phase voltage** in 180° mode. State this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **180° vs 120° Mode:** If a question says "120° conduction", the formulas change — phase fundamental becomes $(\sqrt{3}/\pi)V_{dc}$, not $(2/\pi)V_{dc}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam!)**

### 1-Phase Full-Bridge VSI:
- ✅ **4 switches** in H-bridge topology with **4 anti-parallel diodes**.
- ✅ **180° mode** is the standard — pairs (S1,S2) and (S3,S4) alternately conduct for 180° each.
- ✅ R-load: current is **square wave** identical in shape to voltage.
- ✅ RL-load: current is **quasi-square** with exponential rise/decay; **average current = 0**.
- ✅ Freewheeling diodes **must** be present for RL-load operation.
- ✅ Output RMS = Vdc; Fundamental peak = $4V_{dc}/\pi \approx 1.273 V_{dc}$.
- ✅ THD of square wave = **48.3%**.
- ✅ Only **odd harmonics** present in 1-phase output.

### 3-Phase Bridge VSI:
- ✅ **6 switches** in 3-leg topology with 6 anti-parallel diodes.
- ✅ **180° mode** — 3 switches ON at any instant; each switch conducts 180°.
- ✅ **120° mode** — 2 switches ON at any instant; each switch conducts 120°.
- ✅ Phase voltage waveform: **six-step** in 180° mode.
- ✅ Line voltage waveform: **six-step** in 180° mode; contains levels 0, ±Vdc.
- ✅ Line voltage fundamental: $(2\sqrt{3}/\pi)V_{dc} \approx 1.103 V_{dc}$.
- ✅ Phase voltage fundamental: $(2/\pi)V_{dc} \approx 0.637 V_{dc}$.
- ✅ **Triplen harmonics (3, 9, 15, …) cancel in line voltage** but **exist in phase voltage**.
- ✅ Surviving harmonics in line voltage: n = 1, 5, 7, 11, 13, … (i.e., $6k \pm 1$).
- ✅ No DC component in either phase or line voltages (square-wave symmetry).
- ✅ No even harmonics due to half-wave symmetry.

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize:
- ✅ $4/\pi = 1.273$ (1-Phase fundamental / Vdc ratio)
- ✅ $2\sqrt{3}/\pi = 1.103$ (3-Phase line fundamental / Vdc ratio)
- ✅ $2/\pi = 0.637$ (3-Phase phase fundamental / Vdc ratio)
- ✅ $\sqrt{6}/\pi = 0.7799$ (3-Phase line RMS fundamental / Vdc ratio)
- ✅ $\sqrt{2}/\pi = 0.450$ (3-Phase phase RMS fundamental / Vdc ratio)
- ✅ $\sqrt{2/3} = 0.8165$ (3-Phase line RMS total / Vdc ratio for square wave)
- ✅ $\pi/2 = 1.5708$; $\tanh(\pi/2) = 0.9172$

### Formula for Exam Day:
$$\boxed{\text{1-Ph: } \hat{V}_{o,n} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi}; \quad V_{rms} = V_{dc}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{3-Ph Line: } \hat{V}_{ab,n} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{3}\right); \quad \text{Phase: } \hat{V}_{an,n} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{n\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{3}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{1-Ph RL-Load: } I_{max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R}\tanh\!\left(\frac{\pi R}{2\omega L}\right)}$$

> **Final Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **derivation steps** and **numerical problem-solving**. Always show three steps minimum — formula → substitution → final answer with units. Marks are awarded for **method**, not just the final number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 120° and 180° Conduction Mode, and Voltage Control in Inverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 120° and 180° Conduction Mode, and Voltage Control in Inverters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Three-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) — Conduction Modes

A **three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a three-phase AC output of variable frequency and magnitude.
