It consists of **six controlled switches** (typically IGBTs, BJTs, or thyristors with commutation circuits) arranged as three legs (one per phase), with each leg containing an upper and a lower switch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The conduction mode of a VSI defines the duration (in electrical degrees) for which each switch remains in the ON state during one complete cycle (360°) of the output waveform. The two standard modes are **180° conduction** and **120° conduction**.

### 1.2 180° Conduction Mode

In the **180° conduction mode**, each switch conducts for exactly **180°** of the output cycle. The switches are triggered in the sequence: **T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6** at intervals of 60°. At any instant of time, **three switches conduct simultaneously** — one from the upper group (T1, T3, T5) and two from the lower group, or vice versa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point (Board Exam Favourite):** In 180° mode, the conduction sequence is **T1T6 → T1T2 → T3T2 → T3T4 → T5T4 → T5T6**, and each phase terminal gets connected to either the positive DC bus or negative DC bus for half the cycle. **No phase is ever disconnected (floating)**.

### 1.3 120° Conduction Mode

In the **120° conduction mode**, each switch conducts for only **120°** of the output cycle. The triggering sequence remains T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6 at 60° intervals, but each switch is turned OFF 60° before its natural 180° turn-off. At any instant, **only two switches conduct simultaneously** — one from the upper group and one from the lower group.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point:** In 120° mode, each phase terminal is connected to the DC bus for 120°, disconnected (floating) for 60°, and then connected to the opposite rail for 120°. The **floating period** of 60° per half-cycle is the major distinguishing feature.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Real-World Analogy (180° mode):** Think of a **three-handed clock** where each hand is glued to either the 12 o'clock position (positive) or the 6 o'clock position (negative) rail. At any moment, **all three hands are firmly placed on a rail** — you always know exactly where each phase is. This gives a "stiffer" output, which is great for driving loads that don't like floating terminals.

> **Real-World Analogy (120° mode):** Now imagine **only two hands** are placed on a rail at any time, while the **third hand floats freely in the air** for 60°. The floating phase can take any value determined by the load current (back-EMF in motors). This is useful for certain motor drive applications and reduces short-circuit risk.

### 1.5 Voltage Control in Inverters

**Voltage control** in inverters refers to the techniques used to vary the **RMS value of the AC output voltage** of the inverter while maintaining (or controlling) the output frequency. This is essential for **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** because AC motors require **V/f ratio** to be kept constant for proper flux control.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification:** The internal (within inverter) voltage control methods are:
> 1. **Single Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** — 1 pulse per half cycle
> 2. **Multiple Pulse Width Modulation (MPWM)** — multiple pulses per half cycle
> 3. **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (Sinusoidal PWM)** — pulse widths vary sinusoidally
> 4. **Modified Sinusoidal PWM** — third harmonic injection technique
> 5. **Phase Displacement Control** — for multi-bridge inverters

### 1.6 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** The ratio of RMS harmonic content to RMS fundamental. **Lower THD = better waveform quality**.
- **Harmonic Factor (HFₙ):** Measure of individual harmonic contribution.
- **Distortion Factor (DF):** Ratio of fundamental RMS to total RMS.
- **Crest Factor:** Peak value divided by RMS value.
- **Form Factor:** RMS value divided by average value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question asks "compare 180° and 120° conduction", always tabulate **THD**, **peak fundamental component**, **device utilisation**, and **applications** side by side.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 180° Conduction Mode — Detailed Operation

### 2.1.1 Switching Sequence
The six switches T1 to T6 are numbered such that T1, T3, T5 are the **upper switches** (connected to +V_dc) of phases A, B, C respectively, and T4, T6, T2 are the **lower switches** (connected to −V_dc) of phases A, B, C respectively. Each switch is gated for **180°** with a **60° delay** between successive triggers.

### 2.1.2 Conduction Intervals (Step-by-Step)

| Interval | Duration | Conducting Switches | Phase A | Phase B | Phase C |
|----------|----------|---------------------|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | 0°–60° | T1, T6, T5 | +V_dc | −V_dc | −V_dc |
| 2 | 60°–120° | T1, T2, T3 | +V_dc | −V_dc | +V_dc |
| 3 | 120°–180° | T3, T2, T4 | −V_dc | −V_dc | +V_dc |
| 4 | 180°–240° | T3, T4, T5 | −V_dc | +V_dc | +V_dc |
| 5 | 240°–300° | T5, T4, T6 | −V_dc | +V_dc | −V_dc |
| 6 | 300°–360° | T5, T6, T1 | +V_dc | +V_dc | −V_dc |

> [!NOTE]
> The phase terminals are never left floating. This makes 180° mode the **most widely used** configuration for general-purpose VFDs.

### 2.1.3 Line and Phase Voltage Waveforms — 180° Mode
- **Phase voltage** (e.g., V_AN) is a quasi-square wave with amplitude ±(2/3)·V_dc.
- **Line voltage** (e.g., V_AB) is a stepped wave with levels 0, +V_dc, −V_dc, taking values ±V_dc and 0.
- Line voltage is always **120° out of phase** between any two phases.

## 2.2 120° Conduction Mode — Detailed Operation

### 2.2.1 Switching Sequence
Each switch is gated for only **120°**. The ON-time is **delayed by 60°** from the start of the corresponding 180° mode waveform and is **terminated 60° early**. Result: at any instant, only **2 switches** conduct (one upper + one lower from different phases).

### 2.2.2 Conduction Intervals

| Interval | Duration | Conducting Switches | Phase A | Phase B | Phase C |
|----------|----------|---------------------|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | 0°–60° | T1, T2 | +V_dc | −V_dc | Floating |
| 2 | 60°–120° | T2, T3 | Floating | −V_dc | +V_dc |
| 3 | 120°–180° | T3, T4 | −V_dc | Floating | +V_dc |
| 4 | 180°–240° | T4, T5 | −V_dc | +V_dc | Floating |
| 5 | 240°–300° | T5, T6 | Floating | +V_dc | −V_dc |
| 6 | 300°–360° | T6, T1 | +V_dc | Floating | −V_dc |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Floating" phase terminal takes a voltage determined by the load. For a **star-connected balanced load**, the floating phase voltage equals the **average of the other two connected phases**, i.e., **V_floating = (V_other1 + V_other2) / 2**.

### 2.2.3 Line and Phase Voltage Waveforms — 120° Mode
- The **phase voltage** waveform has a **60° zero-level region** in each half cycle.
- The fundamental **RMS phase voltage is reduced** compared to 180° mode.
- The fundamental peak phase voltage = **(√3/2) · (2/3)·V_dc = V_dc/√3**, while in 180° mode it equals **(2/√3)·(V_dc/2)...** (see formulas below).

## 2.3 Comparison Table: 180° vs 120° Conduction

| Parameter | 180° Mode | 120° Mode |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Conduction duration per switch | **180°** | **120°** |
| Switches conducting at any instant | **3** | **2** |
| Floating phase interval | **None** | **60° per half cycle** |
| Peak fundamental phase voltage | (2/π)·V_dc ≈ 0.637·V_dc | (√3/π)·V_dc ≈ 0.551·V_dc |
| RMS phase voltage (fundamental) | √6·V_dc/(2π) ≈ 0.39·V_dc | (3·V_dc)/(2π√2) ≈ 0.337·V_dc |
| RMS line voltage (fundamental) | (√3/π)·V_dc ≈ 0.55·V_dc | (3/π)·V_dc ≈ 0.955·V_dc (different) |
| THD (line voltage) | ~31.08% | ~28.9% (slightly better) |
| Short-circuit risk | Higher (3 devices ON) | Lower (only 2 devices ON) |
| Applications | General purpose VFDs, UPS | High-power drives, CSI load |

## 2.4 Voltage Control Methods — Theory

### 2.4.1 Single Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM / 1-PWM)
- **One pulse per half cycle** of width **2d** centred at the middle.
- The pulse width **2d = 2·δ**, where δ is the pulse half-width.
- **Output RMS voltage** = V_dc · √(d/π).
- The output frequency **f₀** is fixed (no frequency control here, only voltage control).
- This is the **simplest** voltage control method.

### 2.4.2 Multiple Pulse Width Modulation (MPWM)
- **Multiple pulses per half cycle**, all of **equal width** `2d` and **equal spacing**.
- Number of pulses per half cycle = **p**.
- **Output RMS voltage** = V_s · √(p·d/π), where V_s = V_dc.
- Higher **p** → waveform approaches sine → lower THD → higher switching losses.

### 2.4.3 Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)
- A **triangular carrier wave** of frequency f_c is compared with a **sinusoidal reference wave** of frequency f_o.
- The intersection points generate the gating pulses.
- **Modulation index m_a = V_control / V_triangle** controls output voltage.
- **Frequency ratio m_f = f_carrier / f_reference** controls output frequency indirectly.
- **Output voltage RMS** ≈ m_a · (V_dc/√2) for linear region.

### 2.4.4 Modified Sinusoidal PWM
- **Third harmonic injection** into the reference sine wave to **flatten the peak**.
- Permits higher modulation index (m_a up to **1.15–1.27**) before entering overmodulation.
- **DC bus utilisation improves by ~15%**.

### 2.4.5 Phase Displacement Control
- Used in **multi-bridge (multi-pulse) inverters**.
- Each bridge produces a quasi-square wave; the bridges are phase-shifted by an angle **α**.
- The resultant waveform has more steps and lower THD.
- The fundamental RMS voltage = **(2·V_dc/π) · cos(α/2)** for two-bridge series.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | V_LL(rms,180) = √(2/3)·V_s | RMS line voltage, 180° mode | Volts |
| 2 | V_ph(rms,180) = V_s/√3 | RMS phase voltage, 180° mode (star load) | Volts |
| 3 | V_ph(peak,180) = (2/3)·V_s | Peak phase voltage, 180° mode | Volts |
| 4 | V_ph(peak,120) = (√3/3)·V_s = V_s/√3 | Peak phase voltage, 120° mode | Volts |
| 5 | V_LL(rms,120) = (3·V_s)/(2π)·√2 | RMS line voltage, 120° mode | Volts |
| 6 | V_rms(1-PWM) = V_s·√(2d/π) | RMS of single PWM output | Volts |
| 7 | V_rms(MPWM) = V_s·√(p·(2d)/π) | RMS of multiple PWM | Volts |
| 8 | m_a = V_control / V_tri | Modulation index | dimensionless |
| 9 | m_f = f_carrier / f_ref | Frequency ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | V_01 = m_a·(V_dc/2) | Fundamental RMS of SPWM (linear) | Volts |
| 11 | V_01(phase-shift) = (2·V_dc/π)·cos(α/2) | Two-bridge phase-shift RMS | Volts |
| 12 | THD = √((V_rms² − V_01²)/V_01) | Total Harmonic Distortion | % |
| 13 | HF_n = V_0n / V_01 | Harmonic factor of n-th order | dimensionless |
| 14 | DF = V_01 / V_rms | Distortion factor | dimensionless |
| 15 | CF = V_peak / V_rms | Crest factor | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **V_s** = V_dc = DC bus voltage. For star-connected load, V_ph = V_LL/√3 only for **sinusoidal** waveforms; for quasi-square waves, the relationship is different and must be derived using Fourier analysis.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric Vehicle (EV) drives:** SPWM in 180° mode is the workhorse for traction inverter control.
- **Solar PV inverters:** Sinusoidal PWM with third-harmonic injection maximises DC bus utilisation.
- **UPS systems:** Phase-shifted multi-pulse PWM achieves near-sinusoidal output without bulky filters.
- **HVAC and industrial pumps:** V/f-controlled 180° VSI is the standard variable-speed drive topology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Fourier Analysis of 180° Mode Phase Voltage (Quasi-Square Wave)

The phase voltage waveform V_AN(t) in 180° conduction mode is a quasi-square wave that alternates between +(2/3)·V_s and −(2/3)·V_s, with 120° ON and 60° transition through zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** Board examiners frequently ask for the **fundamental RMS** of 180° mode. You must show the Fourier integral explicitly.

### Derivation Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Define the waveform over one period.**
The quasi-square wave V_AN(ωt) over 0 to 2π takes values:
- +(2/3)·V_s for 0 < ωt < 2π/3
- −(2/3)·V_s for π < ωt < 5π/3
- 0 for 2π/3 < ωt < π and for 5π/3 < ωt < 2π

**Step 2 — Express as odd quarter-wave symmetric Fourier series.**
Due to quarter-wave symmetry, only **odd harmonics** of **sine** terms exist:

$$
V_{AN}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Fourier coefficient B_n.**

$$
B_n = \frac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} V_{AN}(\omega t) \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

Within 0 to π/2, the waveform is +(2/3)·V_s (since the first 60° is in the +ve region). Splitting the integral appropriately:

$$
B_n = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{2V_s}{3} \left[ \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the inner integral.**

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n} \right]_{0}^{\pi/3} = \frac{1 - \cos(n\pi/3)}{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into B_n.**

$$
B_n = \frac{8V_s}{3\pi n} \left[ 1 - \cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{3}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — For the fundamental (n = 1):**

$$
B_1 = \frac{8V_s}{3\pi} \left[ 1 - \cos(60°) \right] = \frac{8V_s}{3\pi} \cdot \left(1 - 0.5\right) = \frac{8V_s}{3\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{4V_s}{3\pi}
$$

**Step 7 — Fundamental peak phase voltage.**

$$
\hat{V}_{AN,1} = \frac{4V_s}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244 \cdot V_s
$$

**Step 8 — Fundamental RMS phase voltage.**

$$
V_{AN,1(rms)} = \frac{\hat{V}_{AN,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4V_s}{3\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{3\pi} \approx 0.3002 \cdot V_s
$$

**Step 9 — RMS line voltage (V_AB = V_AN − V_BN) is shifted 120°, so:**

$$
V_{AB,1(rms)} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{AN,1(rms)} = \frac{2\sqrt{6}\,V_s}{3\pi} \approx 0.5198 \cdot V_s
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result:** **V_LL(rms, fundamental, 180°) = (√6/π)·V_s ≈ 0.7797·V_s** (this is the TOTAL RMS of the quasi-square wave, but the **fundamental RMS is 0.5198·V_s**). Many students confuse the two — **read the question carefully!**

## 3.2 Fourier Analysis of 120° Mode Phase Voltage

The 120° mode phase voltage has a 60° zero-level in each half cycle.

**Step 1 — Waveform definition.**
- +(2/3)·V_s for 0 < ωt < π/3
- 0 for π/3 < ωt < 2π/3
- −(2/3)·V_s for 2π/3 < ωt < 4π/3
- 0 for 4π/3 < ωt < 5π/3
- +(2/3)·V_s for 5π/3 < ωt < 2π

**Step 2 — Fourier coefficient:**

$$
B_n = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{2V_s}{3} \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{8V_s}{3\pi n}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{3}\right)\right]
$$

The form is **identical** to the 180° case, but the **discontinuity intervals** differ, leading to a different total RMS and a different THD.

**Step 3 — Fundamental peak (n=1):**

$$
B_1 = \frac{4V_s}{3\pi} = \frac{4V_s}{3\pi} \approx 0.4244 \cdot V_s
$$

**Wait — the fundamental amplitude is the same?** Yes, because the first 60° of the wave is identical in both modes. The difference shows up in the **line voltage** and in the **total harmonic content**.

**Step 4 — RMS line voltage (120° mode, total):**

$$
V_{LL,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot V_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot \ldots = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

After proper integration:

$$
V_{LL,rms}(120°) = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.8165 \cdot V_s
$$

Compare with 180° mode: V_LL,rms(180°) = √(2/3)·V_s ≈ 0.8165·V_s. **Wait — they are equal!**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reconciliation:** The **total RMS** of the line voltage is identical in both modes (**0.8165·V_s**), but the **fundamental RMS** differs: 0.5198·V_s (180°) vs 0.4501·V_s (120°). Hence, **180° mode has a stronger fundamental component**, while 120° mode has slightly more harmonic content in the line voltage.

## 3.3 RMS of Single-Pulse PWM Output

A single-pulse waveform has amplitude V_s, pulse width 2d centred at π/2, with zeros elsewhere in [0, π].

**Step 1 — Mean square value:**

$$
V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\pi/2 - d}^{\pi/2 + d} V_s^{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_s^{2}}{\pi} \cdot 2d = \frac{2V_s^{2} d}{\pi}
$$

**Step 2 — Take square root:**

$$
V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{2d}{\pi}}
$$

**Step 3 — Range of d:** 0 ≤ d ≤ π/2. For full square wave, d = π/2 → V_rms = V_s. ✓

## 3.4 RMS of Multiple-Pulse PWM Output

**Step 1 — There are p pulses per half-cycle, each of width 2d.**

$$
V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot p \cdot 2d \cdot V_s^{2} = \frac{2pV_s^{2} d}{\pi}
$$

**Step 2 — RMS expression:**

$$
V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{2pd}{\pi}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **maximum value** occurs when pulses just touch: 2d · p = π → d = π/(2p) → V_rms,max = V_s. So **MPWM can never exceed the DC link voltage** in RMS terms.

## 3.5 Sinusoidal PWM — Output Voltage Formula

**Step 1 — In the linear region (m_a ≤ 1), the fundamental peak of the PWM output is:**

$$
\hat{V}_{01} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — For m_a > 1 (overmodulation), the output saturates at:**

$$
\hat{V}_{01,max} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi} \quad \text{(square-wave limit)}
$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental RMS:**

$$
V_{01,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{(linear region)}
$$

## 3.6 Phase Displacement Control — Two-Bridge Inverter

**Step 1 — Each bridge produces a quasi-square wave of fundamental peak (4V_dc/3π)?** No — each bridge's full-bridge produces ±V_dc. So each bridge's fundamental peak = (4V_dc/π).

**Step 2 — The two bridges are phase-shifted by α. The combined fundamental peak:**

$$
\hat{V}_{01,combined} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi} \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental RMS:**

$$
V_{01,rms} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_{dc}}{\pi} \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Voltage control range:**
- α = 0° → V_01,rms = 2√2·V_dc/π (maximum)
- α = 180° → V_01,rms = 0 (minimum, full cancellation)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase VSI Power Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCPLUS[POSITIVE DC BUS VDC] --> FUSE[FUSE AND DC LINK CAPACITOR]
    FUSE --> NODEA
    FUSE --> NODEB
    FUSE --> NODEC
    NODEA --> T1SW[T1 UPPER SWITCH PHASE A]
    NODEA --> T3SW[T3 UPPER SWITCH PHASE B]
    NODEA --> T5SW[T5 UPPER SWITCH PHASE C]
    T1SW --> PHASEA[TERMINAL A TO LOAD]
    T3SW --> PHASEB[TERMINAL B TO LOAD]
    T5SW --> PHASEC[TERMINAL C TO LOAD]
    PHASEA --> T4SW[T4 LOWER SWITCH PHASE A]
    PHASEB --> T6SW[T6 LOWER SWITCH PHASE B]
    PHASEC --> T2SW[T2 LOWER SWITCH PHASE C]
    T4SW --> DCMINUS[NEGATIVE DC BUS GROUND]
    T6SW --> DCMINUS
    T2SW --> DCMINUS
    PHASEA --> LOADA[LOAD PHASE A]
    PHASEB --> LOADB[LOAD PHASE B]
    PHASEC --> LOADC[LOAD PHASE C]
    LOADA --> STAR[STAR POINT N]
    LOADB --> STAR
    LOADC --> STAR
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the standard six-switch three-phase VSI topology. Each phase leg (A, B, C) contains two complementary switches (T1-T4, T3-T6, T5-T2) connected across the DC bus.

## 4.2 Conduction Sequence Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[INVERTER CONDUCTION MODE] --> Q180[180 DEGREE MODE]
    START --> Q120[120 DEGREE MODE]
    Q180 --> S1[SWITCH CONDUCTS 180 DEGREES]
    Q120 --> S2[SWITCH CONDUCTS 120 DEGREES]
    S1 --> C1[THREE SWITCHES ALWAYS ON]
    S2 --> C2[TWO SWITCHES ALWAYS ON]
    C1 --> P1[NO FLOATING PHASE]
    C2 --> P2[SIXTY DEGREE FLOATING PER HALF CYCLE]
    P1 --> APP1[USED IN GENERAL PURPOSE VFD AND UPS]
    P2 --> APP2[USED IN HIGH POWER CSI DRIVES]
    C1 --> F1[TRIPLES HIGHER FUNDAMENTAL OUTPUT]
    C2 --> F2[LOWER SHORT CIRCUIT RISK]
```

## 4.3 Voltage Control Methods — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    VC[VOLTAGE CONTROL IN INVERTERS] --> INT[INTERNAL METHODS]
    VC --> EXT[EXTERNAL METHODS NOT IN SYLLABUS]
    INT --> SP1[SINGLE PULSE WIDTH MODULATION]
    INT --> SP2[MULTIPLE PULSE WIDTH MODULATION]
    INT --> SP3[SINUSOIDAL PWM]
    INT --> SP4[MODIFIED SINUSOIDAL PWM]
    INT --> SP5[PHASE DISPLACEMENT CONTROL]
    SP1 --> F1A[ONE PULSE PER HALF CYCLE]
    SP2 --> F2A[MULTIPLE EQUAL PULSES PER HALF CYCLE]
    SP3 --> F3A[SINE VERSUS TRIANGLE COMPARISON]
    SP3 --> F3B[MA MODULATION INDEX FCONTROL FREF]
    SP4 --> F4A[THIRD HARMONIC INJECTION]
    SP4 --> F4B[IMPROVED DC BUS UTILISATION UP TO 15 PERCENT]
    SP5 --> F5A[PHASE SHIFT ANGLE ALPHA BETWEEN BRIDGES]
    SP5 --> F5B[USED IN MULTI PULSE INVERTERS]
```

## 4.4 PWM Generation — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    REF[SINUSOIDAL REFERENCE WAVE FREF] --> COMP[VOLTAGE COMPARATOR]
    TRI[TRIANGULAR CARRIER WAVE FC] --> COMP
    COMP --> GATE[GATING PULSES TO SWITCHES]
    GATE --> SW1[PHASE A LEG T1 AND T4]
    GATE --> SW2[PHASE B LEG T3 AND T6]
    GATE --> SW3[PHASE C LEG T5 AND T2]
    SW1 --> OUT[THREE PHASE PWM OUTPUT TO LOAD]
    SW2 --> OUT
    SW3 --> OUT
    OUT --> FBACK[FEEDBACK FOR VOLTAGE REGULATION]
    FBACK --> REF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure Insight:** The **Sinusoidal PWM** block diagram shows the closed-loop voltage regulation path. By adjusting the amplitude of the reference sine (and hence m_a), the output RMS voltage is controlled in real time — this is the heart of every modern variable-frequency drive.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: 3 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**Q: Compare 180° and 120° conduction modes of a three-phase VSI in terms of (i) number of switches conducting at any instant, (ii) presence of floating phase, and (iii) fundamental RMS line voltage.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Number of switches conducting:** 180° mode → **3 switches** conduct at any instant; 120° mode → **2 switches** conduct.
- **(ii) Floating phase:** 180° mode → **No floating phase** (all three terminals always connected to +V_dc or −V_dc); 120° mode → **60° floating interval** per half cycle for each phase.
- **(iii) Fundamental RMS line voltage:** Both modes produce a **stepped waveform** with the same total RMS of (V_s/√2) ≈ 0.8165·V_s, but the **fundamental RMS** differs: **0.5198·V_s for 180° mode** vs **0.4501·V_s for 120° mode** — i.e., 180° mode delivers a **stronger fundamental component**.

> [!NOTE]
> For 3-mark questions, use **bullet points** and **box the key numerical values**. KTU examiners award 1 mark per clear point.

### Question 2: 3 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Q: List any three internal voltage control methods of a single-phase inverter. Mention the role of modulation index (m_a) in Sinusoidal PWM.**

**Model Answer:**
- Three internal voltage control methods: **Single Pulse Width Modulation (1-PWM)**, **Multiple Pulse Width Modulation (MPWM)**, and **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)**. (Other acceptable: Modified SPWM, Phase Displacement Control).
- **Modulation index m_a = V_control / V_triangle** in Sinusoidal PWM.
- It **controls the RMS value of the output voltage** linearly in the region 0 ≤ m_a ≤ 1: V_01,rms = (m_a · V_dc)/(2√2).
- Beyond m_a = 1, the inverter enters **overmodulation** and eventually saturates to square-wave operation at m_a ≈ 3.24.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **180° conduction mode** of a three-phase voltage source inverter with a neat circuit diagram. Draw the phase and line voltage waveforms for one complete cycle and derive the expression for the **fundamental RMS line voltage**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A three-phase bridge inverter operates in **120° conduction mode** with a DC input of V_s = 400 V. Determine:
- (i) The RMS value of the fundamental component of the line voltage.
- (ii) The total RMS line voltage.
- (iii) The RMS value of the fundamental component of the phase voltage.
- (iv) The Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of the line voltage.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**1. Circuit Operation:**
The three-phase VSI consists of **6 switches** (T1–T6) arranged in three legs. Each switch conducts for **180°** with sequential triggering at **60° intervals**: T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6. At any instant, three switches are ON.

**2. Conduction Sequence Table:**

| Interval | Conducting Switches | V_AN | V_BN | V_CN | V_AB |
|----------|---------------------|------|------|------|------|
| 0°–60° | T1, T6, T5 | +V_s | 0 | −V_s | +V_s |
| 60°–120° | T1, T2, T3 | +V_s | −V_s | 0 | +V_s |
| 120°–180° | T3, T2, T4 | 0 | −V_s | +V_s | +V_s |
| 180°–240° | T3, T4, T5 | −V_s | 0 | +V_s | −V_s |
| 240°–300° | T5, T4, T6 | −V_s | +V_s | 0 | −V_s |
| 300°–360° | T5, T6, T1 | 0 | +V_s | −V_s | −V_s |

**3. Derivation of Fundamental RMS Line Voltage:**

The line voltage V_AB is a **quasi-square wave** with amplitude ±V_s, having a 60° zero-level. The Fourier series of V_AB contains only odd harmonics:

$$
V_{AB}(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots} B_n \sin(n\omega t)
$$

The fundamental component is:

$$
B_1 = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \cdot \ldots
$$

Using direct integration for the line voltage waveform (amplitude +V_s for 60°, 0 for 60°, −V_s for 60°, 0 for 60°, then repeats with sign inversion):

$$
B_1 = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{3V_s}{\pi}
$$

**Fundamental peak line voltage:**

$$
\hat{V}_{AB,1} = \frac{3V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.9549 \cdot V_s
$$

**Fundamental RMS line voltage:**

$$
V_{AB,1(rms)} = \frac{3V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{2\pi} \approx 0.6752 \cdot V_s
$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical:

Given: **V_s = 400 V**, **120° conduction mode**.

**Reconfirming the analytical relations for 120° mode:**

The fundamental peak of the **phase voltage** in 120° mode is:

$$
\hat{V}_{AN,1} = \frac{2V_s}{\pi} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.5513 \cdot V_s
$$

The **fundamental peak line voltage** is √3 times the phase:

$$
\hat{V}_{AB,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \hat{V}_{AN,1} = \frac{3V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.9549 \cdot V_s
$$

**Same as 180° mode for the line fundamental!**

**(i) RMS of fundamental line voltage:**

$$
V_{AB,1(rms)} = \frac{3V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3 \times 400}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1200}{4.4429} \approx 270.04 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Total RMS line voltage:**
The line voltage waveform (120° mode) has amplitude ±V_s for 60° intervals and zero for 60° intervals in each half cycle.

$$
V_{AB,rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} V_s^{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_s^{2}}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{V_s^{2}}{3}
$$

$$
V_{AB,rms} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 230.94 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) RMS of fundamental phase voltage:**

$$
V_{AN,1(rms)} = \frac{\hat{V}_{AN,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 400}{\pi\sqrt{2}} \approx 155.88 \text{ V}
$$

**(iv) THD of line voltage:**

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{AB,rms}^{2} - V_{AB,1(rms)}^{2}}}{V_{AB,1(rms)}} = \frac{\sqrt{(230.94)^{2} - (270.04)^{2}}}{270.04}
$$

The numerator is **negative** under this calculation, which signals that the **fundamental RMS exceeds the total RMS** — this is impossible and indicates a mismatch in the assumed waveform.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout #1:** For 120° conduction, the **line voltage waveform** is **not** the same as in 180° mode. The correct total RMS line voltage for 120° mode is **V_s·√(2/3) ≈ 0.8165·V_s**, NOT V_s/√3. Always re-derive from the actual waveform shape.

**Corrected (ii) Total RMS line voltage:**

$$
V_{AB,rms}(120°) = V_s \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 400 \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 400 \times 0.8165 \approx 326.60 \text{ V}
$$

**Corrected (iv) THD:**

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{AB,rms}^{2} - V_{AB,1(rms)}^{2}}}{V_{AB,1(rms)}} = \frac{\sqrt{(326.60)^{2} - (270.04)^{2}}}{270.04}
$$

$$
= \frac{\sqrt{106670 - 72922}}{270.04} = \frac{\sqrt{33748}}{270.04} = \frac{183.71}{270.04} \approx 0.6803
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{THD} \approx 68.03\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently use the 180° mode formulas for 120° mode problems, leading to wrong numerical answers. **Always draw the waveform first**, then derive formulas from it. The presence/absence of zero levels and their duration changes the Fourier coefficients significantly.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** technique for voltage control in a single-phase full-bridge inverter. Define **modulation index (m_a)** and **frequency ratio (m_f)**, and explain how the output voltage is controlled by varying m_a.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-phase full-bridge inverter uses **SPWM** with the following parameters:
- DC link voltage: **V_dc = 300 V**
- Modulation index: **m_a = 0.8**
- Triangular carrier peak: **V_tri = 5 V**

Calculate:
- (i) The RMS value of the fundamental output voltage.
- (ii) The peak value of the fundamental output voltage.
- (iii) The total RMS output voltage (assuming the switching frequency is high enough that the harmonics form an ideal square).
- (iv) Comment on the THD.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**1. Block Diagram Description:**

The SPWM technique uses **two signals**:
- **Reference signal:** A sinusoidal wave of frequency f_o (desired output frequency) and amplitude V_control.
- **Carrier signal:** A triangular wave of frequency f_c (typically much higher, f_c >> f_o) and amplitude V_tri.

These two are fed into a **comparator**. Whenever V_sine > V_tri, the comparator output is HIGH, turning ON one pair of switches (say T1, T2); when V_sine < V_tri, the other pair (T3, T4) is ON.

**2. Definitions:**

- **Modulation index:** $m_a = \dfrac{V_{control}}{V_{triangle}}$
- **Frequency ratio:** $m_f = \dfrac{f_{carrier}}{f_{reference}} = \dfrac{f_c}{f_o}$

**3. Voltage Control:**
- In the **linear region** (m_a ≤ 1), the **fundamental RMS** of the output is: V_01,rms = (m_a · V_dc) / (2√2).
- As **m_a increases** from 0 to 1, the **RMS output voltage increases linearly** from 0 to V_dc/(2√2).
- Beyond m_a = 1, the inverter enters **overmodulation** and the relationship becomes nonlinear; the output saturates to a square wave at m_a = 3.24 (in the limit).

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

Given: V_dc = 300 V, m_a = 0.8, V_tri = 5 V (not directly needed for output voltage).

**(i) Fundamental RMS output voltage:**

$$
V_{01,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.8 \times 300}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{240}{2.828} \approx 84.85 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Peak fundamental output voltage:**

$$
\hat{V}_{01} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{0.8 \times 300}{2} = 120 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) Total RMS output voltage:**
For a full-bridge square-wave inverter (worst-case limit):

$$
V_{rms,total} = V_{dc} = 300 \text{ V}
$$

But for SPWM at m_a = 0.8 with typical high m_f, the **total RMS** is dominated by the DC link and modulation structure. The total RMS for SPWM in the linear region can be approximated by:

$$
V_{rms,total} \approx \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 212.13 \text{ V}
$$

(This represents the RMS if the bridge switches between +V_dc and −V_dc in a symmetric bipolar fashion.)

**(iv) THD Comment:**

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms,total}^{2} - V_{01,rms}^{2}}}{V_{01,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{(212.13)^{2} - (84.85)^{2}}}{84.85}
$$

$$
= \frac{\sqrt{44999 - 7200}}{84.85} = \frac{\sqrt{37799}}{84.85} = \frac{194.42}{84.85} \approx 2.291
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{THD} \approx 229.1\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout #2:** The THD looks "huge" because we are treating the bipolar SPWM output (alternating between +V_dc and −V_dc) as the total. In practice, the **THD is referenced to the load-side filtered waveform**, not the switched waveform. Most of the harmonic energy is at high frequencies near f_c and its multiples, which are easily filtered by small LC filters. The **Distortion Factor (DF)** after filtering is what really matters in VFD applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **General KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Module 3:**
> 1. **Sign convention for thyristors:** T1, T3, T5 are the upper group (anode to +V_dc); T4, T6, T2 are the lower group (cathode to −V_dc). Reversing this loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Star vs Delta load:** For star-connected balanced load, V_ph = V_LL/√3 only holds for sinusoidal waveforms. For quasi-square waves, derive the relationship using KVL at the star point.
> 3. **Total RMS vs Fundamental RMS:** Always specify which one your formula computes. A common trap: examiners ask for the "RMS line voltage" (which means total RMS = 0.8165·V_s) and students give the fundamental RMS (0.5198·V_s).
> 4. **Forgetting the 60° zero-level in 120° mode:** Leads to wrong Fourier coefficients.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **180° conduction:** Each switch ON for 180°; **3 switches** conducting at any instant; **no floating phase**; conduction pattern T1T6 → T1T2 → T3T2 → T3T4 → T5T4 → T5T6.
- **120° conduction:** Each switch ON for 120°; **2 switches** conducting at any instant; **60° floating** per half cycle; conduction pattern T1T2 → T2T3 → T3T4 → T4T5 → T5T6 → T6T1.
- **Peak fundamental phase voltage (180°):** (4V_s)/(3π) ≈ 0.4244·V_s.
- **Peak fundamental phase voltage (120°):** (√3·V_s)/π ≈ 0.5513·V_s.
- **Peak fundamental line voltage (both modes):** (3V_s)/π ≈ 0.9549·V_s.
- **Total RMS line voltage (both modes):** V_s·√(2/3) ≈ 0.8165·V_s.
- **THD of 180° mode line voltage:** ≈ **31.08%**.
- **THD of 120° mode line voltage:** ≈ **68%** (higher because of the 60° zero gaps in the waveform).
- **1-PWM:** V_rms = V_s·√(2d/π); one pulse per half cycle.
- **MPWM:** V_rms = V_s·√(2pd/π); p pulses per half cycle, each of width 2d.
- **SPWM (linear region):** V_01,rms = m_a·V_dc/(2√2); pulse widths vary sinusoidally.
- **Modified SPWM:** Adds third harmonic (15.47% of fundamental) to flatten the reference, increasing usable m_a from 1.0 to **1.15** (≈15% better DC bus utilisation).
- **Phase displacement control:** Used in multi-bridge inverters; V_01,rms = (2√2·V_dc/π)·cos(α/2); α = 0° → max voltage; α = 180° → zero voltage.
- **V/f ratio:** Maintained constant in VFDs to keep air-gap flux constant in induction motors.
- **Harmonics eliminated by SPWM:** Low-order harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th) are heavily suppressed; dominant harmonics cluster around m_f and its sidebands.
- **Switching loss trade-off:** Higher m_f → better waveform quality, **higher switching losses**. Optimal m_f is chosen based on the application (audio → low; UPS → moderate; traction → high).
- **Applications of 180° VSI:** General-purpose VFDs, UPS, solar inverters, induction motor drives.
- **Applications of 120° VSI:** Current Source Inverters (CSI), very high-power drives, where lower short-circuit risk is critical.
- **Star-point voltage:** For 180° mode, V_N = sum of all three phase voltages / 3. For a balanced 180° VSI with star load, V_N contains only **triplen harmonics** (3rd, 9th, 15th, ...), and the line voltage (V_AB = V_AN − V_BN) has these triplen harmonics cancelled, leaving 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, ...

> **Final Tip:** Always carry the **KTU Formula Sheet** (Section 2.5) into the exam hall. Memorise the **3 switch / 2 switch** rule and the **fundamental peak line voltage = 3V_s/π** for both modes — these are the most-tested facts in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Width Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pulse Width Modulation # 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a sophisticated power electronic technique used to encode the magnitude of an analog signal (reference / modulating signal) into the **varying width of pulses** within a fixed switching period. In the context of electric drives and power converters (specifically **DC-AC Voltage Source Inverters — VSI**), PWM is the principal method of generating a controllable AC output voltage from a fixed DC bus by chopping the DC link into a train of rectangular pulses of regulated width. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **Course Outcome CO3** (Analyze and design power electronic converters for drive applications), PWM is the heart of modern variable-frequency drives (VFDs) because it simultaneously controls: - **Magnitude of fundamental output voltage** - **Frequency of fundamental output voltage** - **Harmonic profile of the load current** The two reference waveforms central to PWM generation are: - **Reference / Modulating signal:** $v_r = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (low-frequency desired sine wave) - **Carrier signal:** $v_c$ (high-frequency triangular wave, frequency $f_c$) PWM pulses are produced by **comparing** the reference sine wave with the triangular carrier. Whenever $v_r > v_c$, the upper switch of the inverter leg is turned ON; otherwise it is OFF. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The student is expected to understand the **single-pulse, multiple-pulse, and sinusoidal PWM** techniques, derive the **RMS output voltage and harmonic content**, and appreciate the role of **modulation index (M)** and **frequency modulation ratio (mf)** in shaping the inverter output. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to control the **average brightness** of a room light using only a wall switch (no dimmer). The light is either fully ON or fully OFF — you cannot directly set it to, say, "40% brightness." What if you repeatedly flick the switch very fast? If in every 1-second window, the bulb is ON for 0.4 s and OFF for 0.6 s, the **average perceived brightness** is 40% of full brightness. Your eye cannot resolve the rapid flickering, so it integrates the light and you see "dim" light. **That flickering is exactly PWM!** - The **switching period** $T_s$ = 1 second (the window). - The **ON time** (pulse width) = 0.4 s. - The **duty cycle** $D$ = 40%. In an inverter: - $T_s$ is fixed by the high-frequency carrier (e.g., a few kHz). - The pulse width is varied every cycle to **mimic a sine wave** of the desired output frequency. - The **average voltage delivered to the motor** in each short switching interval is controlled by this pulse width. By varying the pulse widths sinusoidally over time, the load (motor) sees a fundamental sine wave of the desired magnitude and frequency, but the actual switching waveform is rectangular pulses. > [!NOTE] > **The key constants / metrics you MUST remember in BOLD:** > - **Modulation Index:** $M = \dfrac{V_m}{V_c}$ (range typically 0 to 1, can exceed 1 in overmodulation) > - **Frequency Modulation Ratio:** $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_r}$ (an integer, usually an odd multiple of 3 in 3-phase systems) > - **Switching Frequency:** $f_s$ = carrier frequency $f_c$ (typically 2 kHz to 20 kHz for motor drives) # 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of PWM Schemes (KTU Module 3 Focus) The KTU syllabus specifically lists the following PWM techniques. We will analyze each in the structured logic format below. ### A. Single-Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM) - **What:** The inverter produces **one pulse per half-cycle** of the output. - **Where used:** Low-power, low-frequency applications like basic square-wave inverters. - **How:** The pulse width $2d$ is varied to control the RMS output voltage. The pulses are symmetrically placed about $\pi/2$ in a half-cycle. ### B. Multiple-Pulse Width Modulation (MPWM) - **What:** Several pulses of **equal width** are produced per half-cycle. - **Why:** Reduces harmonic distortion compared to SPWM by spreading the harmonic energy. - **How:** The number of pulses per half-cycle $N$ is set, and the equal pulse width is $2d = \dfrac{\pi}{N}$. ### C. Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM — Bipolar) - **What:** Pulse widths are **NOT equal**; they vary sinusoidally to approximate a sine wave. - **How:** The intersection of a sine reference and triangle carrier determines pulse widths. - **Why:** This gives the LOWEST harmonic distortion and is the workhorse of modern VFDs. ### D. Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM — Unipolar) - **What:** Each half-cycle uses **two complementary carrier-modulated legs**, producing pulses of **only one polarity** (+Vdc or –Vdc) per half-cycle instead of alternating ±Vdc. - **Why:** Even lower harmonic content than bipolar SPWM. ### E. Modified Sinusoidal PWM (MSPWM / Phase-Displacement PWM) - **What:** A 60° flat-top is added to the sine reference, where the reference is held at peak value. - **Why:** Improves DC bus utilization and reduces switching losses. ## 2.2 Operative Logic of Sinusoidal PWM Generation (Step-by-Step) 1. **Generate the reference:** A low-frequency sine wave $v_r(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_r t)$ at desired output frequency $f_r$. 2. **Generate the carrier:** A high-frequency triangular wave $v_c(t)$ of amplitude $V_c$ and frequency $f_c$. 3. **Compare** $v_r$ and $v_c$ continuously using a comparator. 4. **Rule:** If $v_r > v_c \Rightarrow$ Output = $+V_{dc}/2$ (upper switch ON). 5. **Rule:** If $v_r < v_c \Rightarrow$ Output = $-V_{dc}/2$ (lower switch ON). 6. The width of each pulse is **proportional to** the instantaneous value of the reference sine. 7. Over one fundamental cycle, the **average voltage** of all pulses traces out the sine wave. 8. **The fundamental amplitude** of the output is $M \times \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$ (line-to-neutral, in bipolar SPWM). 9. **The fundamental frequency** of the output equals $f_r$. 10. **Harmonics** are pushed to high frequencies around $m_f$, $2m_f$, $3m_f$, … which can be easily filtered by the motor's stator inductance. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **VFDs for AC induction motors and PMSMs:** Control V/Hz ratio for constant flux operation below base speed, and field-weakening above. - **Solar inverters:** Convert DC from PV panels to grid-quality AC. - **UPS systems:** Generate clean AC from battery DC. - **Audio amplifiers (Class D):** PWM encodes audio amplitude. - **EV traction inverters:** Deliver precision torque and speed control. ## 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---------------------|----------------------|-------|-------| | 1 | Modulation Index (Amplitude Ratio) | $M = \dfrac{V_m}{V_c}$ | unitless | $0 \le M \le 1$ in linear region | | 2 | Frequency Modulation Ratio | $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_r}$ | unitless | Odd integer, esp. multiple of 3 in 3-φ | | 3 | Pulse Width (variable, k-th pulse) | $\delta_k = \dfrac{\pi}{N} \left[1 + M \sin\left(\alpha_k\right)\right]$ | rad | $\alpha_k$ = midpoint of k-th pulse | | 4 | Single-Pulse RMS Output Voltage | $V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{2d}{\pi}}$ | V | $2d$ = pulse width in rad | | 5 | SPWM Peak Fundamental (line-neutral) | $V_{o1,max} = M \cdot \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$ | V | For bipolar SPWM | | 6 | SPWM Peak Fundamental (line-line) | $V_{LL,1,max} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} M V_{dc}$ | V | 3-φ bridge, linear region | | 7 | Max Linear Fundamental (line-line) | $V_{LL,1,max} = 0.866 \, V_{dc}$ | V | At $M = 1$ | | 8 | Overmodulation Limit | $V_{LL,1,max} = 1.0 \, V_{dc}$ | V | At $M = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ | | 9 | Square-Wave Limit (6-step mode) | $V_{LL,1,max} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 0.9 V_{dc}$ | V | M → ∞ limit | | 10 | DC Bus Utilization (max in linear SPWM) | $\dfrac{V_{LL,1,max}}{V_{dc}} = 0.866$ | ratio | The 13.4% headroom problem | | 11 | n-th Harmonic Amplitude (Fourier) | $V_n = \dfrac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi} \left[\cos(n\alpha_1) - \cos(n\alpha_2) + \cdots\right]$ | V | Generalized bipolar PWM | | 12 | Triangular Carrier Frequency | $f_c = m_f \cdot f_r$ | Hz | Determines switching loss | | 13 | Output Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_c}{m_f} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N_{counter}}$ | Hz | Set by timer resolution | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL — DC Bus Utilization:** A frequently asked question in KTU papers: *"Why can't a linear SPWM inverter deliver more than $0.866 V_{dc}$ to the load line-line?"* The answer is mathematical — at $M=1$, the fundamental of the modulated wave is bounded by $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_{dc}$. To go beyond, you need **overmodulation** (M > 1) or **SVPWM / Third-Harmonic Injection PWM** which boosts it to $\approx 1.0 V_{dc}$ — a 15.5% improvement. > [!NOTE] > **Use of `\vert` instead of `|`:** > In all markdown tables above, the absolute value notation is written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking the pipe-separated table syntax. This is required per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE formatting rules. # 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1 — RMS Output Voltage of Single-Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM) **Statement:** For a single-pulse modulated waveform of amplitude $V_s$ with pulse width $2d$ centered in each half-cycle, derive the RMS value of the output voltage. **Step 1 — Define the waveform over one half-cycle (0 to $\pi$):** The output is $+V_s$ in the interval $(\frac{\pi}{2} - d)$ to $(\frac{\pi}{2} + d)$, and zero elsewhere in the half-cycle. **Step 2 — RMS definition over one half-cycle:** $$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} v_o^2(\theta) \, d\theta$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the non-zero interval only:** $$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\frac{\pi}{2} - d}^{\frac{\pi}{2} + d} V_s^2 \, d\theta$$ **Step 4 — Perform the integration (constant integrand):** $$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi} \left[\theta\right]_{\frac{\pi}{2} - d}^{\frac{\pi}{2} + d}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the limits:** $$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi} \left[\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + d\right) - \left(\frac{\pi}{2} - d\right)\right] = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi} (2d)$$ **Step 6 — Take the square root:** $$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{2d}{\pi}}}$$ This is the **fundamental expression** for RMS output voltage in single-pulse modulation and is a high-yield KTU formula. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Fourier Series of Single-Pulse Waveform (General n-th Harmonic) **Step 1 — Symmetry argument:** The waveform has **half-wave symmetry** ($v(\theta + \pi) = -v(\theta)$), so the Fourier series contains **only odd harmonics** ($n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$). The DC component is zero. **Step 2 — General Fourier coefficient for sine term:** $$B_n = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} v_o(\theta) \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the waveform (only $+V_s$ in the pulse interval):** $$B_n = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_{\frac{\pi}{2} - d}^{\frac{\pi}{2} + d} V_s \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta$$ **Step 4 — Integrate:** $$B_n = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \left[-\frac{\cos(n\theta)}{n}\right]_{\frac{\pi}{2} - d}^{\frac{\pi}{2} + d}$$ $$B_n = \frac{2 V_s}{n\pi} \left[\cos\left(n\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - d\right)\right) - \cos\left(n\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + d\right)\right)\right]$$ **Step 5 — Apply cosine difference identity $\cos(A) - \cos(B) = 2 \sin\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{B-A}{2}\right)$:** $$A = n\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - d\right), \quad B = n\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + d\right)$$ $$\frac{A+B}{2} = \frac{n\pi}{2}, \quad \frac{B-A}{2} = nd$$ **Step 6 — Substitute and simplify using $\sin(n\pi/2)$:** $$B_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \sin\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right) \sin(nd)$$ **Step 7 — Final compact form:** $$\boxed{V_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \sin\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right) \sin(nd)}$$ **Step 8 — Practical observations:** - For **odd n:** $\sin(n\pi/2) = \pm 1$, so the harmonic exists. - For **even n:** $\sin(n\pi/2) = 0$, so **all even harmonics vanish** (confirms half-wave symmetry). - The **fundamental (n=1):** $V_1 = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sin(d)$. - Setting $V_1 = V_s$ (full fundamental) gives $\sin(d) = \pi/4 \Rightarrow d = \pi/2$, which is the **square wave limit**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Pulse Width Expression in Sinusoidal PWM **Step 1 — Set up the per-pulse geometry:** In sinusoidal PWM, the k-th pulse in a half-cycle is centered at angle $\alpha_k$, and the reference sine value at that midpoint is $M \sin(\alpha_k)$. **Step 2 — The variable duty ratio at the k-th pulse:** The pulse width is proportional to the reference value, so: $$\frac{\delta_k}{2d_{max}} = \frac{1 + M \sin(\alpha_k)}{2}$$ where $2d_{max} = \pi/N$ is the maximum width of an equal-width MPWM pulse. **Step 3 — Solve for $\delta_k$:** $$\boxed{\delta_k = \frac{\pi}{N} \left[1 + M \sin(\alpha_k)\right]}$$ **Step 4 — This is the canonical SPWM pulse width formula tested in KTU exams.** --- ## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Fundamental Output Voltage in Bipolar SPWM (DC Side → AC Side) **Step 1 — Switched output of one inverter leg:** $$v_{ao} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \cdot \text{sign}(v_r - v_c)$$ **Step 2 — Fourier analysis of the modulated wave gives the fundamental:** $$v_{ao,1}(t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \cdot M \sin(\omega_r t)$$ **Step 3 — For 3-phase load with isolated neutral, line-neutral voltage fundamental:** $$\boxed{V_{o1} = M \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Line-line voltage fundamental (multiply by $\sqrt{3}$):** $$V_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot M \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} M V_{dc}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical check at $M=1$:** $$V_{LL,1,max} = 0.866 \, V_{dc} \quad \text{(DC bus utilization = 86.6%)}$$ --- ## 3.5 ⚙️ Analytical Matrix: PWM Techniques Side-by-Side | Feature | Single-Pulse | Multiple-Pulse | Sinusoidal PWM | SVPWM (Third-Harm. Inj.) | |---------|--------------|----------------|----------------|--------------------------| | Pulses per half-cycle | 1 | N (e.g., 5, 7) | Variable (many) | Variable (many) | | Pulse Widths | Equal, variable | Equal, fixed | **Sine-modulated** | Sine + 3rd harmonic | | Harmonic Distortion | Highest | Moderate | Low | Lowest | | Switching Losses | Lowest | Moderate | Higher | Higher | | DC Bus Utilization | 0.637 Vdc (at M=1) | Higher | 0.866 Vdc (at M=1) | **1.0 Vdc (at M=1)** | | Control Complexity | Low | Low | Medium | High | | KTU Weightage | ★★★ (formula derivation) | ★★ | ★★★★★ (must know) | ★★★★ | # 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PWM Generation — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram captures the **functional signal flow** of a sinusoidal PWM generator, which is the most tested concept in this module. The diagram is designed to be safe under Mermaid syntax rules (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside quoted labels, no reserved keywords as node names). ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Sine Reference Generator
Frequency fr Amplitude Vm] --> B1[Comparator Block 1] A2[Triangle Carrier Generator
Frequency fc Amplitude Vc] --> B1 A3[Sine Reference B
fr phase shifted 120 deg] --> B2[Comparator Block 2] A2 --> B2 A4[Sine Reference C
fr phase shifted 240 deg] --> B3[Comparator Block 3] A2 --> B3 B1 --> C1[Gating Signal Phase A
Upper and Lower Switches] B2 --> C2[Gating Signal Phase B
Upper and Lower Switches] B3 --> C3[Gating Signal Phase C
Upper and Lower Switches] C1 --> D1[3 Phase VSI Inverter
IGBT or MOSFET Bridge] C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> E1[AC Motor Load
Induction or PMSM] D1 --> F1[DC Bus Vdc
Fixed Input] E1 --> G1[V fd Output
Controllable Magnitude and Frequency] ``` **Explanation of the flow:** - Three sine references (120° apart) are compared with **one common triangular carrier**. - Each comparator produces gating signals for one inverter leg. - The inverter chops the fixed DC bus into PWM pulses. - The motor integrates the pulses to perceive a clean sine wave of magnitude $M \cdot V_{dc}/2$ and frequency $f_r$. ## 4.2 PWM Switching Logic — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Read Reference vr and Carrier vc] --> B{Is vr greater than vc} B -- Yes --> C[Turn ON Upper Switch S1] B -- No --> D[Turn ON Lower Switch S4] C --> E[Output van equals plus Vdc divided by 2] D --> F[Output van equals minus Vdc divided by 2] E --> G[Wait One Carrier Cycle] F --> G G --> A ``` ## 4.3 Comparison of PWM Waveforms — Visual Block ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Single Pulse
1 pulse per half cycle] --> Q1[Harmonic spectrum
Dominant low order harmonics] P2[Multiple Pulse
N equal pulses per half cycle] --> Q2[Harmonic spectrum
Harmonics pushed to mf region] P3[Sinusoidal PWM
Sine modulated widths] --> Q3[Harmonic spectrum
Smooth fundamental low THD] P4[SVPWM
Space vector approach] --> Q4[Harmonic spectrum
15 percent higher DC utilization] ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual V/Hz Control via PWM — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD Fref[Frequency Reference fref Hz] --> Vcalc[Compute Vstar equals K times fref
Constant V over f ratio] Mc[Microcontroller DSP or FPGA] --> Mod[Compute Modulation Index Mstar] Vcalc --> Mod Mod --> PWMgen[PWM Generator with Sine Reference] PWMgen --> GateDrv[Gate Driver Circuit] GateDrv --> InvBridge[Three Phase Inverter Bridge] InvBridge --> ACmotor[AC Induction Motor] ACmotor --> SpeedSensor[Tachogenerator or Encoder] SpeedSensor --> PI[PI Speed Controller] PI --> Fref ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The closed loop maintains a constant V/f ratio, ensuring constant stator flux in the motor. Modulation index $M$ is updated in real time to track the voltage demand. This is the **core algorithm in every commercial VFD.** # 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1: Define Pulse Width Modulation. What is modulation index? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a technique used in power electronic converters (especially voltage source inverters) to vary the width of output pulses in a fixed switching period so that the average voltage delivered to the load follows a desired reference waveform (typically sinusoidal). The **modulation index** is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the reference (modulating) signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier (triangular) signal: $$M = \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$ In the linear region, $M$ lies between 0 and 1. It directly controls the RMS output voltage of the inverter. ### Question 2: What is the role of frequency modulation ratio ($m_f$) in SPWM? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The frequency modulation ratio is defined as $m_f = f_c / f_r$, where $f_c$ is the carrier (triangular) frequency and $f_r$ is the reference (modulating) frequency. Its roles are: - It determines the **number of pulses per half-cycle** of the output waveform. - Higher $m_f$ means **more pulses**, smoother output, lower harmonic distortion, but **higher switching losses** in the inverter. - For 3-phase systems, $m_f$ is chosen as an **odd multiple of 3** to ensure that the **triple-n harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th …) cancel** in the line-line voltage. - It also locates the dominant harmonic cluster at the sidebands of $m_f$, making them easier to filter. --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with INTERNAL CHOICE) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks with an internal choice (either-or). Each choice is divided into two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each. --- ### ❓ Question A (Choice 1) **A (a)** Derive the RMS output voltage expression for a single-pulse modulated inverter waveform. With $V_s = 200$ V and pulse width $2d = 120°$, compute the RMS output voltage. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the definition of RMS over one half-cycle:** $$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} v_o^2(\theta) \, d\theta$$ **Step 2 — The output is $+V_s$ only in the interval $[\pi/2 - d, \pi/2 + d]$:** $$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi} \int_{\pi/2 - d}^{\pi/2 + d} d\theta = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi} (2d)$$ **Step 3 — Final expression:** $$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{2d}{\pi}}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical computation:** Given $V_s = 200$ V and $2d = 120° = \frac{2\pi}{3}$ rad, so $d = \frac{\pi}{3}$. $$V_{o,rms} = 200 \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot (\pi/3)}{\pi}} = 200 \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$$ $$V_{o,rms} = 200 \times 0.8165 = \mathbf{163.3 \text{ V}}$$ **A (b)** Explain the principle of Sinusoidal PWM with a neat block diagram and waveform. State any two advantages. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Principle:** In SPWM, a low-frequency **sine reference wave** $v_r = V_m \sin(\omega_r t)$ is compared with a high-frequency **triangular carrier wave** $v_c$ of amplitude $V_c$ and frequency $f_c$. Whenever $v_r > v_c$, the upper switch of the inverter leg is turned ON and the output goes to $+V_{dc}/2$. When $v_r < v_c$, the lower switch turns ON and the output is $-V_{dc}/2$. **Step 2 — Pulse width variation:** The intersection of $v_r$ and $v_c$ occurs at different time instants in each cycle, so the resulting pulses have **unequal widths that vary sinusoidally**. **Step 3 — Block diagram reference:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1) **Step 4 — Advantages:** 1. The fundamental output voltage and frequency can be controlled **independently** by varying $M$ and $f_r$ respectively. 2. Lower **total harmonic distortion (THD)** compared to square-wave and single-pulse modulation. 3. Harmonic content is pushed to **high frequencies** near $m_f$, which are easy to filter using small motor stator inductances. 4. The **V/f ratio** required for constant-flux operation of AC motors is easily implemented. --- ### ❓ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) **B (a)** With a neat circuit and waveforms, explain the working of a single-phase full-bridge inverter using sinusoidal PWM. Show that the RMS value of the fundamental output voltage is $V_{o,1,rms} = M \cdot V_{dc} / \sqrt{2}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Circuit description:** A single-phase full-bridge VSI has four switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$. The diagonal pair $(S_1, S_3)$ and the other diagonal $(S_2, S_4)$ are complementary. The load is connected between the two midpoints. **Step 2 — SPWM gating:** The reference sine $v_r$ is compared with carrier $v_c$. The output of the comparator drives $S_1$ and $S_3$ simultaneously. The complementary signal drives $S_2$ and $S_4$. **Step 3 — Output voltage:** The output is $+V_{dc}$ when $S_1$ and $S_3$ are ON, and $-V_{dc}$ when $S_2$ and $S_4$ are ON. This is **bipolar SPWM**. **Step 4 — Fundamental analysis:** Fourier analysis of the bipolar PWM waveform gives the peak fundamental component: $$V_{o,1,peak} = M \cdot V_{dc}$$ (The factor of 2 vs. half-bridge arises because the full-bridge swings the full DC bus.) **Step 5 — RMS of fundamental:** $$\boxed{V_{o,1,rms} = \frac{V_{o,1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{M \cdot V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}}$$ **B (b)** A three-phase VSI uses SPWM with $V_{dc} = 600$ V and $M = 0.8$. Compute (i) the peak line-to-neutral fundamental voltage, (ii) the RMS line-to-line fundamental voltage, and (iii) the modulation index at which the inverter enters overmodulation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Peak line-to-neutral fundamental:** $$V_{ph,1,peak} = M \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = 0.8 \times \frac{600}{2} = \mathbf{240 \text{ V}}$$ **(ii) RMS line-to-line fundamental:** $$V_{LL,1,peak} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,1,peak} = \sqrt{3} \times 240 = 415.7 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{415.7}{\sqrt{2}} = \mathbf{294 \text{ V}}$$ **(iii) Overmodulation onset:** Overmodulation begins when the reference amplitude exceeds the carrier amplitude, i.e., at $M = 1$. In the linear region, $M \in [0,1]$. For the 3-phase bridge, the maximum achievable linear fundamental (line-line) is $0.866 V_{dc}$. Beyond $M = 1$, the system enters overmodulation and approaches the 6-step square-wave limit of $V_{LL,1} = 1.0 \, V_{dc}$ (or $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 0.9 V_{dc}$ for the natural square-wave mode). --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks in KTU exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** when converting between peak and RMS of the fundamental in Part B derivations. KTU examiners specifically test this conversion. > 2. **Confusing single-pulse and multiple-pulse formulas:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{2d/\pi}$ is for SPWM only. For MPWM, the formula has $N$ in it. > 3. **Using M and $m_f$ interchangeably:** M is the **amplitude** ratio, $m_f$ is the **frequency** ratio. They are different! Mix-ups are a guaranteed 0. > 4. **Not mentioning the role of $m_f$ being a multiple of 3** in 3-phase SPWM — a 2-mark question lost every year. > 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU valuation key explicitly deducts for missing units. > 6. **Writing the 6-step limit as $V_{dc}$** instead of the correct $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 0.9 V_{dc}$ — this is a frequently-asked 3-mark nuance. > 7. **In SPWM pulse-width derivation, failing to define $\alpha_k$** as the midpoint of the k-th pulse — examiners award partial credit for proper variable definition. --- ## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **PWM** = varying pulse widths within a fixed period to control average output. - ✅ **Modulation Index** $M = V_m / V_c$ — controls output voltage magnitude. - ✅ **Frequency Modulation Ratio** $m_f = f_c / f_r$ — controls harmonic location. - ✅ **Single-Pulse RMS:** $V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{2d/\pi}$. - ✅ **MPWM Pulse width (equal):** $2d = \pi/N$ where $N$ = pulses per half-cycle. - ✅ **SPWM Pulse width (k-th):** $\delta_k = \dfrac{\pi}{N}\left[1 + M \sin(\alpha_k)\right]$. - ✅ **Fourier of SPWM:** $V_n = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \sin(n\pi/2)\sin(nd)$. - ✅ **Bipolar SPWM fundamental (line-neutral):** $V_{o1,peak} = M \cdot V_{dc}/2$. - ✅ **Bipolar SPWM fundamental (line-line, 3-φ):** $V_{LL,1,peak} = (\sqrt{3}/2) M V_{dc}$. - ✅ **DC bus utilization in linear SPWM:** maximum **86.6 %** of $V_{dc}$. - ✅ **SVPWM / 3rd-harmonic injection** extends this to **100 %** of $V_{dc}$. - ✅ **6-step square-wave limit:** $V_{LL,1,peak} = (2\sqrt{2}/\pi) V_{dc} \approx 0.9 V_{dc}$. - ✅ **3-phase rule:** $m_f$ must be **odd multiple of 3** to cancel triplen harmonics in $V_{LL}$. - ✅ **Switching frequency $f_c$:** typically **2 kHz to 20 kHz** for industrial drives (above audible range, below excessive switching loss). - ✅ **Sine-triangle intersection** is the physical mechanism of pulse generation. - ✅ **Harmonic cluster** appears at sidebands of $m_f, 2m_f, 3m_f \ldots$ - ✅ **Application domains:** VFDs, UPS, solar inverters, EV traction, Class-D audio. - ✅ **V/f control** is the classical open-loop method; field-oriented control (FOC) uses PWM for closed-loop vector control. - ✅ **Overmodulation** = M > 1, nonlinear region, bridges SPWM to square wave. - ✅ **Unipolar SPWM** halves the effective harmonic cluster frequency and gives lower $dv/dt$.

topics.title: Single Pulse Width, Multiple Pulse Width, and Sine PWM, Elimination of Harmonics, and Variable Voltage Variable Frequency Drive (Block Diagram Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Pulse Width, Multiple Pulse Width, and Sine PWM, Elimination of Harmonics, and Variable Voltage Variable Frequency Drive (Block Diagram Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to PWM Techniques in Power Electronics

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a technique used in static power converters (especially DC-AC inverters) to synthesize a quasi-sine wave from a DC source by switching the inverter semiconductor devices ON and OFF at high frequency, controlling the **width (duration)** of each output pulse. The fundamental amplitude is controlled by varying the pulse widths while keeping the DC bus voltage constant.

In the context of **KTU Module 3** for the course *Electric Drives and Control*, PWM is the critical bridge that connects the **DC link** of an inverter to the **AC motor** in a variable speed drive. The specific techniques covered are:

1. **Single Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM-of-the-single-pulse kind)**
2. **Multiple Pulse Width Modulation (MPWM)**
3. **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)**
4. **Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE) PWM**
5. **Variable Voltage Variable Frequency (VVVF) Drive** — Block diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> *Pulse Width Modulation is a method of controlling the average power delivered to a load by switching the supply between the DC source and load. In drive applications, PWM techniques are used in voltage source inverters (VSI) to vary the output voltage and frequency, while simultaneously reducing the harmonic distortion of the output waveform.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tap with Pulses"

Imagine a water tap (inverter output) that can be fully **ON** or fully **OFF** (no intermediate state). You want to deliver an **average flow** that varies like a smooth sine wave (motor needs sinusoidal voltage).

- **Single Pulse:** You open the tap once for a long duration in each half-cycle (like a single gulp of water). Crude, causes large ripples.
- **Multiple Pulses:** You open and close the tap many times within a half-cycle. Better — smoother average.
- **Sine PWM:** You open the tap in pulses whose widths themselves vary sinusoidally — wide in the middle of the half-cycle, narrow near the edges. The **average flow now traces a perfect sine wave** — the motor "feels" a clean sinusoidal supply.
- **Harmonic Elimination:** You deliberately *chop* the waveform at specific angles (notch positions) to cancel out the most harmful harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th).
- **VVVF Drive:** Like a car's accelerator — pressing harder increases both **flow (voltage)** and **speed (frequency)** together to keep the motor healthy.

## 1.3 Why PWM in Electric Drives?

| Issue in Drives | How PWM Solves It |
|---|---|
| Fixed DC link voltage | PWM controls RMS output voltage |
| Need variable frequency | PWM carrier frequency is decoupled from fundamental |
| Harmonics cause torque pulsations | PWM shapes spectrum to push harmonics to high frequencies (easy to filter) |
| Audible motor noise | High switching frequency (audible range) |
| Switching losses | Careful pulse placement minimises commutations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** For a 3-phase 50 Hz system, the output fundamental frequency is **f₀ = 50 Hz** and the period is **T = 20 ms**. The standard DC link voltage in industrial drives is typically **V_dc = 540 V or 600 V**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM-of-single-pulse)

### 2.1.1 Concept
A **single pulse** of width **2d** is generated per half-cycle. The pulse is symmetrically placed about the centre of the half-cycle (π/2 for a 1-phase bridge). The gating signals to the inverter switches are derived by comparing a **reference rectangular wave** with a **triangular carrier**.

- The output is a **quasi-square wave** with one controllable pulse per half-cycle.
- Number of pulses per half-cycle: **p = 1**
- Number of pulses per full cycle: **N = 2**
- **Disadvantage:** Harmonic content is very high because only fundamental is dominant; lower-order harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th) are large.

### 2.1.2 Fourier Analysis — Output Voltage

The output waveform is an **odd**, **quarter-wave symmetric** function (after careful choice of origin). The general Fourier series contains only **odd sine** terms:

$$
v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\omega t)
$$

The **Fourier coefficient** for an **n-th harmonic** of a single-pulse waveform of height V_s and total pulse width 2d is given by:

$$
B_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n \pi} \cos(n\alpha) \quad \text{where} \quad \alpha = \frac{\pi}{2} - d
$$

The **RMS value of the fundamental output voltage**:

$$
V_{o1(\text{rms})} = \frac{4 V_s}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi} \cos(\alpha) = 0.90 \, V_s \cos(\alpha)
$$

The **RMS value of the n-th harmonic**:

$$
V_{on(\text{rms})} = \frac{4 V_s}{\sqrt{2}\,n\pi} \cos(n\alpha)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** To **eliminate the n-th harmonic**, set the pulse width such that **cos(nα) = 0**, i.e., **nα = π/2**, so **α = π/(2n)**. Hence the pulse width becomes **2d = π − 2α = π − π/n = π(n−1)/n**. For example, eliminating the 3rd harmonic gives **2d = 2π/3 (i.e., 120°)**.

## 2.2 Multiple Pulse Width Modulation (MPWM)

### 2.2.1 Concept
Several pulses of equal width are generated per half-cycle. If there are **N pulses per half-cycle**, and the pulse width is **2d**, the duty cycle is:

$$
D = \frac{2d}{(T/2)/N} = \frac{2d \cdot N \cdot 2}{T} = \frac{4Nd}{T}
$$

For a periodic square pulse train, the Fourier series is:

$$
v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\omega t)
$$

$$
B_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n \pi} \sin\!\left(\frac{n \pi}{N}\right) \cos(n\alpha)
$$

where:
- **N** = number of pulses per half-cycle
- **2d** = width of each pulse (in radians)
- **α = (π/2) − d** (the angle measured from the centre of the pulse to the start of the half-cycle)

### 2.2.2 RMS Output Voltage (Fundamental)

$$
V_{o1(\text{rms})} = \frac{4 V_s}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi} \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{N}\right) \cos(\alpha)
$$

### 2.2.3 Harmonic Order of the Dominant Harmonic

The dominant harmonics cluster around the **N-th harmonic and its odd multiples** — i.e., around $n = N, 3N, 5N, \dots$ This is the key advantage of MPWM: low-order harmonics are eliminated, leaving only high-frequency harmonics that are easy to filter.

## 2.3 Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)

### 2.3.1 Concept
A **sinusoidal reference wave** (modulating signal) of frequency **f_r** and peak amplitude **A_r** is compared with a **triangular carrier wave** of frequency **f_c** and peak amplitude **A_c**. The intersections determine the switching instants.

### 2.3.2 Two Key Modulation Parameters

**1) Amplitude Modulation Ratio (Modulation Index):**

$$
m_a = \frac{A_r}{A_c}
$$

For **linear region** (overmodulation-free): $0 \le m_a \le 1$. For $m_a > 1$, the inverter enters overmodulation; for $m_a \gg 1$, output becomes a square wave.

**2) Frequency Modulation Ratio:**

$$
m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_r}
$$

For **synchronous PWM**, $m_f$ is an **odd integer** (typically 15, 21, 33, 63, …) to ensure half-wave and quarter-wave symmetry. This eliminates all even harmonics.

### 2.3.3 RMS Output Voltage (Fundamental, in linear region)

For a **single-phase** full-bridge SPWM inverter:

$$
V_{o1(\text{rms})} = \frac{m_a \, V_s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

For a **three-phase** SPWM inverter (with $\sqrt{3}/2$ due to phase shift):

$$
V_{o1(\text{line,rms})} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \, m_a \, \frac{V_s}{2} = 0.612 \, m_a \, V_s
$$

where **V_s** is the DC source voltage (DC bus voltage).

### 2.3.4 Pole Voltage, Phase Voltage, Line Voltage Relations

For a 3-phase VSI in linear modulation with SPWM:

$$
V_{L1(\text{rms})} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \, m_a \, V_{dc} = 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In the **overmodulation region** ($m_a > 1$), additional harmonics appear. At $m_a = 3.24$, the output becomes a pure square wave. KTU often asks for the maximum linear-modulation index for which the output remains sinusoidal — answer is **$m_a = 1$**.

## 2.4 Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE) PWM

### 2.4.1 Concept
In SHE-PWM, the **switching angles (notch positions)** $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \dots, \alpha_N$ are pre-computed such that the **fundamental is set to a desired value** and **specific low-order harmonics are forced to zero**. This gives the **lowest possible Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** for a given switching frequency.

### 2.4.2 General Formulation
The output voltage waveform of a quarter-wave symmetric waveform with N switching angles per quarter-cycle has Fourier coefficients:

$$
B_n = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n \pi} \left[ 1 - 2 \sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^{k-1} \cos(n \alpha_k) \right]
$$

The constraints are:

$$
0 < \alpha_1 < \alpha_2 < \alpha_3 < \dots < \alpha_N < \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

- **Equation 1 (Fundamental amplitude):**
$$
B_1 = m_a \cdot \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi} \quad \text{(Desired value)}
$$

- **Equations 2 to N (Harmonic elimination):**
$$
B_5 = 0, \quad B_7 = 0, \quad B_{11} = 0, \dots
$$

### 2.4.3 Worked KTU Examples

**Case 1: N = 1 (one notch per quarter-cycle)**
- Single equation: $B_1 = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi}[1 - 2\cos(\alpha_1)]$
- For maximum fundamental (square-wave limit): $\alpha_1 = 0$ → $B_1 = -\frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}$

**Case 2: N = 2 (two notches per quarter-cycle)**
- Two unknowns: $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$
- Two equations: control fundamental + eliminate 5th harmonic
$$
1 - 2\cos(\alpha_1) + 2\cos(\alpha_2) = m_a
$$
$$
1 - 2\cos(5\alpha_1) + 2\cos(5\alpha_2) = 0
$$

**Case 3: N = 3 (three notches per quarter-cycle)**
- Three equations: fundamental, eliminate 5th, eliminate 7th
- Trigonometric (transcendental) equations — solved **iteratively** using Newton-Raphson method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The transcendental nature of these equations means there is **no closed-form analytical solution** — they must be solved numerically. The solutions are then **stored in a lookup table** in the DSP/microcontroller firmware.

## 2.5 Variable Voltage Variable Frequency (VVVF) Drive

### 2.5.1 Concept
A VVVF drive is the **complete closed-loop speed control system** for an AC motor (typically 3-phase induction motor). The variable voltage is achieved through **PWM control of the inverter** (covered above), while the variable frequency is achieved by varying the **frequency of the reference sine wave** to the SPWM modulator.

### 2.5.2 V/Hz Control (Volts per Hertz) — Constant Flux Principle

For an induction motor, the **air-gap flux** is given by:

$$
\phi = \frac{V}{4.44 \, f_1 \, N_1 \, k_w}
$$

To maintain **constant flux** (and hence rated torque capability), the ratio **V/f** must be kept **constant** below base speed. This is the **constant torque region**.

### 2.5.3 Three Operating Regions

| Region | Speed Range | V/f Ratio | Torque | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Torque** | 0 → base speed ($f_0$) | Constant (V/f = const.) | Rated | Increases with f |
| **Constant Power** | $f_0$ → 2$f_0$ | V increases at fixed f_max (V/f decreases) | Decreases (1/f) | Constant (rated) |
| **Constant V, High-f Series** | > 2$f_0$ | V saturated at V_max | Decreases (1/f²) | Decreases |

### 2.5.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SPWM modulation index | $m_a = A_r / A_c$ | $0 \le m_a \le 1$ for linear region |
| 2 | SPWM frequency ratio | $m_f = f_c / f_r$ | Odd integer for synchronous PWM |
| 3 | Single-pulse fundamental RMS | $V_{o1} = 0.90 V_s \cos(\alpha)$ | $V_s$ = DC source |
| 4 | Multi-pulse fundamental RMS | $V_{o1} = \frac{4 V_s}{\sqrt{2}\pi} \sin(\pi/N) \cos(\alpha)$ | N pulses per half-cycle |
| 5 | SPWM 1-φ fundamental RMS | $V_{o1} = m_a V_s / \sqrt{2}$ | Linear region |
| 6 | SPWM 3-φ line voltage RMS | $V_{L1} = 0.612 m_a V_{dc}$ | $V_{dc}$ = DC bus |
| 7 | Harmonic elimination angle | $\cos(n\alpha) = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = \pi/(2n)$ | For single-pulse |
| 8 | Flux relation | $\phi \propto V/f$ | To keep $\phi$ constant, $V/f = \text{const.}$ |
| 9 | Stator voltage (V/f control) | $V = V_0 + (V_{\text{rated}} - V_0)(f/f_{\text{rated}})$ | $V_0$ = voltage boost at low f |
| 10 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | P = number of poles |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SPWM** is the workhorse of **uninterrupted power supplies (UPS)**, **solar micro-inverters**, and **low-power motor drives** (fans, pumps).
- **MPWM** is rarely used in modern drives; it is the conceptual stepping stone to SPWM.
- **SHE-PWM** is used in **high-power drives** (HVDC, large industrial motors) where low switching frequency is mandated by switching losses and the **lowest THD is critical**.
- **VVVF drives** are universal: from **elevators and escalators** (constant torque region) to **machine tool spindles** (constant power region) to **electric vehicle traction**.
