# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Pulse Modulation — Gating Signal Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPSources["Reference and Carrier Generation"]
        REF[Rectangular Reference Wave]
        CAR[Triangular Carrier Wave]
    end
    REF --> CMP1
    CAR --> CMP1
    CMP1[Comparator OP1] --> GATE[Gating Pulses to Inverter Switches]
    GATE --> INV[Single Phase Full Bridge Inverter]
    INV --> LOAD[AC Motor Load]
    INV --> OUT[Quasi Square Wave Output with Single Pulse per Half Cycle]
```

> **Block Description:** A rectangular reference wave of desired fundamental frequency is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier. The comparator output is a train of gating pulses. Each pulse has width $2d$, controllable by the duty cycle of the rectangular reference.

## 4.2 Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) — Natural Sampling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPWMGen["SPWM Signal Generator"]
        SIN[Sinusoidal Reference f_r Amplitude A_r]
        TRI[Triangular Carrier f_c Amplitude A_c]
    end
    SIN --> CMP2
    TRI --> CMP2
    CMP2[Comparator OP2] --> GATE2[PWM Gating Pulses]
    GATE2 --> VSI[Three Phase VSI Inverter]
    VSI --> VOUT[Variable Voltage Variable Frequency AC Output]
    VSI --> FB[Current and Speed Feedback]
    FB --> CTRL[Drive Controller]
    CTRL --> SIN
```

> **Block Description:** A sinusoidal reference (slow-varying) is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier. Intersection points define the **switching instants**. The result is a 2-level PWM waveform whose fundamental tracks the reference sinusoid with amplitude proportional to $m_a = A_r/A_c$.

## 4.3 Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHEArch["SHE PWM Generator"]
        LUTROM[Lookup Table ROM with Pre computed Angles]
        MA[m_a Reference]
        FRQ[Frequency Reference f_r]
    end
    LUTROM --> SEL
    MA --> SEL
    SEL[Angle Selector] --> TIMER[Timer and Counter for Switching Angles]
    FRQ --> TIMER
    TIMER --> GATE3[Gating Pulses for Inverter]
    GATE3 --> HVSI[High Power Inverter]
    HVSI --> HLOAD[High Power Motor or Grid]
```

> **Block Description:** Switching angles $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, ..., \alpha_N$ are pre-computed offline (for the desired $m_a$ and harmonic elimination order) and stored in a ROM. The real-time controller reads the angles based on $m_a$ and frequency reference, generating precise gating pulses. The transcendental equations are solved **only once** at design time.

## 4.4 VVVF Drive — Complete Block Diagram (KTU Board Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUP["Power Supply Section"]
        ACMAINS[3 Phase AC Mains 50 Hz]
        RECT[Uncontrolled Diode Rectifier 6 Pulse Bridge]
        DCBUS[DC Link Filter C LC]
    end
    subgraph CTRL["Control Section"]
        REFV[V Reference Volt]
        REFF[F Reference Hz]
        VF[V over f Generator with Boost]
        CARV[Triangular Carrier Generation]
        COMPV[Comparator for SPWM]
    end
    subgraph POW["Power Section"]
        INV[Three Phase VSI Inverter with IGBTs]
        GATE[Gating Circuit and Driver]
    end
    subgraph FBK["Feedback Section"]
        MS[Motor Speed Sensor Encoder or Tacho]
        CS[Current Sensor CT or Shunt]
    end
    subgraph LOAD["Load"]
        IM[3 Phase Induction Motor]
        MECH[Mech Load Pump Fan Compressor]
    end
    ACMAINS --> RECT
    RECT --> DCBUS
    DCBUS --> INV
    REFV --> VF
    REFF --> VF
    VF --> COMPV
    CARV --> COMPV
    COMPV --> GATE
    GATE --> INV
    INV --> IM
    IM --> MECH
    MS --> VF
    CS --> VF
    IM --> MS
    INV --> CS
```

> **Block-by-Block Description (for KTU 14-mark answers):**
> 1. **3-φ AC Mains** → 415 V, 50 Hz input.
> 2. **Diode Rectifier** → Converts AC to DC. Output = 1.35 × $V_{LL}$ = 560 V (line-to-line) for 415 V mains.
> 3. **DC Link Filter** (C or LC) → Smooths rectified DC to nearly ripple-free.
> 4. **V/f Generator** → Computes required voltage from frequency reference using $V = V_0 + (V_{\text{rated}} - V_0)(f/f_{\text{rated}})$.
> 5. **Triangular Carrier Generator** → Fixed high frequency (e.g., 2 kHz – 20 kHz).
> 6. **Comparator** → Generates SPWM gating signals.
> 7. **Gate Driver + IGBT Inverter** → Converts DC to 3-phase variable-V, variable-f AC.
> 8. **Induction Motor** → Mechanical output.
> 9. **Speed + Current Feedback** → Closes the loop for accurate speed regulation.

## 4.5 Modulation Method Comparison — Mermaid Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[PWM Modulation Methods]
    ROOT --> SP[Single Pulse SPWM]
    ROOT --> MP[Multiple Pulse MPWM]
    ROOT --> SIN[Sinusoidal SPWM]
    ROOT --> SHE[Selective Harmonic Elimination SHE]
    SP --> SP1[1 Pulse per Half Cycle]
    SP --> SP2[Crude Spectrum]
    SP --> SP3[Used in Simple Square Wave Inverters]
    MP --> MP1[N Pulses per Half Cycle]
    MP --> MP2[Better Low Order Harmonics]
    MP --> MP3[Superseded by SPWM]
    SIN --> SIN1[Sinusoidal Modulating Signal]
    SIN --> SIN2[ma and mf Parameters]
    SIN --> SIN3[Industry Standard for Drives]
    SHE --> SHE1[Pre Computed Switching Angles]
    SHE --> SHE2[Zero Specific Harmonics]
    SHE --> SHE3[Used in High Power Applications]
```

> **Caption:** This decision tree summarises when each PWM technique is used. SPWM is the workhorse; SHE is the premium choice for high-power systems; single-pulse and multi-pulse are mainly academic/legacy.

## 4.6 Operating Regions of VVVF Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R1["Region 1 Constant Torque"]
        R1F[0 to f_rated 50 Hz]
        R1V[Voltage Ramps Linearly with f]
        R1T[Torque at Rated]
        R1P[Power Increases with f]
    end
    subgraph R2["Region 2 Constant Power"]
        R2F[f_rated to 2f_rated 50 to 100 Hz]
        R2V[Voltage Clamped at V_rated]
        R2T[Torque Decreases as 1 over f]
        R2P[Power at Rated]
    end
    subgraph R3["Region 3 Series"]
        R3F[Above 2f_rated]
        R3V[Voltage at V_rated]
        R3T[Torque Decreases as 1 over f squared]
        R3P[Power Decreases]
    end
    R1 --> R2 --> R3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) and state its advantages in electric drives.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a technique of controlling the average voltage delivered to a load by switching the supply between ON and OFF states at high frequency, with the duration (width) of the ON-state controlled to produce a desired average.

**Advantages in electric drives:**
1. **Variable voltage and variable frequency** can be obtained from a fixed DC source.
2. **Lower harmonic distortion** in the output current (and hence reduced torque pulsations).
3. **Improved power factor** at the AC mains side.
4. **Reduced acoustic noise** due to high switching frequency (above audible range).
5. **Regeneration capability** — bi-directional power flow possible.

---

### **Question 2: What is meant by "modulation index" in SPWM? What is its range in the linear region?**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **modulation index (m_a)** in Sinusoidal PWM is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the **sinusoidal reference (modulating) signal** to the peak amplitude of the **triangular carrier signal**:

$$
m_a = \frac{A_r}{A_c}
$$

**Linear region range:** $0 \le m_a \le 1$

- For $m_a \le 1$, the PWM output is in the **linear region** and the fundamental output voltage is directly proportional to $m_a$.
- For $m_a > 1$, the inverter enters the **overmodulation region** and additional harmonics appear.
- At $m_a = 3.24$ (extreme overmodulation), the output waveform degenerates into a pure **square wave**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the working of **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** for a single-phase full-bridge inverter with the help of necessary waveforms. Derive the expression for the RMS value of the fundamental output voltage in terms of the modulation index $m_a$ and the DC input voltage $V_s$.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A single-phase full-bridge SPWM inverter has $V_s = 230$ V DC. The modulation index is $m_a = 0.85$ and the frequency modulation ratio is $m_f = 15$. Find:
- (i) RMS value of the fundamental output voltage.
- (ii) Frequency of the output fundamental and carrier.
- (iii) RMS value of the 15th harmonic component.

---

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Working of SPWM

**Waveform Description:**

A **sinusoidal reference wave** $v_r(t) = A_r \sin(\omega_r t)$ of frequency $f_r$ (the desired output frequency) is compared with a **triangular carrier wave** $v_c(t)$ of frequency $f_c$ and amplitude $A_c$ (much higher than $A_r$).

The comparator output is **HIGH** when $v_r > v_c$ and **LOW** when $v_r < v_c$. This comparator output serves as the **gating signal** for the inverter switches.

**Switching Logic for a 1-φ Full-Bridge Inverter:**

- When comparator output is HIGH: switches S1 and S2 are ON; S3 and S4 are OFF → output = $+V_s$.
- When comparator output is LOW: switches S3 and S4 are ON; S1 and S2 are OFF → output = $-V_s$.

The result is a **two-level PWM waveform** switching between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$.

**PWM Characteristics:**
- The **width of each pulse** varies sinusoidally — widest at the peak of the sine reference and narrowest at the zero crossings.
- The **number of pulses per half-cycle** = $m_f / 2$ (for $m_f$ odd).
- The **fundamental component** of this PWM waveform is a sine wave with peak amplitude $\hat{V}_{o1} = m_a \cdot V_s$.

**Derivation of RMS Fundamental Voltage:**

In the linear region ($m_a \le 1$), the PWM process can be analysed as a linear amplifier. The peak amplitude of the fundamental is:

$$
\hat{V}_{o1} = m_a \cdot V_s
$$

The RMS value of the fundamental is:

$$
V_{o1(\text{rms})} = \frac{\hat{V}_{o1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{m_a V_s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Block diagram of SPWM inverter:**

```
Sinusoidal Reference → Comparator ← Triangular Carrier
                            ↓
                       Gating Pulses
                            ↓
                  Single-Phase Full-Bridge Inverter
                            ↓
                    PWM Output Waveform
```

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $V_s = 230$ V (DC source)
- $m_a = 0.85$
- $m_f = 15$
- Assume output fundamental frequency $f_r = 50$ Hz (standard)

**Solution:**

**(i) RMS value of fundamental output voltage:**

$$
V_{o1(\text{rms})} = \frac{m_a V_s}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.85 \times 230}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{195.5}{1.414} \approx 138.2 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Frequency of output and carrier:**

- Output fundamental: $f_r = 50$ Hz
- Carrier frequency: $f_c = m_f \times f_r = 15 \times 50 = 750$ Hz

**(iii) RMS value of 15th harmonic:**

For SPWM, the dominant harmonics appear at the **carrier frequency and its sidebands** — i.e., at $n = m_f = 15$, and at $n = m_f \pm 2 = 13$ and $17$. The amplitude of the dominant $m_f$-th harmonic (approximation for large $m_f$):

$$
\hat{V}_{o,m_f} \approx \frac{4 V_s}{m_f \pi} J_0\!\left(\frac{m_a m_f \pi}{2}\right) \approx \frac{4 \times 230}{15 \pi} \times 1 = \frac{920}{47.12} \approx 19.5 \text{ V (peak)}
$$

The RMS value:

$$
V_{o,15(\text{rms})} = \frac{19.5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 13.8 \text{ V}
$$

This harmonic is at $15 \times 50 = 750$ Hz, which is easily filtered by a small LC filter.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **Single Pulse Width Modulation** technique for a 1-phase full-bridge inverter. Derive the expression for the amplitude of the $n$-th harmonic component. How is the 3rd harmonic eliminated in this scheme?

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A 3-phase induction motor drive is controlled by a VVVF inverter with V/f = constant. The rated values are: 415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole, 1440 rpm. Calculate:
- (i) The synchronous speed and slip at rated condition.
- (ii) The voltage required at 25 Hz and 75 Hz (with voltage boost of 30 V).
- (iii) The synchronous speed at 75 Hz.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Single-Pulse Modulation

**Concept:**

In **single-pulse modulation**, the output of a 1-phase full-bridge inverter is a single rectangular pulse of width $2d$ per half-cycle, centred about $\pi/2$ (i.e., 90°). The output waveform alternates between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ (or 0 and $V_s$ for half-bridge).

**Waveform Properties:**
- **Odd** function: only sine terms in Fourier series.
- **Quarter-wave symmetric**: only **odd** harmonics present.

**Fourier Analysis:**

The output is:

$$
v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\omega t)
$$

The amplitude of the $n$-th harmonic (derived in SECTION 3.1):

$$
B_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi} \cos(n\alpha)
$$

where $\alpha = \frac{\pi}{2} - d$ is the angle from the centre of the pulse to the start of the half-cycle.

**Fundamental RMS:**

$$
V_{o1(\text{rms})} = \frac{4 V_s}{\sqrt{2}\,n\pi} \cos(\alpha) = 0.90 \, V_s \cos(\alpha)
$$

**Elimination of the 3rd Harmonic:**

To eliminate the 3rd harmonic, set $B_3 = 0$:

$$
\cos(3\alpha) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3\alpha = \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{\pi}{6} = 30°
$$

Therefore, the pulse width is:

$$
2d = \pi - 2\alpha = \pi - \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3} = 120°
$$

Hence, a **120° pulse** eliminates the **3rd harmonic**.

**In general**, eliminating the $n$-th harmonic requires:

$$
\cos(n\alpha) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{\pi}{2n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2d = \pi - \frac{\pi}{n} = \frac{\pi(n-1)}{n}
$$

| Harmonic to Eliminate | $\alpha$ | Pulse Width $2d$ |
|---|---|---|
| 3rd | 30° | 120° |
| 5th | 18° | 144° |
| 7th | 12.86° | 154.3° |
| 9th | 10° | 160° |

---

#### Part (b) — VVVF Drive Numerical

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{rated}} = 415$ V, $f_{\text{rated}} = 50$ Hz
- $P = 4$ poles, $N_{\text{rated}} = 1440$ rpm
- Voltage boost $V_0 = 30$ V

**Solution:**

**(i) Synchronous speed and slip at rated condition:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\text{Slip} = s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 = 4\%
$$

**(ii) Voltage at 25 Hz and 75 Hz:**

Using the V/f equation:

$$
V(f) = V_0 + \left(\frac{V_{\text{rated}} - V_0}{f_{\text{rated}}}\right) \cdot f
$$

**At 25 Hz** (constant torque region):

$$
V(25) = 30 + \left(\frac{415 - 30}{50}\right) \times 25 = 30 + \frac{385}{50} \times 25 = 30 + 192.5 = 222.5 \text{ V}
$$

**At 75 Hz** (constant power region — voltage clamped at rated):

Since 75 Hz > 50 Hz, the inverter has reached rated voltage:

$$
V(75) = V_{\text{rated}} = 415 \text{ V}
$$

The V/f ratio at 75 Hz is $415/75 = 5.53$ V/Hz (lower than rated $415/50 = 8.3$ V/Hz) — flux is **weakened**.

**(iii) Synchronous speed at 75 Hz:**

$$
N_s(75) = \frac{120 \times 75}{4} = 2250 \text{ rpm}
$$

The rotor speed will be slightly less (e.g., at 4% slip): $N_r = 2250 \times (1 - 0.04) = 2160$ rpm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 4 vs 2 in the single-pulse formula.** The $B_n = (4V_s/n\pi) \cos(n\alpha)$ form is for **quarter-wave symmetric waveform normalised over T/4**. Using $2V_s/n\pi$ without the quarter-wave factor is a common error.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_s$ (DC source) with $V_{dc}$ (DC bus).** In 1-phase full-bridge, $V_s$ is the DC source, so $V_{dc} = V_s$. In 3-phase, $V_{dc} = V_s$ (the rectifier output) but the per-phase peak is $V_{dc}/2$.
> 3. **Confusing modulation index with frequency ratio.** $m_a$ controls voltage, $m_f$ controls harmonic content — do not swap them.
> 4. **Forgetting V/f boost in low-frequency operation.** If you just use $V = (V_{\text{rated}}/f_{\text{rated}}) \times f$ without the boost $V_0$, you will under-flux the motor at low speeds.
> 5. **Stating "linear region" but using formulas beyond $m_a = 1$.** The formula $V_{o1} = m_a V_s / \sqrt{2}$ is valid only for $m_a \le 1$.
> 6. **In SHE-PWM, claiming closed-form solutions exist.** Always mention "solved numerically using Newton-Raphson method and stored in lookup table."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bulletins:**

### **A. Single Pulse Modulation**
- ✅ One pulse of width $2d$ per half-cycle.
- ✅ Harmonics: only **odd** (quarter-wave symmetry).
- ✅ Formula: $B_n = (4V_s/n\pi)\cos(n\alpha)$ where $\alpha = \pi/2 - d$.
- ✅ To eliminate $n$-th harmonic: $2d = \pi(n-1)/n$.
- ✅ Application: simple square-wave inverters; rarely used in modern drives.

### **B. Multiple Pulse Modulation**
- ✅ $N$ equal-width pulses per half-cycle.
- ✅ Formula: $B_n = (4V_s/n\pi)\sin(n\pi/N)\cos(n\alpha)$.
- ✅ Dominant harmonics at $n = N, 2N, 3N, ...$ (high frequency).
- ✅ Limitation: equal pulse widths cause sub-optimal harmonic profile.

### **C. Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)**
- ✅ Sinusoidal reference vs triangular carrier.
- ✅ $m_a = A_r/A_c$ (amplitude ratio) — range $[0, 1]$ linear.
- ✅ $m_f = f_c/f_r$ (frequency ratio) — odd integer, e.g., 15, 21, 33.
- ✅ 1-φ RMS fundamental: $V_{o1} = m_a V_s/\sqrt{2}$.
- ✅ 3-φ line RMS fundamental: $V_{L1} = 0.612 m_a V_{dc}$.
- ✅ Dominant harmonic at $n = m_f$ and $n = m_f \pm 2$.
- ✅ Application: **universal** — UPS, solar, drives.

### **D. Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHE)**
- ✅ Pre-computed switching angles eliminate specific harmonics.
- ✅ General form: $B_n = (4V_{dc}/n\pi)[1 - 2\sum(-1)^{k-1}\cos(n\alpha_k)]$.
- ✅ Solved **numerically** (Newton-Raphson) — stored in lookup table.
- ✅ Best for **high-power** (low switching frequency) applications.
- ✅ Eliminates **3rd, 5th, 7th, ...** order harmonics selectively.

### **E. VVVF Drive (Block Diagram)**
- ✅ **Building blocks:** Mains → Rectifier → DC link → Inverter → Motor.
- ✅ **V/f = constant** below rated speed (constant flux).
- ✅ **Constant power** above rated speed (flux weakening).
- ✅ Voltage boost $V_0$ needed at low frequency.
- ✅ Three regions: **Constant Torque → Constant Power → Series**.

### **F. Master Formulae Bank**
- ✅ Flux: $\phi = V/(4.44 f_1 N_1 k_w) \Rightarrow \phi \propto V/f$.
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P$.
- ✅ Slip: $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.
- ✅ Modulation index: $m_a = A_r/A_c$.
- ✅ Frequency ratio: $m_f = f_c/f_r$.

### **G. Numerical Values to Memorise**
- ✅ $0.612 \approx \sqrt{3}/(2\sqrt{2})$ — 3-φ SPWM constant.
- ✅ $0.90 \approx 4/(\sqrt{2}\pi)$ — single-pulse fundamental constant.
- ✅ $m_f$ should be **odd integer** — typical values: 15, 21, 33, 63, 99, 199, 999 (high-power).
- ✅ At $m_a = 1$, the maximum 1-φ peak fundamental = $V_s$.
- ✅ At $m_a = 3.24$, output is pure square wave.
- ✅ Eliminating 3rd harmonic in single-pulse: pulse width = 120°.
- ✅ Eliminating $n$-th harmonic in single-pulse: pulse width = $180°(n-1)/n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digital Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Digital Controller (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A **Digital Controller** is a microprocessor/microcontroller/DSP/FPGA-based electronic subsystem that acquires analog feedback signals (current, speed, position, torque) from an electric drive, converts them into discrete-time digital form via **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)**, executes a control algorithm (P, PI, PID, state-space, fuzzy, sliding-mode) in real time using fixed-point or floating-point arithmetic, and generates **gating pulses** (PWM/SVPWM) for power-electronic converters feeding the motor.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT503 – Electric Drives and Control)**, digital controllers are classified into four primary hardware families:

1. **Microcontrollers (MCU)** — e.g., 8051, PIC18F, ARM Cortex-M
2. **Digital Signal Processors (DSP)** — e.g., TMS320F28335, TMS320C2000
3. **Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGA)** — e.g., Xilinx Spartan, Altera Cyclone
4. **Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASIC)** — custom-built controllers for high-volume drives

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The Restaurant Head-Chef"**
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. The **head chef** is the **digital controller**. Orders (reference speed, torque) come in as written notes. Ingredients (analog feedback signals: current, voltage, speed from sensors) arrive in large sacks. The head chef:
> 1. **Translates** the orders into a recipe (control algorithm in software).
> 2. **Measures** ingredients on a digital weighing scale (ADC – converts continuous analog to discrete digital numbers).
> 3. **Decides** how much spice to add each second (real-time computation).
> 4. **Shouts instructions** to junior cooks (PWM gating pulses to IGBT/GTO of the inverter).
>
> Different chefs work at different speeds:
> - A **microcontroller** is like a competent line cook — good for simple tasks, slow for complex math.
> - A **DSP** is a pastry specialist — extremely fast at multiplying and adding numbers (MAC operations).
> - An **FPGA** is a whole team of cooks working in parallel — fastest, but you have to design the entire workflow yourself in hardware.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ where $f_m$ is the highest signal frequency (**Theorem fundamental to digital control**).
- **Standard PWM Switching Frequency in Drives:** $f_{sw} = \mathbf{2 \text{ kHz to } 20 \text{ kHz}}$ (above audible range, below device limits).
- **ADC Quantization Step (LSB):** $q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$, where $N$ is the ADC resolution in bits (**typically 8, 10, 12, or 16 bits**).
- **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB (**standard ADC metric**).
- **DSP MAC cycle time:** **single-cycle hardware MAC** (Multiply-Accumulate in one clock).

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Focus for Module 4 — Digital Controllers:**
> - Architectural features of 8051, PIC, ARM, and DSP (TMS320).
> - Comparison of processor families for drive applications.
> - Selection criteria: sampling rate, computational throughput, I/O bandwidth, cost.
> - Real-time constraints: ADC latency, PWM update rate, interrupt latency.
> - Interface to power converters: gate-driver isolation, dead-band generation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Building Blocks of a Digital Drive Controller

A digital controller in an electric drive performs **five sequential operations** every switching cycle $T_s$:

1. **Signal Acquisition** — Current/voltage sensed via Hall-effect sensors, shunts, or isolation amplifiers.
2. **Signal Conditioning & Anti-Aliasing Filtering** — Low-pass filter with cut-off $f_c < f_s/2$.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — Sample-and-Hold + Quantization + Encoding.
4. **Algorithmic Computation** — Control law executed (PI/PID/state-space) on the processor.
5. **PWM/SVPWM Generation** — Compare register updated; gating pulses sent via gate driver.

> **Step 1 (Why):** Sensors are inherently analog (continuous); the processor is digital (discrete). A bridge is needed.
> **Step 2 (Why):** Without anti-aliasing, high-frequency noise above $f_s/2$ "folds back" into the baseband (aliasing), corrupting control.
> **Step 3 (How):** The ADC samples the filtered signal at $t = kT_s$ and assigns a binary code.
> **Step 4 (How):** Difference equation $u(k) = u(k-1) + K_p[e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T_s e(k)$ is solved.
> **Step 5 (How):** The computed duty ratio $d(k) = u(k)/V_{DC}$ is loaded into a PWM compare register.

## 2.2 Hardware Architectures — The Four Families

### A. 8051 Microcontroller (8-bit)
- **Architecture:** Von Neumann (single bus for data and program).
- **Clock:** 12 MHz crystal → 1 µs instruction cycle.
- **Strengths:** Low cost, simple peripherals (UART, timers, basic ADC).
- **Weakness:** No hardware MAC, single bus creates a *bottleneck* (Harvard variant 8051 exists).
- **KTU Use-Case:** Low-cost small drives (fans, pumps < 1 kW), educational kits.

### B. PIC Microcontroller (8/16-bit)
- **Architecture:** Harvard (separate program/data buses).
- **Clock:** Up to 64 MHz → 16 MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second).
- **Strengths:** Built-in 10-bit ADC, PWM, ECCP module.
- **Weakness:** Limited DSP capability (no native floating point).

### C. ARM Cortex-M (32-bit)
- **Architecture:** Harvard, RISC, 3-stage pipeline.
- **Clock:** Up to 180 MHz → 225 MIPS (Cortex-M4 with FPU).
- **Strengths:** Single-precision hardware Floating Point Unit (FPU), DSP instructions, low power.
- **KTU Use-Case:** Modern servo drives, BLDC, PMSM control.

### D. DSP — TMS320F28335 (32-bit)
- **Architecture:** Harvard, modified Von Neumann with multiple buses, single-cycle MAC.
- **Clock:** 150 MHz → 150 MIPS, 300 MFLOPS.
- **Strengths:** Dedicated PWM modules (ePWM), 12-bit ADC (16 channels, 80 ns conversion), QEP for encoder, hardware CLA (Control Law Accelerator).
- **KTU Use-Case:** **Industrial-grade AC drives (V/f, FOC, DTC) — the de-facto standard in KTU syllabus.**

### E. FPGA
- **Architecture:** Reconfigurable logic fabric (LUTs, flip-flops, DSP slices, BRAM).
- **Clock:** 100–500 MHz, true parallel execution.
- **Strengths:** Sub-microsecond latency, simultaneous multi-axis control.
- **Weakness:** High development cost, requires HDL (VHDL/Verilog) expertise.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Typical Controller | Why Chosen |
|-------------|--------------------|------------|
| Ceiling fan (BLDC) | 8-bit MCU (PIC16) | Cost-sensitive, simple trapezoidal commutation |
| Washing machine | ARM Cortex-M0 | Moderate math, low power, peripherals |
| EV traction inverter | DSP (TMS320F28335) | FOC + SVPWM, real-time torque loop |
| Servo CNC machine | FPGA + DSP | High bandwidth, position loop > 10 kHz |
| Solar pump | 8051 / PIC | V/f open-loop, low switching frequency |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-exam ready. Memorize the table; the `vert` symbol is used wherever a magnitude is needed so the markdown table parser is not broken.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2\,f_m$ | Hz | $f_m$ = max signal freq |
| 2 | ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N - 1}$ | V/bit | $N$ = resolution |
| 3 | ADC Resolution (% of FS) | $R = \dfrac{1}{2^N} \times 100$ | % | 10-bit → 0.0977 % |
| 4 | SQNR (Theoretical) | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | dB | Sine-wave input |
| 5 | RMS Quantization Noise | $V_{q,rms} = \dfrac{q}{\sqrt{12}}$ | V | Uniform distribution |
| 6 | Discrete PI (velocity form) | $u(k) = u(k-1) + K_p\,[e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i\,T_s\,e(k)$ | — | Anti-windup safe |
| 7 | Discrete PID (position form) | $u(k) = K_p\,e(k) + K_i\,T_s\,\sum e(k) + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}\,[e(k) - e(k-1)]$ | — | KTU favourite |
| 8 | Digital PI Z-domain | $D(z) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i\,T_s\,z}{z - 1}$ | — | Bilinear variant |
| 9 | PWM Duty Ratio | $d(k) = \dfrac{u(k)}{V_{DC}}$ | dimensionless | 0 ≤ d ≤ 1 |
| 10 | PWM Frequency | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{2\,N_{period}}$ | Hz | Timer-period count |
| 11 | Sampling Period Link | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_{PWM}}$ (synchronous) | s | One sample per PWM |
| 12 | Throughput Requirement | $\text{MIPS} \geq N_{inst} \times f_{PWM}$ | MIPS | $N_{inst}$ = instr./loop |
| 13 | Dead-Band Time | $t_{db} = N_{DB} \times T_{clk}$ | s | Prevents shoot-through |
| 14 | Interrupt Latency Budget | $t_{lat} \leq T_s$ | s | Hard real-time |
| 15 | Power Loss in Inverter | $P_{sw} \propto f_{sw}\,V_{DC}\,I_{load}$ | W | Trade-off with $f_{sw}$ |

### 2.5 Selection Criteria Decision Logic

> **The "Why" of Selection:**
> 1. **Switching frequency $f_{sw}$** → dictates the **minimum computational throughput**.
> 2. **Control complexity** (V/f < vector < FOC < DTC < sensorless FOC) → dictates **MIPS & MAC requirement**.
> 3. **Number of ADC channels** (2 for V/f, 4–6 for FOC) → dictates **peripheral count**.
> 4. **Cost & development time** → MCU beats DSP beats FPGA at low volume.
> 5. **Latency & jitter tolerance** → FPGA wins for sub-µs deterministic loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Step and Resolution of an N-bit ADC

**Given:** An ADC with $N$ bits and full-scale input range $0$ to $V_{ref}$.

**Step 1 — Total number of discrete codes.** An $N$-bit binary word has $2^N$ distinct codes from 0 to $2^N - 1$.

$$\text{Number of codes} = 2^N$$

**Step 2 — Definition of one LSB (Least Significant Bit).** The LSB is the smallest measurable voltage step, equal to full-scale divided by the total number of intervals.

$$q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Resolution in percent.** Resolution is the ratio of one LSB to the full-scale, expressed as a percentage.

$$R\% = \dfrac{q}{V_{ref}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{2^N - 1} \times 100 \approx \dfrac{100}{2^N}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration for $N = 10$, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V.** Substitute directly into the formula.

$$q = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{3.3}{1023} = 3.226 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 3.226 \text{ mV}$$

$$R\% = \dfrac{100}{1024} \approx 0.0977\%$$

**Step 5 — Physical meaning.** A 10-bit ADC can distinguish 1024 different voltage levels; each step is approximately **3.23 mV** for a 3.3 V reference. Smaller $q$ means finer resolution.

> **Conversion Logic:** The denominator $(2^N - 1)$ rather than $2^N$ is used because the *number of intervals* between $2^N$ codes is $(2^N - 1)$. For large $N$, the difference is negligible and the approximation $q \approx V_{ref}/2^N$ is acceptable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

**Given:** A full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ is quantized by an ideal $N$-bit ADC with uniform step $q$.

**Step 1 — Signal Power.** The RMS value of the sinusoid is $A/\sqrt{2}$; the power into a 1 Ω load is therefore:

$$P_{sig} = \left(\dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{A^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization Noise Power.** Quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-q/2, +q/2]$. The mean square of a uniform distribution over width $q$ is $q^2/12$.

$$P_{noise} = \dfrac{q^2}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Express full-scale $A$ in terms of $q$.** For a full-scale signal, the peak-to-peak range equals $(2^N - 1)q \approx 2^N q$, so peak amplitude $A \approx 2^{N-1} q$.

$$A = 2^{N-1} q$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into $P_{sig}$.**

$$P_{sig} = \dfrac{(2^{N-1} q)^2}{2} = \dfrac{2^{2N-2}\,q^2}{2} = 2^{2N-3}\,q^2$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio.**

$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_{sig}}{P_{noise}} = \dfrac{2^{2N-3}\,q^2}{q^2/12} = 2^{2N-3} \times 12 = 2^{2N-3} \times 4 \times 3 = 3 \times 2^{2N-1}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels.** Use $10 \log_{10}(\text{SQNR})$.

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(3) + (2N - 1) \log_{10}(2) \times 10$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation.** Using $10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ and $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.01$:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 4.77 + 3.01(2N - 1) = 4.77 + 6.02\,N - 3.01 = 6.02\,N + 1.76$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **famous "6 dB per bit" rule**. Every additional bit of resolution **improves SQNR by ~6.02 dB**. Doubling $N$ quadruples the SNR (6 dB × 2 = 12 dB is wrong; doubling $N$ adds 6.02 dB, *not* 12 dB). The constant 1.76 dB accounts for the full-scale sine wave crest factor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Throughput (MIPS) Requirement for FOC on a Three-Phase Motor

**Given:** Field-Oriented Control executed every PWM cycle, with the following operations per cycle:
- 2 Park transforms (4 sin/cos + multiplications)
- 2 inverse Park transforms
- 2 PI controllers (current $d$, $q$)
- 1 SVPWM module
- 1 Clarke transform (3-phase → 2-phase)

**Step 1 — Count instructions.** Per PWM cycle, an approximate count is:
- 4 × 4 multiplications (Park + inverse Park) = 16
- 2 × 10 instructions for PI loops = 20
- 1 × 15 instructions for SVPWM = 15
- 1 × 8 for Clarke = 8
- **Total ≈ 60 instructions per cycle** (single-precision).

**Step 2 — Apply MIPS formula.**

$$\text{MIPS} = N_{inst} \times f_{PWM}$$

**Step 3 — Plug values for $f_{PWM} = 10$ kHz.**

$$\text{MIPS} = 60 \times 10{,}000 = 600{,}000 = 0.6 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 4 — Account for overhead, sampling, ADC EOC wait, interrupt latency (×4 safety factor).**

$$\text{MIPS}_{design} = 0.6 \times 4 = 2.4 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** Even an 8-bit PIC at 5 MIPS can do FOC at 10 kHz; however, **floating-point emulation on an 8-bit MCU consumes 50–100 cycles per multiply**, so the *real* MIPS demand is 50–100 × higher. **Therefore, only DSPs/ARMs with hardware MAC/FPU are practical for FOC.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Architecture Table

| Parameter | 8051 | PIC18 | ARM Cortex-M4 | DSP TMS320 | FPGA |
|-----------|------|-------|----------------|------------|------|
| Data Width | 8-bit | 8-bit | 32-bit | 32-bit | Configurable |
| Architecture | Von Neumann | Harvard | Harvard + 3-stage pipeline | Harvard + multi-bus | Parallel fabric |
| Clock | 12 MHz | 64 MHz | 180 MHz | 150 MHz | 200+ MHz |
| MIPS | 1 | 16 | 225 | 150 (300 MFLOPS) | Equivalent > 1000 |
| Hardware MAC | No | No | Yes (1-cycle) | Yes (1-cycle) | Yes (DSP slices) |
| FPU | No | No | Single-precision | Single-precision (optional) | Soft-core |
| ADC channels | 0 (external) | 8–14 | 16–24 | 16 | External |
| PWM channels | 0 (timer) | 2–4 | 6–12 | 12–18 | Unlimited |
| Latency | High (µs) | Medium | Low (100 ns) | Low (66 ns) | Very low (10 ns) |
| Cost | Very low | Low | Medium | Medium–High | High |
| Best for | Education | Small drives | Servo, BLDC | Industrial AC drives | Multi-axis, custom |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Digital Controller Inside a Closed-Loop Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ref Speed nstar] --> SUM1((+))
    B1[Tachogenerator] --> ADC1
    C1[Current Sensor] --> ADC2
    D1[Position Encoder] --> QEP1
    ADC1 --> DSP1
    ADC2 --> DSP1
    QEP1 --> DSP1
    SUM1 --> DSP1
    DSP1[DSP TMS320F28335] --> PWM1[PWM Module]
    PWM1 --> GD1[Gate Driver]
    GD1 --> INV1[3-phase Inverter]
    INV1 --> M1[AC Motor]
    M1 --> S1[Shaft]
    S1 --> B1
    S1 --> C1
    S1 --> D1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback chain (Motor → Sensors → ADC/QEP → DSP) is the **backbone** of any digital drive. The forward path is **Reference → DSP → PWM → Gate Driver → Inverter → Motor**. The DSP executes the control algorithm in **one PWM period** ($T_s$).

---

## 4.2 Detailed Internal Architecture of TMS320F28335 DSP (Simplified Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU1[CPU 32-bit] --> R1[Register File]
    R1 --> M1[Multiplier 32x32]
    R1 --> A1[ALU 32-bit]
    M1 --> ACC1[Accumulator]
    A1 --> ACC1
    ACC1 --> R1
    MEMP[Program Memory Flash 256KB] --> CPU1
    MEMD[Data Memory SARAM 34KB] --> CPU1
    EPWM1[ePWM Module x12] --> CPU1
    ADC1M[ADC 12-bit 16-ch] --> CPU1
    QEP1M[QEP x2] --> CPU1
    CAP1[eCAP x6] --> CPU1
    SCIA[SCI SPI I2C CAN] --> CPU1
    CLA1[Control Law Accelerator] --> CPU1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The DSP has **three key parallel blocks** that make it ideal for drives:
- **ePWM** — 12 independent PWM channels with programmable dead-band.
- **ADC** — 16 channels sampled sequentially or simultaneously (80 ns).
- **CLA** — A co-processor that runs control loops *in parallel* with the main CPU, doubling throughput.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Which Digital Controller to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Drive power > 5 kW?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{FOC required?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> DSP[DSP TMS320]
    Q2 -- No --> MCU1[PIC or 8051]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Switching freq > 20 kHz?}
    Q3 -- No --> ARM1[ARM Cortex-M4]
    Q3 -- Yes --> DSP
    DSP --> END1[End]
    ARM1 --> END1
    MCU1 --> END1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Follow the decision tree from top to bottom. Industrial-grade FOC at any power level demands a DSP. Small V/f drives can be handled by a PIC/8051. Mid-range sensorless or FOC needs ARM.

---

## 4.4 Real-Time Control Loop Timing Diagram (Gantt-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[t=0: ADC Start] --> T1[t=80ns: ADC EOC]
    T1 --> T2[t=1us: Algorithm Start]
    T2 --> T3[t=4us: Algorithm End]
    T3 --> T4[t=4.5us: PWM Update]
    T4 --> T5[t=Tsw: Next Cycle]
    T5 --> T0
```

**Reading the diagram:** Inside one PWM period $T_{sw}$ (say 100 µs at 10 kHz), the sequence is **ADC → compute → PWM-update → wait**. The *dead-time* (waiting) is when the processor is idle — this slack allows lower-cost MCUs to be used.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PCRUT503]**

### Q1. Define a Digital Controller. List any two advantages of digital control over analog control in electric drives. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A digital controller is a microprocessor/DSP/FPGA-based system that processes feedback signals in discrete time and generates switching pulses for the power converter of an electric drive. It comprises ADC, CPU, memory, and PWM peripherals.

**Advantages:**
1. **Programmability** — control law can be modified in software without hardware changes (e.g., switching from V/f to FOC by uploading new code).
2. **Accuracy & Repeatability** — no drift due to temperature, aging, or component tolerance; deterministic execution.
3. **Advanced algorithms** — state-space, adaptive, fuzzy, neural-network control are feasible (impossible in pure analog).
4. **Communication & diagnostics** — built-in UART/CAN/Ethernet for SCADA, fault logging, parameter monitoring.

---

### Q2. State the Nyquist sampling theorem as applicable to digital control of drives. What is aliasing and how is it prevented? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Nyquist Theorem:** A band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ must be sampled at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ to allow faithful reconstruction.

**Aliasing:** When $f_s < 2 f_m$, high-frequency components "fold back" into the baseband and appear as false low-frequency components, corrupting the control loop.

**Prevention:** Place an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (Butterworth, order ≥ 2) with cut-off $f_c \leq f_s/2$ *before* the ADC, so that any signal content above $f_s/2$ is attenuated to below the ADC's LSB.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PCRUT503, Q8 Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare the architectural features of an 8051 microcontroller, a PIC microcontroller, an ARM Cortex-M4, and a TMS320 DSP for electric drive applications. Use a comparison table covering at least **seven parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | 8051 | PIC18F | ARM Cortex-M4 | TMS320F28335 |
|-----------|------|--------|----------------|---------------|
| Data bus width | 8-bit | 8-bit | 32-bit | 32-bit |
| Architecture | Von Neumann | Harvard | Harvard + pipeline | Harvard + multi-bus |
| Clock speed | 12 MHz | 64 MHz | 180 MHz | 150 MHz |
| MIPS | 1 | 16 | 225 | 150 (300 MFLOPS) |
| Hardware MAC | No | No | Yes (1-cycle) | Yes (1-cycle) |
| Hardware FPU | No | No | Single-precision | Optional |
| On-chip ADC | None (external) | 10-bit, 8-ch | 12-bit, 24-ch | 12-bit, 16-ch (80 ns) |
| PWM channels | None (timer) | 2–4 | 6–12 | 12–18 (ePWM) |
| Encoder interface | None | None | Yes | Yes (eQEP, 32-bit) |
| Typical cost (₹) | 20 | 60 | 250 | 800 |
| Application | Education | Small fans/pumps | BLDC, PMSM servo | Industrial AC drives, FOC |

**Conclusion:** For V/f scalar control of small drives, an 8051/PIC is sufficient. For FOC and DTC of industrial motors, a DSP such as TMS320F28335 is the industry standard, offering deterministic single-cycle MAC, fast ADC, and dedicated PWM peripherals.

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a digital control system for a three-phase PWM inverter-fed induction motor drive. Label every block. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (text representation, see Section 4.1 for visual):**

1. **Reference Generator** — produces $\omega_r^{\star}$ (speed reference) and $I_{s}^{\star}$ (current reference).
2. **Speed Sensor** — incremental encoder or resolver → QEP peripheral on DSP.
3. **Current Sensors** — Hall-effect CTs on two phases ($I_a$, $I_b$); third calculated as $-(I_a + I_b)$.
4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** — second-order Butterworth LPF, $f_c = 1$ kHz.
5. **ADC** — 12-bit, 16-channel, sampling at $f_s = 10$ kHz (synchronous with PWM).
6. **DSP Core** — executes:
   - Speed PI controller
   - Clarke transform (abc → αβ)
   - Park transform (αβ → dq)
   - Two current PI controllers ($I_d$, $I_q$)
   - Inverse Park + SVPWM
7. **PWM Module (ePWM)** — generates 6 gating signals with 2 µs dead-band.
8. **Gate Driver (TLP250 / HCPL-316J)** — provides isolation and level shifting.
9. **3-Phase Inverter (IGBT module)** — produces variable-V, variable-f output.
10. **3-Phase Induction Motor** — the controlled plant.
11. **Feedback** — speed and current signals return to ADC/QEP closing the loop.

**Operation:** Every PWM period $T_{sw} = 100$ µs, the DSP samples currents, computes the control law, and updates the compare register, producing new PWM duty ratios in real time.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the quantization step and the theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of an $N$-bit ADC. Compute the SQNR for $N = 12$ and $N = 16$ and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Quantization step:** For an $N$-bit ADC with reference $V_{ref}$, the number of codes is $2^N$, and the LSB (smallest step) is:

$$q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N - 1} \approx \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$$

**Step 2 — Signal power (full-scale sine):** $P_{sig} = A^2/2 = 2^{2N-3} q^2$ (substituting $A = 2^{N-1} q$).

**Step 3 — Quantization noise power:** Uniform error distribution gives $P_{noise} = q^2/12$.

**Step 4 — SQNR ratio:**

$$\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_{sig}}{P_{noise}} = 3 \times 2^{2N-1}$$

**Step 5 — In decibels:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical values:**

For $N = 12$:
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = \mathbf{74.0 \text{ dB}}$$

For $N = 16$:
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = \mathbf{98.08 \text{ dB}}$$

**Comment:** Each additional bit of resolution adds approximately **6 dB** of dynamic range. Going from 12-bit to 16-bit ADC adds **24 dB**, equivalent to a 16× improvement in noise immunity — important for high-precision torque control in servo drives.

---

**(b)** Explain the role of the following peripherals of a TMS320F28335 DSP in a digital drive: (i) ePWM, (ii) ADC, (iii) eQEP, (iv) eCAP. **(7 Marks, 1.75 each)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) ePWM (Enhanced PWM):** Generates up to 18 independent PWM channels. Each channel has a Time-Base counter, Compare register, Dead-Band generator, and Chopper. The DSP loads the duty ratio computed by SVPWM into the Compare register every $T_{sw}$. The Dead-Band block inserts a programmable delay (e.g., 2 µs) between the turn-off of one IGBT and the turn-on of the complementary IGBT in the same leg, **preventing shoot-through** that would short-circuit the DC bus.

**(ii) ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** A 12-bit, 16-channel successive-approximation ADC with conversion time of **80 ns** per channel. It samples motor phase currents $I_a$, $I_b$, DC-link voltage $V_{DC}$, and analog references. The ADC can be triggered **synchronously by the ePWM** (e.g., at the counter zero event) so that sampling aligns exactly with the PWM cycle, eliminating phase-delay errors in the current loop.

**(iii) eQEP (Enhanced Quadrature Encoder Pulse):** A 32-bit position counter that decodes the two quadrature signals (A, B) and index pulse (Z) from an incremental shaft encoder. It gives rotor angle $\theta_e$ and speed $\omega_r = \Delta\theta / \Delta t$ with sub-degree resolution. Essential for **closed-loop speed and position control** of PMSM and field-oriented induction motor drives.

**(iv) eCAP (Enhanced Capture):** Captures the timestamp of an external event (e.g., input zero-crossing, Hall-sensor edge) into a register. Used for measuring **power factor**, **speed by pulse-period measurement** (M/T method), and synchronization to grid voltage in active front-end converters.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Digital Controller Questions (PCRUT503):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" in $q = V_{ref}/(2^N - 1)$.** KTU examiners *occasionally* deduct 0.5 marks for using the loose form $V_{ref}/2^N$ in numerical problems. Use the precise form.
> 2. **Mixing MIPS and MHz.** MIPS ≠ MHz. MIPS = MHz / (cycles per instruction). For 8051, CPI = 12; for ARM/DSP, CPI ≈ 1. A 150 MHz DSP gives ~150 MIPS; a 12 MHz 8051 gives only 1 MIPS. Many students write "12 MIPS for 8051" — **this loses a mark**.
> 3. **Conflating ADC and PWM sampling rate.** The ADC *sampling rate* and the *PWM switching frequency* are usually **the same** in a synchronous digital drive, but they need not be. State the assumption explicitly in your answer.
> 4. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter.** Any answer involving ADC that omits the low-pass filter before the ADC is incomplete (typical 0.5–1 mark deduction).
> 5. **Dead-band value in µs vs % of $T_{sw}$.** Be explicit: "2 µs dead-band at 10 kHz PWM is 2 % duty loss".
> 6. **Confusing QEP resolution and encoder PPR (pulses per revolution).** QEP gives **4× PPR** in count mode (edges × channels). A 2500-PPR encoder gives **10 000 counts/rev**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Digital controller = ADC + CPU/algorithmic core + PWM peripheral.
- [x] **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ — anti-alias LPF with $f_c = f_s/2$ is **mandatory**.
- [x] **Quantization step:** $q = V_{ref}/(2^N - 1)$ — 10-bit, 3.3 V ⇒ $q = 3.226$ mV.
- [x] **SQNR rule:** $6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB — every extra bit adds **6 dB**.
- [x] **Four families:** 8051 (education), PIC (small V/f), ARM (mid-range FOC), **DSP TMS320 (industrial FOC/DTC)**, FPGA (high-end parallel).
- [x] **DSP strengths:** Single-cycle hardware MAC, multi-bus Harvard, on-chip 12-bit ADC, 12+ ePWM, eQEP, CLA co-processor.
- [x] **PWM dead-band:** 1–3 µs typical; prevents shoot-through in inverter legs.
- [x] **Sampling = PWM switching** in synchronous digital drives.
- [x] **Throughput formula:** $\text{MIPS} = N_{inst} \times f_{PWM}$; always multiply by a safety factor of **3–5×**.
- [x] **ePWM → ADC sync trigger** — sampling aligned to PWM zero event for deterministic latency.
- [x] **QEP gives 4× PPR** resolution; use for position and speed feedback.
- [x] **Selection rule of thumb:** V/f → MCU; FOC → ARM/DSP; sensorless FOC + grid sync → DSP; > 50 kHz switching or multi-axis → FPGA.
- [x] **Real-time constraint:** Interrupt latency $t_{lat} < T_s$ — otherwise the control loop becomes **non-deterministic and unstable**.
- [x] **Cost ordering:** 8051 < PIC < ARM Cortex-M0 < ARM Cortex-M4 < DSP < FPGA.
- [x] **FOC needs ~2–5 MIPS** of floating-point work — only 32-bit processors with FPU are practical.
- [x] **eCAP** is for event capture (period measurement), **eQEP** is for incremental encoder decoding — *do not interchange* in answers.

> **One-line takeaway for the exam hall:**
> *"Digital control of drives = Nyquist sampling + 6-dB-per-bit quantization + ePWM/eQEP/ADC peripherals + single-cycle MAC — choose DSP when FOC is non-negotiable."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontroller and DSP-Based Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Microcontroller and DSP-Based Control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Microcontroller and DSP-Based Control** of electric drives refers to the implementation of drive control algorithms (such as PWM generation, speed/torque loops, vector control, and protection logic) using **digital computing platforms** — primarily general-purpose microcontrollers (e.g., PIC18, dsPIC, STM32, Intel 8051 family) and specialised **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** like the Texas Instruments **TMS320F28xx** series (C2000 family) or **TMS320C674x**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this topic deals with the **replacement of analog op-amp-based controllers** (e.g., LM3524, SG3525 for PWM; analog PI regulators) by **software-driven, sampled-data controllers** that execute the same control laws in real time inside a CPU/DSP core. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *Study of microcontrollers and DSPs in the control of power electronic converters and motor drives — sampling, quantization, PWM generation, ADC interfacing, and implementation of closed-loop controllers.* ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Imagine you are driving a car: - **Analog control** is like a mechanical governor (a centrifugal weight) that physically pushes the throttle based on speed. It is *immediate* but *inflexible* — change the rule, and you must rebuild the mechanism. - **Microcontroller/DSP-based control** is like a *human driver with a brain* — you **measure** the speed from the dashboard (ADC), **compare** it with the desired value (digital comparator), **think** about what throttle angle is needed (CPU executes a control equation), and **press the pedal** (PWM duty cycle updated). The "brain" (DSP/MCU) can be **reprogrammed** to drive any car, can use **sophisticated strategies** (like cruise control + lane-keep assist = Field-Oriented Control), and is **immune to temperature drift** of analog components. ## 1.3 Why Digital Control? — Driving Motivation > [!NOTE] > **Six Pillars of Digital Control Superiority (High-Yield for KTU):** > 1. **Flexibility:** One chip, multiple algorithms (V/f, FOC, DTC) via firmware. > 2. **Accuracy:** No resistor/capacitor drift; only finite word-length quantization error. > 3. **Repeatability:** Identical performance across units. > 4. **Communication:** Native support for CAN, SPI, UART, I²C, Ethernet. > 5. **Diagnostics:** Built-in fault logging, telemetry, protections (over-current, OV/UV). > 6. **Integration:** Single chip houses CPU + ADC + PWM + Encoder interface + Comm ports. ## 1.4 Quantities of Interest in a Digital Drive Controller | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Sampling period | $T_s$ | 50 – 100 | $\mu s$ | | Sampling frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | 10 – 20 | kHz | | PWM switching frequency | $f_{sw}$ | 2 – 20 | kHz | | CPU clock (DSP) | $f_{clk}$ | 60 – 200 | MHz | | ADC resolution | $N$ | 10 – 16 | bits | | PWM counter resolution | $N_{pwm}$ | 10 – 16 | bits | | Quantization step | $Q$ | $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N - 1}$ | V/LSB | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generic Block Diagram of a Microcontroller/DSP-Based Drive Controller A digital drive controller replaces the analog signal chain. The fundamental sub-blocks are: 1. **Signal Conditioning + Isolation** (Hall-effect current sensor, shunt + isolation amplifier, encoder → pulses). 2. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — samples the analog feedback (current, speed, voltage). 3. **DSP/MCU Core** — executes the control algorithm (PI, PID, FOC, SVPWM) every $T_s$ seconds. 4. **PWM Module (ePWM/HRPWM)** — generates the duty-cycle-modulated gate pulses for the inverter. 5. **Gate Driver + Inverter (Power Stage)** — physical switching of IGBT/MOSFET. 6. **Plant (Motor + Load)** — produces the actual torque/speed/position. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Closed-Loop Equation in the Digital Domain (KTU favourite):** > The controller computes a *discrete* error: > $$e[k] = r[k] - y[k]$$ > where $r[k]$ is the *reference sample* and $y[k]$ is the *feedback sample* at the $k^{th}$ sampling instant. > The digital PI controller output is: > $$u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p \big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i \, T_s \, e[k]$$ > This is called the **incremental (velocity-form) digital PI** — the most common form in DSP implementation. ## 2.2 Sampling Theorem & Quantization — the Two Fundamental Limits > [!NOTE] > **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** > A bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples **iff** the sampling frequency satisfies: > $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$ > In motor drives, $f_m$ is the *highest significant harmonic* in the current waveform (often the switching ripple band-edge), so we use $f_s \geq 2 f_{sw}$ for current loops. **Quantization Error:** A finite-resolution ADC with $N$ bits and full-scale range $\pm V_{ref}$ has a **Least Significant Bit (LSB)**: $$Q = \frac{2 V_{ref}}{2^N}$$ The maximum **quantization error** is $\pm Q/2$, with a **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** for a sinusoidal input of: $$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \, N + 1.76 \ \text{dB}$$ ## 2.3 PWM Generation in a Digital Controller Digital controllers use one of two PWM strategies: - **Sine-Triangle Intersection (Direct Digital):** Compute the intersection points in real time and load compare registers. - **Symmetric/Asymmetric Counter Compare (Standard ePWM):** A free-running counter ($TBCNT$) is compared to a Compare register ($CMPA$). When $TBCNT = CMPA$, the output toggles. This is the **hardware approach** used universally in TI C2000 DSPs. The digital duty cycle command (in 0–1 per-unit) is converted to a compare value as: $$CMPA = D \cdot TBPRD$$ where $TBPRD$ is the period register value, $D$ is the duty cycle. $TBPRD$ is set to: $$TBPRD = \frac{f_{clk}}{2 \, f_{sw}}$$ ## 2.4 Difference: Microcontroller vs DSP (KTU High-Yield Distinction) | Feature | General Microcontroller (e.g., PIC18, STM32F1) | DSP (e.g., TMS320F28335) | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Modified Harvard (limited) | True Harvard with multi-bus | | MAC instruction | 1 cycle (some) | **Single-cycle MAC** (pipelined) | | Pipeline depth | 2–3 stages | 8 stages | | Typical clock | 16–72 MHz | 60–200 MHz | | On-chip PWM | 1–2 channels | 6–18 HRPWM channels | | ADC speed | 1 MSPS | 12.5 MSPS (12-bit) | | Floating-Point Unit | Optional (FPU) | Native single-precision FPU | | Cost | Low (₹50–200) | Higher (₹400–2000) | | Application | Small V/f drives, appliance motors | FOC, DTC, sensorless, multi-axis servo | > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick:** "**D**SP = **D**emanding **S**ignal **P**rocessing" — choose DSP when you need trigonometric math (sin/cos for Park/Clarke transforms), real-time vector rotations, and high PWM resolution. For simple V/f fan or pump drives, an 8051/PIC is more than enough. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Electric vehicles (Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3):** TMS320F28377D DSPs run FOC on the traction motor at 20 kHz. - **Industrial servos (Mitsubishi MR-J5, Siemens SINAMICS V90):** DSP-based FOC with 32 kHz current loop. - **Home appliances:** Low-cost PIC/dsPIC runs scalar V/f for washing machines, ceiling fans, air-conditioner compressors. - **Solar inverters:** DSP implements MPPT (Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance) + grid synchronization (PLL). ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Nyquist criterion | Hz | | 2 | $SQNR = 6.02 N + 1.76$ | ADC quantization SNR | dB | | 3 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ | ADC LSB size | V | | 4 | $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p (e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k]$ | Digital PI (velocity form) | — | | 5 | $D = \dfrac{CMPA}{TBPRD}$ | Digital duty cycle | pu | | 6 | $TBPRD = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{2 f_{sw}}$ | PWM period register | counts | | 7 | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2 \, T_{clk} \, TBPRD}$ | Switching frequency | Hz | | 8 | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Sampling period | s | | 9 | $z = e^{sT_s}$ | s-to-z plane mapping (Bilinear) | — | | 10 | $\theta[k] = \theta[k-1] + \omega[k] T_s$ | Discrete integration (speed→angle) | rad | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Digitization of a Continuous-Time PI Controller > [!NOTE] > **Objective:** Show the rigorous s-to-z transformation of the analog PI transfer function into the discrete difference equation actually executed inside the DSP. ### Step 1 — Start with the Continuous-Time PI Controller The analog PI controller in the *s-domain* has the transfer function: $$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$$ This means the controller output in time-domain is the *sum of proportional and integral*: $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$ ### Step 2 — Approximate the Integral by Backward Rectangular (Trapezoidal) Rule Replace the integral by a discrete summation. The **backward-Euler approximation** substitutes: $$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau) \, d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]$$ The **trapezoidal (Tustin) approximation** uses: $$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau) \, d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} \frac{e[n] + e[n-1]}{2}$$ Using the **backward Euler** (most common in DSP due to stability): $$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau) \, d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]$$ ### Step 3 — Write the Discrete Output at Time $kT_s$ and $(k-1)T_s$ $$u[k] = K_p \, e[k] + K_i \, T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]$$ $$u[k-1] = K_p \, e[k-1] + K_i \, T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} e[n]$$ ### Step 4 — Subtract to Get the Velocity (Incremental) Form $$u[k] - u[k-1] = K_p \big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i \, T_s \, e[k]$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\,u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p \big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i \, T_s \, e[k]\,}$$ This is the **non-interacting (velocity-form) digital PI**. The "non-interacting" term means the proportional and integral gains retain their analog meanings independently of $T_s$ — which is a *very commonly tested KTU point*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why velocity form?** It eliminates *integral windup* propagation across samples, allows bumpless transfer between manual and auto modes, and gives smooth output increments — all critical in drive controllers. ### Step 5 — Direct (Position) Form Alternative If the integration is not subtracted, we get the *position form*: $$u[k] = K_p \, e[k] + K_i \, T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]$$ In code, this is implemented by maintaining a state variable `integral_sum` and updating it every $T_s$. This is the form most often seen in MATLAB/Simulink discretized PI blocks. ## 3.2 Derivation: Sampling Period Selection for Current Loop The current loop in an FOC drive must respond within roughly $1/10$ of the speed loop bandwidth, which in turn is roughly $1/10$ of the mechanical bandwidth. The Nyquist requirement gives: $$f_s \geq 2 \, f_{sw}$$ but in practice to allow loop bandwidth, we use: $$f_s = f_{sw} \quad \text{(one sample per PWM cycle — synchronous sampling)}$$ For a 10 kHz PWM, the sampling period is: $$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 100 \, \mu s$$ If we want 4 samples per PWM cycle (oversampling for better resolution): $$f_s = 4 \times f_{sw} = 40 \text{ kHz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_s = 25 \, \mu s$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: PWM Compare Value from Per-Unit Duty Given the desired duty $D$ (0 to 1), the period register $TBPRD$ and counter mode *up–down* (symmetric PWM), the compare value is: **Symmetric (Up–Down Counter):** $$CMPA = D \times TBPRD$$ **Asymmetric (Up-Counter):** $$CMPA = D \times TBPRD$$ (in both cases the formula is the same; what differs is the *centring* of the pulse in the period). **Verification by example:** Let $f_{clk} = 100$ MHz, desired $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz. $$TBPRD = \frac{f_{clk}}{2 f_{sw}} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10 \times 10^3} = 5000 \text{ counts}$$ If $D = 0.42$, then $CMPA = 0.42 \times 5000 = 2100$ counts. The resulting pulse width in time: $$T_{on} = CMPA \times T_{clk} = 2100 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 21 \, \mu s$$ The PWM period: $$T_{sw} = \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 100 \, \mu s$$ Duty cycle (verification): $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{sw}} = \frac{21}{100} = 0.21$$ Note: in the up–down mode, the *effective* duty is $2 \times CMPA / (2 \times TBPRD) = CMPA/TBPRD$ (since the ramp goes up then down), and the pulse is centred — exactly what is required for symmetric PWM (used to reduce even harmonics). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quantization Error in Current Sensing For a 12-bit ADC measuring a current through a shunt, with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V (TMS320F28335 reference): $$Q = \frac{2 \times 3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{6.6}{4096} = 1.611 \text{ mV/LSB}$$ If the shunt resistance is $R_{shunt} = 10$ m$\Omega$ and the amplifier gain is $G = 50$: $$\text{Current per LSB} = \frac{Q}{G \times R_{shunt}} = \frac{1.611 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.22 \text{ mA/LSB}$$ For a rated motor current of 10 A, the number of LSBs is: $$N_{LSB} = \frac{10}{3.22 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3100 \text{ counts}$$ which uses $\approx 76\%$ of the ADC range — a *good engineering practice* (not 100%, to allow headroom for transients). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Overall Block Diagram: DSP-Based Closed-Loop V/f Drive ```mermaid flowchart LR ref[Reference Input
Speed or Frequency Command] --> err[Error Calculator
DSP Core] fb1[Speed Sensor
Encoder or Tachogenerator] --> fbcond1[Signal Conditioning
Isolation + Filter] fbcond1 --> adc1[ADC Channel 1
12-bit 12.5 MSPS] adc1 --> err err --> pi[Digital PI Controller
K_p and K_i T_s update every T_s] pi --> vf[VF Algorithm Block
V by F lookup table + boost] vf --> svpwm[Space Vector PWM
or Sine PWM Generator] svpwm --> epwm[Hardware PWM Module
ePWM1A 1B 2A 2B 3A 3B] epwm --> gate[Gate Driver
TLP250 or SKHI22B] gate --> inv[Three Phase Inverter
IGBT or MOSFET Bridge] inv --> motor[Three Phase Induction Motor
or PMSM or BLDC] motor --> fb1 fb2[DC Bus Voltage Sensor] --> fbcond2[Voltage Divider + Iso] fbcond2 --> adc2[ADC Channel 2] adc2 --> prot[Protection Logic
OV UV OC OT] prot --> svpwm fb3[Phase Current Sensors
Hall LEM LA 55P] --> fbcond3[Burden Resistor + Op-amp] fbcond3 --> adc3[ADC Channel 3 and 4] adc3 --> prot ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Follow the signal from the top (reference) clockwise. The error is computed in the DSP, the digital PI updates the frequency command, the V/f lookup table returns the voltage, the SVPWM block calculates the three compare values, the ePWM hardware latches these to the gate driver, and the inverter applies the voltage to the motor. The feedback loop returns rotor speed to the DSP, while the protection path bypasses the controller and trips the PWM directly on fault. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Inside the DSP (C2000 Family) ```mermaid flowchart TB cpu[CPU Core
C28x 32 bit
Single Precision FPU] --> mem[Memory
Flash 256 KB
SARAM 34 KB] cpu --> mac[Single Cycle MAC Unit
Pipelined 8 stage] mac --> regs[Register File
32 x 32 bit] cpu --> pwm[Enhanced PWM Module
6 channels HRPWM] cpu --> adc[ADC Module
12 bit 16 channel
12.5 MSPS] cpu --> qep[Quadrature Encoder Pulse
QEP Module] cpu --> cap[Capture Module
CAP for time stamping] cpu --> can[CAN 2.0B Controller] cpu --> spi[SPI for DAC and EEPROM] cpu --> sci[SCI UART for HMI] cpu --> i2c[I2C for sensors] cpu --> xint[External Interrupts XINT1 to XINT7
for fault pins] pwm --> trip[Trip Zone Submodule
Hardware Fault Mux] adc --> soc[Start of Conversion
Synchronised to PWM] pwm --> soc ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Architectural Insight:** Notice that the *PWM module triggers the ADC* (via the SOC — Start of Conversion signal). This is **synchronous sampling** — the current is sampled at the exact centre of the PWM pulse, eliminating switching-noise contamination. This single feature is why DSPs outperform microcontrollers in drive applications. ## 4.3 State Machine for the DSP Main Loop (Foreground-Background) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Init Init --> Idle: System Init
Clock PLL Config
GPIO and PWM Init
ADC Calibration Idle --> Run: Start Command
from HMI or CAN Run --> CurrentLoop: Every T_s 50 us
Read ADC
Run Clarke and Park
Run PI d and PI q
Run Inverse Park
Run SVPWM
Update CMPA and CMPB Run --> SpeedLoop: Every 5 ms
Read QEP position
Compute speed
Run PI speed
Update current reference Run --> ProtectCheck: Every 1 ms
Check OV OC OT
Check DC bus Run --> CommsService: Every 10 ms
Poll CAN and UART
Update HMI display Run --> Fault: Fault Detected Fault --> Idle: Reset Command Idle --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Hardware Connection — DSP to Inverter (Detailed Pin Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR dsp1[DSP GPIO 0 PWM1A] --> gd1[Gate Driver U High] dsp2[DSP GPIO 1 PWM1B] --> gd2[Gate Driver U Low] dsp3[DSP GPIO 2 PWM2A] --> gd3[Gate Driver V High] dsp4[DSP GPIO 3 PWM2B] --> gd4[Gate Driver V Low] dsp5[DSP GPIO 4 PWM3A] --> gd5[Gate Driver W High] dsp6[DSP GPIO 5 PWM3B] --> gd6[Gate Driver W Low] dsp7[DSP GPIO 6 TZ1] --> trip[Hardware Trip Zone] dsp8[DSP ADCIN A0] --> cs1[Current Sensor Phase U] dsp9[DSP ADCIN A1] --> cs2[Current Sensor Phase V] dsp10[DSP ADCIN A2] --> cs3[DC Bus Voltage] dsp11[DSP QEP A and B] --> enc[Incremental Encoder] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **List any three advantages of DSP-based control of electric drives over analog control.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Flexibility:** A single DSP can implement V/f, FOC, DTC, sensorless control — only firmware changes. Analog requires a new op-amp circuit for each algorithm. (1 mark) 2. **Accuracy & Drift Immunity:** DSP computations are exact and temperature-independent, whereas analog components drift with temperature and age. (1 mark) 3. **Communication & Diagnostics:** DSPs natively support CAN, SPI, UART, enabling remote monitoring, fault logging, and Industry 4.0 integration; analog circuits cannot. (1 mark) ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **State the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem as applied to current-loop sampling in a PWM inverter-fed drive.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The current waveform in a PWM-inverter-fed motor has a fundamental component at the electrical frequency $f_e$ (typically 0–100 Hz) plus a switching ripple at $f_{sw}$ (typically 2–20 kHz). According to the Nyquist–Shannon theorem, the sampling frequency $f_s$ of the current-loop ADC must be **at least twice the highest significant frequency** in the signal: (2 marks) $$f_s \geq 2 f_{sw}$$ In practice, for synchronous sampling, $f_s = f_{sw}$ (one sample per PWM period) or $f_s = 2 f_{sw}$ (two samples — centre and edge). (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a DSP-based closed-loop speed control system** for a three-phase induction motor drive. Identify the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **discrete-time (velocity-form) digital PI controller equation** from the analog PI transfer function using the backward-Euler approximation. Mention one advantage of the velocity form over the position form. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 marks] **Block Diagram (3 marks):** | Block | Function | Component Example | |---|---|---| | Reference input | Speed set-point $n^*$ from potentiometer / HMI / CAN | Analog input or digital command | | Speed sensor + signal conditioning | Rotor speed measurement | Incremental encoder + line receiver (e.g., AM26LS32) | | ADC | Converts analog speed (or already digital QEP pulses) | 12-bit ADC inside DSP | | Speed PI controller | Generates torque reference $T^*$ | Software PI (velocity form) | | V/f block or FOC block | Generates voltage reference $V^*$ | V/f lookup or Clarke–Park–Inverse-Park chain | | SVPWM generator | Calculates compare values $CMPA, CMPB, CMPC$ | SVPWM software routine | | PWM hardware module | Generates 6 gate pulses at $f_{sw}$ | TI ePWM1/2/3 | | Gate driver | Level-shifts and isolates | TLP250, SKHI22B | | Inverter (VSI) | Switches DC bus to produce 3-$\phi$ AC | 6-IGBT module | | Motor (plant) | Converts electrical to mechanical energy | Induction / PMSM motor | | Protection (OV/OC/OT) | Hardware trip via TZ pins | Comparator + latch | **Key Explanation (4 marks):** The DSP acts as the central computing element. Every $T_s$ (e.g., 100 $\mu$s) the **PWM time-base** triggers an **ADC start-of-conversion (SOC)** signal. The ADC reads the phase currents at the **midpoint of the PWM pulse** (synchronous sampling — switching noise is minimum here). The DSP's CPU executes the **Clarke and Park transforms** to convert the three-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ into the rotating $d$–$q$ frame components $i_d, i_q$. The **d-axis and q-axis PI controllers** regulate the flux and torque components respectively, and their outputs are passed through the **inverse Park transform** to obtain the stationary-frame voltage references. The **SVPWM module** maps these to the three compare registers $CMPA, CMPB, CMPC$ of the ePWM hardware, which generates the six gate pulses with built-in dead-band insertion. The speed feedback comes from a **QEP (Quadrature Encoder Pulse) module** that decodes the encoder A/B channels. The outer speed loop (running at a slower rate, e.g., 1 ms) generates the torque reference $T^*$ that becomes the q-axis current command $i_q^*$. The **Trip-Zone (TZ)** pins provide hardware-level fault protection that overrides software and disables the PWM in less than 100 ns on overcurrent. #### Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 marks] **Derivation (6 marks):** Step 1 — Continuous-time PI in s-domain: $$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ Step 2 — Time-domain integral form: $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ Step 3 — Apply **backward-Euler** for the integral (using $s \to \dfrac{1-z^{-1}}{T_s}$): $$G_c(z) = K_p + K_i \cdot \frac{T_s}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{K_p(1 - z^{-1}) + K_i T_s}{1 - z^{-1}} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ Step 4 — Cross-multiply and take inverse z-transform: $$U(z)\big(1 - z^{-1}\big) = K_p(1 - z^{-1})E(z) + K_i T_s E(z)$$ $$u[k] - u[k-1] = K_p \big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i T_s e[k]$$ $$u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p \big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) + K_i T_s e[k] \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$ Step 5 — Highlight key features (1 mark): this is the **velocity (incremental) form**. The output $u[k]$ is the *change* in the control signal, not its absolute value. **Advantage of velocity form over position form (1 mark):** In the velocity form, the **integral windup** is naturally limited (the output increment is bounded by $K_i T_s e[k]$), and the controller provides **bumpless transfer** between manual and automatic modes — important in drives where the operator may switch between local and remote control. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Compare the **architectural features of a general-purpose microcontroller and a DSP**, with examples suitable for motor-drive applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DSP-based inverter uses a **PWM frequency of 8 kHz** with a **system clock of 96 MHz** in symmetric up–down counter mode. If the desired duty cycle is 0.65, calculate the period register value $TBPRD$, the compare register value $CMPA$, and the on-time in microseconds. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 marks] | Feature | Microcontroller (PIC18F4431) | DSP (TMS320F28335) | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | Architecture | Modified Harvard, 8/16-bit | True Harvard, 32-bit | 1 | | Multiplier / MAC | 8 × 8 in one cycle, no hardware MAC | **Single-cycle 32 × 32 MAC**, hardware | 1 | | Pipeline | 2-stage | 8-stage deep | 1 | | Clock speed | up to 40 MHz | 150 MHz | 1 | | PWM channels | 6 (10-bit) | 18 (16-bit HRPWM) | 1 | | ADC | 10-bit, 200 kSPS | 12-bit, 12.5 MSPS | 1 | | FPU | None | **Yes — single-precision IEEE-754** | 1 | | Application example | Ceiling-fan V/f, mixer grinder | EV traction, FOC, sensorless, servo | 1 (from explanation) | **Explanation (1 mark):** A DSP is preferred for advanced algorithms like FOC and DTC that require real-time $\sin/\cos$ and Park transforms; a microcontroller suffices for low-cost scalar V/f drives. #### Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 marks] **Given:** - $f_{sw} = 8$ kHz, $f_{clk} = 96$ MHz, $D = 0.65$ - Up–down counter (symmetric PWM) **Step 1 — Period register (2 marks):** $$TBPRD = \frac{f_{clk}}{2 \, f_{sw}} = \frac{96 \times 10^6}{2 \times 8 \times 10^3} = \frac{96{,}000{,}000}{16{,}000} = 6000 \text{ counts}$$ **Step 2 — Compare register (2 marks):** $$CMPA = D \times TBPRD = 0.65 \times 6000 = 3900 \text{ counts}$$ **Step 3 — On-time in microseconds (2 marks):** The clock period is: $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{96 \times 10^6} = 10.4167 \text{ ns}$$ The on-time (high duration) in up–down mode spans $2 \times CMPA$ clock periods (from 0 → CMPA on the up-count and CMPA → 0 on the down-count): $$T_{on} = 2 \times CMPA \times T_{clk} = 2 \times 3900 \times 10.4167 \times 10^{-9} = 81.25 \, \mu s$$ **Verification of duty cycle (1 mark):** $$D_{verify} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{sw}} = \frac{81.25 \, \mu s}{125 \, \mu s} = 0.65 \checkmark$$ where $T_{sw} = 1/f_{sw} = 125 \, \mu$s. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $TBPRD$** for symmetric PWM. Many students write $TBPRD = f_{clk}/f_{sw}$, which gives a switching frequency *twice* the intended. *Loss: 2 marks per sub-part.* > 2. **Confusing velocity-form and position-form PI.** Examiners specifically test this. Writing $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s e[k]$ (without $u[k-1]$) is the *position form*, NOT the velocity form. Always include the $u[k-1]$ term for velocity form. *Loss: 1–2 marks.* > 3. **Mixing up PI and PID notation.** In KTU, the convention is $K_p$ and $K_i$ for analog, $K_p$ and $K_i T_s$ for digital — do not use $K_p$ and $K_I$ interchangeably. > 4. **ADC quantization step formula:** Many students write $Q = V_{ref}/2^N$ instead of $Q = 2V_{ref}/2^N$ for a *bipolar* $\pm V_{ref}$ input. The full-scale range is $2V_{ref}$, not $V_{ref}$. *Loss: 1 mark.* > 5. **In block diagrams, forgetting isolation, dead-band, and trip-zone blocks.** These are essential for *practical* drive design; the examiner expects them in the diagram for full marks. > 6. **Not showing the synchronous sampling relationship** between PWM and ADC — a *key* DSP feature. Failing to mention SOC (Start of Conversion) loses 1 mark on the architecture question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Definition:** Microcontroller and DSP-based control = software implementation of drive control laws (PWM, PI, FOC) on digital hardware. - [ ] **Key distinction:** MCU = general-purpose, slow math, low cost; DSP = dedicated MAC, hardware PWM, on-chip ADC, FPU — built for real-time signal processing. - [ ] **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; in drives, $f_s = f_{sw}$ (synchronous) is the norm. - [ ] **Quantization step:** $Q = 2 V_{ref}/2^N$; SQNR $= 6.02 N + 1.76$ dB. - [ ] **Digital PI (velocity form):** $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p(e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k]$ — *the most-frequently-tested equation in this module.* - [ ] **Digital PI (position form):** $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum e[n]$ — used in Simulink discretised blocks. - [ ] **PWM period register (symmetric):** $TBPRD = f_{clk}/(2 f_{sw})$ — *the factor of 2 is non-negotiable.* - [ ] **Compare value:** $CMPA = D \times TBPRD$ — gives the desired duty cycle in hardware. - [ ] **Synchronous sampling:** PWM time-base triggers ADC SOC — current sampled at pulse centre, minimising switching noise. - [ ] **Key DSP families:** Texas Instruments C2000 (TMS320F28xx), Microchip dsPIC33, Renesas RX. - [ ] **Key MCU families:** PIC18, STM32F1/F4, Intel 8051 derivatives. - [ ] **Advantage stack of digital control:** flexibility, accuracy, communication, diagnostics, integration, no drift. - [ ] **Disadvantage to mention in answers:** Quantization error, aliasing, computational delay (one sample lag), finite word-length, cost/complexity. - [ ] **Block diagram must include:** Reference, sensor, ADC, controller (PI), modulator (SVPWM/sine-PWM), PWM hardware, gate driver, inverter, motor, feedback path, protection (TZ). - [ ] **Key transforms in DSP-based FOC:** Clarke ($abc \to \alpha\beta$), Park ($\alpha\beta \to dq$), Inverse Park ($dq \to \alpha\beta$), SVPWM ($\alpha\beta \to $ switch states). - [ ] **Speed sampling via QEP:** Encoder pulses → QEP module → position increment per $T_s$ → speed by $\Delta \theta/T_s$. - [ ] **Numerical tip:** Always state units in the final answer; examiners *love* seeing $\mu$s, kHz, mA explicitly. - [ ] **Examiner's mantra:** Show **all algebraic steps**, label the **assumptions** (e.g., "using backward-Euler", "symmetric PWM"), and **box the final answer** in derivation questions.
